thingsToDoShidaKartli.heroTitle: Things to Do in Shida Kartli: Uplistsikhe, Gori, and Ateni Sioni
thingsToDoShidaKartli.content:
Shida Kartli, in central Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — is a region built on history, with a depth of ancient and recent sites packed into a compact area close to Tbilisi. It's easy to fold into almost any itinerary, and although many travelers pass through on a quick stop, it rewards more: the rock-hewn city of Uplistsikhe, the layered 20th-century history at Gori, an exquisite early-medieval church in a quiet valley, and a working rural landscape between them. Here are the things most worth doing.

What are the best things to do in Shida Kartli?

The highlights are exploring the cave city of Uplistsikhe, visiting Gori and its fortress and Stalin Museum, and seeing the early-medieval Ateni Sioni church in its vineyard valley — with the nearby UNESCO monuments of Mtskheta a natural add-on. Between the sites, the wide Mtkvari valley and its farming villages reward slow travel. The region works equally well as a focused day trip from Tbilisi or a richer overnight for those interested in Georgian history.

Explore Uplistsikhe cave city

Uplistsikhe is the standout — one of the oldest cities in the Caucasus, carved into sandstone cliffs above the Mtkvari River about 10 kilometers east of Gori. Established around the first millennium BC, it was a major religious, political, and trading center for well over a thousand years, with streets, halls, tunnels, storage chambers, and wine-pressing spaces cut directly into the living rock. A large ceremonial hall has a carved stone ceiling that imitates a timber roof, and at the summit a 9th-century Christian basilica was built from stone salvaged from the earlier pre-Christian structures below — a vivid layering of religious eras. Walking the worn paths, ducking through the tunnels, and standing on the open plateau with its views over the Mtkvari valley gives a physical sense of early urban life no museum can match. The exposed sandstone gets very hot at midday in summer, so a morning visit is best in July and August.

Visit Gori and the Stalin Museum

Gori, the regional capital, anchors the area's more recent history. Its medieval fortress, Gori Tsikhe, sits on the volcanic hill at the city's center and gives the best view over the Mtkvari valley. The Stalin Museum is the region's single most visited attraction, built around Stalin's preserved childhood house, his personal effects and documents, and his private railway carriage. Worth knowing before you go: the museum is essentially a Soviet-era shrine, largely celebratory in tone and notably quiet on the repression and mass deaths of the Stalin period, so it's best approached critically — as a window onto how that history was (and is) presented, rather than a balanced account. Engaging with it that way makes it a genuinely interesting and important stop. Beyond the museum, Gori is an ordinary, real regional city — markets, residential streets, cafés near the central square — that has weathered more than its share of upheaval.

Discover Ateni Sioni Church

About 12 kilometers south of Gori, the Ateni Sioni Church stands in a narrow, quiet valley of vineyards and orchards along the Tana River. Built in the first half of the 7th century, it's one of the earlier surviving examples of the cross-in-square plan that came to define Georgian church architecture, with fine exterior stone carving and significant portions of an interior fresco cycle (extended in the 11th century). The setting — river below, forested hills rising on both sides, a ruined fortress on the opposite slope reachable on foot — is much of the appeal, and it sees far fewer visitors than the better-known monuments, so a visit here is unhurried and atmospheric. A car or taxi from Gori is needed, as there's no regular public transport into the valley.

Travel through the Mtkvari valley and rural Shida Kartli

The region is as much about the landscape between the sites as the sites themselves. The wide, fertile Mtkvari valley creates a natural travel corridor, the drives between monuments passing open farmland, orchards, and vineyards with the hills and distant mountains beyond. The villages are compact and working — traditional farming, local markets, homegrown produce — and give an unfiltered picture of everyday central-Georgian life. It's a region that rewards looking out the window and stopping when something catches your eye, rather than rushing site to site.

Seek out the lesser-known sites

Beyond the headline attractions, Shida Kartli is dense with smaller, quieter places that add depth: rural churches and monasteries, archaeological remains, and historic villages largely untouched by mass tourism. They rarely appear on a standard itinerary, but for travelers with a little time and a car (or a good local guide), they offer a more personal sense of the region's long history.

Read the region's historical layers

One of Shida Kartli's real strengths is how many eras sit side by side in a small area. In a single day you can move from a pre-Christian cave settlement (Uplistsikhe), to early Christian architecture (Ateni Sioni), to medieval cultural sites, to 20th-century Soviet history (Gori) — a compression of two and a half thousand years of Georgian history that makes the region especially rewarding for travelers who want context, not just sights.

Combine it with Tbilisi, Mtskheta, and the road north

Shida Kartli sits right between major travel points, which makes it easy to include without adding much travel time. It combines naturally with Tbilisi (about an hour east), with Mtskheta (the ancient capital, on the same corridor and itself UNESCO-listed), and with the route north toward Kazbegi along the Georgian Military Highway. A common, satisfying day pairs Mtskheta, Gori, and Uplistsikhe; with more time, Ateni Sioni and the quieter valleys round it out.

A note on the South Ossetia boundary

The main sites — Uplistsikhe, Gori, Ateni Sioni, Mtskheta — are all well away from the administrative boundary with South Ossetia (the Tskhinvali region) and are visited normally year-round. The northern foothills near that boundary are a sensitive area and not somewhere to explore independently; if you're drawn to the more remote north of the region, go with local guidance [NEEDS: confirm any current local advice on which northern-foothill areas are appropriate to visit].

Slow down and take it in

For all its history, Shida Kartli is an unhurried place. There are fewer crowds than in the headline destinations, the interactions are more relaxed and genuine, and even a half-day here adds real depth and context to a Georgia trip — a quieter, more reflective counterpoint to the busier regions. That balance is the point: Shida Kartli isn't about one highlight so much as understanding Georgia as a whole.

faq.title: What are the top things to do in Shida Kartli?
faq.content: Explore the rock-hewn cave city of Uplistsikhe, visit Gori with its fortress and Stalin Museum, and see the early-medieval Ateni Sioni church in its vineyard valley, with the UNESCO monuments of nearby Mtskheta a natural addition. Between the sites, the Mtkvari valley and its farming villages reward slow travel. It works as a day trip from Tbilisi or a richer overnight.

faq.title: Is Uplistsikhe worth visiting?
faq.content: Yes — it's one of the oldest cities in the Caucasus and the region's standout site. Carved into sandstone cliffs above the Mtkvari about 10 km east of Gori, it has streets, halls, tunnels, and a ceremonial hall cut into the rock, plus a 9th-century church at its summit. Visit in the morning in summer, as the exposed rock gets very hot at midday.

faq.title: Should I visit the Stalin Museum in Gori?
faq.content: It's the region's most visited attraction and an important point of engagement with 20th-century history — but it's essentially a Soviet-era shrine, largely celebratory and quiet on the repression of the Stalin era. Approached critically, as a window onto how that history is presented rather than a balanced account, it's a genuinely interesting stop.

faq.title: Can you visit Shida Kartli as a day trip from Tbilisi?
faq.content: Easily. Gori is about an hour west of Tbilisi with frequent trains and marshrutkas, and Uplistsikhe is a short hop beyond. A popular day combines Mtskheta, Gori, and Uplistsikhe; with a car you can add the quieter Ateni Sioni and the Tana valley.

faq.title: What is Ateni Sioni?
faq.content: An early-medieval church about 12 km south of Gori, built in the first half of the 7th century in the cross-in-square plan, with fine stone carving and interior frescoes. It sits in a beautiful, quiet vineyard valley with a ruined fortress on the hillside opposite — well off the main tourist routes and reached by car or taxi from Gori.

shidaKartli.heroTitle: Shida Kartli, Georgia: Uplistsikhe, Gori, and the Historic Heart
shidaKartli.content:
Shida Kartli — "Inner Kartli" — is the central region of Georgia, the country in the South Caucasus, occupying the broad valley of the Mtkvari River and its tributaries between Tbilisi to the east and the Surami range to the west, with the Greater Caucasus foothills rising to the north and the Trialeti and Likhi ranges marking the southern and western margins. It is the geographical and, in many ways, the historical heart of the country — the territory around which the Georgian kingdoms consolidated, fragmented, and reconsolidated across two millennia. For travelers, it rewards those who look beyond the obvious landmarks (and the obvious landmarks are significant enough) to a landscape that carries its history unusually close to the surface.

What is Shida Kartli known for?

Shida Kartli is best known as the cradle of the Georgian state — the heartland of the ancient Kingdom of Kartli (Iberia), with its first capital at Mtskheta. Its standout sites are Uplistsikhe, one of the oldest rock-hewn cities in the Caucasus; Gori, the regional capital and birthplace of Stalin; the early-medieval Ateni Sioni church in its quiet vineyard valley; and, just at its eastern edge, the UNESCO-listed monuments of Mtskheta. It's the most accessible region from Tbilisi and a rich day-trip or short-stay destination for history and architecture.

The region's administrative center is Gori, but Shida Kartli is best understood not as a set of towns but as a continuous historical landscape, in which settlements, fortifications, churches, cave cities, and ordinary farming villages exist in a density and variety that reflects the area's long role as the core of Georgian political life. The Mtkvari valley here is wide and fertile, the fields and orchards well tended, and the villages connected by roads through a countryside where the medieval and the contemporary coexist without much self-consciousness.

Historical significance

The Kartli region — of which Shida Kartli is the inner, central portion — is where the Georgian state first took coherent form. The Kingdom of Kartli, known in ancient sources as Iberia, was established here in the 4th century BC with its capital at Mtskheta, just north of modern Tbilisi at the confluence of the Mtkvari and Aragvi rivers. For the next millennium, the Kartli kings navigated the competing pressures of Persian, Roman, Byzantine, and later Arab power while maintaining a degree of continuity and developing the cultural and religious identity that would come to define Georgian civilization. Christianity was adopted as the state religion in 337 AD — earlier than almost anywhere in the world — and the early Christian monuments that followed are among the most significant in the South Caucasus.

The region's central position made it the primary target during invasions and the primary prize during reunifications. The Arab occupation of the 7th–9th centuries, the Seljuk devastation of the 11th, the Mongol invasions of the 13th, the Ottoman and Persian campaigns of the 16th and 17th — all passed through Shida Kartli and left their marks. The Georgian Golden Age of the 12th and early 13th centuries, when the unified kingdom under David the Builder and Queen Tamar reached its greatest extent, was anchored in this central valley, and the prosperity of that period is visible in the quality of the religious architecture that survives.

The 20th and 21st centuries added their own layers. Gori's identity as Stalin's birthplace brought a particular kind of Soviet attention, and the conflict of August 2008 — when Russian forces advanced down the Mtkvari valley to within striking distance of Tbilisi before withdrawing — gave the region's geopolitical sensitivity a contemporary dimension that residents feel acutely, particularly in the communities nearest the administrative boundary with South Ossetia (the Tskhinvali region). That part of Shida Kartli has remained outside Tbilisi's control since the early 1990s and occupied since 2008, and the boundary area continues to see periodic "borderization" — fencing and the shifting of the de facto line that has at times cut villagers off from their own farmland. It's a living part of the region's story, and a sensitive one.

Uplistsikhe

The most ancient and in some ways most remarkable site in Shida Kartli is Uplistsikhe, the rock-hewn city carved into sandstone cliffs above the north bank of the Mtkvari about 10 kilometers east of Gori. The settlement was established in the early Iron Age, around the first millennium BC, and served as one of the most important urban and religious centers in the region for well over a thousand years. The name translates as "fortress of the lord" — the lord being a pre-Christian deity rather than the Christian God — and the site's origins as a center of indigenous Georgian religious practice predate Christianity by many centuries.

The scale of what was carved here is genuinely impressive. The settlement spread across a large area of cliff face and plateau, with rooms, streets, tunnels, water channels, and specialized spaces cut directly into the living sandstone. Identifiable spaces include what appear to be apothecary rooms with carved stone shelving, a large ceremonial hall whose carved ceiling imitates the coffers of a timber roof in stone, storage chambers, wine-pressing facilities, and a network of connecting tunnels that allowed movement through the settlement away from the exposed streets. At the highest point, a 9th-century Christian basilica was built using stone salvaged from the earlier pre-Christian structures below — a physical layering of religious traditions that is one of the site's more eloquent details.

Uplistsikhe remained occupied and significant through the early medieval period, serving at times as a royal residence and administrative center. It was eventually abandoned in the later medieval period as the political center of gravity shifted definitively to Tbilisi, and left to the erosion and vegetation that have shaped what visitors see today. The sandstone preserves the carved forms with remarkable clarity, and walking the settlement — the worn paths between chambers, the tunnels that require ducking, the open plateaus with their views across the Mtkvari valley — gives a physical sense of early urban life that no museum display can match.

Gori

Gori, the regional capital with a city population of around 42,000, has its own detailed guide in this series. Within the regional picture, it's the logistical center of Shida Kartli and the natural base for visiting both Uplistsikhe to the east and the Ateni valley to the south. The medieval fortress, Gori Tsikhe, sits on the rocky volcanic hill that gives the city its name and offers the best elevated view over the Mtkvari valley and the plain. The Stalin Museum — whatever one makes of its curatorial approach — is the region's single most visited attraction and an unavoidable point of engagement with the 20th-century history that shaped so much of modern Georgia.

The city itself has a provincial character that is neither especially picturesque nor remarkable but is entirely real — the markets, residential streets, and small cafés and restaurants near the central square give a straightforward picture of daily life in a regional Georgian city that has seen more than its share of historical turbulence without becoming dramatic about it or wholly defined by it.

Ateni Sioni

About 12 kilometers south of Gori, in the narrow valley of the Tana River where it cuts through the hills above the Mtkvari, the Ateni Sioni Church stands in a setting of exceptional quietness and natural beauty. Built in the first half of the 7th century, it's one of the earlier surviving examples of the cross-in-square (tetraconch) plan that became the dominant form of Georgian ecclesiastical architecture through the medieval period. The exterior carries stone relief carvings and inscriptions of real quality, and the interior retains significant portions of its original fresco cycle, extended in the 11th century when Shida Kartli was part of the unified kingdom at its height.

The valley setting — vineyards and orchards on the lower slopes, the river below the church, forested hills rising steeply on both sides — is much of what makes Ateni Sioni one of the more rewarding religious sites in the region. It sits well off the main circuits and sees far fewer visitors than Mtskheta or the better-known Mtkvari valley monuments, which gives a visit an unhurried quality that suits the place. A ruined medieval fortress, Ateni Castle, overlooks the valley from the opposite hillside and can be reached on foot from the church, adding an architectural counterpoint to the monument below.

The villages of the Ateni valley are among the more traditionally agricultural in Shida Kartli, their character shaped by the vineyards on the slopes. The Ateni microclimate — sheltered, south-facing, with well-drained hillside soils — produces high-quality grapes, and local wine production, though small, has a long history here.

The landscape and agricultural character

Away from the specific monuments, the broader landscape of Shida Kartli rewards slow travel rather than itinerary-driven hopping between sites. The Mtkvari valley at this latitude is wide and productive, its floor shifting between grain fields, orchards, and vineyards depending on terrain and the families who work it. The villages are compact and functional, their architecture a mix of traditional Georgian forms and Soviet-era construction characteristic of rural central Georgia.

The Greater Caucasus foothills north of the valley are dotted with smaller settlements and connected by secondary roads that climb through increasingly dramatic terrain toward the Liakhvi and Prone river valleys. These northern districts bore the most direct consequences of the 2008 conflict and the displacement associated with the South Ossetian administrative zone, and the area still carries that recent history visibly — rebuilt communities, some abandoned settlements, and the markers of the boundary zone. Travelers should be aware that the immediate vicinity of the administrative boundary is sensitive and best approached with local guidance; the main valley sites (Uplistsikhe, Gori, Ateni) are well away from it.

Mtskheta

Although administered as a separate municipality, Mtskheta — the ancient capital of Kartli and one of the most important historical sites in Georgia — is geographically and historically inseparable from the Shida Kartli story. At the confluence of the Mtkvari and Aragvi 20 kilometers north of Tbilisi, at the eastern edge of the region, Mtskheta was the capital of the Kartlian kingdom for roughly a thousand years and retains a concentration of early medieval religious monuments — Svetitskhoveli Cathedral, the Jvari Monastery on its cliff above the confluence, and the Samtavro Monastery in the town — among the finest surviving examples of Georgian ecclesiastical architecture. The town and its monuments were designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1994. Mtskheta has its own dedicated guide in this series, but its proximity to Gori and Uplistsikhe makes it a natural part of any Shida Kartli itinerary.

Getting there and around

Shida Kartli is the most accessible region in Georgia from Tbilisi. Gori lies about 85 kilometers west on the main highway, roughly an hour in normal traffic, and the road continues to Kutaisi and Batumi. Regular marshrutka and train services connect Gori to Tbilisi through the day. Uplistsikhe is most conveniently reached by taxi from Gori (around fifteen minutes), or by marshrutka along the Tbilisi road, alighting at the site turnoff. The Ateni valley needs a car or taxi from Gori and isn't served by regular public transport. A car gives the most flexibility for the secondary roads and smaller villages, particularly in the northern foothills and the Ateni and Tana valleys to the south.

When to visit

Shida Kartli is comfortable to visit year-round, and its proximity to Tbilisi makes it a strong day trip in any season. Spring (April–June) offers the most pleasant conditions for the outdoor sites — Uplistsikhe's plateau and the Ateni valley especially benefit from mild temperatures and the green of the surrounding farmland. Summer is warm and the valley fully productive, though the exposed sandstone of Uplistsikhe gets intensely hot at midday, so morning visits are better in July and August. Autumn brings harvest activity to the Ateni vineyards and clear weather that extends the comfortable season through October. Winter is mild at valley elevations, the major monuments stay accessible, and the reduced visitor numbers give the sites a welcome quiet.

faq.title: Where is Shida Kartli and what is it known for?
faq.content: Shida Kartli ("Inner Kartli") is the central region of Georgia, in the Mtkvari valley between Tbilisi and the Surami range, with Gori as its capital. It's the historic heartland of the ancient Kingdom of Kartli (Iberia) and is known for the rock-hewn city of Uplistsikhe, Gori and its Stalin Museum, the Ateni Sioni church, and the nearby UNESCO monuments of Mtskheta.

faq.title: What is Uplistsikhe?
faq.content: Uplistsikhe is one of the oldest cities in the Caucasus, carved into sandstone cliffs above the Mtkvari River about 10 km east of Gori. Established around the first millennium BC, it was a major urban and religious center for over a thousand years, with rooms, streets, tunnels, and a ceremonial hall cut into the rock, plus a 9th-century church at its summit. The name means "fortress of the lord."

faq.title: Is Shida Kartli a good day trip from Tbilisi?
faq.content: Yes — it's the most accessible region from Tbilisi. Gori is about 85 km (roughly an hour) west on the main highway, with frequent trains and marshrutkas, and Uplistsikhe is a short hop beyond. Many travelers combine Mtskheta, Gori, and Uplistsikhe in a single day, or add the quieter Ateni Sioni with a car.

faq.title: What is Ateni Sioni?
faq.content: Ateni Sioni is an early-medieval church about 12 km south of Gori, built in the first half of the 7th century in the cross-in-square plan, with fine stone carving and an interior fresco cycle. It stands in a beautiful, quiet vineyard valley with a ruined fortress on the hillside opposite — well off the main tourist circuits.

faq.title: Is it safe to visit Shida Kartli near the South Ossetia boundary?
faq.content: The main sites — Uplistsikhe, Gori, Ateni Sioni, Mtskheta — are well away from the administrative boundary and visited normally year-round. The immediate vicinity of the boundary with South Ossetia (the Tskhinvali region) is a sensitive area best approached only with local guidance; it isn't somewhere to wander independently.

thingsToDoSamtskheJavakheti.heroTitle: Things to Do in Samtskhe-Javakheti: Vardzia, Rabati, and Borjomi
thingsToDoSamtskheJavakheti.content:
Samtskhe-Javakheti, in southern Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — is one of the most varied regions in the country, combining historical landmarks, forests, fortresses, and high volcanic plateaus within a single southern tier not far from Tbilisi. It's a region where every stop feels different: cave monasteries, a restored hilltop fortress, a forested spa town, and the spare, lake-dotted Javakheti highlands. Here are the things most worth doing.

What are the best things to do in Samtskhe-Javakheti?

The highlights are the spectacular Vardzia cave monastery; the restored Rabati fortress in Akhaltsikhe; the spa town and forests of Borjomi; and the high volcanic plateau of Javakheti with its lakes, including Lake Paravani. Add the ancient Khertvisi Fortress on the road to Vardzia and the region's mix of Georgian, Armenian, and Ottoman heritage. It's a region best given several days, ideally combining history in the north with the remote landscapes of the south.

Explore the Vardzia cave monastery

Vardzia is the region's signature site and one of the great monuments of Georgia: a 12th-century cave monastery carved into a cliff above the Mtkvari River, built under King George III and expanded by Queen Tamar. At its peak it held around 3,000 rooms across thirteen levels; a 1283 earthquake collapsed the cliff's outer face and exposed the caves, giving it the honeycomb appearance you see today. Visiting means walking through interconnected cave rooms, tunnels, and stairways, seeing the main Church of the Dormition with its remarkable 12th-century frescoes — including a famous portrait of Queen Tamar — and taking in the views over the Mtkvari valley below. It remains an active monastery with a small resident community, and as a place of worship it's appreciated if you dress modestly (a scarf is useful for entering the church). One of the highlights of any trip to Georgia.

Visit Rabati fortress in Akhaltsikhe

The Rabati fortress, on a hilltop above Akhaltsikhe, is one of the most distinctive architectural sites in the country — a complex that layers a medieval Georgian citadel, an Ottoman mosque with a minaret, a Georgian Orthodox church, a caravanserai, and a synagogue within one set of walls, reflecting the town's mixed history. A major restoration completed in 2012 rebuilt much of it, so it's polished and visitor-friendly, with museums, towers, and courtyards to explore. Worth knowing: the reconstruction was heavy, and how much is original versus rebuilt is debated — some find it impressive, others a little theme-park — so it's best approached as a vivid introduction to the region's layered past rather than an untouched ruin. Akhaltsikhe also makes the natural base for Vardzia and Khertvisi.

Relax in Borjomi and explore the forests

At the northern end of the region, Borjomi is a complete change of pace — a 19th-century spa town famous for its mineral water, set in a deep forested gorge. You can taste the naturally carbonated water at the source in the central park, walk the forest trails, and use the town as the gateway to the Borjomi-Kharagauli National Park, one of the largest protected forest areas in the Caucasus and the region's best-developed base for hiking. This is the part of Samtskhe-Javakheti to slow down in. (Borjomi has its own detailed guide in this series.)

Discover the Javakheti plateau and its lakes

The southern half of the region is a different world: a high volcanic plateau between 1,700 and 2,500 meters, all open grassland, basalt outcrops, and big skies, scattered with volcanic lakes. The largest, Lake Paravani, is the biggest lake in Georgia, sitting at around 2,073 meters. The landscape is spare and windswept — unlike anywhere else in Georgia — and the towns of Akhalkalaki and Ninotsminda are predominantly Armenian-speaking, giving the area a distinct cultural feel. It's also an important zone for migratory and breeding waterbirds. This is remote, off-circuit travel: rewarding for its emptiness and its lakes, best in summer, and most practical with your own vehicle.

Stop at Khertvisi Fortress

On the road between Akhaltsikhe and Vardzia, Khertvisi Fortress is among the oldest fortifications in Georgia, with origins attributed to the 2nd century BC and standing walls largely from the medieval period. It occupies a dramatic triangular promontory at the confluence of the Mtkvari and Paravani rivers, the walls dropping to the water on two sides — a striking, strategically chosen site that's right beside the road and combines naturally with a Vardzia visit.

Seek out lesser-known cave sites

Vardzia isn't the only rock-cut site in this valley. A few kilometers from it, Vanis Kvabebi (the Vani Caves) is an older, smaller 8th-century cave monastery — a maze of tunnels across multiple levels with a defensive wall from 1204 and two churches — far quieter and more rugged than Vardzia. Smaller cave churches and hermit cells are also scattered through the Mtkvari gorge between Khertvisi and Vardzia, evidence of the same tradition of rock-cut religious life. One important caveat: Vanis Kvabebi has reportedly been closed to visitors in recent years because of rockfall risk, so check its current status before planning to go in.

Experience the region's cultural diversity

Samtskhe-Javakheti has a long history of cultural interaction, and you feel it as you travel. The architecture shifts between Georgian, Ottoman, and Armenian influences; the south, near the Armenian and Turkish borders, has a markedly different character from the Georgian heartland; and the mix of communities, traditions, and religious buildings adds real depth to a trip here. It's one of the region's quieter rewards, threaded through everything else.

Drive the scenic routes

Much of the experience is the road itself. Travelling through Samtskhe-Javakheti takes you from forested gorges through river valleys to open plateau, the landscape changing character as you go, with viewpoints and historical stops along the way. The Akhaltsikhe–Vardzia road through the deepening Mtkvari gorge is especially rewarding, and a car gives the freedom to stop where the scenery and the sites invite it.

Combine history and nature over several days

The region's real advantage is variety: within a relatively short area you can visit an ancient cave city, explore medieval fortresses, walk in a national park, and cross a remote volcanic plateau. Because the sites are spread out, it's best given several days. A natural structure is to start with Borjomi and its forests, spend a day on Akhaltsikhe and Rabati, devote a day to Vardzia and the Mtkvari gorge (with Khertvisi on the way), and — with extra time — add the Javakheti plateau and its lakes. A car or a private tour makes the dispersed sites far easier to link together.

Why Samtskhe-Javakheti is worth exploring

Few regions in Georgia offer this range: a marquee monument in Vardzia, a one-of-a-kind fortress at Rabati, forest and mineral water at Borjomi, and the remote, high landscapes of Javakheti. It rewards both a focused history-and-nature route and a slower multi-day exploration, and it consistently surprises travelers who expect a single character and find instead something layered and varied — one of the strongest additions to a Georgia itinerary for anyone wanting to go beyond the obvious.

faq.title: What are the top things to do in Samtskhe-Javakheti?
faq.content: Explore the Vardzia cave monastery, visit the Rabati fortress in Akhaltsikhe, relax in Borjomi and walk in the Borjomi-Kharagauli National Park, and cross the high Javakheti plateau with its lakes, including Lake Paravani. The ancient Khertvisi Fortress on the road to Vardzia is an easy add-on. The region rewards several days combining history and nature.

faq.title: Is Vardzia worth visiting?
faq.content: Very much — it's one of Georgia's great monuments. The 12th-century cave monastery, carved into a cliff above the Mtkvari and built under Queen Tamar, has interconnected cave rooms, tunnels, and a main church with famous 12th-century frescoes, including a portrait of Queen Tamar. It's still an active monastery, so modest dress is appreciated, and the views over the river valley are superb.

faq.title: Why visit Rabati fortress?
faq.content: Rabati, above Akhaltsikhe, is one of Georgia's most distinctive sites — a hilltop complex layering a Georgian citadel, an Ottoman mosque, a church, a caravanserai, and a synagogue. A 2012 restoration rebuilt much of it, so it's polished and easy to explore; the heavy reconstruction is debated, so it's best seen as a vivid introduction to the region's mixed history rather than an untouched ruin.

faq.title: What is there to do on the Javakheti plateau?
faq.content: The Javakheti plateau is a remote, high volcanic landscape (1,700–2,500 m) of open grassland and lakes, including Lake Paravani, the largest in Georgia. It's a place for scenic driving, lake views, birdwatching, and experiencing the predominantly Armenian towns of Akhalkalaki and Ninotsminda. It's far off the main circuit, best in summer, and most practical with your own vehicle.

faq.title: How many days do you need for Samtskhe-Javakheti?
faq.content: It's best over several days, as the sites are spread out. A common structure is Borjomi and its forests, then Akhaltsikhe and Rabati, then Vardzia and the Mtkvari gorge (with Khertvisi on the way), plus an optional day for the Javakheti plateau and lakes. A car or private tour makes linking the dispersed sites much easier.

samtskheJavakheti.heroTitle: Samtskhe-Javakheti, Georgia: Vardzia, Akhaltsikhe, and the Javakheti Plateau
samtskheJavakheti.content:
Samtskhe-Javakheti is the largest region in Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — by area, and one of the most varied in landscape, history, and cultural character. It occupies the southern tier of the country, stretching from the forested Borjomi gorge in the north to the high volcanic plateaus of the Javakheti highlands near the Armenian and Turkish borders in the south — about 200 kilometers that take in an extraordinary range of terrain, climate, and human history. The name reflects its two historically distinct halves: Samtskhe, the northern and western zone of medieval Georgian principalities, forested valleys, and the Mtkvari River corridor; and Javakheti, the high southern plateau of volcanic lakes, open grassland, and a predominantly Armenian-populated landscape that feels, in places, closer to the Armenian highlands than to the rest of Georgia. Together they make a region that rewards sustained exploration and tends to surprise travelers who arrive expecting a single character and find instead something genuinely layered.

What is Samtskhe-Javakheti known for?

Samtskhe-Javakheti is best known for the spectacular cave monastery of Vardzia, carved into a cliff above the Mtkvari and built under Queen Tamar in the 12th century; for the restored Rabati fortress in Akhaltsikhe, with its layered Georgian, Ottoman, and other heritage; and for the spa town and forests of Borjomi in the north. To the south, the Javakheti plateau offers a spare, high-altitude landscape of volcanic lakes — including Lake Paravani, the largest in Georgia — and important bird life. It also holds the ancient Khertvisi Fortress and the Sapara Monastery.

Historical background

The territory of Samtskhe-Javakheti has been inhabited and contested for an exceptionally long time. The Mtkvari valley corridor was one of the primary routes connecting Anatolia with the South Caucasus, and its strategic importance brought successive waves of settlement, conquest, and cultural exchange over several millennia. The pre-Christian Trialeti culture left remarkable Bronze Age remains in the northern parts of the region; the medieval Georgian principalities of Samtskhe, Javakheti, and Tao-Klarjeti developed here through the early medieval period; and the region served as the southwestern frontier of the unified Georgian kingdom during its Golden Age in the 12th and 13th centuries.

The medieval period left the most visible monuments. The cave monastery of Vardzia, the fortified town of Akhaltsikhe, and a network of smaller churches, monasteries, and fortifications across the valleys and ridges all date primarily from the 10th through 13th centuries, when the Georgian kingdom was at its most powerful and architecturally ambitious. The Mongol invasions of the 13th century ended this period, and the following centuries turned the region into a prolonged battleground between Georgian, Ottoman, and Persian interests. Ottoman control of the Samtskhe heartland lasted from the early 17th century until the Russo-Turkish War of 1828–29, when Russian forces captured Akhaltsikhe and absorbed the region into the empire. The Ottoman period left its own architectural legacy — most visibly in the mosque and the overall character of the Rabati complex in Akhaltsikhe — and significantly changed the region's demographics, as much of the Georgian Christian population was displaced or converted and replaced by Muslim populations from Anatolia and the wider Ottoman world.

Russian annexation in 1829 brought further change, including the settlement of Armenian communities from Anatolia and Persia on the Javakheti plateau and the invitation of German Lutheran colonists to the Borjomi and Akhaltsikhe areas. The Armenians of Javakheti, their community substantially reinforced by refugees from the 1915 massacres in eastern Anatolia, now form the majority population of the southern plateau districts. The German settlers were deported in 1941. Soviet rule reorganized the region's economy and left its architectural imprint on the towns, and post-independence Georgia has worked to reintegrate Javakheti — whose Armenian majority has at times raised grievances about economic development and language accommodation — into the national mainstream.

Vardzia

The cave monastery of Vardzia, carved into the cliffs of the Erusheti Mountain above the Mtkvari River about 70 kilometers south of Akhaltsikhe, is one of the most extraordinary historical monuments in Georgia and the wider Caucasus. Construction began under King George III in the 1150s and was substantially expanded under his daughter, Queen Tamar, in the late 12th century, reaching its greatest extent in the years around 1185–1205 when the Georgian kingdom was at the height of its power and cultural confidence.

At its peak the complex held roughly 3,000 rooms across thirteen stories of cliff face, including a principal cave church dedicated to the Dormition of the Virgin, a throne room, a pharmacy, a wine cellar, a system of irrigation channels that watered the cliff gardens, and living quarters for a monastic community that may have numbered several hundred. The whole was connected internally by tunnels cut through the rock, allowing movement through the settlement without exposure on the cliff face. A catastrophic earthquake in 1283 — striking just decades after the Mongol invasions had already weakened the kingdom that built Vardzia — collapsed the outer section of the cliff and exposed about two-thirds of the rooms to the open air, giving the site its characteristic honeycomb appearance of open cave mouths stacked across the cliff.

The earthquake that damaged the structure also, paradoxically, preserved much of what remained: the outer section fell away cleanly rather than crushing the interior, leaving the inner chambers intact with their original features. Several of the cave chapels retain their 12th-century fresco cycles in a state of preservation that is remarkable after eight centuries of exposure. The principal church holds frescoes of exceptional quality, including a portrait of Queen Tamar that is one of the oldest surviving representations of the queen who presided over Georgia's Golden Age. A small monastic community still lives and worships at Vardzia, maintaining a thread of religious continuity from the 12th-century foundation to the present.

The approach follows the Mtkvari gorge south from Akhaltsikhe through a landscape that grows more dramatic as the river cuts deeper into the volcanic rock. The cliff at Vardzia is visible from the road well before the entrance, and the scale of the excavation — the sheer number of cave openings across the full height and width of the exposed cliff — is one of those sights that photographs convey imperfectly and direct experience makes immediately clear.

Akhaltsikhe and Rabati

Akhaltsikhe, the regional capital with a city population of around 45,000, sits in the Potskhovi River valley at about 1,000 meters and has been the administrative center of Samtskhe since the medieval period. Its defining landmark is the Rabati fortress complex, occupying a large hilltop above the city and holding within its walls an unusual assembly of elements from different eras: a medieval Georgian citadel, an Ottoman-era mosque with a slender minaret, a Georgian Orthodox church, a caravanserai, and a synagogue — a mixture that reflects the successive layers of control and community that shaped the town. The complex underwent a major and controversial restoration, completed in 2012, that rebuilt and in places substantially reconstructed the structures within the walls. The result is visually impressive and well organized for visitors, but it has prompted real debate about the limits of historical restoration — the line between conserving what survives and rebuilding what was lost isn't always clearly maintained. That debate is itself part of what makes Rabati worth engaging with thoughtfully.

Akhaltsikhe is also the natural base for the wider region. Vardzia is about 70 kilometers south, and the Khertvisi Fortress — a medieval fortification of considerable antiquity above the confluence of the Mtkvari and Paravani rivers — lies roughly 40 kilometers along the same road. The Sapara Monastery, a 13th-century complex in a forested gorge 12 kilometers from Akhaltsikhe, preserves some of the finest medieval Georgian fresco painting in the region and combines easily with a visit to the town. (Akhaltsikhe has its own detailed guide in this series.)

Borjomi

Borjomi, at the northern end of the region where the Mtkvari enters through a deep forested gorge, has its own detailed guide. In the regional context, it's the most accessible and most visited entry point into Samtskhe-Javakheti from Tbilisi and central Georgia, and its character — a 19th-century imperial spa town in one of the most beautiful forested gorges in the country — is completely different from the historical and plateau landscapes to the south. The Borjomi-Kharagauli National Park, beginning at the edge of town, is one of the largest protected forest areas in the Caucasus and provides the region's most developed infrastructure for hiking and nature-based tourism.

The Javakheti plateau

The southern part of the region — Javakheti proper — is physically and culturally distinct from Samtskhe to the north. The plateau rises to elevations between 1,700 and 2,500 meters, its surface a landscape of open volcanic grassland, basalt outcrops, and a series of large glacial and volcanic lakes whose still surfaces reflect the enormous skies above. The largest, Lake Paravani, covers about 37.5 square kilometers at around 2,073 meters — the largest lake in Georgia and one of the highest substantial water bodies in the South Caucasus. The plateau has a spare, wind-swept beauty unlike anything else in Georgia, and the sense of openness and elevation — the horizon extending in most directions across a landscape with almost no vertical interruption — startles visitors used to the country's typically enclosed mountain and valley scenery.

The towns of Akhalkalaki and Ninotsminda are the administrative centers of the Javakheti districts. Both are predominantly Armenian-speaking communities with their own distinct cultural life, and the churches, markets, and daily interactions give a picture of Georgian provincial life quite different from the ethnic-Georgian mainstream. Road connections have improved markedly in recent years — the main route from Akhaltsikhe to Akhalkalaki and on to the Armenian border at Bavra is now paved and reasonably maintained — but the plateau remains far less visited than the Samtskhe monuments, and travel here keeps a genuinely off-circuit quality.

The Javakheti highlands are also significant for their bird life. The lakes and wetlands of the plateau support important populations of migratory and breeding waterbirds, and the area has been identified as a significant zone for bird conservation in the South Caucasus. Pelicans, a range of waders, and other species use the plateau lakes during migration, and dedicated birdwatchers visit specifically for these during the spring and autumn passage.

Khertvisi and the Mtkvari gorge

The stretch of the Mtkvari valley between Akhaltsikhe and Vardzia, where the river cuts progressively deeper into the volcanic rock as it descends from the plateau, holds a series of historical sites along the gorge walls that together make the drive one of the more historically dense road journeys in Georgia. Khertvisi Fortress, at the confluence of the Mtkvari and Paravani rivers, is among the oldest fortifications in Georgia — origins are attributed to the 2nd century BC, with the present walls dating from the medieval period (the standing walls largely from the 14th century). The fortress occupies a triangular promontory where the two rivers meet, its walls dropping to the water on two sides and accessible only across a narrow neck from the plateau above — a site chosen with the strategic intelligence visible throughout the medieval Georgian tradition of fortification.

Between Khertvisi and Vardzia the gorge narrows and deepens, the road cut into the cliff above the river and the basalt walls rising on both sides. Scattered cave churches and hermit cells are visible in the cliff faces — smaller and less organized than Vardzia, but evidence of the same tradition of rock-cut religious life that ran through this whole river valley in the medieval period.

Getting there and around

The main entry to Samtskhe-Javakheti from Tbilisi is through Borjomi, reached by road in about two and a half to three hours. From Borjomi the road continues south through the Mtkvari valley to Akhaltsikhe, a further 50 kilometers and roughly 45 minutes. Marshrutkas run from Tbilisi to Akhaltsikhe via Borjomi with reasonable frequency, making public transport a viable way to reach the regional center. From Akhaltsikhe, onward travel to Vardzia, Khertvisi, and the Javakheti plateau needs a private car or taxi, as public-transport connections to the more remote sites are limited and infrequent. The road south from Akhaltsikhe to Vardzia follows the Mtkvari gorge and is paved and well maintained. The plateau roads to Akhalkalaki and Lake Paravani are paved on the main routes, but secondary roads across the plateau can be rough and require appropriate vehicles in poor weather. A car is strongly recommended for anyone covering the full range of the region's sites.

When to visit

The most comfortable and rewarding seasons are late spring through early autumn (May through October), though different parts of the region have quite different optimal windows. The Borjomi gorge and the Akhaltsikhe area are pleasant from April through October, spring especially so when the forests are fully green and the rivers running high. Vardzia and the Mtkvari gorge are accessible year-round but most comfortable from May through October — the exposed cliff and the approach road can be cold and icy in winter, though the site stays open. The Javakheti plateau has a short summer season: the high elevation means long, harsh winters, with snow from November through April and temperatures well below freezing, and even summer nights can be cold. The plateau is at its best from June through September, when the grasslands are green, the lakes fully ice-free, and the high-meadow wildflowers in bloom. Autumn (September into October) brings clear skies, lower temperatures, and the start of the migratory bird season on the lakes.

faq.title: Where is Samtskhe-Javakheti and what is it known for?
faq.content: It's the largest region in Georgia by area, in the south of the country, running from the Borjomi gorge to the high Javakheti plateau near the Armenian and Turkish borders. It's best known for the Vardzia cave monastery, the Rabati fortress in Akhaltsikhe, the spa town of Borjomi, and the volcanic lakes of the Javakheti highlands, including Lake Paravani, the largest in Georgia.

faq.title: What is Vardzia?
faq.content: Vardzia is a 12th-century cave monastery carved into a cliff above the Mtkvari River, about 70 km south of Akhaltsikhe. Begun under King George III and expanded by Queen Tamar, it once held around 3,000 rooms across thirteen levels. A 1283 earthquake collapsed the cliff's outer face and exposed the caves, giving it its honeycomb look; its main church keeps a famous 12th-century portrait of Queen Tamar, and a small monastic community still lives there.

faq.title: Is Rabati fortress in Akhaltsikhe worth visiting?
faq.content: Yes. The hilltop complex layers a medieval Georgian citadel, an Ottoman mosque, a church, a caravanserai, and a synagogue — reflecting the town's mixed history. A major 2012 restoration rebuilt much of it; it's impressive and visitor-friendly, though the heavy reconstruction is debated among those who care about conservation. Akhaltsikhe also makes the best base for Vardzia, Khertvisi, and Sapara Monastery.

faq.title: What is the Javakheti plateau like?
faq.content: It's a high volcanic plateau (1,700–2,500 m) of open grassland and large lakes, including Lake Paravani, the largest in Georgia, at around 2,073 m. The landscape is spare, windswept, and unusually open for Georgia, with a predominantly Armenian population in towns like Akhalkalaki and Ninotsminda. It's an important area for migratory birds and remains well off the main tourist circuit.

faq.title: How do you get to Samtskhe-Javakheti?
faq.content: The usual entry from Tbilisi is via Borjomi (about 2.5–3 hours by road), continuing south to Akhaltsikhe (another ~50 km). Marshrutkas run from Tbilisi to Akhaltsikhe via Borjomi, but reaching Vardzia, Khertvisi, and the Javakheti plateau needs a car or taxi, as public transport to the remoter sites is limited. The Akhaltsikhe–Vardzia road is paved and well maintained.

thingsToDoRacha.heroTitle: Things to Do in Racha: Khvanchkara Wine, Nikortsminda, and the Mountains
thingsToDoRacha.content:
Racha, in northwestern Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — is one of the country's most peaceful and traditional regions, combining mountain landscapes, rural life, and a distinctive wine culture. It's far less visited than Kakheti or Svaneti, which is much of the appeal: this isn't a place for ticking off major sights so much as for slowing down, exploring the valleys, and meeting the people who live there. Here are the things most worth doing.

What are the best things to do in Racha?

The highlights are tasting Khvanchkara wine at small family wineries, visiting the 11th-century Nikortsminda Cathedral with its remarkable stone carving, exploring the regional town of Oni, walking the lakeshore at Shaori, and simply travelling through the forested mountain valleys and traditional villages. Autumn, with the grape harvest and the turning forests, is the standout season. Racha rewards an unhurried, immersive style of travel rather than a checklist.

Taste Khvanchkara wine at a family winery

Racha's signature experience is its wine. The region is the home of Khvanchkara, Georgia's most famous naturally semi-sweet red, made from the indigenous Aleksandrouli and Mujuretuli grapes in a small zone around the villages of Khvanchkara and Nagvazao. Tasting here is a world away from a commercial cellar door: most production is in the hands of small family operations, and a visit usually means sitting in a courtyard or cellar, tasting from a barrel or qvevri, and eating whatever the household has prepared. It's personal, informal, and rooted in traditional methods. Beyond Khvanchkara, Racha also makes wine from rarer varieties like the white Tetra, found almost only here, so a fuller tasting around Ambrolauri goes well beyond the famous semi-sweet red. Because these are family operations rather than set-up visitor wineries, it's best to arrange a visit in advance.

Visit Nikortsminda Cathedral

Nikortsminda Cathedral, about 12 kilometers east of Ambrolauri, is the region's architectural highlight and one of the finest medieval churches in Georgia. Built in 1010–1014 under Bagrat III, the first king of unified Georgia, it's celebrated above all for the extraordinary exterior stone carving covering its facade — biblical scenes, ornamental borders, and symbolic imagery worked into the local green-grey stone with a precision often compared to the best work at Gelati. It stands in a quiet rural setting among village farmland, remains an active church, and rewards a slower visit than most travelers plan for.

Walk the shore at Shaori Lake

A few kilometers from Ambrolauri, the Shaori Reservoir sits at around 1,000 meters, its still surface reflecting the forested hills around it. Created in the Soviet era as a hydroelectric scheme, it has settled into the landscape over the decades into a quietly attractive mountain lake. The shoreline paths make for easy, relaxed walking, it's used for trout fishing, and it's at its best around sunrise and sunset and in autumn, when the turning trees double in the reflections. One of the region's most restful spots.

Explore Oni, the heart of Racha

Oni, the historic center of the region, gives a good feel for everyday Rachan life. It's a small, quiet town worth walking through, and it's home to a historic synagogue reflecting the long-established Jewish community of the area. From Oni you can reach surrounding villages and landscapes, and it makes a natural base for exploring the upper part of the valley. (Oni has its own page with more detail.)

Travel through the mountain valleys

In Racha the journey really is part of the experience. The drive through the region runs along forested mountain roads, river valleys, and scenic viewpoints, passing traditional villages along the way. The roads vary in quality and the mountain driving is demanding in places, but the scenery — especially the upper Rioni valley above Ambrolauri, where the valley narrows and the high peaks come into view — is the reward. A car is the practical way to take it in, as public transport beyond the main towns is limited.

Stay in a guesthouse and experience rural life

Racha is one of the best regions in Georgia for genuine rural hospitality. Family-run guesthouses, homemade Georgian meals, and direct, unhurried interaction with local people are the norm, and the welcome feels natural rather than commercial — you're often a guest in a household rather than a customer. Staying overnight in the villages, rather than passing through on a day trip, is what lets you experience the region at its own pace.

Hike between villages and through the forests

Racha suits light-to-moderate hiking. Forest trails, walks between villages, and routes to mountain viewpoints give plenty of options without the technical demands of high-altitude trekking, and the forested, green terrain is rewarding to be on foot in. It's a good region for walkers who want scenery and quiet rather than serious mountaineering — though for the higher and more remote terrain, local knowledge and appropriate transport are needed.

Discover the smaller villages

Many of Racha's best moments aren't headline sights but small villages — settlements of traditional wooden and stone houses, quiet and largely untouched, with a strong local identity shaped by the region's long isolation. Spending time in these villages, seeing the rhythm of agricultural life, gives a deeper connection to Racha than any single monument. It's the kind of travel the region is made for.

Come in autumn for the harvest and the colors

If you can choose your timing, autumn is the season in Racha. The forests turn through vivid reds, oranges, and yellows in September and October, the grape harvest brings the wine villages to life, and the combination of stable weather and that particular quality of mountain-autumn light makes it the most rewarding time to be here. The harvest runs a little later than in Kakheti — late September into October, depending on the variety and the year.

Slow down

Above all, Racha is a place to slow down. There are no crowds or busy tourist zones, the pace is calm, and the reward is a more personal, more meaningful kind of travel than the main circuits offer. It suits travelers who want to experience a quieter, more traditional side of Georgia — and it pairs naturally with neighboring Imereti to the south and, for the well-prepared, the high mountains beyond.

faq.title: What are the top things to do in Racha?
faq.content: Taste Khvanchkara wine at a small family winery, visit the 11th-century Nikortsminda Cathedral, explore the town of Oni, walk the shore of Shaori Lake, and travel through the forested mountain valleys and traditional villages. Racha is about slow, immersive travel rather than major attractions, and it's especially rewarding in autumn.

faq.title: Can you do wine tasting in Racha?
faq.content: Yes — it's the region's signature experience. Racha is the home of Khvanchkara, a naturally semi-sweet red made from the indigenous Aleksandrouli and Mujuretuli grapes. Tastings happen at small family wineries and are personal and informal rather than commercial, so it's best to arrange a visit in advance. The harvest runs late September into October.

faq.title: Is Nikortsminda Cathedral worth visiting?
faq.content: Yes. Built in 1010–1014 under Bagrat III, it's one of Georgia's finest medieval churches, known above all for its exceptional exterior stone carving. It sits in a quiet rural village about 12 km east of Ambrolauri and is still an active church — a highlight for anyone interested in Georgian architecture and history.

faq.title: Is Racha good for hiking?
faq.content: It's well suited to light-to-moderate hiking — forest trails, village-to-village walks, and routes to mountain viewpoints, in green and scenic terrain. It's more about accessible walking and scenery than technical mountaineering; for the higher, remoter routes you'll want local knowledge and suitable transport.

faq.title: When is the best time to visit Racha?
faq.content: Roughly May to October, with autumn (September–October) the standout for the grape harvest and the turning forests. The main road is open year-round, but the secondary mountain roads can be difficult or impassable from November to April, so the warmer months are best for exploring the valleys and villages.

racha.heroTitle: Racha, Georgia: Mountain Wine Country and the Khvanchkara Valley
racha.content:
Racha is a mountain region in northwestern Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — occupying a high valley system on the southern slopes of the Greater Caucasus, between Svaneti to the north, Imereti to the south, and Shida Kartli to the east. It's one of the smallest and most sparsely populated regions in the country, reached by a single main road that climbs from the Imereti lowlands and ends, essentially, at the upper valleys — there's no through route to anywhere else. That dead-end quality is central to what Racha is. The road goes in and comes back out the same way, so everyone who comes has made a deliberate choice to, and the region has developed on its own terms rather than as a transit zone or a stop on the way to somewhere more famous.

What is Racha known for?

Racha is best known for Khvanchkara, Georgia's most famous naturally semi-sweet red wine, made from the indigenous Aleksandrouli and Mujuretuli grapes in a small zone in the valley. Beyond wine, it's known for the magnificent 11th-century Nikortsminda Cathedral and its stone carving, the forested mountain scenery of the Rioni valley, the quiet Shaori Reservoir, and a strong, distinct regional identity. It's one of Georgia's most remote and least-touristed regions — beautiful, traditional, and visibly depopulating.

The Rioni River rises in the high terrain of Racha and flows west through the region's main valley before descending into Imereti and reaching the Black Sea at Poti. The valley is the spine of the region, and the main settlements — Ambrolauri, the regional capital, and the smaller towns and villages along the river and its tributaries — follow its course through forested slopes, narrow meadows, and farm plots worked by the same families for generations. The population has fallen sharply over the past three decades as younger residents have moved to Tbilisi and the lowland cities, and many upper-valley villages are now only partly inhabited. That gives parts of Racha a slightly melancholy quality — a way of life contracting rather than growing — but it also means what remains is genuinely unaltered, and travelers who make the effort to reach the upper valleys find a landscape and a human environment that feel removed from the pressures shaping the rest of Georgia.

History and identity

Racha has been inhabited since antiquity, and the valley's relative inaccessibility gave it a degree of political independence through much of the medieval period — the local lords of Racha kept their own authority within the broader framework of Georgian royal power, and the region was never fully absorbed into the administrative systems of the empires that controlled the lowlands. That independence left its mark in a strong, distinct regional identity Rachans maintain with pride. The local dialect of Georgian has its own characteristics, the traditional architecture of the upper villages differs from lowland forms, and the wine culture — organized around a set of indigenous grape varieties found almost nowhere else — gives the region a vinous identity as particular as anything in Kakheti.

The Soviet period brought collective farming and some infrastructure — the main road, the Shaori hydroelectric reservoir, light industry in Ambrolauri — but didn't fundamentally alter the region's agricultural character. Independence brought the collapse of the collective system and a wave of emigration that hasn't fully reversed, leaving Racha both genuinely traditional and visibly depopulating.

Khvanchkara and the wines of Racha

Racha's most internationally recognized product is Khvanchkara, a naturally semi-sweet red wine made from two indigenous grapes — Aleksandrouli and Mujuretuli — grown in a precisely defined microclimate around the villages of Khvanchkara and Nagvazao in the central valley. The combination of the mountain-valley climate, the particular soils of this stretch of the Rioni, and traditional qvevri fermentation produces a wine with a deep garnet color, natural residual sweetness balanced by firm tannin, and an aromatic complexity that sets it clearly apart from artificially sweetened semi-sweet wines. Khvanchkara became especially famous in the Soviet period — it was reportedly among Stalin's preferred wines — and that distribution across the USSR gave it recognition far beyond its small production area.

The zone suitable for authentic Khvanchkara is small: the Aleksandrouli and Mujuretuli grapes need the particular temperatures, soils, and exposure of this part of the valley to develop the sugar levels that let natural fermentation stop with residual sweetness intact. Grown elsewhere, these varieties produce wine of a different character, so the appellation is genuinely place-specific rather than a brand name. Most production remains in the hands of small family operations rather than large wineries, and visiting the Khvanchkara area means informal arrangements with local producers — sitting in a courtyard or cellar, tasting from a barrel or qvevri, eating whatever the household has prepared. The directness of these encounters is a large part of their appeal, and because they're family operations rather than commercial cellars, they're best arranged ahead rather than as walk-ins.

Beyond Khvanchkara, Racha produces wines from a wider range of indigenous varieties, including Tetra, a white grape found almost exclusively here, and several lesser-known reds that local producers are increasingly working with as interest in Georgia's viticultural diversity has grown. A thorough tasting around Ambrolauri covers a spectrum well beyond the famous semi-sweet red and gives a fuller picture of what this mountain-valley viticulture actually makes.

Nikortsminda Cathedral

The most architecturally significant monument in Racha is Nikortsminda Cathedral, in the village of the same name about 12 kilometers east of Ambrolauri. Built in 1010–1014 during the reign of Bagrat III of Georgia — the first king of the unified Georgian kingdom — it's considered one of the finest examples of medieval Georgian ecclesiastical architecture, notable above all for the extraordinary quality and quantity of its exterior stone relief carvings. The facade is covered in intricately worked decorative programs — biblical scenes, ornamental borders, symbolic imagery, geometric patterns — executed in the local green-grey stone with a precision and confidence that speak to the skill and ambition of the early-11th-century craftsmen. The carving is frequently compared to the best work at Gelati and Svetitskhoveli, and the comparison holds: this is among the finest surviving Georgian medieval stone sculpture.

The cathedral stands in a quiet rural setting amid the farmland of Nikortsminda village, with forested hills rising around it. It remains an active place of worship, with a small parish community maintaining the building and grounds. The interior retains fragments of fresco painting (the surviving frescoes mainly date from the 17th century), though the exterior carving is the primary artistic achievement. The cathedral was damaged in the 1991 Racha earthquake and subsequently restored. Because Nikortsminda sits off the main tourist circuits and needs a deliberate detour from the valley road, visits here are rarely rushed — the combination of the architecture and the peaceful setting makes it one of those monuments that rewards more time than most visitors initially plan to give it.

Shaori Reservoir

A few kilometers from Ambrolauri, the Shaori Reservoir fills a widening of the Shaori valley at around 1,000 meters, its still surface reflecting the forested hillsides that rise on all sides. It was created in the Soviet era as part of a hydroelectric scheme, and bears no resemblance to a natural lake in its origins — but the decades since have given the surrounding landscape time to settle, and the result is a body of water in forested mountain terrain that is quietly attractive in a way that owes nothing to its industrial genesis. It's used for fishing — trout are the main catch — and the shoreline paths make for easy walking. It's most rewarding in autumn, when the deciduous trees among the conifers on the hillsides turn and the reflections in the still water combine with the low October light.

The upper valleys

The roads climbing above Ambrolauri into the upper tributaries of the Rioni lead to the most remote and least altered parts of Racha — villages where traditional stone-and-timber architecture has survived partly through use and partly through simple neglect, where the agricultural calendar still organizes daily life, and where the connection to the landscape is immediate. The upper Rioni valley, continuing east from Ambrolauri, passes through increasingly dramatic scenery as the valley narrows and the high peaks come into view, with wide alpine meadows, clear rivers, and the kind of undisturbed mountain environment that takes effort to reach and rewards it proportionally.

A note on Shovi: the upper Rioni valley was the site of a major natural disaster on 3 August 2023, when a glacial mudflow descended from the high mountains and destroyed the resort settlement of Shovi, killing dozens of people. It was one of the worst natural disasters in Georgia's recent history. The site is not a functioning resort and is not a place to visit; the area remains affected, and access and safety conditions in this part of the upper valley should be checked carefully and locally before any travel here. We mention it because older guides still describe Shovi as a working spa resort, which is no longer the case.

The northern valleys of Racha, reached by secondary roads climbing toward the main Caucasus ridge, lead to some of the most isolated inhabited territory in the region. These areas see very few visitors and need good local knowledge and appropriate transport, but for prepared travelers they offer a Caucasian mountain landscape and way of life that is increasingly rare anywhere in the region.

Getting there and around

Racha is about four to five hours from Tbilisi by road, the route passing through Imereti and climbing into the Rioni valley on a road that is scenic but demanding, with long sections of mountain driving. From Kutaisi it's shorter, around two and a half to three hours. Marshrutkas connect Ambrolauri to Tbilisi and Kutaisi, though the schedule is limited and the journey long. Within the region, reaching Nikortsminda, the Khvanchkara villages, Shaori, and the upper valleys is most practical by car — public transport within Racha is infrequent and the more remote spots aren't served at all. The mountain roads vary considerably in quality, and the upper-valley routes in particular need a vehicle with adequate ground clearance, especially in wet conditions.

When to visit

The accessible season for most of Racha runs May through October, with the main road from Imereti open year-round but the secondary mountain roads potentially difficult or impassable from November through April. Spring brings the forests to life and the rivers to their highest, and the valley is genuinely beautiful from late April as the vegetation responds to the warmth. Summer is warm in the valley and cool in the higher terrain, making June and July pleasant for both valley exploration and higher walking. Autumn is widely considered the best season: the grape harvest brings activity to the wine villages, the forests turn through their full range of color in September and October, and the combination of stable weather, harvest atmosphere, and the particular quality of autumn light in a mountain valley makes it the most rewarding time for most travelers. The harvest in Racha runs slightly later than in Kakheti — from late September into October, depending on variety and the year.

faq.title: Where is Racha and what is it known for?
faq.content: Racha is a remote mountain region in northwestern Georgia, on the southern slopes of the Greater Caucasus between Svaneti, Imereti, and Shida Kartli, with Ambrolauri as its capital. It's best known for Khvanchkara wine, the 11th-century Nikortsminda Cathedral, and the forested Rioni valley — one of Georgia's least-visited and most traditional regions.

faq.title: What is Khvanchkara wine?
faq.content: Khvanchkara is a naturally semi-sweet red wine from Racha, made from the indigenous Aleksandrouli and Mujuretuli grapes grown in a small, specific zone around the villages of Khvanchkara and Nagvazao. Its natural sweetness comes from the grapes and the mountain microclimate, not added sugar. It was famous across the Soviet Union and reportedly a favorite of Stalin.

faq.title: Is Nikortsminda Cathedral worth visiting?
faq.content: Yes. Built in 1010–1014 under Bagrat III, the first king of unified Georgia, it's one of the finest medieval Georgian churches, celebrated above all for its exceptional exterior stone carving — comparable to the best work at Gelati. It sits in a quiet rural setting about 12 km east of Ambrolauri and is an active church.

faq.title: How do you get to Racha?
faq.content: By road: about four to five hours from Tbilisi, or two and a half to three hours from Kutaisi, on a scenic but demanding mountain route. Marshrutkas link Ambrolauri to both cities but run on a limited schedule. Within Racha, a car is by far the most practical way to reach the cathedral, the wine villages, Shaori, and the upper valleys.

faq.title: When is the best time to visit Racha?
faq.content: Roughly May to October. Autumn (September–October) is the most rewarding, with the grape harvest and the forests turning color, though the harvest here runs a little later than in Kakheti. Spring and summer are beautiful too; the secondary mountain roads can be difficult or impassable from November to April.

thingsToDoMtskhetaMtianeti.heroTitle: Things to Do in Mtskheta-Mtianeti: Mtskheta, the Military Highway, and Kazbegi
thingsToDoMtskhetaMtianeti.content:
Mtskheta-Mtianeti, directly north of Tbilisi in Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — is one of the most complete regions in the country, combining religious heritage, a famous scenic drive, and high Caucasus landscapes in a compact, easy-to-reach area. For first-time visitors it's close to essential: it runs from the UNESCO-listed monuments of the ancient capital up the Georgian Military Highway to the dramatic peaks around Mount Kazbek, packing some of Georgia's most memorable experiences into a single corridor. Here are the things most worth doing.

What are the best things to do in Mtskheta-Mtianeti?

The highlights are visiting Mtskheta's UNESCO monuments (Svetitskhoveli Cathedral and Jvari Monastery); driving the Georgian Military Highway with its viewpoints; stopping at Ananuri fortress above its turquoise reservoir; skiing or hiking at Gudauri; and reaching Stepantsminda (Kazbegi) for the iconic Gergeti Trinity Church beneath Mount Kazbek. The Russia-Georgia Friendship Monument and the remote Truso valley round out the route. Much of it can be done as a day trip from Tbilisi, though the mountains reward longer.

Visit Mtskheta, the spiritual capital

Start in Mtskheta, one of the oldest cities in Georgia and the spiritual center of the Georgian Orthodox Church. The two essential sites are the Svetitskhoveli Cathedral — the great 11th-century cathedral (built 1010–1029) at the heart of the old town, said to hold the burial place of Christ's robe, which makes it one of the most sacred sites in Georgian Orthodoxy — and the Jvari Monastery, the late-6th/early-7th-century church on the cliff above, one of the earliest examples of the Georgian cross-in-square plan. From the Jvari terrace the view over the confluence of the Aragvi and Mtkvari rivers, with Svetitskhoveli below in the town, is among the most celebrated panoramas in the country. Both are UNESCO World Heritage Sites; both are active churches, so modest dress is required (covered shoulders and legs, and a headscarf for women). Just 20 minutes from Tbilisi, Mtskheta is the natural starting point for the region. (It has its own detailed guide in this series.)

Drive the Georgian Military Highway

The Georgian Military Highway is one of the great scenic drives of the Caucasus, and travelling it is an experience in itself. Running north from the Mtskheta junction up the Aragvi valley to the Russian border, it climbs through landscapes that change character with the altitude — forested gorges, the turquoise Zhinvali Reservoir, open sub-alpine slopes, the high Jvari Pass, and finally the sheer-walled Dariali Gorge. There are viewpoints and photo stops the whole way, and the route ties together most of the region's highlights, so much of a trip here is really about the road and what you stop for along it.

Stop at Ananuri fortress

Ananuri, on the highway above the Zhinvali Reservoir, is one of the most photographed sites in Georgia — but it rewards more than a quick photo. The 17th-century fortress complex has two churches within its walls (the larger, the 1689 Church of the Assumption, carries fine carved-stone ornament), plus defensive towers and walls that give a clear sense of a Georgian feudal stronghold. The setting, with the medieval stonework above the turquoise water and forested hills, is the defining image; the views from the upper walls are excellent in clear weather. Worth an hour rather than ten minutes.

Gudauri: mountains and adventure

Gudauri, at around 2,200 meters, is Georgia's main high-altitude mountain resort. In winter it's the country's premier ski and snowboard destination, with open, above-treeline terrain; in summer it's a base for paragliding (the flights over the valley are a signature Gudauri experience), hiking, and mountain walks. The wide-open alpine setting and the easy access from the highway make it a key stop on any mountain itinerary. (Gudauri has its own detailed guide in this series.)

Kazbegi and the Gergeti Trinity Church

Stepantsminda — still widely called Kazbegi — is the gateway to the high Caucasus, and its great highlight is the Gergeti Trinity Church: a 14th-century church standing alone at 2,170 meters on a ridge above the town, with Mount Kazbek (5,054 meters) rising behind it. The image of the church against the snow-capped peak is one of the most iconic in Georgia. You can reach it on foot from Stepantsminda (a steep but straightforward walk of a couple of hours) or by 4x4 taxi from the village. It's an active church, so modest dress and a headscarf for women are required. (Stepantsminda/Kazbegi has its own detailed guide.)

Hike in the Kazbegi area

The Kazbegi district is one of the best places in Georgia for mountain walking. Beyond the popular trail up to Gergeti Trinity Church, there are walks to waterfalls and viewpoints and full-day hikes into the surrounding valleys — including the longer trek toward the Gergeti glacier for experienced, well-prepared walkers. The high terrain is most reliable for hiking in summer (July and August), and the scenery is consistently spectacular. For the longer and higher routes, good preparation and, in places, local guidance are sensible.

See the Russia-Georgia Friendship Monument

Near Gudauri, the Russia-Georgia Friendship Monument is one of the best panoramic stops on the highway. It's a large circular structure decorated with colorful mosaic murals, built in the Soviet era (1983, marking the 200th anniversary of the Treaty of Georgievsk), perched on the edge above a deep mountain valley. The murals are striking, but the real draw is the view out over the Devil's Valley and the surrounding peaks — a near-mandatory photo stop on the drive north.

Explore the Truso Valley

For something quieter and more off the beaten path, the Truso valley, branching west from the highway near Kazbegi, is a rewarding detour. It's known for its travertine mineral springs and the rust-and-ochre colors they stain the ground, along with abandoned villages, medieval ruins, and defensive towers, all in a peaceful, remote setting. It sees far fewer visitors than the Gergeti church and suits travelers looking for a more solitary mountain experience.

Make the most of the route

One of the best things about Mtskheta-Mtianeti is how much sits along a single road. The landscape changes dramatically as you climb, there are frequent reasons to stop, and you can reach several major highlights in one day. How you do it depends on your time: a day trip from Tbilisi can cover Mtskheta, Ananuri, and Gudauri; a long day can extend to Kazbegi and the Gergeti church; and a multi-day trip lets you add real hiking, the Truso valley, and a slower pace in the mountains. Marshrutkas run the highway from Tbilisi's Didube station, but a car or private transfer gives far more freedom to stop along the way.

Why Mtskheta-Mtianeti is essential
