Kisi, another indigenous white, has drawn particular attention in recent years for its performance in qvevri winemaking, producing amber wines of considerable depth and aging potential.

Saperavi is the undisputed red grape of Kakheti and one of the most important Georgian varieties internationally. Deeply pigmented — the name means “dye” or “painter” in Georgian, a reference to its colored flesh as well as skin — and naturally high in both tannin and acidity, it makes reds of considerable structure and aging potential, ranging from full-extraction qvevri styles to more polished versions made in stainless steel and oak. The Kindzmarauli and Akhasheni appellations, producing naturally semi-sweet Saperavi wines from specific microclimates in the eastern Alazani valley, are among the most commercially successful Georgian wine categories internationally and have given Saperavi its widest recognition.

Georgia has over 500 documented indigenous grape varieties — the large majority found in Kakheti and the surrounding regions — and a significant revival of interest in minor and neglected varieties has been underway since the 2000s. Producers working with grapes like Tavkveri, Shavkapito, Chinuri, and others have expanded the palette of what Kakhetian wine can be, and the most adventurous tastings now go well beyond the established Rkatsiteli-and-Saperavi framework.

The Rtveli (grape harvest)

The grape harvest — the Rtveli — runs through September into October depending on the variety and the year's weather, and it's the single most important event in the Kakhetian agricultural and cultural calendar. It's a communal effort involving extended family networks, neighbors, and often willing visitors who take part in exchange for food, wine, and an experience no organized tour can fully replicate. During harvest the vineyards are alive from early morning, the air carries the smell of fresh juice and fermenting grape from the processing underway in every village, and the energy of the season is palpable throughout the region in a way that's simply absent at other times.

Taking part in the Rtveli — picking grapes alongside a family, carrying the harvest to the cellar, watching the juice flow into the qvevri — gives a physical, sensory understanding of the winemaking tradition that no tasting or tour can match. Many guesthouses and wine estates offer harvest participation as part of their autumn programming, and the period from the third week of September through the second week of October is consistently the most rewarding time to visit for anyone whose primary interest is wine and its culture. (Dates shift year to year with the weather, so it's worth confirming timing close to your trip.)

Telavi and the regional towns

Telavi, the administrative center of Kakheti, is the practical hub of the region and has its own detailed guide in this series. As a base for wine tourism and for the major monuments of the Alazani valley — Alaverdi Cathedral, Tsinandali Estate, Ikalto Monastery, and Gremi — it's unmatched for accommodation range, restaurant quality, and convenience. The city itself has genuine historical character, centered on the Batonis Tsikhe fortress that served as the royal residence of the Kakhetian kings in the 18th century.

Sighnaghi, on its ridge above the western Alazani valley with 18th-century defensive walls and panoramic views, is the most visually distinctive of the smaller towns and the one that has most successfully built a tourism economy around its architecture and setting. Kvareli, at the eastern end of the valley nearest the Caucasus foothills, gives access to the Kindzmarauli appellation and the Nekresi Monastery. Gurjaani, in the central valley, is less visited than either Telavi or Sighnaghi but offers a more direct engagement with the everyday agricultural life of the region, alongside its own architectural monument, the unusual twin-domed Kvelatsminda Church. (Sighnaghi, Kvareli, and Gurjaani each have their own detailed guides in this series.)

The monasteries

The concentration of significant medieval monasteries in Kakheti is among the highest of any region in Georgia, and visiting them adds a historical dimension that the wine culture alone can't provide.

Alaverdi Cathedral, rising from the flat Alazani valley floor about 20 kilometers east of Telavi, is one of the tallest and most impressive medieval religious buildings in Georgia — its 11th-century structure visible across the vineyards from a considerable distance, its interior holding frescoes and architectural details of the highest quality. The monastery is active, and the combination of the monastic community, the agricultural setting, and the medieval architecture gives it a completeness that purely architectural monuments lack. Bodbe Convent, just below Sighnaghi, is the burial place of St. Nino and one of the most important pilgrimage destinations in Georgia. Nekresi Monastery, on a forested hillside above the eastern valley, preserves buildings from the earliest centuries of Georgian Christianity in a setting of considerable natural beauty. Ikalto Monastery near Telavi combines religious architecture with the remains of the medieval academy that made it one of the most significant intellectual institutions in the Georgian world.

Food and the supra

Kakhetian cuisine rests on the same foundations as Georgian cooking generally — walnut-based sauces, herb-rich salads, freshly baked breads, slow-cooked meat dishes — but with the specific character of an agricultural region where the raw ingredients are excellent and the link between food production and consumption is direct. The local cheese, a fresh cow's-milk variety of clean, slightly tangy flavor, differs from the sulguni of western Georgia and the Imeretian cheese made across the ridge. The bread baked in the tone oven — the elongated shotis puri of eastern Georgia — is among the best in the country. Mtsvadi, meat grilled over vine cuttings whose aromatic smoke flavors the fat, is the characteristic Kakhetian preparation for special occasions and the dish most associated with the outdoor feasting of the harvest season.

The supra — the traditional Georgian feast presided over by a tamada who leads the table through a sequence of toasts — is experienced throughout Georgia, but in Kakheti it has a particular character shaped by the closeness of the wine and the directness of the agricultural setting. A supra at a family winery, with the qvevri visible in the cellar floor beneath the table and the vineyards outside the door, connects the ritual of the feast to the physical reality of its production in a way that urban restaurant versions can't replicate.

Getting there and around

Kakheti is roughly 90 to 140 kilometers from Tbilisi depending on the destination within the region, the journey taking between one and a half and two and a half hours by road. Two main routes connect the capital with the Alazani valley: the northern route via the Gombori Pass, scenic and forested, descending into Kakheti through Telavi; and the more direct southern route across the Kartli plains and through the Iori valley, faster and less dramatic. Marshrutkas run from Tbilisi's Samgori metro station to Telavi, Sighnaghi, Kvareli, and other towns with reasonable frequency. A car gives significantly more flexibility for reaching the villages, family wineries, and monastery sites off the main public-transport routes — and for the harvest in particular, independent transport transforms the range of experiences available. Many Tbilisi-based operators run day trips and multi-day wine tours to Kakheti, a practical option for those without a vehicle.

When to visit

September and October — the harvest season — are the peak of the Kakhetian year and the most rewarding time to visit for anyone interested in wine culture. The combination of the active harvest, the fermenting wine, the clear autumn light over the valley, and the general intensity of the season gives the region a character at this time that's simply unavailable otherwise. Spring (April through early June) brings green vineyards, wildflowers on the Caucasus foothills, and comfortable temperatures well suited to driving and walking the valley. Summer is warm and productive — the vines in full growth, the landscape fully alive — but July and August can be genuinely hot in the valley, and the harvest hasn't yet begun, which removes the element most specific to Kakheti. Winter brings cold temperatures and bare vineyards; the stripped-down austerity has its own appeal for some, but most family wineries and smaller guesthouses run reduced schedules outside the main season.

faq.title: Where is Kakheti and what is it known for?
faq.content: Kakheti is the easternmost region of Georgia, in the Alazani valley between the Greater Caucasus and the Gombori ranges. It's Georgia's main wine region, producing most of the country's wine and home to the ancient qvevri winemaking tradition. Beyond wine, it's known for major medieval monasteries (Alaverdi, Bodbe, Nekresi, Ikalto), the autumn Rtveli harvest, and the towns of Telavi and Sighnaghi.

faq.title: What is qvevri wine?
faq.content: Qvevri wine is made in large clay vessels buried in the cellar floor, where the grapes ferment and age — for whites, in extended contact with their skins for several months. This produces deep amber (or “orange”) wines with a tannic structure more like red wine and a distinctive complexity. The method is around 8,000 years old in Georgia and is recognized as UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage. Rkatsiteli is the classic white grape for it; Saperavi is the great red.

faq.title: What is the best time to visit Kakheti for wine?
faq.content: The grape harvest, or Rtveli — roughly late September through the second week of October — is the most rewarding time, when the vineyards are being picked, the wine is fermenting, and many estates and guesthouses offer harvest participation. Spring (April–June) is also lovely for the green valley and comfortable weather. Exact harvest dates shift year to year with the weather, so confirm timing close to your trip.

faq.title: What monasteries should I visit in Kakheti?
faq.content: The standouts are Alaverdi Cathedral (a towering 11th-century church rising from the vineyards east of Telavi), Bodbe Convent below Sighnaghi (the burial place of St. Nino and a major pilgrimage site), Nekresi (with buildings from the earliest centuries of Georgian Christianity), and Ikalto near Telavi (a monastery with the remains of a famous medieval academy). Together they give Kakheti a strong historical dimension beyond its wine.

faq.title: How do you get to Kakheti from Tbilisi?
faq.content: It's about 90–140 km depending on where you're going, roughly 1.5–2.5 hours by road. Two routes connect them: the scenic northern route over the Gombori Pass to Telavi, and the faster, less dramatic southern route across the Kartli plains. Marshrutkas run from Tbilisi's Samgori station to the main towns, but a car (or an organized tour) gives far more flexibility for reaching family wineries and monasteries off the main routes.

thingsToDoImereti.heroTitle: Things to Do in Imereti: Gelati, Prometheus Cave, and the Canyons
thingsToDoImereti.content:
Imereti, in central western Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — is one of the most accessible and varied regions in the country. Centered on Kutaisi, it offers a mix of cultural landmarks, cave systems, canyon landscapes, and local experiences, all within relatively short travel distances. Thanks to Kutaisi International Airport, Imereti is often the first region visitors explore when arriving in western Georgia — and its compactness means you can cover a lot without long drives. Here are the things most worth doing.

What are the best things to do in Imereti?

The highlights are the UNESCO-listed Gelati Monastery and the Bagrati Cathedral above Kutaisi; Prometheus Cave, with its lit chambers and underground boat ride; and the Okatse and Martvili canyons. Add the Sataplia reserve with its dinosaur footprints, the faded Soviet spa town of Tskaltubo, and the region's quieter wine tradition. Kutaisi itself makes the base, and most of these are easy day trips from it.

Explore Kutaisi

Kutaisi is the main hub of Imereti and the natural starting point for the region — Georgia's third-largest city and the historical capital of the west. It has historical landmarks, lively local markets, walkable streets, and views along the Rioni River, and compared to Tbilisi it feels more relaxed and compact, easy to explore on foot over a day. It also has the transport links and range of accommodation that make it the practical base for everything else in the region. (Kutaisi has its own detailed guide in this series.)

Visit Bagrati Cathedral

Bagrati Cathedral is one of Kutaisi's defining landmarks, standing on Ukimerioni Hill above the city with panoramic views over the Rioni River and the surrounding valley. Built in the early 11th century under King Bagrat III, it's an important symbol of Georgian history and unity. Worth knowing: after a major reconstruction completed in 2012, UNESCO removed Bagrati from the World Heritage List in 2017, judging that the rebuilding had compromised its authenticity — so unlike nearby Gelati, it no longer holds World Heritage status. That doesn't diminish the visit: the hilltop setting and the views over Kutaisi are among the best in the city, and the debate over its restoration is itself part of what makes it interesting.

Visit Gelati Monastery

Gelati Monastery is one of the most important cultural sites in Georgia and a UNESCO World Heritage Site in its own right. Founded in 1106 by King David the Builder as both a monastery and an academy of learning, it was one of the leading intellectual centers of the medieval Georgian world. The complex is known for its architecture and, above all, its art: the main cathedral holds the finest surviving medieval mosaics in Georgia, including a celebrated 12th-century apse mosaic of the Virgin and Child, alongside centuries of frescoes. David the Builder is buried beneath the main gate. Nine kilometers from Kutaisi and still an active monastery, it's an essential stop in the region.

Explore Prometheus Cave

Prometheus Cave, near Tskaltubo, is one of the largest and most impressive cave systems in Georgia and the region's most popular natural attraction. Visitors follow a lit walkway through a series of large underground halls filled with stalactites and stalagmites formed over millions of years, ending with a short boat ride on an underground river. It's well organized and accessible, suitable for most travelers regardless of fitness, and stays cool year-round — a particularly welcome stop in summer. Allow about an hour and a half to two hours.

Visit Sataplia Nature Reserve

Just outside the Kutaisi city limits, Sataplia combines nature and geology in a compact, easy stop. The reserve has a small cave, Cretaceous-period dinosaur footprints preserved in the limestone, forest walking paths through relict Colchic forest, and a glass-floored observation platform with views over the Rioni valley and the city. It works as a two-to-three-hour outing — accessible and varied without being demanding, and especially good for visitors with children.

Walk along Okatse Canyon

Okatse Canyon is known for the elevated walkway built along the edge of its cliffs, giving views straight down into a deep gorge. The route is a structured path with a slightly adventurous feel where the walkway cantilevers out over the drop, and the walk in to the canyon entrance and back is part of the experience. It's a rewarding half-day from Kutaisi for the dramatic perspective it gives onto the karst landscape.

Explore Martvili Canyon

Martvili Canyon offers a different kind of canyon experience from Okatse. Here you can take a short boat ride through the narrow canyon passages, walk the paths above the water, and see waterfalls and rock formations along the way. The turquoise color of the Abasha River running through the limestone walls makes it one of the most visually striking places in western Georgia. It sits on the Samegrelo border about an hour and fifteen minutes from Kutaisi and is frequently combined with Okatse in a single day. (Martvili also appears in the Samegrelo guide in this series.)

Visit Tskaltubo

Tskaltubo offers a completely different atmosphere from the rest of the region. The town is known for its grand Soviet-era sanatoriums, many now abandoned and in various states of decay, which create an unusual setting that combines 20th-century history and architecture. Some spa facilities still operate and parts of the town are being slowly revived, but much remains in atmospheric decline — a draw in itself for travelers interested in Soviet history and urban exploration. It's about 12 kilometers from Kutaisi and close to Prometheus Cave. (Tskaltubo has its own detailed guide in this series.)

Discover Imeretian wine

Wine in Imereti is a quieter, less commercialized alternative to Kakheti, built on indigenous western-Georgian grapes — whites like Tsolikouri, Tsitska, and Krakhuna, and reds like Otskhanuri Sapere. Made by the traditional qvevri method but usually with shorter skin contact than in Kakheti, Imeretian amber whites are often more approachable for those new to skin-contact wine while still carrying real complexity. Tasting here typically happens in small, family-run wineries, which makes for a more intimate, direct experience — though it usually needs an introduction through a guesthouse or local contact rather than a walk-in, since these producers rarely have formal visitor facilities.

Drive through the Imereti countryside

One of the best ways to experience Imereti is simply to drive it. The short distances between sites let you combine cultural landmarks, natural landscapes, and small villages in a single day, through the characteristic mixed farmland and forested hills of western Georgia. A car gives the most freedom, especially for reaching the smaller wineries and villages off the main routes, though organized day tours from Kutaisi cover the major sites efficiently for travelers without their own transport.

When to visit

Spring and summer are ideal for the nature and outdoor sites, when the canyon rivers run full and the landscapes are at their greenest — though the cave and indoor sites are comfortable year-round, and the cave is especially welcome in summer heat. Autumn is quieter and well suited to wine experiences, with the Imeretian harvest active and the forested hills changing color. Winter is mild at valley elevation and the main sites stay accessible, though some smaller family wineries and rural guesthouses run reduced schedules outside the main season.

faq.title: What are the top things to do in Imereti?
faq.content: Visit the UNESCO-listed Gelati Monastery and the Bagrati Cathedral above Kutaisi, explore Prometheus Cave with its underground boat ride, and walk the Okatse and Martvili canyons. Add the Sataplia reserve (with its dinosaur footprints), the Soviet spa town of Tskaltubo, and a tasting at a small Imeretian family winery. Most are easy day trips from Kutaisi.

faq.content: Yes — Gelati is a UNESCO World Heritage Site in its own right. Founded in 1106 by King David the Builder as a monastery and academy, it holds the finest surviving medieval mosaics in Georgia, including a 12th-century apse mosaic of the Virgin and Child. It's nine kilometers from Kutaisi and still active. (The nearby Bagrati Cathedral was removed from the World Heritage List in 2017 after its reconstruction, so it no longer carries the status.)

faq.title: What is Prometheus Cave like?
faq.content: Prometheus Cave, near Tskaltubo, is one of the largest and most impressive caves in Georgia. A lit walkway leads through a series of big underground halls full of stalactites and stalagmites, finishing with a short boat ride on an underground river. It's well organized and accessible for most visitors, stays cool year-round, and takes about 1.5–2 hours.

faq.title: Can you visit Okatse and Martvili canyons in one day?
faq.content: Yes — they're often combined into a single day trip from Kutaisi. Okatse has a dramatic cantilevered cliff-edge walkway over a deep gorge; Martvili (on the Samegrelo border) is a wider canyon with turquoise water, boat rides, and waterfalls. A car or an organized tour covers both comfortably in a day.

faq.title: Is Imeretian wine worth trying?
faq.content: Yes — it's a quieter, more intimate alternative to Kakheti. Imereti's indigenous grapes (Tsolikouri, Tsitska, Krakhuna) and shorter skin-contact qvevri whites make amber wines that are often more approachable for newcomers while still complex. Tastings happen in small family wineries, which usually need an introduction through a guesthouse or local contact rather than a walk-in.

imereti.heroTitle: Imereti, Georgia: Kutaisi, Gelati Monastery, and the Caves of the Karst
imereti.content:
Imereti is the central region of western Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — occupying the broad valley of the Rioni River and its tributaries between the Likhi range to the east (the low watershed ridge that has historically divided western from eastern Georgia) and the mountainous borders of Racha and Svaneti to the north. It is the most populous region in western Georgia and one of the most historically significant in the country, with a recorded history reaching back to the ancient Kingdom of Colchis and a medieval legacy that includes some of the finest ecclesiastical architecture and most important intellectual institutions Georgia ever produced. Kutaisi, the regional center, is one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the Caucasus; the caves, canyons, and forested landscapes around it are among the most visited natural sites in the country; and the wine tradition of Imereti — quieter and less internationally promoted than Kakheti's — represents a genuinely distinct approach to Georgian winemaking that deserves more attention than it usually gets.

What is Imereti known for?

Imereti is best known for Kutaisi (one of the oldest cities in the Caucasus and a major low-cost flight hub), the medieval Gelati Monastery and the Bagrati Cathedral above the city, and the karst landscape of caves and canyons — Prometheus Cave, and the Okatse and Martvili canyons. It's also home to the faded Soviet spa town of Tskaltubo, the Sataplia reserve with its dinosaur footprints, and a distinctive western-Georgian wine tradition built on grapes like Tsolikouri and Tsitska. Compact and well connected, it packs heritage, nature, and wine into a small area.

What makes Imereti useful for travelers

Beyond the quality of what it contains, Imereti's real advantage is accessibility and compactness. The main sites sit within a relatively small area, connected by roads that are good by Georgian standards, and anchored by an international airport that has made Kutaisi one of the most convenient entry points into Georgia from Europe. A traveler with a week here can cover medieval monasteries, underground cave systems, dramatic river canyons, Soviet-era spa ruins, traditional wineries, and a functioning provincial city with real character — without spending excessive time in transit.

Geography and landscape

The Rioni River is the geographical spine of Imereti, rising in the Greater Caucasus to the north and flowing west across the region before continuing through Samegrelo to the Black Sea. The valley broadens considerably through central Imereti, creating the relatively flat agricultural land around Kutaisi that gives the region its character as a productive farming area as well as a historically significant one. The hills framing the valley on both sides — densely forested, cut by smaller river gorges, and underlain by the karst limestone responsible for the region's caves and canyons — rise to greater heights toward the northern and eastern margins.

The karst geology deserves particular attention, since it explains much of Imereti's natural appeal. Limestone landscapes develop characteristic underground drainage, and millennia of water percolating through soluble rock have produced the cave systems, gorges, and canyons that make western Georgia one of the most remarkable karst environments in the Caucasus. Prometheus Cave near Tskaltubo, the Okatse and Martvili canyons, and numerous smaller caves and sinkholes are all expressions of the same process — and the results (turquoise rivers in sheer limestone walls, underground chambers of extraordinary scale, stalactite formations representing millions of years of slow mineral deposition) give Imereti a natural dimension that complements its historical and cultural assets.

History

Imereti's history is long and layered. Its most ancient layer is Colchian: modern Imereti falls within the heartland of ancient Colchis, the kingdom of the eastern Black Sea coast that appears in Greek sources from the first millennium BC and occupies a central place in the myth of the Argonauts and the Golden Fleece. How far that mythology maps onto actual events remains debated, but the archaeological evidence of sophisticated Bronze and Iron Age cultures in the Rioni valley is substantial, and the metalworking traditions of ancient Colchis — gold and bronze objects of considerable refinement — are represented in museum collections throughout Georgia.

The medieval period left the most visible legacy. Kutaisi served as the capital of the unified Georgian kingdom under the Bagrationi dynasty in the 10th and 11th centuries, and the building of the Bagrati Cathedral and, shortly afterward, the Gelati Monastery gave physical form to a kingdom at the height of its power and cultural confidence. After the capital moved to Tbilisi, Imereti continued as a significant cultural and religious region, and Gelati in particular developed into one of the most important intellectual institutions in medieval Georgia, its academy functioning alongside Ikalto in Kakheti as a center of theological and philosophical scholarship.

The fragmentation of the unified kingdom in the 15th century produced the Kingdom of Imereti as a distinct political entity, which maintained a complicated existence — alternately autonomous, contested, and under Ottoman pressure — until Russian annexation in 1810. The 19th century brought the standard reorganization of Russian imperial administration, and the region developed through the late imperial and Soviet periods as an agricultural and light-industrial area centered on Kutaisi, which kept a degree of economic and cultural significance throughout.

Kutaisi

Kutaisi merits its own guide — and has one — but its role within Imereti is the essential starting point for the region. As the third-largest city in Georgia and the historical capital of the west, it provides the infrastructure, transport connections, and concentration of cultural institutions that make it the natural base for regional exploration. Its own sites — the Bagrati Cathedral on its hill above the Rioni, the central market, the riverside promenade, and the fabric of a working Georgian provincial city — are worth a day before moving out to the surrounding region.

The city's centrality is reinforced by the airport. Kutaisi International Airport, about 25 kilometers from the center, handles a growing number of routes from European cities, predominantly low-cost carriers, and has transformed access to western Georgia over the past decade. Arriving in Imereti directly from abroad, without transiting Tbilisi, is now a straightforward option that meaningfully changes the calculus of western Georgia as a destination. (Kutaisi has its own detailed guide in this series.)

Gelati Monastery

Nine kilometers northeast of Kutaisi, on a forested hillside above a tributary of the Rioni, Gelati is the most important medieval monument in Imereti and one of the most significant historical sites in Georgia. Founded in 1106 by King David the Builder as both a religious complex and an academy of learning, it was conceived on a scale and with an intellectual ambition that placed it among the leading institutions of the medieval Georgian world. David himself is buried here, beneath the threshold of the main gate — a deliberate act of humility that remains one of the most discussed gestures in Georgian royal history.

The main Cathedral of the Virgin contains the finest surviving medieval mosaics in Georgia, including a 12th-century apse mosaic of the Virgin and Child that represents a high point of Georgian medieval art. The frescoes covering the walls span multiple centuries of production, and reading the layers — earlier work surviving in fragments, later additions filling the gaps, the whole a visual record of the monastery's long history — rewards careful attention. The monastery is active, with a resident community, and the hillside setting gives it a contemplative quality that the more heavily visited sites in Tbilisi and Mtskheta sometimes lack. Gelati is a UNESCO World Heritage Site in its own right — a designation that reflects a significance extending well beyond Georgia's national heritage.

Bagrati Cathedral

The Bagrati Cathedral, built between 1003 and 1008 under King Bagrat III as a physical expression of the newly unified Georgian kingdom, stands on Ukimerioni Hill above Kutaisi with views over the Rioni valley. Its history since construction has been complicated: Ottoman forces destroyed much of it with gunpowder in 1691, and the ruins stood for three centuries before a controversial reconstruction, completed in 2012, rebuilt the dome and portions of the walls. That reconstruction had a notable consequence — Bagrati and Gelati had been jointly inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 1994, but in 2017 the World Heritage Committee removed Bagrati Cathedral from the listing entirely, judging that the reconstruction had compromised its authenticity and integrity, while retaining Gelati as a standalone World Heritage Site. The debate around the restoration reflects broader questions about conservation philosophy that the site embodies in unusually direct form. Whatever position one takes, the cathedral and its hilltop setting remain impressive, and the views from the grounds over Kutaisi and the Rioni valley are among the best in the city.

Prometheus Cave

About 20 kilometers northwest of Kutaisi near the town of Tskaltubo, Prometheus Cave is the most visited and most thoroughly organized natural attraction in Imereti. The system runs through a series of chambers of varying scale and character, containing stalactite and stalagmite formations developed over millions of years in the karst limestone. A lit walkway guides visitors through the main sequence, and a short boat section on an underground river makes a memorable finale. The largest chambers are genuinely impressive in scale, and the cave holds a constant temperature of around 14°C regardless of the season outside — welcome in summer, when the contrast with the heat above is particularly pleasant. The visit takes roughly an hour and a half to two hours and suits visitors of all ages and fitness levels.

Okatse and Martvili canyons

The two main canyon systems accessible from Kutaisi offer different but complementary takes on the karst landscape. Okatse Canyon, about 40 kilometers from the city, is a narrow, dramatically walled gorge reached via a cantilevered walkway suspended above the canyon edge, giving vertiginous views straight down to the river far below; it ends at a platform over the most dramatic section, and the combination of sheer walls, drop, and distant water rewards the relatively straightforward approach.

Martvili Canyon, about 55 kilometers northwest of Kutaisi in the Samegrelo border area, is wider and more classically scenic — the Abasha River running through limestone walls of exceptional color, with optional boat rides through the narrower sections where overhanging walls and turquoise water create a striking effect. The two are frequently combined in a single day trip from Kutaisi, one in the morning and the other in the afternoon, with a drive between them through the characteristic mixed agricultural and forested landscape of western Georgia. (Martvili also features in the Samegrelo guide in this series, as it sits on the regional boundary.)

Sataplia Nature Reserve

A few kilometers from the Kutaisi city limits, Sataplia preserves a small area of relict Colchic forest — the subtropical forest type that once covered much of the western Caucasus lowlands — alongside Cretaceous-period dinosaur footprints preserved in the limestone surface, a small cave, and a glass-floored observation platform with views over the Rioni valley and the city. The footprints, discovered in the 1930s, include tracks of both herbivorous and carnivorous dinosaurs and add one of the more unusual historical layers to a region already rich in them. The reserve works well as a two-to-three-hour excursion — accessible and varied without being demanding, and particularly good for visitors with children.

Tskaltubo

The spa town of Tskaltubo, about 12 kilometers from Kutaisi, has its own guide in this series, but its place within Imereti deserves mention. The combination of natural thermal mineral springs, a remarkable concentration of grand Soviet-era sanatorium buildings in various states of decay and partial revival, and easy proximity to Prometheus Cave makes it one of the more unusual and historically layered stops in the region. For travelers interested in 20th-century history, Soviet architecture, or simply destinations that operate at a different register from the standard heritage-and-nature circuit, it offers a genuinely distinctive experience within easy reach of Kutaisi. (Tskaltubo has its own detailed guide in this series.)

Imeretian wine

Imereti has its own winemaking tradition — older, more geographically extensive, and more stylistically distinctive than its modest international profile suggests. The main grape varieties — Tsolikouri, Tsitska, and Krakhuna for whites; Otskhanuri Sapere and Dzelshavi for reds — are indigenous to western Georgia and produce wines quite different in character from the Kakhetian varieties that dominate the international conversation about Georgian wine. Imeretian whites made by the traditional qvevri method, with partial skin contact typically shorter than the full extended maceration used in Kakheti, produce amber wines of moderate depth and freshness — more approachable for palates new to skin-contact wines than the intensely tannic full-maceration Kakhetian styles, while still carrying the complexity the qvevri method provides.

Most Imeretian wine is made in small family operations spread across villages throughout the region. These producers rarely have organized visitor infrastructure — a visit typically means a prior introduction through a guesthouse or local contact, then sitting in a cellar or courtyard while the host opens successive vessels and explains each. The informality and directness of these encounters is much of their appeal, and for travelers whose main interest in Georgia is the wine culture, Imereti alongside Kakheti gives a complete picture of the country's winemaking rather than just one half of it.

Getting around

Imereti's compactness is a genuine practical advantage. The distances from Kutaisi to the main sites are all manageable as day trips: Gelati about 20 minutes by road, Prometheus Cave around 30 minutes, Okatse Canyon about an hour, Martvili Canyon an hour and fifteen minutes, Sataplia under 15 minutes. A car gives the most flexibility, especially for reaching smaller wineries and villages off the main routes, but organized day tours from Kutaisi cover the major natural and historical sites efficiently for those without their own transport. Marshrutkas connect Kutaisi with Tbilisi, Batumi, and other cities, and the train network is an alternative for longer intercity journeys.

When to visit

Spring (April through June) is generally the most rewarding season — the Rioni valley is at its greenest, the canyon rivers run full from snowmelt and spring rain, the cave is particularly pleasant as its cool interior contrasts with the warming exterior, and visitor numbers are below the summer peak. Summer is warm and the landscapes fully productive, with the canyons and cave manageable in the heat given the shade and water involved, though July and August bring the largest crowds to the main sites and advance planning matters more. Autumn (September through October) offers excellent conditions for wine-related visits — the Imeretian harvest, while less celebrated than the Kakhetian Rtveli, is active and rewarding — alongside pleasant temperatures and the changing colors of the forested hillsides. Winter is mild at valley elevation and the cave and indoor sites are accessible year-round, though some smaller family wineries and rural guesthouses run reduced schedules outside the main season.

faq.title: Where is Imereti and what is it known for?
faq.content: Imereti is the central region of western Georgia, in the Rioni valley around Kutaisi. It's known for Kutaisi (one of the oldest cities in the Caucasus, with an international airport), the medieval Gelati Monastery and the Bagrati Cathedral, and a karst landscape of caves and canyons — Prometheus Cave and the Okatse and Martvili canyons. It also has the Soviet spa town of Tskaltubo and a distinctive western-Georgian wine tradition.

faq.title: Is Gelati Monastery worth visiting?
faq.content: Yes — it's the most important medieval monument in Imereti and a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Founded in 1106 by King David the Builder as a monastery and academy, it holds the finest surviving medieval mosaics in Georgia, including a 12th-century apse mosaic of the Virgin and Child, along with centuries of frescoes. David the Builder is buried beneath the main gate. It's nine kilometers from Kutaisi and still an active monastery.

faq.title: Is Bagrati Cathedral still a UNESCO site?
faq.content: No. Bagrati Cathedral, built 1003–1008 under King Bagrat III, was jointly inscribed with Gelati in 1994, but in 2017 UNESCO removed it from the World Heritage List because its 2012 reconstruction was judged to compromise its authenticity. Gelati was retained as a standalone site. Bagrati is still well worth visiting — its hilltop setting above Kutaisi offers some of the best views in the city — but it no longer carries World Heritage status.

faq.title: What is Prometheus Cave like?
faq.content: Prometheus Cave, about 20 km from Kutaisi near Tskaltubo, is Imereti's most popular and best-organized natural attraction. A lit walkway leads through a sequence of large karst chambers full of stalactite and stalagmite formations, ending with a short boat ride on an underground river. It stays around 14°C year-round, and the visit takes about 1.5–2 hours. It suits all ages and fitness levels.

faq.title: Can you visit the Okatse and Martvili canyons from Kutaisi?
faq.content: Yes — both are easy day trips and are often combined into one. Okatse (about 40 km) is a narrow gorge with a cantilevered walkway giving dizzying views down to the river; Martvili (about 55 km, on the Samegrelo border) is a wider, scenic canyon with turquoise water and optional boat rides. A car or an organized tour from Kutaisi covers both comfortably in a day.

thingsToDoSamegrelo.heroTitle: Things to Do in Samegrelo: Martvili Canyon, Megrelian Food, and the Road to Svaneti
thingsToDoSamegrelo.content:
Samegrelo, in western Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — is one of the greenest and most varied regions in the country, combining canyons, forests, and rivers with a distinct local culture and cuisine. It sits between the Black Sea and the mountains of Svaneti, so it's often experienced as part of a wider western-Georgia journey — but it has enough on its own to be worth stopping for. Centered on Zugdidi, with its sites mostly within short distances, it's an easy region to explore. Here are the things most worth doing.

What are the best things to do in Samegrelo?

The highlights are Martvili Canyon, with its turquoise water and boat rides; the Dadiani Palace in Zugdidi, with its Napoleonic connection; the scenic drive up to Mestia and Svaneti; and the region's famously rich Megrelian cuisine. For the adventurous, there's the wilder Balda Canyon and the demanding multi-day trek to the remote Tobavarchkhili Lakes. It's a green, less-touristed region that rewards both a quick stop and a slower stay.

Explore Martvili Canyon

Martvili Canyon is the best-known natural attraction in Samegrelo and one of the highlights of western Georgia. The Abasha River has cut a narrow gorge through pale limestone, producing vertical rock walls dropping to intensely turquoise water, with small waterfalls and dense vegetation all around. You can walk the path along the rim for views down into the gorge, then take a short boat ride through the narrower sections for a closer perspective on the rock and water. The setting feels enclosed and calm — a different kind of landscape from Georgia's open valleys — and the site is well organized, with maintained paths, boat service, and facilities at the entrance. It's commonly combined with the nearby Okatse Canyon as a full day out. (Martvili has its own detailed guide in this series.)

Discover Balda Canyon

For something wilder, Balda Canyon is a more adventurous, less-developed alternative to Martvili. It's a river canyon with natural pools, a waterfall at the end of the trail, and — depending on conditions — a rope crossing over the water. It feels raw and unstructured compared to Martvili, which is much of its appeal for travelers wanting something more active and hands-on. Because it's far less developed, it suits people comfortable with rougher, less-marked terrain, and access can vary with the season and water levels.

Trek to the Tobavarchkhili Lakes

For experienced, well-equipped hikers, the Tobavarchkhili Lakes — “Silver Lakes” in Megrelian — are one of the most remote and rewarding destinations in Samegrelo, a cluster of glacial lakes high in the Egrisi Range at around 2,500–2,650 meters. This is a serious undertaking, not a casual hike: the classic route runs roughly 45 kilometers over four to six days, crossing high passes, rivers, and slopes that can hold snow even in summer, with no shelters, no mobile coverage, and trails that are at times hard to follow. It's genuinely one of the most challenging treks in Georgia, and the strong advice is to go with an experienced guide (and often pack horses), with proper gear and a buffer day for weather. The reward is some of the most untouched mountain wilderness in the country. The season is short — roughly mid-July to mid-September.

Visit Zugdidi and the Dadiani Palace

Zugdidi, the regional capital, is the best place to get a feel for Samegrelo — a quiet city of broad streets, markets, and everyday life, with its standout sight the Dadiani Palace. The former residence of the princes of Samegrelo, it's now a museum holding medieval Georgian religious objects, the family's belongings across generations, and — most unexpectedly — one of Napoleon's bronze death masks, acquired through the marriage of Princess Salome Dadiani to Napoleon's great-nephew Achille Murat. The palace sits in one of Georgia's oldest botanical gardens, and together they give real context for the region's past and its surprising connections beyond Georgia. (Zugdidi has its own detailed guide in this series.)

Drive from Zugdidi to Mestia

One of the most impressive experiences in Samegrelo is the drive north toward Svaneti. The road from Zugdidi up the Enguri valley climbs from flat green farmland, through the steep-walled Enguri gorge (passing the huge Enguri Dam and its blue-green reservoir), to the high Svan valleys around Mestia — a complete landscape transformation in about three hours. It's one of the most dramatic road journeys in Georgia, and very much part of the experience rather than just transport. Even if Svaneti is your main goal, the drive itself is a highlight of any time spent in Samegrelo.

Explore the countryside

Beyond the named sites, much of Samegrelo's character shows in its countryside. Driving the rural roads takes you past small villages, agricultural land, and dense forest — the exceptionally green, well-watered landscape that defines the region. It's unhurried, low-key travel, and a good way to see everyday Megrelian life away from any tourist infrastructure. A car gives the freedom to wander and stop where the scenery invites it.

Taste Megrelian cuisine

Samegrelo has one of the strongest, most distinctive food traditions in Georgia, known for being richer and spicier than the national norm — it uses adjika, the hot-pepper-and-garlic paste, generously. Signature dishes include the double-cheese Megrelian khachapuri, kharcho (a spiced beef, rice, and walnut soup), gebzhalia (fresh cheese in a mint-and-cream sauce), and elarji (a cornmeal-and-sulguni porridge pulled into elastic threads). The region's sulguni cheese is considered by many the best in Georgia. Eating well here is genuinely one of the main reasons to visit, and the food is best experienced in a local family or guesthouse setting.

Experience local life

More than most Georgian regions, Samegrelo offers an unstructured, local experience. Spending time here often means simply observing daily life, talking with local people, and settling into a slower pace, in a region with a strong cultural identity and relatively few other tourists. It's a place to engage with rather than tick off — and travelers who do tend to come away with a clearer sense of this particular corner of western Georgia.

When to visit

Spring and summer are best for the landscapes and outdoor activities, when everything is at its greenest and the canyons and trails are in full swing (the Tobavarchkhili trek specifically needs mid-July to mid-September). Autumn is quieter and more relaxed, with the forests turning and the summer humidity easing. July and August can be hot and humid in the lowlands, though the canyons and forests stay pleasant; winter is mild at lower elevations and the quietest time of year.

faq.title: What are the top things to do in Samegrelo?
faq.content: Visit Martvili Canyon for its turquoise water and boat rides, explore the Dadiani Palace in Zugdidi (home to one of Napoleon's death masks), drive the scenic road up toward Svaneti, and eat your way through the rich Megrelian cuisine. For the adventurous, there's the wilder Balda Canyon and the demanding multi-day trek to the remote Tobavarchkhili Lakes.

faq.title: Is Martvili Canyon worth visiting?
faq.content: Yes — it's the region's top natural attraction. The Abasha River has carved a dramatic limestone gorge with vivid turquoise water, a rim walking path, and short boat rides through the narrow sections. It's well set up for visitors and is usually combined with the nearby Okatse Canyon as a full-day trip from Zugdidi or Kutaisi.

faq.title: How hard is the Tobavarchkhili Lakes trek?
faq.content: It's one of the most challenging treks in Georgia — a roughly 45 km route over four to six days at 2,500–2,650 m, with high passes, river crossings, snow even in summer, no shelters, and no mobile coverage. It's strongly recommended to go with an experienced guide and proper gear, ideally between mid-July and mid-September. The reward is some of the most untouched wilderness in the country.

faq.title: What is there to do in Zugdidi?
faq.content: The main sight is the Dadiani Palace, the former residence of the princes of Samegrelo, now a museum with medieval Georgian religious objects, the family's belongings, and one of Napoleon's death masks — acquired through Princess Salome Dadiani's marriage to Napoleon's great-nephew Achille Murat. It sits in one of Georgia's oldest botanical gardens. Zugdidi is also a good place to experience everyday Megrelian life.

faq.title: What food should I try in Samegrelo?
faq.content: Megrelian cuisine is among Georgia's richest and spiciest. Try the double-cheese Megrelian khachapuri, kharcho (spiced beef, rice, and walnut soup), gebzhalia (cheese in mint-and-cream sauce), and elarji (cornmeal-and-sulguni porridge). The local sulguni cheese is considered by many the finest in Georgia.

samegrelo.heroTitle: Samegrelo, Georgia: Megrelian Culture, Martvili Canyon, and the Gateway to Svaneti
samegrelo.content:
Samegrelo is a region in western Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — occupying the lowland and foothill terrain between the Black Sea coast to the west, the Enguri River to the north (which separates it from Abkhazia), and the rising slopes of the Greater Caucasus that lead up to Svaneti. Its administrative center is Zugdidi, a city of around 75,000 that serves as the region's practical and cultural hub. Samegrelo isn't a region that announces itself with a single dramatic landmark. Its appeal is more diffuse and gradual: a landscape of exceptional greenness and fertility, a distinct cultural and linguistic identity, a history centered on the Dadiani dynasty that ruled here for centuries, and its position as the main gateway to Svaneti, which means most visitors to the high Caucasus pass through it — though fewer stop long enough to understand what the region offers on its own terms.

What is Samegrelo known for?

Samegrelo is best known as the gateway to Svaneti, the distinct culture and language of the Megrelian people, and the Dadiani dynasty whose Zugdidi palace holds one of Napoleon's death masks. Its standout natural site is Martvili Canyon, with its turquoise river and boat rides, and it's home to the vast Enguri Dam and the ancient fortress city of Nokalakevi. It's also celebrated for Megrelian cuisine — among the richest and spiciest in Georgia — and its prized sulguni cheese.

The Megrelians and their language

The people of Samegrelo call themselves Megrelians — Megreli in their own language — and they speak Megrelian, a South Caucasian language related to but distinct from Georgian and mutually unintelligible with it in spoken form. Like Svan in the neighboring mountains, Megrelian is one of the older languages of the Caucasus, preserving features of the ancient Colchic world that once dominated the eastern Black Sea coastal zone. It's spoken as a first language by most of the region's population, with Georgian used in education, official contexts, and communication with the rest of the country.

This linguistic distinctiveness is part of a broader cultural identity that sets Samegrelo apart. Megrelian traditions, music, food, and social customs have their own character, and regional pride in this identity is genuine and pervasive. Travelers who engage with local people here, rather than simply passing through, tend to find a culture that is warm and hospitable in the distinctly Georgian way but also clearly particular to this corner of the country.

History and the Dadiani dynasty

Samegrelo's political history is inseparable from the Dadiani family, the noble dynasty that governed the region as the Principality of Samegrelo (Mingrelia) from the 16th century until Russian annexation in the mid-19th century. The Dadiani princes held a degree of autonomy through the shifting pressures of Ottoman, Persian, and Russian imperial competition, and at points formed marriage ties with European royalty — most famously the marriage of Princess Salome Dadiani to Achille Murat, a great-nephew of Napoleon Bonaparte (a grandson of Napoleon's sister Caroline Bonaparte and Marshal Joachim Murat), in 1868. That connection brought a number of Napoleonic relics to Samegrelo and left them in the family's possession.

The Dadiani Palace in Zugdidi, the family's principal residence in the later years of the principality, is now one of the more interesting regional museums in Georgia. Its collection reflects the family's unusual position at the meeting point of Caucasian, Ottoman, and European influences: medieval Georgian religious objects and manuscripts, family belongings across several generations, diplomatic gifts, and — most unexpectedly — items connected to Napoleon, including one of the small number of bronze death masks of the emperor, acquired through the Murat marriage. The palace stands in a botanical garden established by the Dadiani princes, one of the oldest in Georgia, whose collection of exotic trees and plants, accumulated over more than a century, gives the grounds a pleasantly overgrown, faintly faded grandeur.

Zugdidi itself is quiet by Georgian standards, its broad streets and Soviet-era residential blocks giving it a somewhat sleepy character that belies its importance as a regional center. The city was significantly affected by the wave of internally displaced people from Abkhazia after the 1992–93 war, and the presence of this community — many of whom have now lived in Zugdidi for over thirty years without being able to return home — is part of the social and political reality of the city and the wider region. (Zugdidi has its own detailed guide in this series.)

Landscape and climate

Samegrelo is among the wettest and most consistently green regions in Georgia, receiving substantial rainfall year-round as moist Black Sea air is forced upward by the Caucasus foothills. The result is dense mixed forest, lush farmland, and a general fertility that contrasts sharply with the drier, more open terrain of eastern Georgia. The air carries a dampness and softness that visitors from the east notice immediately, and the vegetation — towering chestnut and oak forests, dense undergrowth, climbing plants covering abandoned structures — has an almost subtropical exuberance in the warmer months.

The rivers are central to the landscape. The Enguri, the Rioni, the Tskhenistskali, and numerous smaller tributaries drain the Caucasus slopes and cut through the lowlands in gorges and valleys that shape the region's geography and create some of its most striking natural sites. The rivers run fast and cold, fed by glacier melt and mountain rainfall, their turbid grey-green color — from suspended glacial sediment — characteristic of the fast-flowing Caucasus streams.

Martvili Canyon

The most visited natural site in Samegrelo is Martvili Canyon, about 55 kilometers northeast of Zugdidi in the foothills above the lowland plain. The canyon is cut by the Abasha River through limestone formations, creating a gorge of real visual drama — vertical walls of pale rock dropping to a river of intense turquoise, the color produced by the limestone geology and the water's mineral content. A walking path along the rim gives views down into the gorge, and short boat rides through the narrower sections offer a closer perspective on the rock walls and the water.

The canyon is well organized for visitors, with a maintained path, boat service, and basic facilities at the entrance, and is typically combined with Okatse Canyon further east as a natural full-day excursion. Martvili town itself is close by, with a medieval monastery on a hillside above the surrounding farmland — a well-preserved complex with wide views across the lowland plain toward the Black Sea horizon. (Martvili has its own detailed guide in this series.)

The road to Svaneti

The single most important practical role Samegrelo plays in Georgian travel is as the gateway to Svaneti. The main road connecting the rest of Georgia to Mestia runs north from Zugdidi up the Enguri valley, climbing through the gorge that separates the lowland Samegrelo basin from the high mountain world above. The journey from Zugdidi to Mestia takes about three hours and is one of the more dramatic road trips in Georgia — the transformation from the flat, green farmland around Zugdidi, through the narrow, steep-walled Enguri gorge, to the high Svan valleys above is complete and rapid, covering in a few hours a vertical and visual range that would take days on foot.

The Enguri Hydroelectric Dam, encountered early in the climb from Zugdidi, is one of the highest concrete arch dams in the world, its structure rising about 271 meters above the gorge floor and impounding a large reservoir whose deep blue-green water fills the valley for several kilometers behind it. The scale of the structure against the gorge walls is impressive, and the viewpoint from the road above gives a sense of the engineering involved. It has been operational since 1978 (with construction completed later) and remains a major source of Georgia's electricity. One geopolitical note worth knowing: the dam sits in Georgian-controlled territory, but the power station is partly located in Abkhazia, and its output has long been shared across the boundary — a quiet reminder of the region's unresolved situation.

Nokalakevi

About 50 kilometers southwest of Zugdidi, near the town of Senaki, the archaeological site of Nokalakevi preserves the ruins of what was once one of the most important fortified cities in western Georgia. Known in ancient sources as Archaeopolis, it was a major urban center of the Colchic and later Lazic kingdoms, occupied from at least the 5th century BC through the medieval period, with the ruins of its defensive walls, towers, and internal structures spread across a considerable area on the bank of the Tekhuri River. It was the scene of significant military operations during the Byzantine–Persian wars of the 6th century AD, when it served as a key Byzantine stronghold in the struggle for control of the eastern Black Sea region.

Nokalakevi is less visited than Georgia's more accessible historical sites but rewards the detour for travelers interested in the ancient and early medieval history of the western Caucasus. The ruins are substantial and the setting, on a river bend below forested hills, is attractive. Archaeological work here has run for decades and produced finds spanning the full chronological range of occupation.

Megrelian cuisine

Megrelian cuisine is one of the most distinctive regional food traditions in Georgia, with a well-deserved reputation for intensity. Where other Georgian regional cuisines moderate their use of adjika — the pungent paste of hot peppers, garlic, and spices fundamental to Georgian cooking — Megrelian cooking uses it generously. The result is food that is richer, hotter, and more assertively flavored than the pan-Georgian norm.

The Megrelian version of khachapuri uses a double layer of cheese, both inside the dough and melted across the top, for a more intense dairy richness than other regional variants. Kharcho, a soup of beef, rice, walnuts, and a quantity of spice that varies by cook but is never mild, is one of the most famous Megrelian dishes and has spread throughout Georgian cooking. Gebzhalia is a fresh-cheese preparation particular to the region, served in a warm sauce of mint and cream that cools the fiercer flavors elsewhere in the meal. Elarji, a thick porridge of cornmeal and sulguni cheese pulled into long elastic threads, is a Megrelian staple that is simultaneously simple and deeply satisfying. The sulguni of Samegrelo — a lightly brined, semi-firm cheese with a layered, slightly elastic texture — is considered by many the finest in Georgia, and appears throughout the regional cuisine: fresh, smoked, fried, melted, or simply eaten with bread.

Getting there and around

Zugdidi is connected to Tbilisi by both road and rail, the train taking about five hours through the Rioni valley and the lowland plains of western Georgia, and the road journey around four hours. From Zugdidi, marshrutkas and shared taxis run to the main towns and villages, and the road north to Mestia departs from the city center. A car gives the most flexibility for the natural sites and the smaller villages off the main routes. Martvili Canyon and Okatse Canyon are most practically visited by car or as part of an organized tour from Zugdidi or Kutaisi.

When to visit

Samegrelo is a year-round destination in the sense that the lowland climate rarely becomes truly extreme, but the most rewarding seasons are spring and early summer — when the landscape is at its greenest and the rivers running high from snowmelt — and autumn, when the forests take on color and temperatures ease from the summer humidity. July and August can be genuinely hot and humid in the lowlands, though the canyon sites and forested areas stay pleasant. Winter is mild and rarely snowy at lower elevations, and the region is quieter than at any other time — an advantage for those who prefer to travel without crowds, though some smaller natural sites may have reduced facilities outside the main season.

faq.title: Where is Samegrelo and what is it known for?
faq.content: Samegrelo is a green, fertile region in western Georgia, between the Black Sea, the Enguri River (which borders Abkhazia), and the foothills leading up to Svaneti, with Zugdidi as its center. It's known for the distinct Megrelian language and culture, the Dadiani dynasty and its Zugdidi palace, Martvili Canyon, the Enguri Dam, and a famously rich, spicy regional cuisine.

faq.title: What is there to see in Zugdidi?
faq.content: The main sight is the Dadiani Palace, the former residence of the princes of Samegrelo, now a museum holding medieval Georgian religious objects, the family's belongings, and — most unexpectedly — one of Napoleon's death masks, acquired through the marriage of Princess Salome Dadiani to Napoleon's great-nephew Achille Murat. The palace sits in one of Georgia's oldest botanical gardens.

faq.title: Is Martvili Canyon worth visiting?
faq.content: Yes. Martvili Canyon, about 55 km from Zugdidi, is a dramatic limestone gorge with vivid turquoise water, a rim walking path, and short boat rides through the narrow sections. It's well set up for visitors and is usually combined with the nearby Okatse Canyon as a full-day trip from Zugdidi or Kutaisi.

faq.title: Why does everyone pass through Samegrelo on the way to Svaneti?
faq.content: Because the main road to Mestia and Upper Svaneti runs north from Zugdidi up the Enguri valley — there's no other practical route. The roughly three-hour drive climbs from green lowlands through the Enguri gorge (passing the huge Enguri Dam) to the high Svan valleys, one of the most dramatic road journeys in Georgia.

faq.title: What is Megrelian food like?
faq.content: It's among the richest and spiciest in Georgia, using the hot-pepper-and-garlic paste adjika generously. Signature dishes include the double-cheese Megrelian khachapuri, kharcho (a spiced beef, rice, and walnut soup), gebzhalia (cheese in mint-and-cream sauce), and elarji (cornmeal-and-sulguni porridge). The region's sulguni cheese is considered by many the best in Georgia.

guria.heroTitle: Guria, Georgia: Tea Country on the Black Sea
guria.content:
Guria is a small region in western Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — wedged between the Black Sea coast to the west, the Adjara highlands to the south, Samegrelo to the north, and the Imereti uplands to the east. It's one of the smallest regions in the country by area and one of the least visited by international travelers, which has left it with an unhurried, lived-in quality that its more celebrated neighbors — Batumi to the south, Kutaisi to the east — have partly traded away for tourism infrastructure. The regional center is Ozurgeti, a quiet town of around 20,000 people that serves as the administrative and commercial hub without drawing much attention to itself. Guria's real character is outside the town: the rolling green hills, the tea plantations on the lower slopes, the forested ridges rising toward the Meskheti range, and the stretch of Black Sea coast that forms the region's western edge.

What is Guria known for?

Guria is best known as the birthplace of Georgian tea, with a tea-growing tradition stretching back to the 1840s and a specialty-tea revival now centered here. Beyond tea, the region is known for the magnetic black-sand beaches of the resort town of Ureki, the dramatic cloud-sea views from the Gomi ridge, an exceptionally green and rainy landscape, and a local culture famous within Georgia for sharp wit and humor. It's a quiet, authentic corner of western Georgia, well off the main tourist track.

The climate is among the wettest in Georgia, shaped by the Black Sea and the way the coastal hills trap moisture moving inland. Rain falls throughout the year rather than in a single season, and the result is a landscape of persistent greenness — hills covered in vegetation from valley floor to ridgeline, rivers running full even in summer, and a general sense of land that's fertile in itself rather than only through irrigation and effort. That same climate, which makes ordinary cereal farming difficult, turns out to be ideal for tea — and tea, more than anything, has shaped Guria's agricultural and cultural identity over the past century and a half.

Tea: history and revival

Tea cultivation in Georgia began in the 1840s, with the first experimental plantings made in the Ozurgeti area of Guria on the initiative of Russian imperial agronomists looking to develop a domestic tea industry for the empire. The Guruli hills — acidic soils, high rainfall, mild winters, warm humid summers — proved well suited to Camellia sinensis, and cultivation expanded steadily through the late 19th century. By the Soviet period, Georgian tea (grown mainly in Guria, Adjara, Samegrelo, and Abkhazia) had become one of the major agricultural products of the Caucasus, and the terraced plantations covering the hillsides of western Georgia were a defining feature of the Soviet landscape here.

Soviet production prioritized volume over quality, and the large-scale mechanized harvesting and processing of the collective system produced tea that was functional rather than distinguished. The collapse of the Soviet agricultural system in the early 1990s devastated the industry: the collectives dissolved, the processing infrastructure fell into disrepair, and many plantations were abandoned as the economic model that sustained them disappeared. For two decades Georgian tea retreated from its former prominence, and the hillside plantations of Guria grew over.

The revival underway since the 2010s takes a fundamentally different approach. Rather than rebuilding industrial-scale Soviet production, the new Georgian tea industry is built around small family operations producing specialty teas — hand-picked, carefully processed, often organic — that compete on quality rather than quantity. Guria is at the center of this, with a growing number of small producers whose teas have drawn attention from specialty buyers in Europe, Japan, and North America. The most ambitious are working with traditional Georgian tea varieties that were neglected in the Soviet period in favor of higher-yielding but lower-quality cultivars, and the results have surprised markets used to defining quality tea by an East Asian origin.

Visiting a tea producer in Guria usually means walking the plantation itself — the low, dense bushes covering the hillside, their new growth a brighter green than the mature leaves below — watching or joining the hand-picking, and following the leaf through withering, rolling, oxidation, and drying to the finished tea. Conducted on small family farms rather than in organized visitor facilities, these visits have a directness and genuine engagement with the craft comparable to the best wine visits in Kakheti or Racha. The harvest runs from May through October, with the first spring flush — the young growth after winter dormancy — considered the finest of the year. One practical note: because these are small family farms rather than set-up visitor operations, a tea visit is best arranged in advance rather than as a walk-in.

Gomi Mountain and the sea of clouds

In eastern Guria, where the hills rise toward the Meskheti range that forms the boundary with Adjara and Samtskhe-Javakheti, the Gomi ridge reaches close to 2,000 meters and is known for a weather phenomenon that makes it one of western Georgia's more unusual viewpoints. The humid maritime climate and the ridge's elevation combine to create conditions where a sea of cloud often forms in the valleys below while the ridge stays clear above it — especially around sunrise and sunset, when the temperature difference between warm valley air and the cooler ridge produces a fog layer that fills the landscape below. On the mornings it forms fully, the effect is striking: the forested valleys and the distant Black Sea coast vanish beneath a white surface, and the ridge stands above it as if on an island.

Reaching the Gomi area means a road that climbs steeply from the valley floor and gets rougher as it gains elevation. Simple guesthouses and wooden cottages operate in the warmer months, which is what makes it possible to be on the ridge at dawn when the cloud is most likely. The surrounding terrain offers walking through subalpine meadows and forest, with views north toward the Samegrelo lowlands and the distant Greater Caucasus and south toward the Adjarian highlands on clear days. Worth being honest about: the cloud sea is a weather event, not a guarantee — it forms under the right conditions and not on demand, so an overnight stay improves your odds far more than a day trip.

The Black Sea coast and Ureki

Guria's western edge meets the Black Sea along a stretch of coast that includes the resort town of Ureki, known throughout Georgia for its magnetic black-sand beaches. The dark sand comes from the volcanic and mineral content of the rivers draining the Caucasian highlands, deposited along this section of coast by longshore drift, and its relatively high iron content gives it measurable magnetic properties. A medical tradition grew up around the sands — the claim that exposure benefits various circulatory and musculoskeletal conditions — and has sustained a health-tourism industry in Ureki since the Soviet period, when it was developed as a sanatorium destination.

In practice, Ureki is a calm, family-oriented Georgian seaside resort rather than an international beach destination. The water is relatively sheltered and the waves gentle, the resort infrastructure modest but functional and improving, and the crowd is mostly Georgian plus a growing number of visitors from neighboring countries — the summer atmosphere lively and unpretentious. On the famous sand: the magnetic property is real in a mineralogical sense, and the traditional health claims are widely believed locally, but they aren't something we'd present as medically established, so treat the sands as a distinctive feature rather than a treatment. The combination of dark sand, Black Sea water, and the green Gurian hills inland gives Ureki a visual character that sets it apart from the lighter beaches further along the coast.

Lanchkhuti and the central region

The town of Lanchkhuti, between Ozurgeti and the coast, sits in the lower Gurian hills where the steeper tea terrain gives way to flatter farmland approaching the sea. The surrounding villages preserve the traditional domestic architecture of rural Guria — wooden houses on stone foundations with the carved decorative detail characteristic of western Georgian building, set among gardens of fruit trees and tea bushes. The village churches, reflecting several centuries of ecclesiastical building, give points of historical and architectural interest without approaching the significance of the major monuments in neighboring Imereti or Adjara.

Guria also has a distinct cultural personality. Gurians have a long-standing reputation in Georgian cultural lore for a particular sharp wit — a tradition of wordplay, irony, and quick verbal exchange that sets social interaction here apart from the more formal hospitality of eastern Georgia. It's the kind of thing that's hard to verify on a short visit, but it's consistently reported by Georgian travelers who know the region, and it adds a dimension to encounters with local people that goes beyond the universal Georgian warmth.

Rivers and regional boundaries

The Choloki River, flowing west through southern Guria to the Black Sea, marks the historical boundary between Guria and Adjara and has served as a significant administrative and cultural border line through various political configurations. The area around the Choloki crossing carries that boundary-zone history, and the transition from the predominantly Christian Gurian communities to the mixed Christian-Muslim demographic of Adjara becomes gradually apparent as you move south through the lower hills toward Batumi.

The Natanebi River, in the north, similarly marks the boundary with Samegrelo. The forested hills between the two rivers are the heart of Guria — where the tea plantations are densest, the landscape most characteristic, and the regional identity most strongly felt.

Getting there and around

Guria sits on the main Tbilisi–Batumi transport corridor, and its primary towns, Lanchkhuti and Ozurgeti, are served by both the railway and the main highway. The train from Tbilisi to Ozurgeti takes around four hours, and marshrutkas connect the region to both Tbilisi and Batumi with reasonable frequency. Ureki is reachable from Tbilisi by train (there's a station serving the resort) and by marshrutka. Within the region, reaching the tea plantations, the Gomi ridge, and the interior villages needs a car or taxi, as public transport beyond the main towns is limited. The roads through the tea hills are narrow and winding but generally passable in a standard vehicle, though the higher Gomi approaches benefit from better ground clearance.

When to visit

Guria works year-round at the coast and lower elevations, with Ureki functioning mainly as a summer resort from June through September. The tea harvest — May through October — is the most rewarding window for the plantations, with the first flush in May and early June rated the finest by most producers. The landscape is at its greenest in spring and early summer, when the rainfall has saturated the hillsides and the tea bushes are growing actively. Autumn is quieter and pleasant, with mild temperatures and the harvest mood extending from the tea fields into the fruit orchards and the small-scale family winemaking that supplements many Gurian households' income. The Gomi ridge is most rewarding from late spring through early autumn, when the cloud phenomenon is most likely and access to the higher terrain is reliable.

faq.title: Where is Guria, and what is it known for?
faq.content: Guria is a small region in western Georgia, between the Black Sea coast, the Adjara highlands, Samegrelo, and Imereti, with Ozurgeti as its center. It's best known as the birthplace of Georgian tea and home to a specialty-tea revival, the magnetic black-sand beaches of Ureki, and the cloud-sea views of the Gomi ridge.

faq.title: Can you visit tea farms in Guria?
faq.content: Yes. Guria is the heart of Georgia's specialty-tea revival, and a number of small family producers welcome visitors to walk the plantation, see the hand-picking, and follow the leaf through processing. Because these are small farms rather than set-up visitor centers, it's best to arrange a visit in advance. The harvest runs May to October, with the spring first flush considered the finest.

faq.title: What are the magnetic sands of Ureki?
faq.content: Ureki's beaches have dark, iron-rich volcanic sand with measurable magnetic properties, deposited along the coast by the rivers draining the Caucasus. A health-tourism tradition since the Soviet era attributes benefits to the sands; the magnetism is real mineralogically, though the medical claims aren't established. Ureki is a calm, family-friendly summer resort.

faq.title: What is Gomi Mountain and the "sea of clouds"?
faq.content: Gomi is a ridge in eastern Guria reaching close to 2,000 meters, known for a phenomenon where cloud fills the valleys below while the ridge stays clear above — most often around sunrise and sunset. Staying overnight in a ridge guesthouse gives the best chance of seeing it, since it depends on the right weather and isn't guaranteed.

faq.title: How do you get to Guria?
faq.content: Guria sits on the main Tbilisi–Batumi corridor. Ozurgeti and Lanchkhuti are served by train (Tbilisi–Ozurgeti is roughly four hours) and by marshrutka from both Tbilisi and Batumi, and Ureki has its own rail stop. To reach the tea farms, Gomi, and the interior villages you'll want a car or taxi, as public transport beyond the towns is limited.

thingsToDoGuria.heroTitle: Things to Do in Guria: Tea Farms, Gomi Mountain, and the Coast
thingsToDoGuria.content:
Guria, in western Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — packs tea culture, green landscapes, a quiet stretch of Black Sea coast, and a remarkable mountain viewpoint into one of the country's smallest regions. Travel here feels slower and more local than in the better-known destinations: less structured, less crowded, and built more around daily life and nature than around tourist infrastructure. Tea is the thread running through it all. Here are the main things to do.

What are the best things to do in Guria?

The highlights are visiting a family tea farm and tasting Georgian specialty tea; catching the "sea of clouds" from the Gomi ridge at dawn; relaxing on the magnetic black-sand beach at Ureki; and travelling slowly through the green tea-growing hills and traditional villages. Guria's appeal is its combination of mountains, coast, and tea in a small area, with very few other tourists around.

Visit a tea farm and taste Georgian tea

The single best reason to come to Guria is its tea. This is the birthplace of Georgian tea cultivation, and the region is now at the heart of a specialty-tea revival built on small family farms rather than the old Soviet industrial plantations. A visit usually means walking the plantation — the low, dense bushes covering the hillside — seeing or joining the hand-picking, and following the leaf through withering, rolling, oxidation, and drying before tasting the finished tea with the people who made it. It's informal and personal, often combined with homemade local food, and closer in spirit to a Kakheti family-cellar wine visit than to a commercial tour. The harvest runs May through October, with the spring first flush rated the finest of the year.

Because these are working family farms rather than set-up visitor centers, a tea visit is best arranged in advance rather than dropped in on.

See the "sea of clouds" from Gomi Mountain

In eastern Guria, the Gomi ridge rises to close to 2,000 meters and is known for a striking weather phenomenon: cloud fills the valleys below while the ridge stays clear above, so the forested hills and the distant coast vanish under a white surface and the ridge seems to float above it. It's most likely around sunrise and sunset, when the temperature difference between warm valley air and the cooler ridge forms the fog layer. Simple guesthouses and wooden cottages operate up there in the warmer months, which is what lets you be on the ridge at dawn when the effect is most likely, and the surrounding subalpine meadows and forest are good for walking, with long views toward the Greater Caucasus and the Adjarian highlands on clear days.

Worth setting expectations honestly: the cloud sea is a weather event, not a guarantee — it appears under the right conditions and not on demand, so an overnight stay improves your chances far more than a quick day trip.
