The current bathhouse buildings date mostly from the 17th to 19th centuries, though baths have occupied this valley in various forms for well over a thousand years (the domes themselves are a later addition, built to retain heat over the semi-underground bathing halls, with skylights to admit light and let the sulfuric steam escape). Several still operate, ranging from simple public facilities to elaborately fitted private establishments.

The most photographed is the Chreli Abano — the "Colorful" or "Painted" Bath, also known as the Orbeliani Baths — whose blue-tiled, Persian-influenced facade stands out sharply against the plain brick around it. That aesthetic reflects the long period of Iranian cultural influence over this part of Georgia, and the facade is one of the clearest surviving architectural expressions of it in Tbilisi; renovated and reopened in 2017, Chreli Abano is now the most polished (and most tourist-popular) of the baths. Other well-known establishments include Bathhouse No. 5 (among the oldest and most traditional) and King Erekle's Bath.

The standard arrangement is a series of private rooms, each with a sunken stone or tiled pool fed by the sulfur water, hired by the hour for individuals or small groups. There are also cheaper public baths, which are typically sex-segregated and more communal in feel. The water is naturally warm and strongly mineralized, with a sulfur content that gives it a distinctive smell — the hydrogen-sulfide, rotten-egg note that most people stop noticing within a few minutes. Its therapeutic reputation, particularly for skin conditions and joint problems, has been maintained continuously since the medieval period, and the baths have long served as social spaces where business was done, relationships kept up, and community life organized — well beyond simple hygiene.

A kisi (a vigorous deep-scrubbing exfoliation with a coarse glove, performed by an attendant called a mekise) is available at most of the established baths and is very much part of the authentic experience for those willing to submit to it — the result leaves the skin noticeably cleaner than any shower could manage. Various massages are also offered, with quality varying considerably between establishments.

The sulfur baths are not designed for the easily startled: the water is hot, the smell is present, and in the older establishments the rooms are functional rather than luxurious. The newer and more tourist-oriented baths (Chreli Abano among them) have upgraded their facilities considerably and make a more comfortable introduction; the older places have a patina and an authenticity the renovated versions can't fully replicate. It's a genuine trade-off, and worth choosing deliberately.

The district

Walking through Abanotubani without entering a bath is worthwhile for the architecture and atmosphere alone. Its streets are among the narrowest and most atmospheric in the Old Town, and the combination of the domed bath buildings, the traditional houses on the surrounding slopes, the small bridges over the Tsavkisistsqali, and Narikala Fortress on the ridge directly above creates an environment that feels distinct from the rest of the historic center. The steam rising from the dome skylights in cool weather gives the district a slightly otherworldly quality that photographers find irresistible.

The small gorge of the Tsavkisistsqali, on the eastern edge of the district, leads south to the Leghvtakhevi Waterfall and the entrance to the National Botanical Garden — connecting the bath district to a natural landscape that begins just meters from the urban fabric. Bathhouses, gorge, waterfall, and garden together make the southeastern corner of the Old Town one of the most concentrated areas of interest in the whole city.

Abanotubani is also the lower approach to Narikala: the paths climbing from the bath district through the residential streets above lead directly to the fortress walls and the Mother of Georgia (Kartlis Deda) statue on the ridge, making the district a natural starting point for the ascent to the city's most important elevated viewpoint.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: Abanotubani, Old Tbilisi — at the foot of Narikala Fortress, along the Tsavkisistsqali stream, southeastern edge of the Old Town.
  • The district: Freely accessible at all hours; free to walk through.
  • The baths: Several operate daily, typically from morning until late evening (some, including the Orbeliani/Chreli Abano, open late into the night). Private rooms are charged per room by the hour; cheaper public baths are usually sex-segregated. Kisi scrubs and massages cost extra. Booking ahead (especially for Chreli Abano in high season) is wise.
  • Combine with: Narikala Fortress and the Mother of Georgia statue (up the slope), the Leghvtakhevi Waterfall and the National Botanical Garden (up the gorge), and the rest of the Old Town just across the way.

faq.title: What is Abanotubani?
faq.content: Abanotubani is the historic sulfur-baths district of Old Tbilisi, built over natural hot springs at the foot of Narikala Fortress. The name means "bath district" in Georgian, and the neighborhood is recognizable by its low brick domes, which cover semi-underground bathhouses fed directly by geothermal sulfur water at around 38–40°C. People have bathed here for over 1,500 years — by legend the springs led King Vakhtang Gorgasali to found Tbilisi on the spot in the 5th century, and the city's name comes from the Georgian word for "warm." Several bathhouses still operate today, offering private rooms, the traditional kisi scrub, and massages.

faq.title: Why do the sulfur baths matter to Tbilisi's history?
faq.content: They're effectively the reason the city exists. By the founding legend, King Vakhtang Gorgasali was hunting in the 5th century when his falcon chased a pheasant into a hot spring and the bird came out cooked; impressed by the water, the king decided to build his capital here. Whether or not the story is literally true, the name Tbilisi comes directly from tbili, "warm," for these springs. By the 13th century some 65 bathhouses were recorded in the city, Marco Polo wrote about them, and over the centuries they served as social hubs as much as places to wash.

faq.title: What is the Chreli Abano (Orbeliani Baths)?
faq.content: Chreli Abano — the "Colorful" or "Painted" Bath, also known as the Orbeliani Baths — is the most photographed bathhouse in Abanotubani, instantly recognizable by its blue-tiled, Persian-influenced facade, which reflects the long period of Iranian cultural influence over this part of Georgia. Renovated and reopened in 2017, it's now the most polished and most tourist-popular of the baths, with modern private rooms and a spa section. Other well-known bathhouses include the traditional Bathhouse No. 5 and King Erekle's Bath.

faq.title: What is a kisi, and what is the bath experience like?
faq.content: A kisi is a vigorous deep-scrubbing exfoliation done with a coarse glove by an attendant (a mekise) — it leaves the skin far cleaner than a normal shower and is very much part of the authentic experience. The baths themselves are functional rather than luxurious in the older establishments: the water is hot and strongly mineralized, and there's a sulfur (rotten-egg) smell that most people stop noticing within a few minutes. Most visitors hire a private room by the hour; cheaper public baths also exist and are usually sex-segregated. The newer, tourist-oriented baths are more comfortable; the older ones have more patina and atmosphere.

faq.title: Can you just walk through Abanotubani without bathing, and what's nearby?
faq.content: Yes — walking the district is free and worthwhile for the architecture and atmosphere alone, with its domed bathhouses, narrow streets, small bridges over the stream, and Narikala Fortress rising directly above. Just beyond, the small Tsavkisistsqali gorge leads to the Leghvtakhevi Waterfall and the entrance to the National Botanical Garden, and paths up the slope climb to Narikala and the Mother of Georgia statue. It's one of the most concentrated areas of interest in the whole city, and a natural starting point for the climb up to the fortress.

chreliAbano.heroTitle: Chreli Abano (the Orbeliani Baths): Tbilisi's Colorful Sulfur Bath
chreliAbano.content:
In the Abanotubani district of Tbilisi, capital of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus), where most bathhouses present their plain brick domes to the street with little elaboration, the Chreli Abano stands apart at once. Its facade is covered in Persian-influenced tilework — blue, turquoise, and white geometric and floral patterns under pointed arches, topped with a pair of small minaret-like turrets — so distinctive that first-time visitors often mistake it for a mosque. Also known as the Orbeliani Baths, it is the most photographed building in Abanotubani and one of the most recognizable facades in Tbilisi, and the contrast between its ornate exterior and the plain domes of its neighbors makes it more striking still.

What is the Chreli Abano?

Chreli Abano — "the Colorful Bath" — is the most famous of the sulfur bathhouses in Tbilisi's Abanotubani district, also known as the Orbeliani Baths (after the family that once owned it). The bathhouse itself dates to the 17th–18th century, like the other Abanotubani baths, but its celebrated blue-and-turquoise tiled, minaret-topped Persian-style facade was added in the 19th century. Restored between 2013 and 2016 and reopened in 2017, it's now the most polished and visitor-friendly of the district's baths, while still operating as a genuine working bathhouse fed by the natural hot sulfur springs. Inside are private rooms hired by the hour, the traditional kisi scrub, and massages — and it sits in the heart of Abanotubani, below Narikala Fortress.

The colorful facade

The name Chreli Abano means "colorful bath," which rather understates the case. The bathhouse takes its formal name, the Orbeliani Baths, from the noble family that owned it; while the underlying structure belongs to the 17th–18th-century tradition of Persian-influenced bath architecture that shaped Abanotubani, the building was transformed into its ornate "oriental" form in the 19th century, when the tiled facade was applied. Tbilisi's position at the crossroads of Persian, Ottoman, and Russian cultural influence produced a famously eclectic built environment, and the decorative vocabulary chosen here is drawn from the Islamic architectural tradition of Iran: the pointed arches, the geometric and floral tilework, the twin minaret-like turrets, the overall compositional logic. Applied to a public bathhouse in a predominantly Christian Georgian city, the result is entirely characteristic of the layered cultural identity of Old Tbilisi — and so convincingly mosque-like that the confusion is understandable.

The bath experience

Behind the celebrated facade, Chreli Abano is a fully working bathhouse, fed by the same natural geothermal springs that have supplied Abanotubani since the city's founding. The water reaches the surface at around 38–40°C, carrying the mineral content — primarily sulfur compounds — that gives it both its distinctive smell and the therapeutic reputation that has sustained the bathing tradition here for centuries.

The bathhouse offers private rooms for individuals or small groups, each with a stone or tiled pool fed by the hot spring water (it has more than a dozen rooms across a range of sizes and prices, and no communal area — every room is private). Hiring a room by the hour, rather than using a shared public bath, is the standard Abanotubani format and allows a more relaxed, personal experience. The kisi — a vigorous full-body scrub performed by an attendant (a mekise) with a coarse mitt, removing layers of dead skin with real efficiency — is available and considered by most regulars an essential part of the experience rather than an optional extra; a massage on the traditional marble bench (topchan) can be added too.

Chreli Abano sits at the more organized, visitor-oriented end of the Abanotubani spectrum — the 2013–2016 restoration brought modern, well-maintained facilities and a more structured booking process than the smaller, more improvised establishments — without losing the essential character of a working bathhouse. It suits travelers who want to engage with the sulfur-bath tradition without the uncertainty of a more ad-hoc setup. That said, the atmosphere inside stays traditional and unhurried rather than spa-like: this is a bathhouse, not a wellness resort, and that distinction is part of the appeal. (For a rawer, more old-school atmosphere, some of the district's other baths — like the more historic Bathhouse No. 5 — go further in that direction.)

It's a place with history as well as looks: among its past visitors were Alexander Pushkin and Alexandre Dumas, who bathed in Abanotubani's waters on their travels through Tiflis.

The surroundings

Chreli Abano sits in the heart of Abanotubani, among the other bathhouses, the narrow streets of the historic district, and the approaches to the Leghvtakhevi gorge and waterfall and the National Botanical Garden to the south. The walls of Narikala Fortress rise on the ridge above, and the cable-car station (and the Bridge of Peace and Rike Park) are a short walk away. An evening visit, approached through the lamp-lit streets after dinner, is one of the more distinctly Tbilisian experiences a visitor can have — the steam around the dome skylights, the stream running through the gorge nearby, the atmosphere of the neighborhood after dark — a context a daytime visit, pleasant as it is, can't fully match.

Booking ahead is strongly recommended, especially for evenings and weekends, when demand for private rooms is highest; walk-in availability can be unpredictable at busy times.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: 2 Abano Street, Abanotubani, Old Tbilisi — in the heart of the bath district, below Narikala.
  • Hours: Open daily, roughly morning until late evening (commonly around 09:00–23:00).
  • Rooms: Private rooms hired by the hour (prices vary by room size); kisi scrub and massage available at additional cost.
  • Booking: Strongly recommended, especially evenings and weekends — online via the official website or by phone.
  • Website: chreli-abano.ge
  • Combine with: The rest of Abanotubani, the Leghvtakhevi gorge and waterfall, the Botanical Garden, Narikala Fortress and the Mother of Georgia, the Ateshgah fire temple, and (a short walk away) the Bridge of Peace and Rike Park.

faq.title: What is Chreli Abano?
faq.content: Chreli Abano — "the Colorful Bath," also known as the Orbeliani Baths — is the most famous of the sulfur bathhouses in Tbilisi's historic Abanotubani district. It's instantly recognizable for its ornate Persian-style facade of blue and turquoise tilework under pointed arches, topped with small minarets, which leads many first-time visitors to mistake it for a mosque. The bathhouse itself dates to the 17th–18th century; the tiled facade was added in the 19th century. Restored between 2013 and 2016 and reopened in 2017, it's the most polished and visitor-friendly of the district's baths, while still operating as a genuine working sulfur bath.

faq.title: Why is it also called the Orbeliani Baths?
faq.content: "Orbeliani Baths" is its formal name, taken from the Orbeliani family — Georgian nobility — who once owned it. "Chreli Abano" ("Colorful Bath") is the popular name, after its vivid tiled facade. You'll see both names used, sometimes interchangeably, and occasionally the "Motley" or "Blue" Baths as well — they all refer to the same building.

faq.title: What's the bath experience like?
faq.content: You hire a private room by the hour (there are more than a dozen, in a range of sizes and prices — there's no communal area here, every room is private), each with a stone or tiled pool fed by the natural hot sulfur water, which surfaces at around 38–40°C and has a noticeable sulfur smell. The signature add-on is the kisi — a vigorous full-body scrub by an attendant with a coarse mitt that leaves the skin remarkably clean — and a massage on a marble bench can be added too. The atmosphere is traditional and unhurried rather than spa-like; it's polished and comfortable, but it's a working bathhouse, not a wellness resort.

faq.title: How does it compare to the other Abanotubani baths?
faq.content: Chreli Abano is the most photogenic and the most polished — the easiest, most comfortable introduction to the sulfur baths, with modern facilities and structured booking, which also makes it the most popular with visitors. If you want a rawer, more old-school atmosphere, some of the district's other baths (such as the more historic Bathhouse No. 5) lean further that way. None is "better" in absolute terms — it depends whether you prioritize comfort and looks (Chreli Abano) or stripped-back authenticity (the older public baths).

faq.title: Do I need to book, and what are the practicalities?
faq.content: Booking ahead is strongly recommended, especially for evenings and weekends when private rooms are in highest demand — you can reserve online via the official website (chreli-abano.ge) or by phone, and walk-in availability can be unpredictable at busy times. It's at 2 Abano Street in the heart of Abanotubani, open daily from roughly morning to late evening. Pay the kisi attendant (mekise) in cash, and tipping is customary. It's an easy walk from the rest of the Old Town, Narikala, and the Bridge of Peace.

anchiskhatiBasilica.heroTitle: Anchiskhati Basilica: The Oldest Church in Tbilisi
anchiskhatiBasilica.content:
Anchiskhati is the oldest surviving church in Tbilisi, capital of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus), built in the 6th century during the reign of King Dachi of Iberia — the son of Vakhtang Gorgasali who completed the transfer of the Georgian capital from Mtskheta to Tbilisi. That sequence — the founding of the city, the building of its first major church within a generation — places Anchiskhati at the very beginning of Tbilisi's life as a capital. The building has stood in roughly the same spot ever since, outlasting invasions, occupations, and earthquakes across the full arc of the city's fourteen centuries. Roughly contemporary with Hagia Sophia in Constantinople, though far more modest in scale, it is still an active church of the Georgian Orthodox Church today.

What is the Anchiskhati Basilica?

Anchiskhati is the oldest surviving church in Tbilisi — a 6th-century three-nave basilica in the northern part of the Old Town, built under King Dachi around 522–534 and originally dedicated to the Virgin Mary. It takes its present name from the Anchiskhati icon of Christ, brought here in the 17th century from the Ancha monastery in Klarjeti (now in Turkey) for safekeeping. Architecturally it is the only early basilica of its kind still standing in Tbilisi, predating the domed churches that became the Georgian norm from the 9th century on. It is also closely tied to Georgian sacred polyphony: its choir is among the most celebrated keepers of that UNESCO-recognized tradition. The church is small, modest, and still in active use — and easy to walk past if you're not looking for it.

A church easy to miss

Anchiskhati is easy to miss if you don't know to look for it. It sits on a quiet street in the northern Old Town, its modest facade set slightly back from the lane, with none of the dramatic elevation of Metekhi or the overwhelming scale of the Sameba Cathedral. That modesty is part of its character — stepping through the gate into the small courtyard and finding the old stone in front of you produces a quiet satisfaction the more prominent landmarks rarely offer.

Architecture

Anchiskhati is a three-nave basilica — the early Christian church form, with three parallel naves divided by columns and a rounded apse at the eastern end, that predates the domed cross-in-square plan adopted across Georgia from the 9th century onward. (It should not be confused with the Georgian "three-church basilica," where the naves are separated by solid walls; here they are divided by columns in the classic basilica manner.) The basilica was the dominant early church type throughout the Byzantine world, and its survival here makes Anchiskhati the only example of this early type still standing in Tbilisi — a crucial document of the pre-medieval phase of Georgian church architecture, valuable beyond its age alone.

The building has been modified and partly rebuilt several times across its long history — most significantly in the 17th century, when a bell tower was added and parts of the structure were reconstructed after damage from Persian and Ottoman raids. The original 6th-century church was built of cut yellow tuff stone, some of it still visible in the lower walls; the upper walls and the interior columns were rebuilt in brick in later restorations (the apse, entrance arch, and altar window keep their early form). The current appearance reflects these accumulated interventions rather than a single original design — but the essential character of the early basilica survives: the low, horizontal proportions, the three-nave plan, the sense of enclosure and intimacy that the later domed churches, with their vertical drama, deliberately set aside.

The bell tower, added in the 17th century, introduces a vertical element that contrasts with the low body of the church and has become one of the more recognizable parts of its silhouette in the Old Town streetscape. The interior is correspondingly intimate — smaller and lower than any other significant church in the historic center, its three naves divided by columns, the walls carrying frescoes in varying states of preservation (most of what's visible dates from late-17th-century and 19th-century campaigns, over older fragments), and a finely carved wooden iconostasis. The overall atmosphere is one of accumulated age and quiet devotion.

The name and the icon

The church was originally dedicated to the Virgin Mary — it is still formally the Church of the Nativity of the Virgin Mary — and took the name Anchiskhati ("icon of Ancha") in the 17th century, when the icon that gives it that name was brought here. The Anchiskhati icon is a celebrated medieval image of Christ the Savior, originally kept at the Ancha monastery in the Klarjeti region of historically Georgian territory now within Turkey; its famous silver-gilt setting is the work of the renowned 12th-century goldsmith Beka Opizari, a masterpiece of Georgian metalwork. When Klarjeti came under Ottoman pressure, the icon was brought to Tbilisi (around 1664) and enshrined at this church for safekeeping — giving the basilica the name it has carried since. The original icon is now kept in the Georgian National Museum, but the association between the church and this historically significant object remains part of its identity.

A further detail of its history: Anchiskhati is remembered as the church whose bells were never silenced, even through the long centuries of Arab rule over Tbilisi, which earned it the nickname "Church of the Bell." It served, too, as a center of religious and cultural learning — a seminary and school were established here under King Erekle II in the 18th century. Closed during the Soviet period, like most churches, it returned to active worship in the late 1980s.

Georgian polyphony

Anchiskhati has a particular association with Georgian sacred polyphony — the tradition of three-voice harmony that UNESCO recognizes as Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity, and one of Georgia's most distinctive contributions to world music. The Anchiskhati Choir, which sings here during services, is among the most respected keepers of this tradition, and attending a service when the choir is singing is one of the most memorable musical experiences in Tbilisi. The acoustics of the small basilica suit polyphony in a way larger, more reverberant spaces do not — the sound fills the interior without becoming indistinct, and the combination of ancient stone, candlelight, and three-voice harmony is genuinely moving even for visitors with no religious investment in what they're hearing. Service times vary, but Sunday mornings are the most reliable opportunity to hear the choir.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: Ioane Shavteli Street, northern part of the Old Town, Tbilisi.
  • Hours: Open daily, roughly morning to evening; services are held through the week, and the church is open to visitors outside active service periods.
  • Admission: Free.
  • Dress code: Modest dress required — shoulders and knees covered; women are expected to cover their heads (wraps are often available at the entrance).
  • Photography: Generally not permitted inside (to protect the frescoes and the atmosphere of worship); please respect this and any signs.
  • Etiquette: This is an active place of worship — move quietly, and avoid disturbing those praying, especially during services.
  • Combine with: It's an easy walk from the rest of the Old Town — Sioni Cathedral, the Bridge of Peace, Rike Park, and (across the river) Metekhi; the sulfur baths of Abanotubani and Narikala are a short walk south.

faq.title: What is the Anchiskhati Basilica?
faq.content: Anchiskhati is the oldest surviving church in Tbilisi, a 6th-century three-nave basilica in the northern part of the Old Town, built under King Dachi of Iberia around 522–534 and originally dedicated to the Virgin Mary. It's architecturally the only early basilica of its kind still standing in the city, predating the domed churches that became the Georgian norm from the 9th century onward. It takes its present name from the Anchiskhati icon of Christ, brought here in the 17th century for safekeeping, and it's closely tied to Georgian polyphonic singing through its renowned choir. It remains an active church of the Georgian Orthodox Church.

faq.title: How old is it, and who built it?
faq.content: It dates from the 6th century — roughly 522–534 — built during the reign of King Dachi of Iberia, the son and successor of Vakhtang Gorgasali, who completed the move of the Georgian capital from Mtskheta to Tbilisi. That makes it roughly contemporary with Hagia Sophia in Constantinople, though far more modest in scale. It has been damaged and rebuilt many times since — most notably in the 17th century, when the bell tower was added and the upper walls and columns were rebuilt in brick — but the lower walls still contain original 6th-century tuff stonework, and the early basilica character survives.

faq.title: Why is it called "Anchiskhati"?
faq.content: The name means "icon of Ancha." The church was originally dedicated to the Virgin Mary, but in the 17th century it took the name of the Anchiskhati icon — a celebrated medieval image of Christ the Savior, with a famous silver-gilt setting by the 12th-century Georgian goldsmith Beka Opizari. The icon was originally kept at the Ancha monastery in the Klarjeti region (now part of Turkey); when that area came under Ottoman pressure, it was brought to Tbilisi (around 1664) and enshrined here for safekeeping, giving the church its lasting name. The original icon is now in the Georgian National Museum.

faq.title: Can you hear the polyphonic choir there?
faq.content: Yes — Anchiskhati is one of the best places in Tbilisi to hear Georgian sacred polyphony, the three-voice harmony tradition recognized by UNESCO as Intangible Cultural Heritage. The Anchiskhati Choir, which sings during services, is among the most respected keepers of the tradition, and the small basilica's acoustics suit the singing beautifully. Service times vary, but Sunday mornings are the most reliable opportunity. It's a genuinely moving experience even for visitors with no religious connection to the music — just attend respectfully, as a participant in worship rather than a spectator.

faq.title: What should I know before visiting?
faq.content: It's an active church, so dress modestly — shoulders and knees covered, and women are expected to cover their heads (wraps are often available at the door). Photography is generally not permitted inside, to protect the frescoes and the atmosphere of worship. Entry is free, and the church is open daily from roughly morning to evening, though it's busiest and most atmospheric during services. It's small and easy to walk past, on a quiet street in the northern Old Town, and it sits within easy walking distance of Sioni Cathedral, the Bridge of Peace, and the rest of the historic center.

ateshgahFireTemple.heroTitle: The Ateshgah: Tbilisi's Zoroastrian Fire Temple
ateshgahFireTemple.content:
The Ateshgah is a small, ancient brick building on the Old Town slope between Narikala Fortress and the lower streets of Tbilisi, in Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus) — a short uphill walk from the sulfur baths of Abanotubani. It is generally identified as a Zoroastrian fire temple, dating to the period when Sasanian Persian culture and religion shaped much of life in this part of the Caucasus, most likely the 5th to 7th centuries. Often called the northernmost Zoroastrian fire temple in the world, it is easy to walk past without noticing — but for those who seek it out, it offers something the busier sites nearby cannot: a quiet encounter with a layer of Tbilisi's history that predates the city's Christian identity and reflects the religious complexity of a place that has always sat at the meeting point of civilizations.

What is the Ateshgah?

The Ateshgah (from Persian, "place of fire") is an ancient brick fire temple in the Betlemi (Bethlehem) quarter of Old Tbilisi, generally identified as a Zoroastrian place of worship from the Sasanian Persian era — most likely the 5th–7th centuries, though its exact date is uncertain and still debated by historians. It's one of the oldest religious buildings in the city and is often described as the northernmost Zoroastrian fire temple in the world. Over its long life the building was repurposed more than once — including a long spell as a mosque from the 1720s, and later use as a storehouse and a dwelling — before being restored in 2007–2009 and protected under a curved transparent roof. Small, quiet, and tucked into the residential lanes below Narikala, it's one of the Old Town's most atmospheric minor sights.

Historical context

Zoroastrianism — one of the world's oldest continuously practiced religions, built around the veneration of fire as a sacred element — was the dominant faith of the Sasanian Persian Empire and had real influence across the Caucasus territories Persia controlled or contested in late antiquity. Georgia's conversion to Christianity (traditionally dated to around 337 AD) did not immediately or uniformly displace other practices, especially in communities under Persian political and cultural influence. In the 4th–6th centuries, eastern Georgia (Kartli) came repeatedly under Iranian sway, and Zoroastrian practice coexisted with Christianity in Tbilisi — to the point that, alongside the Christian bishop, sources record a "Persian bishop." The fire temple — ateshgah, "place of fire" — was the characteristic building of this tradition, built around a flame kept continuously burning.

The Tbilisi Ateshgah belongs to this world. Its likely period of construction falls within the centuries of Persian–Georgian contest around and after Tbilisi's founding as a capital in the 5th century, when the city's population included Persian administrators, merchants, and soldiers alongside Georgian and Armenian communities, each maintaining their own faith. (Honest caveat: the exact date is genuinely unsettled — estimates range across the Sasanian era, roughly the 4th to 7th centuries, and scholars continue to debate it; what's clear is that it's among the oldest surviving buildings in the city.) That it survived at all owes something to Tbilisi's long-standing reputation as a city where different religions coexisted.

A building of many lives

Rather than simply falling into disuse as Christianity consolidated, the Ateshgah was repurposed across the centuries — which is part of what makes it interesting. After its life as a fire temple it served various functions (one account places an observatory here in the 11th–12th centuries), and from the 1720s it was converted into a mosque during a period of Ottoman rule, which altered its appearance; later still it was used as a storehouse and then as a dwelling. Through all of this the core brick structure of the original temple survived — too modest to be deliberately destroyed, too solid to collapse, and (by one account) quietly left out of the historical record during the Soviet period precisely so as not to draw the attention that might have doomed it.

In 2007–2009 the building was carefully restored (as part of a wider rehabilitation of the historic Betlemi district), its old brickwork cleaned and gently repaired rather than overhauled, and a curved transparent (Perspex) roof added to protect the structure — its original dome having long since collapsed. What you see today, then, is not a raw ruin but a conserved ancient building under a protective canopy, with its layered history legible in the brickwork for those who look.

The structure and the setting

The Ateshgah is a modest, almost square brick building, its simplicity reflecting the functional character of Zoroastrian sacred architecture, which prioritized the sacred fire over architectural elaboration. The interior is a single small space, with a niche where the flame would have been kept; the later mosque conversion and other interventions have left their marks, so reading the original form takes a little imagination. The dome is gone (hence the protective roof), and the building's various lives are written into its fabric.

What it lacks in grandeur it makes up for in setting. From its position on the slope it looks out over the rooftops of the Old Town, with the domed bathhouses of Abanotubani below, the Mtkvari River beyond, and the hills of the far bank rising behind — an intimate, unhurried view, modest by Tbilisi's standards of elevated panoramas but quietly rewarding, and a good sense of the topography of the Old Town that street level doesn't give.

Finding it and visiting

The Ateshgah's obscurity is part of its character, but it does mean finding it takes deliberate navigation. It sits in the Betlemi (Bethlehem) quarter, on Gomi Street, a short way east of the Bethlehem Church and on the slopes northeast of the Mother of Georgia (Kartlis Deda) statue — reached by climbing the narrow lanes that wind up from Abanotubani toward Narikala, often through a small courtyard. It isn't prominently signposted from the main streets, and the approach through the lanes — past old houses, small gardens, and residents going about their day — is itself part of the experience of a corner of the Old Town that runs on its own terms.

It fits naturally into a walking route up this slope: the sulfur baths of Abanotubani at the base, the Ateshgah partway up, and Narikala Fortress and the Mother of Georgia statue at the top — a sequence covering the full range of historical and cultural layers concentrated here, comfortably done in two to three hours.

  • Location: Betlemi (Bethlehem) quarter / Gomi Street, Old Tbilisi — on the slope below Narikala, up from Abanotubani, near the Bethlehem Church.
  • Hours / access: Generally accessible in daylight hours, but its low-key status (and entry via a private courtyard) means there's no fixed schedule — access can be informal and variable, so it may be best visited with a guide or as part of a walk.
  • Admission: Generally free.
  • Time needed: About 10–15 minutes — enough to look at the structure, take in the view, and pause in a place much of the city has half-forgotten.
  • Combine with: Abanotubani, Narikala Fortress, the Mother of Georgia, and the Bethlehem Church — all on the same Old Town slope.

faq.title: What is the Ateshgah in Tbilisi?
faq.content: The Ateshgah (from Persian, "place of fire") is an ancient brick fire temple in the Betlemi quarter of Old Tbilisi, generally identified as a Zoroastrian place of worship from the Sasanian Persian era — most likely the 5th–7th centuries, though its exact date is uncertain and still debated. It's one of the oldest religious buildings in the city and is often called the northernmost Zoroastrian fire temple in the world. Over its long life it was repurposed several times — including a long spell as a mosque from the 1720s — before being restored in 2007–2009 and protected under a curved transparent roof. It's a small, quiet, atmospheric sight on the slope below Narikala Fortress.

faq.title: Is it really a Zoroastrian fire temple?
faq.content: That's the generally accepted identification, and it's why the building carries the name Ateshgah. It dates to the period when eastern Georgia was under strong Sasanian Persian influence, when Zoroastrianism coexisted with Christianity in Tbilisi, and it has the form of a Zoroastrian fire temple — built around a sacred flame. That said, its exact construction date isn't settled (estimates span the Sasanian era, roughly the 4th–7th centuries), and historians continue to debate the details, partly because so little written record survives and because the building was repurposed so many times. What's clear is that it's among the oldest surviving structures in the city and a rare trace of pre-Christian religious life there.

faq.title: Why was it not destroyed when Georgia became Christian?
faq.content: Several reasons. Georgia's conversion to Christianity (traditionally around 337 AD) didn't immediately erase other faiths, and Zoroastrianism persisted in Tbilisi while the city remained under Persian influence; Tbilisi also had a long reputation as a place where different religions coexisted. Crucially, the building never simply sat empty — it was repurposed over the centuries (reportedly an observatory in the 11th–12th centuries, then a mosque from the 1720s, later a storehouse and a dwelling), which kept the structure in use and standing. It was too modest to be a target and too solid to collapse, and so it survived.

faq.title: What is there to see, and what's it like to visit?
faq.content: It's a small, almost square brick building — a single interior space with a niche for the sacred fire — now conserved under a curved transparent protective roof (its original dome collapsed long ago), with the marks of its later lives, including the mosque conversion, visible in the fabric. It's not a grand monument; the appeal is the quiet, the age, and the layered history, plus an intimate view over the Old Town rooftops, the Abanotubani bath domes, and the Mtkvari River. A visit takes only ten or fifteen minutes, and the absence of crowds gives it a real stillness.

faq.title: Where is it, and how do you find it?
faq.content: It's in the Betlemi (Bethlehem) quarter of Old Tbilisi, on Gomi Street, a short way east of the Bethlehem Church and on the slope northeast of the Mother of Georgia statue — reached by climbing the narrow lanes up from the Abanotubani bath district toward Narikala Fortress, sometimes through a small courtyard. It isn't well signposted and its opening can be informal, so it's easy to miss; many visitors find it most easily as part of a walk up the slope (Abanotubani → Ateshgah → Narikala → Mother of Georgia) or with a guide. Entry is generally free.

bazariOrbeliani.heroTitle: Bazari Orbeliani: Tbilisi's Restored Market and Food Hall
bazariOrbeliani.content:
Bazari Orbeliani is a large, three-floor food market and dining hall on Orbeliani Square in central Tbilisi, capital of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus) — a short walk from Rustaveli Avenue and within easy reach of the Old Town. It opened at the end of 2021, on the site of a market that has stood here since 1886, as part of the wider wave of food-hall and gastro-space concepts that have appeared in Georgian cities over the past decade. It's a polished, modern, design-led venue rather than a gritty traditional bazaar — high-quality and photogenic, and a convenient single stop to graze across a lot of Georgian (and international) food, even if it sits at the upscale, visitor-friendly end of the spectrum rather than the everyday-local one.

What is Bazari Orbeliani?

Bazari Orbeliani is a restored historic market building on Orbeliani Square, central Tbilisi, reopened at the end of 2021 as a three-story, roughly 10,000 m² food market and food hall. The ground floor is an agricultural/produce market (with a Carrefour supermarket alongside) selling Georgian cheeses, wine, churchkhela, honey, spices, and other artisan products; the upper floor is a food court of around 30–35 bars, cafés, and restaurants spanning Georgian and many international cuisines; and a rooftop terrace offers drinks and city views. The site has been a market since 1886, and the project is part of the "New Tiflis" rehabilitation that pedestrianized and revived the square. It's a comfortable, central place to sample a wide range of food in one stop — more upscale gastro-hall than traditional bazaar.

The building

The building is worth noting before the food. The site has functioned as a market since 1886, most recently as the Soviet-era collective-farm market, and the recent restoration brought the historic structure back to life as the centerpiece of the revitalized Orbeliani Square (part of the "New Tiflis" urban-renewal project). The restoration kept enough of the original character — the proportions, the materials, the sense of a place long used for commerce — to give the interior a warmth that purpose-built food halls rarely manage. Exposed stone and brick, good natural light, the restored upper levels, and the general activity of a busy market make it a place that's comfortable to spend time in rather than simply pass through.

What the market offers

Bazari Orbeliani is organized across its floors. The produce and market floor carries a curated range of Georgian food: local cheeses (the fresh Imeretian cheese, and the lightly brined, elastic sulguni that turns up in so many Georgian dishes), cured meats, honey from different regions, tkemali and adjika in various preparations, dried herbs and spice blends, dried fruits, and Georgian wine and chacha by the bottle. Churchkhela — the dense, chewy Georgian sweet made by repeatedly dipping a string of walnuts in thickened grape must and drying it — appears in several varieties, the color and flavor shifting with the grape used. The quality is consistently high; the trade-off is that prices are higher than at an ordinary neighborhood market, and this isn't where most locals do their weekly shop.

The food court upstairs is where the breadth shows. Freshly baked khachapuri comes in its regional forms — the round Imeretian version, the boat-shaped Adjarian one with its egg and butter — alongside khinkali, lobio, and grilled dishes, the dough going into the tone oven and emerging hot within minutes. But the offer is genuinely international too: Italian, Spanish, Japanese, Chinese, Pan-Asian, Turkish, and other kitchens sit alongside the Georgian ones, which makes the hall an easy choice for a group with different tastes rather than a purely Georgian experience. Wine — both conventional and traditional qvevri-made amber styles — is available by the glass from several vendors, so you can work through a small tasting of regional styles without committing to a bottle. The rooftop terrace adds cocktails and a view over the city.

The market also runs craft demonstrations and workshops — ceramics, enamel, bread baking, churchkhela- and cheese-making among them — which add a hands-on, cultural dimension beyond eating and shopping. And the vendors tend to be knowledgeable about their products in a way that distinguishes a good food market from a simple retail operation: the cheese and wine sellers can usually explain the regional differences and production methods behind what they're selling — which, in the Georgian context, is also just ordinary hospitality, and makes the place a better introduction to the food than a menu alone.

An honest note on what it is

It's worth being clear about what Bazari Orbeliani is and isn't. It's a modern, polished, somewhat tourist-friendly food hall — frequently compared to the Time Out Market format — rather than a traditional, workaday Georgian bazaar. The quality is high and it's a genuinely pleasant and convenient place to eat and sample products, but if what you're after is the raw, bustling, everyday-local market experience, the sprawling Dezerter Bazaar (near the central station) is the place for that. Bazari Orbeliani is the curated, design-led, central-and-comfortable end of the Tbilisi market spectrum — useful precisely for that, on its own terms.

As part of a Tbilisi itinerary

The market works well as a midday stop on a walking day through central Tbilisi — a place to eat something good and rest before continuing. Its location between Rustaveli Avenue and the Old Town puts it within natural reach of most of the central sights, so combining it with a walk along Rustaveli or through the Old Town needs no special detour. It also works as an early-evening destination, when it's typically at its most active and the lit interior, the food smells, and the rooftop terrace are at their most appealing.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: Orbeliani Square, central Tbilisi — a short walk from Rustaveli Avenue and the Old Town.
  • Layout: Three floors — produce/agricultural market and supermarket (ground), food court of ~30–35 outlets (upper), rooftop terrace with views.
  • Hours: Open daily, roughly morning until late evening; busiest and best from late morning through the evening. (Individual restaurant and stall hours vary.)
  • Good for: A central, comfortable food stop; sampling Georgian products and wine; a group with mixed tastes (lots of international options); a rooftop drink.
  • For a traditional market instead: Dezerter Bazaar, for the raw, local, everyday experience.
  • Combine with: Rustaveli Avenue, the Old Town, and the rest of central Tbilisi — all within easy walking distance.

faq.title: What is Bazari Orbeliani?
faq.content: Bazari Orbeliani is a large, three-floor food market and dining hall on Orbeliani Square in central Tbilisi, reopened at the end of 2021 in a restored historic market building. The ground floor is a produce and artisan-food market (with a supermarket alongside); the upper floor is a food court of around 30–35 bars, cafés, and restaurants serving Georgian and international cuisines; and there's a rooftop terrace with drinks and city views. It's a modern, polished, design-led food hall — convenient and high-quality, and a good single stop to sample a lot of Georgian and international food in the center of the city.

faq.title: How old is the building?
faq.content: The site has been a market since 1886, and most recently housed the Soviet-era collective-farm market. The recent restoration brought the historic structure back into use as the centerpiece of a revived, pedestrianized Orbeliani Square (part of the "New Tiflis" urban-renewal project), reopening at the end of 2021. The restoration kept much of the original character — the proportions, the stone and brick, the feel of a long-used commercial space — which gives the interior a warmth that purpose-built food halls usually lack.

faq.title: Is it a real local market or a tourist spot?
faq.content: It's honestly more of a polished, somewhat tourist-friendly food hall — often compared to the Time Out Market format — than a traditional everyday Georgian bazaar. The quality is high and it's a genuinely pleasant place to eat and shop for good products, but it's at the upscale, curated end of the spectrum, and it's not where most locals do their regular shopping. If you want the raw, bustling, everyday-local market experience, head to the sprawling Dezerter Bazaar near the central station instead. The two serve different purposes, and Bazari Orbeliani is excellent for what it is.

faq.title: What food and drink can you find there?
faq.content: On the market floor: Georgian cheeses (Imeretian, sulguni), cured meats, honey, tkemali and adjika, spices, dried fruits, churchkhela, and Georgian wine and chacha. Upstairs, the food court runs from Georgian classics — khachapuri (Imeretian and Adjarian), khinkali, lobio, grilled dishes — to a wide range of international kitchens (Italian, Spanish, Japanese, Chinese, Pan-Asian, Turkish and more), which makes it easy for a group with different tastes. Several vendors pour wine by the glass, including traditional qvevri-made amber wines, so you can taste across regional styles without buying a bottle. There are also craft demonstrations and workshops, and a rooftop terrace for a drink.

faq.title: Where is it, and when should I go?
faq.content: It's on Orbeliani Square in central Tbilisi, a short walk from Rustaveli Avenue and within easy reach of the Old Town, so it fits into a central walking day with no detour. It's open daily from roughly morning until late evening (individual outlets vary), and is busiest and most appealing from late morning through the evening — it works well as a midday stop to eat and rest, or as an early-evening destination when the hall is liveliest and the rooftop terrace comes into its own.

dezerterBazaar.heroTitle: Dezerter Bazaar: Tbilisi's Great Working Market
dezerterBazaar.content:
The Dezerter Bazaar is the largest and most unfiltered market in Tbilisi, capital of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus) — a sprawling site near the Central Railway Station that operates every day with an energy and density no curated food hall can replicate. This is where the city does its actual shopping: where restaurant owners come early to pick their produce, where family cooks fill their bags with the week's vegetables and cheese, where the agricultural output of Georgia's regions arrives and disperses into the city's kitchens. It is entirely undesigned for visitors — which is precisely what makes it worth visiting, and what makes it the genuine counterpart to the polished food halls elsewhere in the city.

What is the Dezerter Bazaar?

The Dezerter Bazaar is Tbilisi's biggest and most authentic food market — a dense cluster of covered halls and open-air rows beside the Central Railway Station and Station Square, in the northern part of the city. Its name ("Deserters' Market") comes from the early 1920s, when soldiers deserting in the turmoil around the Soviet takeover sold their weapons and gear here. Today it's an agrarian market where the city genuinely shops: seasonal produce from across Georgia, cheeses, meat and fish, spices, tkemali and adjika, churchkhela and pickles, sold from stalls that spill out into the surrounding streets. Loud, crowded, and intensely sensory, it's the unvarnished, everyday-local opposite of the city's tidy gastro-halls — and the best place to see Tbilisi's food culture as it actually is.

The name

The name has a fittingly irregular origin. In the early 1920s, in the turmoil around the First World War, the Russian Civil War, and the 1921 Soviet (Red Army) takeover of Georgia, deserting soldiers reportedly gathered near the railway station to sell their weapons, equipment, and personal possessions — anything that would raise cash. The informal market that grew from those transactions became known as the Deserters' Market — Dezerter Bazari — and the name has persisted through a century of transformation, from a post-conflict improvised exchange into the major permanent market it is today. The current traders have nothing to do with military desertion; the name is simply the market's identity now, worn without much thought.

The market

Dezerter Bazaar covers a substantial area, loosely organized into sections by product type, though the boundaries are fluid and the layout rewards wandering rather than systematic navigation (part of it is a purpose-built covered hall, part open-air rows). The produce section is the most visually striking — seasonal fruit and vegetables from across Georgia (and imported from Turkey in winter) in quantities that reflect the country's agricultural scale. In summer the tomatoes come in varieties that make the supermarket version look like a different species; autumn brings persimmons, quinces, pomegranates, and the squashes and root vegetables of the Georgian harvest; spring brings the first bundles of fresh herbs — tarragon, coriander, purple basil — as fundamental to Georgian cooking as any spice.

The cheese section covers the full range of Georgian dairy, from fresh Imeretian cheese eaten within days of production to aged and smoked varieties. Sulguni appears in abundance — fresh, lightly aged, and smoked over open fire to a deep amber — sold from large wheels cut to order. The tkemali sellers stock the tart plum sauce in its varieties (red and green, mild and sharp), alongside adjika preparations ranging from moderately spiced to genuinely incendiary.

The meat section follows Georgian butchery traditions that differ considerably from what most Western visitors are used to — whole carcasses, unfamiliar cuts, and a directness about the origins of the product that supermarket packaging deliberately hides. The fish section carries fresh Black Sea catch arriving daily alongside the dried and salted fish that are staples of everyday cooking. The spice vendors stock dried herbs, pepper blends, khmeli suneli (the ubiquitous Georgian spice mix), and the dried flowers and botanicals used in both cooking and folk medicine — plus churchkhela, tklapi (fruit leather), nuts, and dried fruit.

Beyond food, the market carries the household miscellany that accumulates in any large market serving real daily needs — cleaning products, cheap tools, religious items, seasonal goods, and quantities of material that resist easy categorization. Toward its edges, the Dezerter Bazaar shades into the broader station-area bazaar (sometimes called Vagzlis Bazroba), several blocks of secondhand clothes, electronics, and assorted goods that surround the railway station — a different world from the fresh produce and dairy of the core market, but part of the same sprawling commercial zone.

The experience

The Dezerter Bazaar is loud, crowded, and intensely sensory in a way no description quite prepares you for. Vendors call prices across the aisles, carts push through narrow passages, and the smell of fresh herbs mingles with meat, spices, and the general productive odor of a large working market. The pace is fast and the space isn't organized for leisurely browsing — this is a functional market where people come to buy specific things efficiently, and moving through it well means being aware of the difference between being a shopper and being an observer.

For those who want to taste before buying, most produce vendors will offer a slice of something without pressure — the informal generosity with samples is part of the market's character. Prices are quoted and paid in cash, and while Georgian is the working language, enough basic transaction vocabulary crosses the language barrier to make buying straightforward with patience and a willingness to use hands and expressions. One practical caution: it's a dense, crowded place right by a busy transport hub, so keep an eye on your belongings — petty pickpocketing isn't unheard of in the station-area crowds.

Morning is the best time — ideally between about 8:00 and 11:00, when the stalls are fully stocked, the produce is freshest, and the market is at its most active. The market is officially open from early morning into the evening, but by early-to-mid afternoon many vendors begin packing up and the selection narrows.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: Near the Central Railway Station and Station Square (Vagzlis Moedani), on Tsinamdzgvrishvili Street, northern Tbilisi (near the Dinamo Arena).
  • Getting there: Easiest by metro to Station Square (red line), then a short walk; or by taxi. It's a transport hub, so it's well connected.
  • Hours: Daily, roughly 7:00–17:00; liveliest in the morning, winding down through the early afternoon.
  • Payment: Cash only (Georgian lari); bring small notes.
  • Tips: Go in the morning; bring a bag; sample before you buy; keep an eye on your belongings in the crowds.
  • Counterpart: For a polished, comfortable food-hall version of Georgian food, see Bazari Orbeliani — the Dezerter Bazaar is the raw, everyday-local original.

faq.title: What is the Dezerter Bazaar?
faq.content: The Dezerter Bazaar is the largest and most authentic market in Tbilisi — a sprawling cluster of covered halls and open-air stalls near the Central Railway Station where the city does its real food shopping. Restaurant owners, home cooks, and the produce of Georgia's farming regions all converge here daily. You'll find seasonal fruit and vegetables, the full range of Georgian cheeses, meat and fish, spices, tkemali and adjika, churchkhela and pickles. It's loud, crowded, and intensely sensory — entirely undesigned for tourists, which is exactly what makes it worth visiting, and the genuine counterpart to the city's polished food halls.

faq.title: Why is it called the "Deserters' Market"?
faq.content: The name comes from the early 1920s. In the turmoil around the First World War, the Russian Civil War, and the 1921 Soviet takeover of Georgia, deserting soldiers gathered near the railway station to sell their weapons, equipment, and personal possessions — anything for cash. The informal market that grew up there became known as the Deserters' Market — Dezerter Bazari in Georgian — and the name stuck through a century of change into the major permanent market it is today. The current traders have nothing to do with desertion; the name is just the market's identity now.

faq.title: What can you buy there?
faq.content: Above all, food: seasonal produce from across Georgia (imported from Turkey in winter), Georgian cheeses including fresh and smoked sulguni cut from large wheels, tkemali plum sauce and fiery adjika, meat (butchered in the traditional Georgian way), fresh Black Sea fish and dried fish, spices including the ubiquitous khmeli suneli, plus churchkhela, fruit leather (tklapi), nuts, dried fruit, and pickles. Beyond food, the edges of the market shade into a broader station-area bazaar of household goods, secondhand clothes, and miscellany. It's also a good place to buy edible souvenirs — spices, churchkhela, cheese — right by a transport hub.

faq.title: When is the best time to go, and how do I get there?
faq.content: Go in the morning — ideally around 8:00 to 11:00 — when the stalls are fully stocked and the market is at its liveliest; it's officially open from early morning into the evening, but the selection narrows through the early afternoon as vendors pack up. It's near the Central Railway Station and Station Square (Vagzlis Moedani) in northern Tbilisi, on Tsinamdzgvrishvili Street; the easiest way there is the metro to Station Square (red line) and a short walk, or a taxi.

faq.title: Any practical tips?
faq.content: Bring cash in small notes (no cards), and a bag for your purchases. Most produce vendors will happily offer a sample before you buy — that generosity is part of the experience. Georgian is the working language, but basic transaction vocabulary and a bit of patience get you through easily. Because it's a dense, crowded place right by a busy station, keep an eye on your belongings — petty pickpocketing isn't unheard of in the station-area crowds. And consider going with a guide if you'd like help navigating, translating, and finding the best stalls.

dryBridgeMarket.heroTitle: The Dry Bridge Market: Tbilisi's Flea Market of Memories
dryBridgeMarket.content:
The Dry Bridge Market is Tbilisi's great open-air flea market — and one of the most atmospheric and genuinely interesting markets in the South Caucasus, in Tbilisi, capital of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus). It took its modern form in the early 1990s, out of economic necessity: when the Soviet Union collapsed and Georgia fell into civil conflict, energy shortages, and near-total economic breakdown, people began bringing their possessions to sell on and around the bridge — family heirlooms, Soviet-era objects, tools, books, anything that could be carried and sold. It has run continuously ever since, evolving from a survival mechanism into a permanent fixture often called the "market of memories." Unlike a conventional souvenir market, its core is real antique, vintage, and Soviet-era material — objects with actual histories, sold by people who often know them.

What is the Dry Bridge Market?

The Dry Bridge Market (Georgian: Mshrali Khidi) is an open-air antiques and flea market in central Tbilisi, a short walk from Rustaveli Avenue. It's staged on and around the Dry Bridge itself and spills into the adjacent Dedaena Park and 9 April Park, where much of the art is sold. It emerged in the early 1990s, when post-Soviet hardship pushed people to sell their belongings, and it has grown into the city's most famous flea market — known for antiques, Soviet memorabilia (medals, badges, militaria), vinyl, cameras, watches, silverware, icons, coins, and the work of Georgian painters sold directly. It runs daily, roughly late morning to late afternoon, and is largest on weekends; cash only, bargaining expected.

Why the bridge is "dry"

The market takes its name from the bridge it sits on — the Dry Bridge (Mshrali Khidi). Built between 1847 and 1851 (begun by the Italian architect Giovanni Scudieri), it originally spanned a branch of the Mtkvari River. In the 1930s, an embankment built to control Tbilisi's chronic flooding redirected the water, and the channel beneath the bridge dried up — leaving the bridge crossing dry land rather than river, and giving it the name it has carried ever since. (So the "dry" is literal: the bridge has long outlived its river.)

What's sold here

What sets the Dry Bridge apart from an ordinary souvenir market is the nature of what's on offer. This isn't primarily a place for mass-produced tourist goods, though those exist at the fringes. The core is antique, vintage, and Soviet-era material — objects with histories, often sold by people who know those histories and will share them. On a busy weekend morning you move past tables of Soviet military medals, orders, and badges, stacks of vinyl records, vintage cameras and optical equipment, old watches in every state of repair, Georgian silverware, ceramics, prayer beads, carpets, propaganda posters, hand-painted icons, coins, stamps, old surgical and scientific instruments, chess sets, samovars and typewriters, and quantities of Soviet domestic technology whose original purpose isn't always obvious. The randomness is the point.

The Soviet memorabilia is among the most historically interesting parts for anyone drawn to the 20th century — medals, orders, and badges from across the Soviet award system, some rare, some common, all carrying the visual language of a political culture that no longer exists. Alongside the official material, the ordinary objects of Soviet life accumulate almost archivally: workers' identity documents, family photographs, children's toys, domestic items whose design captures the particular aesthetic of Soviet consumer culture at different periods.

The art, which has grown over the years and now occupies a substantial part of the market (much of it in Dedaena Park), is sold by painters directly from their stalls — academic realism, Georgian landscape painting, Soviet-era graphic design, and more contemporary work. Quality varies, but prices are modest, the painters are generally happy to talk about their work, and buying directly removes the gallery markup that makes Georgian art expensive in the city's commercial spaces.

Traditional Georgian objects — silverwork, carved wood, old textiles, ceramic vessels — appear throughout in varying degrees of authenticity and condition. The genuinely old pieces take some knowledge to pick out among the reproductions, but they're there, and the pleasure of finding something real among the dross is a big part of the Dry Bridge experience for those with the patience to look.

How the market works

The market is largest and most active on weekends, especially Sunday mornings, when the full extent of the bridge and the surrounding parks fills with vendors. Weekdays are quieter with a smaller selection, though the more serious antique dealers tend to be present throughout the week. Most vendors set up from around 10–11:00, and late morning is the best time to arrive — early enough that the best pieces haven't been picked over, late enough that most stalls are fully set up.

Bargaining is standard and expected, conducted without aggression and usually with good humor on both sides. The opening price on most items is negotiable, and a polite counter-offer is received as a normal part of the transaction, not an affront. Cash in Georgian lari is the only practical payment method.

The layout is informal and changes from visit to visit — some vendors spread their goods on blankets or plastic sheets on the ground, others use tables or improvised displays. The organization is chaotic in a way that rewards slow browsing over purposeful shopping: the interesting things tend to surface when you're not specifically looking for them, which is both the frustration and the appeal of the place. It's entirely outdoors, so heavy rain or snow thins the vendors considerably.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: On and around the Dry Bridge (Mshrali Khidi), with the market extending into Dedaena Park and 9 April Park, central Tbilisi — a short walk from Rustaveli Avenue.
  • Hours: Daily, roughly 10:00/11:00 until late afternoon; largest on weekends. Weather-dependent (thin in heavy rain or snow).
  • Best time: Saturday or Sunday, late morning.
  • Payment: Cash only (Georgian lari); bargaining expected.
  • Tips: Browse slowly; ask vendors about the objects (many know the histories); inspect "antiques" carefully, as reproductions mix with the genuine; buy art directly from the painters in Dedaena Park.
  • Note on export: Genuinely old antiques and icons can be subject to cultural-heritage export rules — if you buy something potentially of age or significance, check the current regulations before trying to take it out of the country.
  • Counterpart: For food rather than objects, see the Dezerter Bazaar (the city's great food market) — a different kind of market entirely.

faq.title: What is the Dry Bridge Market?
faq.content: The Dry Bridge Market (Mshrali Khidi) is Tbilisi's most famous open-air flea market — a daily antiques and vintage market in the center of the city, a short walk from Rustaveli Avenue, staged on and around the Dry Bridge and spilling into the adjacent Dedaena and 9 April parks. It took its modern form in the early 1990s, when post-Soviet economic collapse pushed people to sell their possessions, and it has run continuously since. Often called the "market of memories," it's known above all for genuine antique, vintage, and Soviet-era objects — medals and militaria, vinyl, cameras, watches, silverware, icons, coins — and for Georgian art sold directly by the painters.

faq.title: Why is it called the "Dry Bridge"?
faq.content: The bridge it sits on, built between 1847 and 1851, originally spanned a branch of the Mtkvari River. In the 1930s, an embankment built to control Tbilisi's flooding redirected the water, and the channel beneath the bridge dried up — leaving the bridge crossing dry land rather than river, which gave it the name Mshrali Khidi, "Dry Bridge." The market that grew up on and around it took the same name. So the "dry" is literal: the bridge has long outlived the river branch it was built to cross.

faq.title: What can you find there?
faq.content: A remarkable range of antique, vintage, and Soviet-era material: Soviet medals, orders, and badges; vinyl records; vintage cameras and watches; Georgian silverware and ceramics; hand-painted icons; coins, stamps, propaganda posters; samovars, typewriters, and old domestic technology; and the ordinary objects of Soviet life — documents, photographs, toys. There's also a large art section (much of it in Dedaena Park) where Georgian painters sell directly, and traditional Georgian objects in varying authenticity. It's not mainly a mass-produced-souvenir market — the appeal is the genuine, the random, and the historical.

faq.title: When is the best time to visit, and how does it work?
faq.content: Go on a weekend — especially Sunday morning — when the market is at its fullest; weekdays are quieter with fewer stalls (though serious dealers are around all week). Most vendors set up from around 10–11:00, and late morning is ideal. Bargaining is standard and good-natured: the opening price is negotiable, and a polite counter-offer is expected. Bring cash in Georgian lari — there's no card payment. It's all outdoors, so heavy rain or snow thins it out considerably.

faq.title: Is it different from the Dezerter Bazaar?
faq.content: Yes — completely. The Dry Bridge is an antiques and flea market (objects, art, Soviet memorabilia, collectibles), in the center near Rustaveli Avenue. The Dezerter Bazaar is the city's great food market (produce, cheese, meat, spices), near the Central Railway Station. They're different markets in different places serving different purposes, so it's worth not confusing them — visit the Dry Bridge for objects and history, the Dezerter Bazaar for food and everyday local life.

nationalBotanicalGarden.heroTitle: National Botanical Garden of Georgia: Tbilisi's Hidden Valley
nationalBotanicalGarden.content:
The National Botanical Garden fills a deep gorge immediately behind the Old Town of Tbilisi, capital of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus). Its main entrance is tucked below the Narikala Fortress walls, and its terrain runs southward through a valley that most visitors to Tbilisi never discover — despite it being a few minutes' walk from the sulfur baths and the cable-car station. That near-invisibility is part of what makes it one of the more rewarding places in the city: a genuinely green, quiet valley that opens up unexpectedly out of the dense fabric of the historic district, a complete change of pace from the stone and cobblestone of the Old Town streets above.

What is the National Botanical Garden of Georgia?

The National Botanical Garden of Georgia (formerly the Tbilisi Botanical Garden) is a large historic garden filling the Tsavkisistsqali (Leghvtakhevi) gorge directly below the Narikala Fortress, behind Tbilisi's Old Town. Its origins go back to a royal garden in this valley in the early 17th century; it was formally established as the Tiflis Botanical Garden in 1845, under Russian administration, and has grown ever since into a collection of over 4,500 plant species from Georgia, the Caucasus, and around the world, spread across some 161 hectares of valley and hillside. The valley carries a stream that feeds ponds and drops over the Leghvtakhevi waterfall at its upper end, and the enclosed, wooded gorge stays noticeably cooler than the city above. There's an entrance fee, and it can be entered from the Old Town (Abanotubani) side or from an upper gate by the Mother of Georgia statue.

A garden with a long history

There have been gardens in this valley for centuries. The garden's predecessor was a royal garden established in the early 17th century in the Tsavkisistsqali gorge — the "fortress gardens" of the Georgian kings, growing ornamental and medicinal plants, recorded by European travelers in the 1600s and 1700s. After Georgia's annexation by the Russian Empire, the garden was revived and formally established as the Tiflis Botanical Garden in 1845; it was expanded repeatedly through the 19th and 20th centuries (a plant nursery and floristics center followed later in the 1800s). Today the institution holds a collection of over 4,500 plant species from Georgia, the broader Caucasus, and regions as far afield as China, Japan, the Himalayas, North America, and the Mediterranean, across roughly 161 hectares of valley and hillside — though the part most casual visitors walk occupies a smaller central section of that total.

The garden

The character of the garden is shaped by the gorge it occupies. The valley floor carries the Tsavkisistsqali stream, which feeds a series of small ponds and reaches the Leghvtakhevi waterfall at the upper end of the accessible area; the slopes on both sides rise steeply through forest and cultivated planting to the ridgeline. The enclosed valley, the water, and the tree canopy together create a microclimate noticeably cooler and more humid than the city — which makes the garden especially welcome in the hot Tbilisi summers, when the Old Town streets above can be oppressive.

The planting is organized into sections by geography and botanical category — a Georgian flora section, a rose garden, collections of trees and shrubs from various regions, a greenhouse — though the overall feel is more of a large, pleasantly overgrown park than a rigidly scientific institution. The paths wind through the valley so that you can do either a short visit covering the main highlights or a longer wander up the slopes into quieter corners. The mature trees throughout — many large specimens built up over the garden's long history — give it a settled permanence that newer green spaces lack. (There are also pavilions, old bridges, benches, and a children's playground along the way.)

The Leghvtakhevi waterfall

The waterfall at the upper end is the garden's most dramatic natural feature and the destination most visitors are heading for. The Leghvtakhevi stream drops about 22 meters over a basalt face into a pool at the base, and the falling water, the spray, the surrounding greenery, and the gorge walls make a genuinely refreshing spot, particularly in summer. The walk to it from the main entrance is about 20–30 minutes of easy going, crossing the stream on small bridges and passing through progressively denser vegetation as the valley narrows.

The waterfall can also be reached from the Old Town side without entering through the main gate: a separate gorge path runs in from the streets near Abanotubani and reaches the falls from the lower end. (Note that this gorge walk and the garden are connected but distinct — the short Abanotubani gorge walk to the waterfall is free, while the Botanical Garden proper charges admission.)

Connections to the Old Town

The garden's position makes it a natural part of an Old Town walking day rather than a separate trip. The main entrance is a short walk from Abanotubani, and there's a second, upper entrance by the Mother of Georgia statue on the Sololaki ridge — which means you can enter from the cable-car/ridge side and walk down through the garden, or enter from the bottom and climb up. A satisfying circuit: take the cable car up to Narikala, walk the fortress walls to the Mother of Georgia, then descend into the Botanical Garden from the upper gate, work down through the valley past the waterfall, and come out at the bottom near Abanotubani — a half-day loop covering the best elevated and ground-level parts of the historic district in one continuous route.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: 1 Botanikuri Street, Old Tbilisi — the main entrance is below Narikala Fortress, near Abanotubani; a second entrance is on the Sololaki ridge by the Mother of Georgia statue.
  • Getting there: On foot up Botanikuri Street from Abanotubani; or via the cable car to Narikala / the Mother of Georgia and in through the upper gate.
  • Hours: Daily, approximately 09:00–19:00 (longer in summer).
  • Admission: There is an entrance fee (a few lari).
  • Tips: Wear comfortable shoes (the terrain is hilly and paths can be uneven); allow 1–2 hours for the valley and waterfall, more to explore the slopes; best in spring (planting) and summer (cool shade and the strongest waterfall flow).
  • Combine with: Narikala, the Mother of Georgia, and the cable car (the upper-gate circuit); Abanotubani and the sulfur baths; the Leghvtakhevi gorge walk — the whole southeastern ridge-and-Old-Town cluster.

faq.title: What is the National Botanical Garden of Georgia?
faq.content: It's a large historic garden filling the Tsavkisistsqali (Leghvtakhevi) gorge directly below the Narikala Fortress, behind Tbilisi's Old Town. Its origins go back to a royal garden in this valley in the early 17th century; it was formally established as the Tiflis Botanical Garden in 1845 and has grown into a collection of over 4,500 plant species from Georgia, the Caucasus, and around the world, across some 161 hectares of valley and hillside. The valley carries a stream that feeds ponds and drops over the Leghvtakhevi waterfall, and the enclosed, wooded gorge stays noticeably cooler than the city above — a genuinely green, quiet escape a few minutes from the sulfur baths.

faq.title: How old is it, and what's its history?
faq.content: There have been gardens in this valley for centuries. The predecessor was a royal garden established in the early 17th century — the "fortress gardens" of the Georgian kings, growing ornamental and medicinal plants, recorded by European travelers in the 1600s and 1700s. After Georgia's annexation by the Russian Empire, it was revived and formally established as the Tiflis Botanical Garden in 1845, then expanded repeatedly through the 19th and 20th centuries. Today it's the National Botanical Garden of Georgia, a leading research and conservation institution as well as a public garden.

faq.title: What's the Leghvtakhevi waterfall, and how do I get to it?
faq.content: It's the garden's most dramatic natural feature — the Leghvtakhevi (Tsavkisistsqali) stream dropping about 22 meters over a basalt face into a pool, at the upper end of the accessible area. From the main entrance it's an easy 20–30 minute walk, crossing the stream on small bridges through increasingly dense vegetation. The waterfall can also be reached from the Old Town side via a separate gorge path from near Abanotubani; note that the short Abanotubani gorge walk to the falls is free, while the Botanical Garden itself charges admission.

faq.title: Is there an entrance fee, and what are the hours?
faq.content: Yes — the garden charges a small entrance fee (a few lari); check the current price before you go. It's open daily, roughly 09:00–19:00, with longer hours in summer. (This is separate from the free Leghvtakhevi gorge walk that comes in from the Abanotubani side.)

faq.title: How does it fit into a visit to the Old Town?
faq.content: Very naturally — it's a few minutes from Abanotubani and the cable car, not a separate trip. There's a main entrance at the bottom (off Botanikuri Street, near Abanotubani) and a second, upper entrance on the Sololaki ridge by the Mother of Georgia statue. A good circuit is to take the cable car up to Narikala, walk to the Mother of Georgia, descend into the garden through the upper gate, work down the valley past the waterfall, and come out near Abanotubani — a half-day loop linking the ridge viewpoints and the green gorge below.

operaBalletTheatre.heroTitle: Tbilisi Opera and Ballet Theatre: Rustaveli's Moorish Landmark
operaBalletTheatre.content:
