The Tbilisi Opera and Ballet Theatre is one of the most distinctive buildings on Rustaveli Avenue, capital of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus) — its Moorish facade standing out among the 19th-century European architecture that lines most of the boulevard. The arched entrance, the dense geometric and floral patterns across the exterior, and the sense of a building drawing on several architectural traditions at once make it immediately recognizable, and for a city already known for its eclecticism, a fitting landmark.

What is the Tbilisi Opera and Ballet Theatre?

The Tbilisi Opera and Ballet Theatre, officially named after the Georgian composer Zakaria Paliashvili, is Georgia's main opera house and one of the oldest in the region — a neo-Moorish (Moorish Revival) building on Rustaveli Avenue. The first opera house on the site opened in 1851 (the first in the Caucasus); after a fire in 1874, the present building, designed by the architect Victor Schröter, was completed in 1896. It's the home of Georgian national opera — Paliashvili's Abesalom and Eteri and Daisi are its foundational works — and of the State Ballet of Georgia, running a full season of opera and ballet at prices well below Western European houses. Fully restored and reopened in 2016, it's worth seeing for the architecture and, better still, for a performance.

History

The theatre's history on this site begins in 1851, when the first opera house in the Caucasus opened here — a notable moment for a city that, under Russian imperial administration, had become the cultural capital of the whole region. (The first building was designed by the Italian architect Antonio Scudieri, and the project was initiated by the imperial viceroy Mikhail Vorontsov.) That building was destroyed by fire in 1874, and the present structure — designed by the Baltic German architect Victor Schröter in a neo-Moorish style — was completed in 1896, reopening with Glinka's A Life for the Tsar.

The choice of style was deliberate, and reflected Tbilisi's particular position at the end of the 19th century — at once a Russian imperial administrative center, a Georgian cultural capital, and a meeting point of European and Eastern influences (the neo-Moorish manner was in fact promoted by the Russian authorities as suitable for public buildings in the Caucasus). All of this found its way into a building that put Orientalist decoration over a thoroughly European theater layout.

The institution's story is more than architectural. In the late 19th and early 20th centuries the Tbilisi Opera was one of the leading houses in the Russian Empire, drawing major performers and companies. Tchaikovsky visited Georgia repeatedly in the late 1880s; the great bass Feodor Chaliapin got his early start — his "stage baptism" — in Tbilisi; and Alexandre Dumas, passing through in 1859, was struck by the place. Above all, the theatre is the institutional home of Georgian national opera: Zakaria Paliashvili (1871–1933), whose operas Abesalom and Eteri (1919) and Daisi (1923) are the foundational works of the Georgian repertoire, had his major works premiered here, and the theatre has been named after him since 1937.

The building

The exterior repays a close look before you go in. The facade combines horseshoe arches, geometric tilework, alternating bands of warm yellow and reddish stone, and carved ornament in a manner that references the Islamic architecture of Andalusia and the Maghreb — while remaining, overall, clearly a late-19th-century European theater. The hybrid is typical of the Orientalist fashion of the period, which borrowed Islamic visual culture as a source of decorative richness; but the result here is more successful than many buildings in that tradition — dense without becoming incoherent, with a visual energy the more restrained buildings on either side can't match.

The interior carries the ambition through. The auditorium follows the standard European horseshoe plan, with tiered balconies, red velvet seating, gilt detailing on the balcony fronts, chandeliers, and a painted ceiling — the combination of warmth and formality the best 19th-century opera interiors achieve. The stage curtain, by the artist Sergo Kobuladze, is one of the theatre's signatures. The building was restored to its neo-Moorish form in the Soviet period (1978) and again in a major six-year restoration completed in 2016, which upgraded the facilities while preserving the historic interior; the acoustics serve both voices and orchestra well.

Performances and repertoire

The theatre runs a full season from autumn through spring, with a lighter summer program. The repertoire balances the Georgian national tradition — Paliashvili's works hold a central, recurring place, and Abesalom and Eteri is the traditional season-opener — with the standard international operatic canon, alongside a ballet program (the State Ballet of Georgia, long directed by the noted ballerina Nina Ananiashvili) spanning classical and contemporary work. Production quality is high by regional standards, and prices are well below those of equivalent Western European houses — which makes an evening here genuinely accessible rather than a special-occasion splurge.

Attending a performance is one of the more complete cultural experiences in Tbilisi: the historic building, the Georgian and international repertoire, and the local audience together give the theatre a life that a purely architectural visit can't. For anyone with an interest in opera, ballet, or classical music, checking the program and booking a ticket is well worth it.

Tickets and visiting

  • Location: 25 Rustaveli Avenue, Tbilisi.
  • Tickets: Online via tkt.ge (the main Georgian ticketing platform) and the official site opera.ge, at the box office on Rustaveli Avenue, and sometimes through hotel concierges. Popular performances — premieres, weekend evenings — sell out, so book a few days ahead for the best seats.
  • Box office / hours: The exterior can be admired any time; the box office is open daily during the season (typically from late morning until the evening performance).
  • Website: opera.ge
  • Tips: For a performance, smart-casual or better is appropriate; arrive early to take in the foyers and the interior. Even without a ticket, the facade is worth a stop on any Rustaveli walk.
  • Combine with: A walk along Rustaveli Avenue (Parliament, the museums, the galleries), Freedom Square, and the rest of the 19th-century center.

faq.title: What is the Tbilisi Opera and Ballet Theatre?
faq.content: It's Georgia's main opera house and one of the oldest in the region — a neo-Moorish (Moorish Revival) landmark on Rustaveli Avenue, officially named after the Georgian composer Zakaria Paliashvili. The first opera house on the site opened in 1851 (the first in the Caucasus); after a fire in 1874, the present building, designed by the architect Victor Schröter, was completed in 1896. It's the home of Georgian national opera — Paliashvili's Abesalom and Eteri and Daisi are its foundational works — and of the State Ballet of Georgia, with a full season of opera and ballet. It was fully restored and reopened in 2016.

faq.title: Why is it architecturally distinctive?
faq.content: Because of its neo-Moorish style. The facade combines horseshoe arches, geometric tilework, alternating bands of yellow and reddish stone, and carved ornament referencing the Islamic architecture of Andalusia and North Africa — while the interior follows a standard European horseshoe-plan opera house. That hybrid was typical of the Orientalist architectural fashion of the late 19th century (and was promoted by the Russian authorities as fitting for public buildings in the Caucasus), and it makes the theatre stand out sharply from the more restrained European architecture along Rustaveli Avenue.

faq.title: Who is connected to its history?
faq.content: Above all Zakaria Paliashvili (1871–1933), the foundational Georgian operatic composer, whose works premiered here and after whom the theatre was named in 1937. In its imperial-era heyday it drew major figures — Tchaikovsky visited Georgia repeatedly in the late 1880s, and the great bass Feodor Chaliapin got his early start in Tbilisi; Alexandre Dumas, passing through in 1859, admired the building. Today it's home to the State Ballet of Georgia, long led by the noted ballerina Nina Ananiashvili.

faq.title: How do I get tickets, and is it expensive?
faq.content: Tickets are available online through tkt.ge (the main Georgian ticketing platform) and the official site opera.ge, at the box office on Rustaveli Avenue, and sometimes via hotel concierges. Prices are well below those of equivalent Western European opera houses, which makes an evening here genuinely accessible. Popular performances — premieres and weekend evenings — sell out, so it's worth booking a few days ahead for the best seats. The season runs autumn through spring, with a lighter summer program.

faq.title: Is it worth visiting if I don't attend a performance?
faq.content: Yes, for the architecture — the facade is one of the most striking on Rustaveli Avenue and worth a stop on any walk along the boulevard. But the fuller experience is a performance: the combination of the historic, restored interior, the Georgian and international repertoire, and the local audience gives the theatre a life that an exterior look alone can't. If you have any interest in opera, ballet, or classical music, it's well worth checking the program.

parliamentOfGeorgia.heroTitle: Parliament of Georgia: Rustaveli Avenue's Civic Stage
parliamentOfGeorgia.content:
The Parliament building on Rustaveli Avenue is one of the most recognizable structures on Tbilisi's main boulevard, capital of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus) — its broad colonnaded facade and wide ceremonial steps giving it a presence that holds attention even on a street already lined with major public buildings. It stands on the northern side of Rustaveli, facing Kashveti Church across the avenue, and the combination of its monumental scale and its central location has made it not only the seat of the country's legislature but one of the principal stages for the most consequential moments in modern Georgian political life.

What is the Parliament of Georgia building?

The Parliament of Georgia building, at 8 Rustaveli Avenue in Tbilisi, is the seat of the country's legislature — a monumental Soviet-era structure built between the late 1930s and 1953 as the Government House of the Georgian SSR. Beyond its architecture, it's one of the most historically charged sites in the country: the place where Soviet troops killed 21 demonstrators on 9 April 1989, and where the Rose Revolution unfolded in November 2003. Parliament moved to Kutaisi in 2012 and returned to Tbilisi around 2019, and this building is once again the working seat of the legislature — so it's not open to the public, but its facade and the civic space in front of it are central to any walk along Rustaveli and to understanding Georgia's modern history.

The building

The building was constructed in two stages — the rear (upper) section in the late 1930s and the main section facing Rustaveli completed in 1953 — as the Government House of the Georgian SSR, by the architects Viktor Kokorin and Giorgi Lezhava (with Vladimer Nasaridze). It went up on the site of a demolished 19th-century Russian military cathedral (the Alexander Nevsky cathedral). Its architecture reflects the era — the large arches, the heavy columns, the broad ceremonial steps rising from the avenue, all projecting the institutional authority Soviet public architecture was designed to convey — sitting somewhere between Stalinist monumentalism and the European neoclassical tradition. But it isn't purely generic Soviet: the design also draws on motifs of traditional Georgian architecture and is faced in golden tuff, and its facade was clearly composed in relation to the width of Rustaveli itself rather than simply built as a practical block.

Historical significance

The building's importance to Georgia runs well beyond its architecture. It has been the backdrop to several of the defining moments of the country's recent political history, and for anyone who knows that history, standing in front of it carries a weight the exterior alone doesn't explain.

On 9 April 1989, Soviet troops violently dispersed a peaceful pro-independence demonstration in front of the building, killing 21 people — most of them young women — and injuring hundreds. The troops used sapper spades and toxic gas against the crowd, and the brutality of the response became one of the watershed moments in Georgian consciousness about independence and Soviet rule. The date is now commemorated annually as the Day of National Unity, and the area in front of the Parliament is one of the principal places of remembrance. (The same site had seen earlier tragedy: a 1956 protest here was also suppressed with loss of life.)

The Rose Revolution of November 2003 — the peaceful uprising that ended Eduard Shevardnadze's presidency and brought Mikheil Saakashvili to power — had some of its most dramatic scenes here, when opposition protesters, led by Saakashvili holding a rose, entered the parliamentary chamber during a session and Shevardnadze left office. The images, broadcast worldwide, made this building one of the most recognizable symbols of post-Soviet political change anywhere in the former USSR.

The building was the seat of the Georgian parliament until the legislature was relocated to Kutaisi in 2012 as part of a decentralization initiative. Parliament returned to Tbilisi around 2019, and the Rustaveli Avenue building is once again the working home of the legislature. As an active parliament, it is not open to the public — but it remains the address most associated, in Georgia and abroad, with the country's parliamentary democracy.

The space in front

The wide pavement and open steps in front of the Parliament have long served as Tbilisi's primary civic gathering place — where Georgians assemble to mark national events, voice political opinions, and take part in the public life of the country. That role continues: the space remains the default location for major demonstrations and commemorations. Passing on an ordinary day, with the steps quiet and the traffic moving normally, it takes some imagination to connect the calm present with the history the place carries. But the history is there, and for anyone with an interest in Georgia's modern political story, this is one of the sites where that story is most directly legible.

Visiting

  • Location: 8 Rustaveli Avenue, Tbilisi, on the northern side of the avenue, facing Kashveti Church.
  • Getting there: A short walk along Rustaveli from Liberty Square (Freedom Square) metro station.
  • Access: As a working parliament, the building is not open to the public; it's viewed from the avenue. The facade and the civic space in front are accessible at all times.
  • Best appreciated: As part of a walk along Rustaveli Avenue, with the historical context in mind.
  • Combine with: The rest of Rustaveli Avenue (the Opera, the museums, Kashveti Church opposite, the galleries) and Freedom Square at the avenue's eastern end.

faq.title: What is the Parliament of Georgia building?
faq.content: It's the seat of Georgia's legislature, at 8 Rustaveli Avenue in Tbilisi — a monumental Soviet-era structure built between the late 1930s and 1953 as the Government House of the Georgian SSR. Beyond its colonnaded architecture, it's one of the most historically charged places in the country: the site of the 9 April 1989 tragedy, when Soviet troops killed 21 demonstrators, and of the Rose Revolution in November 2003. Parliament moved to Kutaisi in 2012 and returned to Tbilisi around 2019, and this building is again the working home of the legislature.

faq.title: When and why was it built?
faq.content: It was constructed in two stages — the rear section in the late 1930s, the main section facing Rustaveli finished in 1953 — as the Government House of the Georgian Soviet Socialist Republic, by the architects Viktor Kokorin and Giorgi Lezhava. It stands on the site of a demolished 19th-century Russian military cathedral. Architecturally it sits between Stalinist monumentalism and European neoclassicism, but it also incorporates motifs of traditional Georgian architecture and is faced in golden tuff, with a monumental arcade composed to match the scale of Rustaveli Avenue.

faq.title: What happened here on 9 April 1989?
faq.content: On that day, Soviet troops violently dispersed a peaceful pro-independence demonstration in front of the building, killing 21 people — most of them young women — and injuring hundreds, using sapper spades and toxic gas against the crowd. It became one of the defining moments in Georgian consciousness about independence and Soviet rule, and the date is now commemorated annually as the Day of National Unity. The area in front of the Parliament is one of the main places of remembrance.

faq.title: What was the Rose Revolution?
faq.content: The Rose Revolution of November 2003 was the peaceful uprising that ended Eduard Shevardnadze's presidency and brought Mikheil Saakashvili to power. Its most dramatic scenes played out at this building, when opposition protesters — led by Saakashvili holding a rose — entered the parliamentary chamber during a session, and Shevardnadze left office. The images were broadcast worldwide and made the building one of the most recognizable symbols of post-Soviet political change in the former USSR.

faq.title: Can you go inside, and how do you visit?
faq.content: No — as a working parliament, the building is not open to the public; it's seen from the avenue. The facade and the wide civic space in front are accessible at any time, and it's a short walk along Rustaveli from the Liberty Square (Freedom Square) metro station. It's best appreciated as part of a walk along Rustaveli Avenue, with its modern-political history in mind — that's what gives the quiet, monumental facade its real weight.

gabriadzeMarionetteTheatre.heroTitle: Rezo Gabriadze Marionette Theatre and Clock Tower
gabriadzeMarionetteTheatre.content:
On a small square in the Old Town of Tbilisi, capital of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus), a few steps from the Anchiskhati Basilica, stands one of the most quietly extraordinary things in the city — a leaning brick tower built from mismatched materials, encrusted with ceramic fragments and small sculptures, topped by a miniature angel that emerges on the hour to strike a bell. The tower belongs to the Rezo Gabriadze Marionette Theatre, and both tower and theatre are the work of Rezo Gabriadze himself — playwright, screenwriter, sculptor, painter, and one of the most genuinely original artistic minds Georgia has produced in the modern era.

What is the Rezo Gabriadze Marionette Theatre and Clock Tower?

The Rezo Gabriadze Marionette Theatre is a small, internationally renowned puppet theatre in Tbilisi's Old Town, founded in 1981 by the Georgian artist Rezo Gabriadze (1936–2021). Beside it stands its leaning Clock Tower, built by Gabriadze in 2010 from salvaged old-Tbilisi bricks and hand-painted tiles, deliberately crooked and topped by an angel that strikes a bell on the hour. Twice a day, at noon and 19:00, a short mechanical puppet show called The Circle of Life plays from the tower. The theatre itself stages Gabriadze's marionette plays — among them The Autumn of My Springtime, The Battle of Stalingrad, and Ramona — in an intimate space of about 80 seats. The tower is free to see at any time; performances need tickets and sell out, so book ahead.

The clock and the tower

Gabriadze built the clock tower in 2010, as an addition to the theatre that had been his artistic home since 1981, and the structure is entirely characteristic of his sensibility — playful without being frivolous, handmade in a way that feels deliberate rather than rough, and carrying the quality that runs through all his work: at once funny and melancholy, childlike and profound. The tower leans slightly, its brickwork irregular, its surfaces studded with ceramic details and small found objects that reward close looking; it was built largely from bricks and tiles salvaged from demolished old Tbilisi houses, which gives it its patchwork, storybook feel. It looks as though it has always been there and was built by someone making it up as they went — which is more or less exactly what happened. (Look for the Latin motto on the theatre facade: Extra cepam nihil cogito nos lacrimare — roughly, "May onions be the only reason we cry.")

The mechanical show at the top runs on the hour through the day: a small angel emerges from a door and strikes the bell — a brief moment that draws a little crowd at every hour and is over almost before it begins. Twice daily, at noon and 19:00, a longer animated performance, The Circle of Life, takes place: figures emerge from the tower's facade in a wordless few minutes tracing birth, life, and death. Even by the jaded standards of people who've seen a great many tourist attractions, it's genuinely moving — the kind of thing that produces an unexpectedly strong reaction in adults who arrived expecting a mildly diverting mechanical clock.

The theatre and performances

The theatre stages full marionette productions in the intimate space beside the tower — only about 80 seats, which is part of why it feels the way it does (and why it sells out). Gabriadze's plays — among them The Autumn of My Springtime, The Battle of Stalingrad, and Ramona — are works of real theatrical sophistication that have toured across Europe and beyond, appearing at major festivals (Lincoln Center, Edinburgh, Avignon, the Kennedy Center, the Barbican, and others) and winning serious critical attention well beyond the world of puppet theatre. The productions are in Georgian, but the visual storytelling, the extraordinary craftsmanship of the marionettes, and the emotional intelligence of the writing make them accessible and affecting for non-Georgian speakers in a way spoken drama rarely manages across a language barrier. Attending a performance is one of the more memorable cultural experiences in Tbilisi — book ahead, because the theatre is small and productions sell out.

Rezo Gabriadze

Gabriadze was born in Kutaisi in 1936 and first built his reputation as a screenwriter, working on more than 35 films, including some of the most celebrated of the Soviet era — among them Mimino and Kin-dza-dza!, both still beloved across the former Soviet world. In 1981 he founded the marionette theatre in Tbilisi and spent the following decades building it into an institution with an international reputation that sits well apart from the standard categories of Georgian cultural tourism (his honors include France's Order of Arts and Letters). His visual art — paintings, drawings, small sculptures — has been exhibited widely, and the theatre building itself, with its decorated facades and the clock tower as its centerpiece, is as much a work of art as anything shown inside.

Gabriadze died in 2021, but the theatre he founded continues to operate, and his productions remain in the repertoire; his son, Levan Gabriadze, is himself an actor and film director. The tower and the theatre stand as Gabriadze's most public and most permanent work — a gift to the city that, in the years since the tower went up, has become as much a part of Old Tbilisi's identity as the sulfur baths or the Narikala walls.

The surroundings

The theatre sits in one of the most walkable and characterful parts of the Old Town, among the narrow lanes, traditional houses, and small churches that define the historic core. The Anchiskhati Basilica — the oldest surviving church in Tbilisi — is immediately adjacent. The Bridge of Peace and the Mtkvari riverfront are a short walk south. The streets between here and Abanotubani hold some of the most intact traditional architecture left in the Old Town, and pairing the tower with a wander through this part of the city makes a natural hour or two of Old Tbilisi exploration.

Visiting

  • Location: 13 Shavteli Street, Old Tbilisi (a few steps from the Anchiskhati Basilica).
  • Clock tower: Free and publicly accessible at any time. The angel strikes the bell on the hour; the longer Circle of Life show plays daily at 12:00 and 19:00.
  • Theatre performances: Ticketed; book through the box office or website (gabriadze.com). The theatre is small (~80 seats) and shows sell out — book ahead. Productions are in Georgian.
  • Website: gabriadze.com
  • Tips: Time a casual visit for noon or 19:00 to catch The Circle of Life; there's a café by the theatre. Even without a performance ticket, the tower and the surrounding lanes are worth the stop.
  • Combine with: The Anchiskhati Basilica (next door), Shavteli and the Old Town lanes, the Bridge of Peace and Rike Park, and Abanotubani — a natural Old Town walking cluster.

faq.title: What is the Rezo Gabriadze Marionette Theatre and Clock Tower?
faq.content: It's a small, internationally renowned puppet theatre in Tbilisi's Old Town, founded in 1981 by the Georgian artist Rezo Gabriadze (1936–2021), with a famous leaning clock tower beside it that Gabriadze built in 2010. The tower — deliberately crooked, made from salvaged old-Tbilisi bricks and hand-painted tiles — has an angel that strikes a bell on the hour, and twice a day a short mechanical puppet show called The Circle of Life. The theatre stages Gabriadze's marionette plays in an intimate space of about 80 seats. The tower is free to see; performances are ticketed and sell out.

faq.title: What is the clock-tower show, and when is it?
faq.content: On the hour, every hour, a small angel emerges from a door near the top and strikes the bell — a brief moment that's over quickly. Twice a day, at noon and 19:00, there's a longer animated puppet show called The Circle of Life, in which figures emerge from the tower's facade to trace birth, life, and death in a wordless few minutes. It's surprisingly moving — many adults who came expecting a cute mechanical clock find it genuinely affecting. Time a casual visit for noon or 7 PM to catch it.

faq.title: Can I see a performance, and is it in English?
faq.content: Yes — the theatre stages full marionette productions (among them The Autumn of My Springtime, The Battle of Stalingrad, and Ramona) in the intimate hall beside the tower. They're performed in Georgian, but the visual storytelling, the craftsmanship of the marionettes, and the emotional depth of the writing make them accessible and affecting even for non-Georgian speakers. The theatre is small (about 80 seats) and shows sell out, so book ahead through the box office or gabriadze.com. Attending is one of the more memorable cultural experiences in Tbilisi.

faq.title: Who was Rezo Gabriadze?
faq.content: One of the most original Georgian artists of the modern era (1936–2021) — a screenwriter, playwright, director, painter, sculptor, and puppeteer, born in Kutaisi. He first made his name as a screenwriter, working on more than 35 films including the beloved Soviet-era classics Mimino and Kin-dza-dza!, then founded the marionette theatre in Tbilisi in 1981 and built it into an internationally touring institution. He designed the theatre building and the clock tower himself; both are as much works of art as the productions inside. He died in 2021, and the theatre he founded continues to operate.

faq.title: Where is it, and what can I combine it with?
faq.content: It's at 13 Shavteli Street in the Old Town, a few steps from the Anchiskhati Basilica (the oldest surviving church in Tbilisi), in one of the most walkable, characterful parts of the historic center. The Bridge of Peace and the Mtkvari riverfront are a short walk south, and the lanes toward Abanotubani hold some of the best-preserved traditional architecture in the Old Town. The tower and a wander through this area make a natural hour or two — and the tower (free) plus an evening performance is an ideal combination if you can get a ticket.

rikePark.content:
Rike Park runs along the left bank of the Mtkvari in the heart of Tbilisi, capital of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus) — a long strip of green between the river and the slope rising to the Avlabari district above. It's a relatively recent addition to the city, developed around 2010 as part of the broader modernization of central Tbilisi, and its clean, contemporary design sits in deliberate contrast to the historic Old Town directly across the water. From almost anywhere in the park, looking south across the river, the wooden-balconied houses of the Old Town climb the hillside, Narikala Fortress sits on the ridge above them, and the Mother of Georgia stands against the sky — one of the better vantage points for this view, close to the historic district but with enough distance to take in the whole composition.

What is Rike Park?

Rike Park is a modern landscaped park on the left bank of the Mtkvari River in central Tbilisi, opened around 2010 as part of the city's modernization. It's known less as a destination in itself than as a connector and a viewpoint: the Bridge of Peace links it to the Old Town at one end, and the cable car up to Narikala Fortress runs from the other, while across the river it gives one of the best views of the Old Town, Narikala, and the Mother of Georgia. The park has lawns and riverside paths, an evening musical fountain, an amphitheater, and playful installations (a giant piano, a giant chessboard), and it's framed by two of Tbilisi's most-debated modern structures — the unfinished "two tubes" concert hall and the glass-domed former presidential palace. It's free and open at all times, and at its best in the evening.

A connector and a viewpoint

The park is long and narrow, following the river for several hundred meters, and its layout reflects its role as a connector as much as a destination. The Bridge of Peace — the curved steel-and-glass pedestrian bridge (designed by Michele De Lucchi, opened 2010) — reaches the park at one end, the most direct crossing to the Old Town opposite. The cable-car station for Narikala is at the other end, its glass-floored gondolas climbing over the river to the fortress ridge. That gives the park a natural trajectory: cross the Bridge of Peace into the park, walk the riverfront, take the cable car up to Narikala and the Mother of Georgia, then walk back down through the Old Town streets to the bridge — one of the most satisfying walking circuits in central Tbilisi.

What the park contains

The design is deliberately open — lawns, paths, benches, and planted areas arranged to keep the river views and provide usable public space without over-structuring how people move through it. The musical fountain runs synchronized water-and-light shows on summer evenings, drawing crowds and popular with families, and an open-air amphitheater hosts concerts and events through the warmer months (it's one of the venues for the annual Tbilisoba city festival in October). Scattered through the park are playful installations — a giant piano, a giant chessboard, a climbing wall, a children's maze, sculptures — that make it an easy place to bring kids.

The riverbank promenade along the southern edge is pleasant at any hour, but especially in the early morning, when the light comes from the east and catches the Old Town facades across the water, and in the evening, when the Bridge of Peace lighting comes on and the lit bridge, the illuminated Metekhi Church on its cliff upstream, and their reflections in the river make the most photogenic version of this stretch of the Mtkvari.

The two modern landmarks

Two of Tbilisi's most-debated modern structures frame the park. At the northern end stand the "two tubes" — the unfinished Music Theatre and Exhibition Hall, designed by the Italian architects Massimiliano and Doriana Fuksas and commissioned during the Saakashvili-era modernization. Built externally but never completed or opened inside (funding stopped after the 2012 change of government), the tubes have sat empty for years; their fate is still debated, with proposals ranging from completion to demolition — but they've become an unofficial landmark and a favorite of photographers all the same.

Above the park on the Avlabari slope is the glass-domed presidential palace (the Avlabari Residence), also from the Saakashvili era and designed by Michele De Lucchi — the same architect as the Bridge of Peace. Its juxtaposition with the historic surroundings divided opinion when it was built, and it's worth noting that the main presidential residence later moved elsewhere in the city, so the building's role has shifted; but its glass dome remains an inescapable part of the view from the park.

As part of a Tbilisi itinerary

Rike Park works less as a stand-alone destination than as a connecting space that makes other things easier and more pleasant. Starting a morning walk here, crossing to the Old Town via the Bridge of Peace, spending time in the historic district, and returning by cable car from Narikala — or doing it in reverse — uses the park exactly as it's best suited to be used. And in the evening, with the fountain running and the bridge lit, it's at its most atmospheric purely for its own sake.

Visiting

  • Location: Left bank of the Mtkvari, central Tbilisi — between the Bridge of Peace and the Narikala cable-car station (nearest metro: Avlabari, about a 10-minute walk).
  • Hours: Open-air and freely accessible at all times. The musical fountain runs on summer evenings.
  • Admission: Free (the cable car to Narikala has its own fare).
  • Best time: Early morning for the light on the Old Town; evening for the fountain and the lit Bridge of Peace.
  • Combine with: The Bridge of Peace and the Old Town (one end), the cable car up to Narikala and the Mother of Georgia (the other), and the Metekhi cliff and Avlabari/Sameba just upstream — the core riverfront cluster.

faq.title: What is Rike Park?
faq.content: Rike Park is a modern landscaped park on the left bank of the Mtkvari River in central Tbilisi, opened around 2010 as part of the city's modernization. It's known less as a destination in itself than as a connector and a viewpoint: the Bridge of Peace links it to the Old Town at one end, the cable car up to Narikala Fortress runs from the other, and across the river it gives one of the best views of the Old Town, Narikala, and the Mother of Georgia. It has lawns and riverside paths, an evening musical fountain, an amphitheater, and playful installations like a giant piano and a giant chessboard. It's free and open at all times.

faq.title: How does it connect to the Old Town and Narikala?
faq.content: Directly — that's its main use. The Bridge of Peace (the curved glass pedestrian bridge) crosses from the Old Town into the park at one end, and the cable car to Narikala Fortress runs from the other end, its glass-floored gondolas climbing over the river. The natural circuit is to cross the Bridge of Peace into the park, walk the riverfront, take the cable car up to Narikala and the Mother of Georgia, and walk back down through the Old Town to the bridge — one of the best short walking loops in central Tbilisi.

faq.title: What are the "two tubes" and the glass dome near the park?
faq.content: The "two tubes" at the park's northern end are an unfinished Music Theatre and Exhibition Hall, designed by the Italian architects Massimiliano and Doriana Fuksas during the Saakashvili era. Built on the outside but never completed or opened inside (funding stopped after the 2012 change of government), they've sat empty for years and their future is debated — but they've become an unofficial landmark. The glass-domed building on the slope above the park is the Avlabari presidential palace, also from that era and designed by Michele De Lucchi (the Bridge of Peace architect); the main presidential residence later moved elsewhere, but the dome remains a fixture of the view.

faq.title: Is it good for families, and what's there to do?
faq.content: Yes — it's one of the more family-friendly spots in central Tbilisi. Beyond the lawns and riverside paths, there are playful installations (a giant piano, a giant chessboard, a climbing wall, a children's maze) and an amphitheater that hosts concerts and events in summer, including parts of the annual Tbilisoba festival in October. The musical fountain's evening water-and-light shows are a particular draw on warm nights.

faq.title: When is the best time to visit?
faq.content: Early morning, when the light from the east catches the Old Town facades across the river — and evening, when the musical fountain runs and the Bridge of Peace is lit. The evening view, with the illuminated bridge, the lit Metekhi Church on its cliff upstream, and their reflections in the Mtkvari, is the most photogenic version of this stretch of the river. It's free and open at any hour, so it's easy to fit in around the rest of a riverfront walk.

rikeNarikalaCableCar.heroTitle: Rike–Narikala Cable Car: Tbilisi's Best Few Minutes
rikeNarikalaCableCar.content:
The cable car connecting Rike Park to the Narikala Fortress is one of the most enjoyable few minutes available to any visitor in Tbilisi, capital of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus). The ride takes only two or three minutes, but those minutes pass over one of the most visually concentrated stretches of the city — the Mtkvari River directly below, the sulfur-bath domes of Abanotubani to one side, the tangled rooftops and wooden balconies of the Old Town spreading across the hillside, and the Narikala walls rising ahead. It is, in the most efficient way possible, a summary of what makes Tbilisi look the way it does.

What is the Rike–Narikala Cable Car?

The Rike–Narikala cable car is a short aerial ropeway in central Tbilisi, running from Rike Park (Europe Square) on the left bank of the Mtkvari up to the Narikala Fortress ridge, beside the Mother of Georgia statue. The ride is about 2–3 minutes in glass-sided gondolas over the river and the Old Town rooftops, and the upper station puts you right at the fortress walls and one of the best views in the city. It's the easiest and most enjoyable way up to Narikala — the classic plan is to ride up and walk back down through the Old Town. It runs daily into the evening (weather permitting) and is paid for with a Tbilisi transport card (the same card used for the metro and buses), at a fare of around 2.5 GEL one-way.

The ride

The lower station sits in Rike Park on the left bank of the Mtkvari — the long riverside park below the Avlabari slope that connects to the Old Town across the Bridge of Peace. The cabins are modern and glass-sided, holding a small group of passengers, and the ascent climbs at a steep angle from the river to the fortress ridge, crossing over the Old Town rooftops in a way that feels surprisingly intimate — close enough to make out individual balconies and courtyard gardens rather than just a general cityscape. The descent, looking back toward Rike Park and the left bank with the Mtkvari in the foreground, gives a different but equally good composition.

The experience changes a lot with the time of day. In the afternoon the views are clearest and the city's layout most legible. At sunset, when the low light catches the Old Town facades and the Narikala walls warm to orange-gold, the ride becomes something close to cinematic. In the evening, with the Old Town illuminated and the sulfur-bath domes lit from below, the aerial view over the historic district is unlike anything from street level.

The upper station: Narikala and the Mother of Georgia

The upper station sets passengers down at the level of the Narikala Fortress walls, directly beside the Mother of Georgia — the 20-meter aluminum figure (on the ridge since 1958) holding a sword in one hand and a bowl of wine in the other, one of Tbilisi's most recognized symbols. From this elevated spot the whole geography of the Old Town opens up: the Mtkvari making its bend, the Metekhi Church on its cliff to the east, the dome clusters of Abanotubani, and the maze of streets and courtyards of the historic core below.

The practical value goes beyond the ride. Narikala — the citadel on the ridge since the 4th century, one of the best natural viewpoints in the city — can be reached on foot from the Old Town by steep stairs and paths, but the cable car makes the ascent effortless and saves the climb for the descent, which is far more pleasant done downhill through the Old Town streets. From the upper station, the fortress walls, the Mother of Georgia, the (reconstructed) St. Nicholas Church inside the fortress, and the panoramic ridge terraces are all within an easy walk — making a natural sequence of the cable-car ride, a wander along the walls, and a walk back down into the Old Town.

Getting there and visiting

  • Lower station: Rike Park (Europe Square), left bank of the Mtkvari, central Tbilisi (nearest metro: Avlabari).
  • Upper station: the Narikala Fortress ridge, beside the Mother of Georgia statue.
  • Hours: Daily, roughly from late morning into the evening — exact hours vary by season, and the cable car can pause in high wind or bad weather.
  • Fare/payment: About 2.5 GEL one-way, paid with a Tbilisi transport card (the rechargeable card used for the metro and buses — buy and load it at the station; the card itself costs a small fee). You can't pay the gate directly with cash or a bank card, so get the card first.
  • Best time: Sunset, for the golden light over the Old Town; or after dark, for the illuminated city.
  • Tip: Ride up, then walk down through the Old Town (via Narikala's stairs to Meidan/Betlemi) — the most rewarding way to do it. It can be breezy at the top, so a light layer helps in cooler months.
  • Combine with: Narikala Fortress, the Mother of Georgia, the National Botanical Garden (down the ridge), and — at the lower end — Rike Park, the Bridge of Peace, and the Old Town riverfront.

faq.title: What is the Rike–Narikala cable car?
faq.content: It's a short aerial ropeway in central Tbilisi running from Rike Park (Europe Square) on the left bank of the Mtkvari up to the Narikala Fortress ridge, beside the Mother of Georgia statue. The ride is about 2–3 minutes in glass-sided gondolas, over the river and the Old Town rooftops, and the upper station puts you right at the fortress walls and one of the best views in the city. It's the easiest and most enjoyable way up to Narikala — the classic plan is to ride up and walk back down through the Old Town.

faq.title: How much does it cost, and how do you pay?
faq.content: It's about 2.5 GEL one-way, but you can't pay the gate directly with cash or a bank card — you need a Tbilisi transport card (the same rechargeable card used for the metro and buses). Buy and load it at the station before joining the queue; the card itself costs a small fee. The same card works for the city's metro and buses, so it's worth having anyway.

faq.title: What are the hours, and does weather matter?
faq.content: It runs daily, roughly from late morning into the evening, though exact hours vary by season. Weather matters: the cable car can pause in high wind or poor conditions, so on a very windy day it may not be running. It can also be breezy at the upper station, so a light layer is useful in cooler months.

faq.title: What's at the top?
faq.content: The upper station is right at the Narikala Fortress walls and beside the Mother of Georgia statue (the 20-meter aluminum figure that's one of Tbilisi's symbols). From there, the fortress, the ridge terraces, the reconstructed St. Nicholas Church inside the walls, and a panoramic view over the Old Town, the Mtkvari, Abanotubani, and the Metekhi cliff are all within an easy walk. The National Botanical Garden also lies down the ridge from here.

faq.title: When is the best time to ride?
faq.content: Sunset is the favorite — the low light catches the Old Town facades and the Narikala walls warm to orange-gold. After dark is also striking, with the illuminated Old Town and the lit sulfur-bath domes seen from above. The afternoon gives the clearest, most legible views of the city layout. Whatever the time, the best overall plan is to ride up and walk back down through the Old Town.

rustaveliAvenue.content:
Rustaveli Avenue runs for about 1.5 kilometers through the heart of central Tbilisi, capital of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus), from Freedom Square at its eastern end to Rustaveli Square in the west, where it continues as Kostava Street. It is the city's main ceremonial boulevard and the street that has historically concentrated the greatest density of Georgia's public, cultural, and political institutions in a single walkable corridor. Named after Shota Rustaveli — the 12th-century poet whose epic The Knight in the Panther's Skin is the central work of Georgian literature and a foundational text of Georgian identity — the avenue has been the symbolic spine of Tbilisi since its development in the 19th century, and remains so today.

What is Rustaveli Avenue?

Rustaveli Avenue is the main boulevard of Tbilisi — about 1.5 km from Freedom Square to Rustaveli Square — lined with the country's most important cultural and political institutions: the Georgian National Museum, the Opera and Ballet Theatre, the Rustaveli National Theatre, the Parliament, Kashveti Church, and more. Laid out in the 19th century when Tbilisi was the administrative capital of the Russian Caucasus (it was first called Golovin Avenue, renamed for Rustaveli in 1918), it's grand, European in character, and the city's primary civic stage — the setting for the 9 April 1989 tragedy, the 2003 Rose Revolution, and most major public events. The best way to experience it is simply to walk its length, ideally in both directions and at different times of day.

A boulevard built for a capital

The avenue was laid out when Tbilisi served as the administrative capital of the entire Russian Caucasus (the road, dating from the 1840s, was first named Golovin Avenue after a Russian commander, and renamed for Rustaveli in 1918), and the ambition of that role shows in the scale and character of its buildings. The imperial administration wanted a boulevard expressing the weight of a major regional capital, and the theaters, government buildings, hotels, banks, and educational institutions built along it through the 19th and early 20th centuries were designed accordingly. The result is a street that feels genuinely urban in the European sense — its buildings substantial, its proportions generous — in a way the older, more organic streets of the historic quarter are not.

The buildings and institutions

Walking from east to west, the institutional density is immediately clear. The avenue begins at Freedom Square, where the column of St. George marks the transition from the Old Town to the 19th-century city. Moving west, the Georgian National Museum occupies a large building on the northern side — the main repository of the country's archaeological and historical collections, and the best single introduction to Georgian civilization anywhere. The Kashveti Church of St. George, a small but significant early-20th-century Orthodox church, sits on the southern side, its stone facade a quieter note among the larger institutional buildings; the Georgian Museum of Fine Arts is nearby on the same side.

The Rustaveli National Theatre, one of the most important theatrical institutions in Georgia, holds a prominent place on the southern side. The drama company dates back to 1879, has occupied its ornate Rococo building (built around 1898–1901 for the Artists' Society) on the avenue since 1901, and has carried Rustaveli's name since 1921; through the Soviet period and into independence — under directors including Kote Marjanishvili, Sandro Akhmeteli, and Robert Sturua — its productions have held a reputation that reaches well beyond the country.

The Opera and Ballet Theatre, with its neo-Moorish facade, is one of the most architecturally singular buildings on the avenue, its arches and geometric ornament standing out against the more restrained buildings around it. The Parliament of Georgia occupies a large building on the northern side, giving the avenue a political weight alongside its cultural institutions; directly opposite it stands the Rustaveli Cinema (with the Youth Palace, the former Viceroy's Palace, nearby). At the western end, the Marriott hotel occupies one of the avenue's elegantly restored 19th-century buildings (the former Hotel Majestic), and its ground-floor café terrace is a good vantage point for watching the avenue's pedestrian life. At the far end, near Rustaveli Square and the metro, a statue of Shota Rustaveli bookends the avenue — St. George at one end, the poet it's named for at the other.

History and civic significance

Rustaveli Avenue has been the setting for the most significant public events in modern Tbilisi. The 9 April 1989 tragedy — when Soviet troops used lethal force to disperse a peaceful pro-independence demonstration in front of the Parliament here, killing 21 people (most of them young women) and injuring hundreds — is commemorated annually as the Day of National Unity and remains one of the defining moments in the national consciousness; it sharply accelerated public support for independence. The Rose Revolution of November 2003 also played out on and around the avenue. Political gatherings, cultural celebrations, and national commemorations have all used this space as their primary stage, giving the boulevard a civic resonance beyond its architecture.

Through the Soviet decades, the concentration of theaters, museums, and educational institutions on Rustaveli made it the space where Georgian cultural life was publicly expressed under conditions of political constraint. The writers, artists, and intellectuals who gathered in the cafés along the avenue were doing more than simply socializing, and the street absorbed that history into its character.

Walking the avenue

The most rewarding way to experience Rustaveli is to walk its full length, ideally in both directions at different times of day. The morning light comes from the east and catches the southern facades; the evening light reverses it, and the buildings take on different qualities as the day goes on. The ground floors of most of the major buildings have been opened to cafés, restaurants, and shops, keeping the street level active without the sterility that purely institutional boulevards sometimes have. (Look, too, for the small bronze street sculptures — a tamada, a saxophonist, characters from Pirosmani — scattered along the way.)

The avenue meets several streets that lead off into other parts of the city — south toward the Old Town, north toward the Vera and Vake districts — and treating Rustaveli as a starting point for those explorations, rather than only a destination, gives the walk added purpose. The full length takes a comfortable 30 minutes without stops, or several hours if the museum, the theatre, and the cafés are included.

Getting there and walking it

  • Location: Central Tbilisi, from Freedom Square (east) to Rustaveli Square (west), where it becomes Kostava Street.
  • Length: About 1.5 km.
  • Metro: Liberty Square (Freedom Square, eastern end) and Rustaveli (Rustaveli Square, western end).
  • Access: On foot — wide pavements, pedestrian-friendly throughout, well lit in the evening.
  • Time: ~30 minutes end to end without stops; half a day or more with the museum, theatre, and cafés.
  • Combine with: Freedom Square and the Old Town (south), the individual Rustaveli landmarks (the Opera, the Parliament, the National Museum, the Rustaveli Theatre, Kashveti Church), and the Vera/Vake districts (north).

faq.title: What is Rustaveli Avenue?
faq.content: Rustaveli Avenue is the main boulevard of Tbilisi — about 1.5 km running from Freedom Square in the east to Rustaveli Square in the west, where it continues as Kostava Street. It's lined with the country's most important cultural and political institutions — the Georgian National Museum, the Opera and Ballet Theatre, the Rustaveli National Theatre, the Parliament, Kashveti Church — and it's the city's primary civic stage. Laid out in the 19th century when Tbilisi was the administrative capital of the Russian Caucasus (it was first called Golovin Avenue, renamed for Rustaveli in 1918), it's grand and European in character, and the best way to experience it is on foot.

faq.title: Who is it named after?
faq.content: Shota Rustaveli, the 12th-century Georgian poet whose epic The Knight in the Panther's Skin (Vepkhistqaosani) is the central work of Georgian literature and a foundational text of Georgian national identity. The avenue, originally named Golovin after a Russian commander, was renamed for Rustaveli in 1918, around Georgia's brief first independence. A statue of the poet stands at the western (Rustaveli Square) end of the avenue — with St. George's column at Freedom Square at the other end, the boulevard is bookended by two symbols of the nation.

faq.title: What are the main buildings along it?
faq.content: From the Freedom Square end heading west: the Georgian National Museum (the country's main historical and archaeological collections), Kashveti Church of St. George and the Georgian Museum of Fine Arts on the southern side, the neo-Moorish Opera and Ballet Theatre, the Rococo Rustaveli National Theatre, and the Parliament of Georgia (with the Rustaveli Cinema and the Youth Palace opposite). The Marriott hotel, in a restored 19th-century building at the western end, has a café terrace good for watching the street. Each of these is worth its own visit; together they make Rustaveli a near-complete cross-section of Georgian public life.

faq.title: Why is it historically important?
faq.content: Because it has been the stage for nearly every major event in modern Georgian history. The 9 April 1989 tragedy — when Soviet troops killed 21 demonstrators, most of them young women, in front of the Parliament — happened here and is commemorated as the Day of National Unity; the 2003 Rose Revolution played out on and around the avenue; and political gatherings, celebrations, and commemorations still use it as their primary space. Through the Soviet decades, its concentration of theatres, museums, and cafés also made it the place where Georgian cultural life was publicly expressed under political constraint.

faq.title: How long does it take to walk, and how do I get there?
faq.content: The full 1.5 km takes a comfortable 30 minutes end to end without stops, or half a day to several hours if you go into the National Museum, catch a performance, and stop at the cafés. It's flat, pedestrian-friendly, with wide pavements and good evening lighting. The Liberty Square metro station is at the eastern (Freedom Square) end and the Rustaveli station at the western end, so you can walk it one way and metro back. It also makes a natural starting point for exploring the Old Town (to the south) or the Vera and Vake districts (to the north).

shardeniStreet.heroTitle: Jan Shardeni Street: The Old Town's Social Heart
shardeniStreet.content:
Shardeni Street runs a few hundred meters through the heart of the Old Town of Tbilisi, capital of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus), in the Upper Kala quarter near Sioni Cathedral, down toward Meidan Square and the Mtkvari riverfront. In that short distance it concentrates more of Tbilisi's social energy than almost any other single street in the city. It is not a monument or an institution — it's simply where people eat, drink, talk, and spend their evenings, lined with the outdoor terraces, wine bars, restaurants, and small galleries that make it the most reliably animated part of the historic center after dark.

What is Shardeni Street?

Shardeni Street is a short, pedestrian café-and-wine-bar street in Tbilisi's Old Town — the city's main social hub after dark, packed with terraces, natural-wine bars, restaurants, and galleries. Once a covered market lane in the old trading quarter (the "Dark Row"), it was renamed for the 17th-century French traveler Jean Chardin, who visited Tbilisi in 1672 and left some of the earliest written and graphic records of the city. Today it sits near Sioni Cathedral, runs toward Meidan Square and the riverfront, and is at its liveliest in the evening. There's nothing to pay to walk it; the cost is whatever you eat and drink.

The name: Jean Chardin

The street takes its name from Jean Chardin (Jean-Baptiste Chardin), the 17th-century French jeweler, traveler, and writer who passed through Tbilisi in 1672 on his way to Persia and wrote some of the most valuable documentary accounts of Georgia in the pre-Russian period. His descriptions are unusually observant and free of the political axe-grinding common to contemporary accounts of the Caucasus — written by someone genuinely interested in what he saw rather than in confirming what he already believed. He called Tbilisi one of the most beautiful cities in the world and marveled at its mix of peoples — Armenians, Greeks, Jews, Persians, Indians, and Europeans all in one place — and, importantly, he left an early map and illustrations of Tbilisi that are among the earliest surviving visual records of the city. The street named after him sits, fittingly, in the part of the city he would still recognize.

The street itself is older than its name. In the late medieval period this was a covered market lane — the "Dark Row," named for the awnings that shaded its stalls — part of a trading quarter whose streets were named for their trades (Iron Row, Cotton Row, both of which survive nearby). It was rebuilt and renamed for Chardin in the modern era, becoming the pedestrian café street it is today.

The street

The buildings lining Shardeni are mostly 19th-century structures whose ground floors have been comprehensively taken over by the hospitality trade — their facades largely intact, their interiors converted into the bars, restaurants, and galleries that define the street now. The outdoor terraces that spill onto the lane in the warmer months are the defining feature: small tables along the cobblestones, separated from neighboring places by planters or low wooden dividers, the whole forming a continuous social space that is technically many venues but feels, in practice, like one long outdoor room.

The wine along Shardeni reflects the broader Georgian wine renaissance of the past two decades. The natural-wine bars carry ranges of qvevri-made wines from small producers across Kakheti, Kartli, and the western regions — amber whites, skin-contact wines, traditional and experimental styles — giving serious drinkers access to the full diversity of Georgian winemaking on a single street. The more casual places cover the standard Georgian repertoire alongside international options, and the concentration of choices in such a short distance makes Shardeni a practical spot for groups with varied tastes. At the street's start stands the bronze Tamada (toastmaster) statue — a copy of a small ancient figure found at Vani — a fitting emblem for a street built around wine and the table.

The galleries and boutiques among the bars give the street a cultural dimension beyond food and drink. Several galleries represent contemporary Georgian artists and operate as genuine commercial spaces, and the proximity of art to wine bars is characteristic of the slightly blurred line between cultural and social life that Tbilisi's creative scene tends to work within.

Day and evening

Shardeni's character shifts considerably through the day. In the morning and early afternoon it's quiet — a few people at tables with coffee, the occasional delivery, the galleries open but unhurried. From late afternoon the terraces fill, and by evening the street reaches the warm, slightly crowded, convivial atmosphere that has made it one of the most-visited parts of the Old Town. Street musicians turn up; the conversation spills off the terraces into the lane; and the mix of historic architecture, warm lighting, and density of activity creates the kind of evening atmosphere that's hard to manufacture and that Tbilisi produces here with apparent ease.

Reservations are worth making for dinner at the more popular restaurants on busy weekend evenings; for a drink or a casual meal, a table on a weeknight is usually easy to find.

Visiting

  • Location: Jan Shardeni Street, Old Tbilisi (Upper Kala) — near Sioni Cathedral, running toward Meidan Square and the riverfront, parallel to Bambis Rigi and Rkinis Rigi, with Erekle II Street (another café lane) nearby.
  • Hours: The street is open at all times; venues typically open from mid-morning and run until midnight or later, varying by place.
  • Cost: Free to walk; you pay only for what you order (it's one of the pricier areas in the Old Town, being the most touristed).
  • Best time: Evening, for the terraces and the atmosphere — though the daytime quiet has its own appeal for the galleries.
  • Tip: For a more local or lower-key scene, the nearby lanes (Erekle II, the streets toward Abanotubani) and the markets offer alternatives; Shardeni is the busy, polished heart of it.
  • Combine with: Sioni Cathedral, the Bridge of Peace and the riverfront, Abanotubani and the sulfur baths, Narikala and the Old Town lanes — it's in the middle of the whole Old Town cluster.

faq.title: What is Shardeni Street?
faq.content: Shardeni Street is a short, pedestrian café-and-wine-bar street in Tbilisi's Old Town — the city's main social hub after dark, lined with outdoor terraces, natural-wine bars, restaurants, and small galleries. Once a covered market lane in the old trading quarter (known as the "Dark Row"), it was renamed for the 17th-century French traveler Jean Chardin, who visited Tbilisi in 1672. It sits in the Upper Kala quarter near Sioni Cathedral, runs toward Meidan Square and the riverfront, and is busiest and most atmospheric in the evening.

faq.title: Who was Jean Chardin?
faq.content: A 17th-century French jeweler, traveler, and writer who passed through Tbilisi in 1672 on his way to Persia and wrote some of the most valuable documentary accounts of Georgia in the pre-Russian period. He called Tbilisi one of the most beautiful cities in the world, described its remarkable mix of peoples, and — importantly — left an early map and illustrations of the city that are among the earliest surviving visual records of it. The street is named in his honor.

faq.title: What's it known for?
faq.content: Wine, food, and atmosphere. Shardeni is one of the best places in Tbilisi to sample the Georgian wine renaissance: its natural-wine bars carry qvevri-made wines — amber whites, skin-contact styles — from small producers across Kakheti, Kartli, and the western regions. There are restaurants covering Georgian and international food, and galleries showing (and selling) contemporary Georgian art. The defining feature is the outdoor terraces that turn the whole lane into one long, convivial open-air room on a warm evening.

faq.title: When is the best time to go, and do I need a reservation?
faq.content: Evening is when Shardeni comes alive — the terraces fill from late afternoon, and by night it's warm, busy, and convivial. (Daytime is quiet, which suits browsing the galleries.) For dinner at the more popular restaurants on busy weekend evenings, a reservation is worth making; for a drink or a casual meal, especially on a weeknight, you can usually find a table. Bear in mind it's one of the more touristed — and pricier — corners of the Old Town.

faq.title: Where is it, and what's nearby?
faq.content: It's in the Upper Kala quarter of the Old Town, near Sioni Cathedral, running down toward Meidan (Vakhtang Gorgasali) Square and the Mtkvari riverfront, parallel to the old trade lanes Bambis Rigi and Rkinis Rigi, with Erekle II Street — another café-lined lane — close by. That puts it right in the middle of the Old Town cluster: the Bridge of Peace and the riverfront, Abanotubani and the sulfur baths, and the climb to Narikala are all a short walk away.

sioniCathedral.heroTitle: Sioni Cathedral: Tbilisi's Ancient Cathedral and the Cross of St. Nino
sioniCathedral.content:
Sioni Cathedral stands near the bank of the Mtkvari in the heart of the Old Town of Tbilisi, capital of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus), its relatively modest exterior giving little hint from the street of the historical depth and religious significance it holds. It isn't the largest or most dramatic church in Tbilisi — Sameba Cathedral on its hill holds that position without contest — but Sioni has something no newer building can acquire: roughly fourteen centuries of continuous religious life on the same site, and within its walls one of the most sacred objects in Georgian Christianity.

What is Sioni Cathedral?

Sioni Cathedral is one of the oldest and most important churches in Tbilisi — for centuries the principal cathedral of the Georgian Orthodox Church and the seat of its Catholicos-Patriarch, a role it held until Sameba Cathedral was consecrated in 2004. First built in the 6th–7th century and rebuilt many times since (the present building is based on a 13th-century structure, with later changes), it's named after Mount Zion in Jerusalem. Its great draw is the Cross of St. Nino — the grapevine cross of the woman who brought Christianity to Georgia in the 4th century, venerated here and one of the holiest relics in the country. It's an active church in the middle of the Old Town, free to enter, and one of the most direct encounters available with living Georgian Orthodox religious culture.

A cathedral rebuilt across the centuries

The cathedral takes its name from Mount Zion in Jerusalem — a dedication reflecting early Georgian Christianity's self-conscious relationship with the holy sites of the biblical world. A church was first built here in the 6th–7th century (the medieval annals credit Vakhtang Gorgasali in the 5th century, with construction under Prince Guaram around 575 and completion in the mid-7th), as Tbilisi was consolidating its role as the Kartlian capital and the Georgian church was forming its own identity.

That building was destroyed and rebuilt many times over the centuries — the institution persisting through the physical destruction. It was rebuilt by David IV the Builder in 1112 after he liberated Tbilisi from Arab rule, then ravaged again by Jalal ad-Din (1226), the Khwarazmians (1236), Shah Ismail (1522), an earthquake (1668), and the Persian invasion of 1795 — rising from the ruins each time. The present structure is based on the 13th-century version, with significant changes from the 17th to 19th centuries (the dome dates to 1710, the exterior was renovated in the 19th century). It served as the principal cathedral of the Georgian Orthodox Church, and the seat of the Catholicos-Patriarch, until Sameba was completed in 2004.

The Cross of St. Nino

The object that gives Sioni its particular sanctity is the Cross of St. Nino, venerated as one of the most important relics in Georgian Christianity. St. Nino was a Cappadocian woman — a young missionary, by the Georgian chronicles — who brought Christianity to the Kartlian kingdom in the early 4th century, converting the royal court and setting in motion the adoption of Christianity as the state religion. Her cross is traditionally described as made from two lengths of grapevine bound together with strands of her own hair, forming a cross with drooping arms — the form that became one of the most recognizable symbols of Georgian Christianity (the distinctive Georgian cross, with its slightly bowed horizontal bar, derives directly from this tradition).

The cross is kept and venerated at Sioni, to the left of the altar/iconostasis, enclosed in an ornate silver reliquary (the reliquary was given by King Vakhtang III in the early 14th century). It is one of the primary pilgrimage destinations in Tbilisi; the faithful can venerate it directly on certain feast days, and it can be seen behind protective glass at other times. Sources differ on whether the cross now displayed is the original or a replica (with the original kept safely), so this point is best held lightly. At almost any hour there are people before it — lighting candles, praying, or simply standing in quiet attention. For visitors without a personal religious stake, the chapel and its atmosphere offer one of the most direct encounters available in Tbilisi with the living religious culture that has shaped Georgian identity since the 4th century.

Architecture and interior

Sioni is built from the warm yellow tuff characteristic of Georgia's historic churches (quarried at Bolnisi, southwest of Tbilisi), its proportions balanced and its silhouette dominated by the central drum and dome. Architecturally it's a classic medieval Georgian inscribed-cross (cross-in-square) church, with projecting polygonal apses on the east facade. The exterior carries some carved detail but reads as restrained solidity rather than elaboration — a building rebuilt and repaired so many times that its current form reflects the accumulated pragmatism of many centuries rather than the ambition of any single patron.

The interior is more richly finished than the exterior suggests. The walls carry frescoes, the most prominent of which were painted between 1850 and 1860 by Prince Grigory Gagarin, a Russian artist (and general, later head of the Russian Academy of Arts); he also designed the iconostasis of the period. These 19th-century murals — painted over older Georgian frescoes, some of which were lost in the process — have been criticized by some historians as overly Russified, reflecting the imperial-era tendency to bring Russian Orthodox aesthetics into Georgian churches; but the overall effect is one of warmth and accumulated devotion. In the 1980s, in a partial re-Georgianization, the Georgian artist Levan Tsutskiridze painted new frescoes on parts of the cathedral, and a traditional Georgian stone iconostasis replaced Gagarin's.

The atmosphere in the main body of the cathedral during a service — the chanting, the candlelight, the incense, the movement of worshippers — is one of the more complete immersions in Georgian Orthodox practice available in the city. (Sioni also holds other relics, and its bell tower traces to 1425; it was also, somberly, the place where the Russian imperial manifesto annexing Georgia was first proclaimed in the early 19th century.)

Location and context

Sioni sits at the meeting of several of the main walking routes through the Old Town, which makes it a natural stop on almost any tour of the historic district. The streets around it hold some of the most intact 19th-century commercial architecture in Tbilisi, and the small square in front gives the building room to be seen in its neighborhood. The Anchiskhati Basilica — the oldest surviving church in Tbilisi — is a short walk north; Shardeni Street and its terraces are right alongside; the Bridge of Peace and the riverfront are a few minutes south; and Abanotubani and the sulfur baths lie a short walk east.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: Sioni Street, Old Tbilisi, near the Mtkvari riverfront (in the Upper Kala quarter, by Shardeni Street).
  • Hours: Open daily, from early morning to evening; services are held through the day, and the cathedral is open to visitors outside the most active service periods.
  • Admission: Free.
  • Dress code: Modest dress — shoulders and knees covered; women are expected to cover their heads (scarves are usually available at the entrance). It's an active, much-venerated cathedral, so visit quietly and respectfully, especially around the St. Nino cross and during services.
  • Combine with: The Anchiskhati Basilica, Shardeni Street, the Bridge of Peace and riverfront, Abanotubani, and the wider Old Town — Sioni is at the center of it all.

faq.title: What is Sioni Cathedral?
faq.content: Sioni is one of the oldest and most important churches in Tbilisi — for centuries the principal cathedral of the Georgian Orthodox Church and the seat of its Catholicos-Patriarch, a role it held until Sameba Cathedral was consecrated in 2004. First built in the 6th–7th century and rebuilt many times since (the present building is based on a 13th-century structure, with later changes), it's named after Mount Zion in Jerusalem. Its great draw is the Cross of St. Nino, one of the holiest relics in Georgian Christianity. It's an active church in the heart of the Old Town, free to enter.

faq.title: What is the Cross of St. Nino?
faq.content: It's the grapevine cross of St. Nino, the Cappadocian woman who brought Christianity to Georgia in the early 4th century. By tradition it was made from two lengths of grapevine bound together with strands of her own hair, giving it its distinctive drooping arms — the form from which the Georgian cross, with its slightly bowed horizontal bar, derives. It's venerated at Sioni, kept to the left of the altar in an ornate silver reliquary; the faithful can venerate it directly on certain feast days, and it can be seen behind glass at other times. Sources differ on whether the displayed cross is the original or a replica, so the point is best held lightly. It's one of the primary pilgrimage objects in Tbilisi.

faq.title: How old is the cathedral?
faq.content: The site has had a church since the 6th–7th century (the medieval annals credit Vakhtang Gorgasali in the 5th century, with construction under Prince Guaram around 575). It was destroyed and rebuilt repeatedly over the centuries — rebuilt by David IV the Builder in 1112, then damaged by Jalal ad-Din, the Khwarazmians, Shah Ismail, a 1668 earthquake, and the 1795 Persian invasion, and restored each time. The building you see today is based on the 13th-century version, with significant changes from the 17th to 19th centuries (the dome dates to 1710). So while no single part is fourteen centuries old, the institution and the site are.

faq.title: What's the architecture and interior like?
faq.content: It's a classic medieval Georgian inscribed-cross (cross-in-square) church, built of warm yellow tuff from Bolnisi, with a central dome and projecting polygonal apses — restrained and solid outside. Inside, the prominent frescoes were painted in the 1850s by the Russian artist Prince Grigory Gagarin (over older Georgian frescoes), and have been criticized as overly "Russified"; in the 1980s the Georgian artist Levan Tsutskiridze added new frescoes and a traditional Georgian stone iconostasis replaced Gagarin's. During a service — chanting, candlelight, incense — it offers one of the fullest immersions in Georgian Orthodox practice in the city.

faq.title: How do I visit, and where is it?
faq.content: Sioni is on Sioni Street in the Old Town, near the Mtkvari riverfront and right by Shardeni Street, at the meeting of several main Old Town walking routes. It's free and open daily from morning to evening; as an active, much-venerated cathedral, dress modestly (shoulders and knees covered; women cover their heads, with scarves usually available at the door) and visit quietly, especially around the St. Nino cross and during services. The Anchiskhati Basilica is a short walk north, and the Bridge of Peace and Abanotubani are close by.

tamadaStatue.heroTitle: Tamada (Toastmaster) Statue: Georgia's Spirit of the Feast
tamadaStatue.content:
The Tamada statue stands at the start of Shardeni Street in the Old Town of Tbilisi, capital of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus) — an unpretentious bronze figure of a man raising a drinking horn, easy to walk past and hard to forget once you've stopped to look. It is one of the smaller public sculptures in Tbilisi, and one of the more culturally loaded: a reproduction of one of the most famous artifacts in Georgian archaeology, set in the heart of the city's oldest quarter as a quiet statement about what Georgian culture considers worth commemorating.

What is the Tamada statue?

The Tamada statue is a small bronze sculpture in Tbilisi's Old Town — at the start of Shardeni Street, near Sioni Cathedral — of a tamada, the toastmaster who leads the Georgian feast (the supra). Installed in 2006, it's an enlarged replica of a 7th-century-BC Colchian bronze figurine unearthed at Vani in western Georgia (the original is in the Georgian National Museum). The figure raises a kantsi, the traditional drinking horn, in a toast. It's free, easy to combine with a walk through the Old Town, and one of the most direct expressions in the city of the Georgian culture of hospitality, wine, and the shared table — locals and visitors often rub it for good luck.

An ancient figure

The statue is based on a small bronze figurine discovered at Vani, an ancient site in western Georgia associated with the Colchian civilization, dating to roughly the 7th century BC. The original — a seated man holding a drinking horn in a gesture of offering — is now in the Georgian National Museum, and is one of the most reproduced images in Georgian cultural iconography, turning up on wine labels, tourist materials, and institutional logos across the country. The choice of this figure for a public sculpture (installed in 2006) is deliberate: it connects the living Georgian tradition of the supra and the tamada to a lineage reaching back two and a half thousand years — suggesting that the ritual of the shared feast, and the toastmaster who presides over it, is not a recent habit but something embedded in the deepest layers of Georgian civilization.

The tamada and the supra

The tamada is the toastmaster of the Georgian feast — the person the gathering recognizes to lead the meal through its sequence of toasts and to set the tone of the table. The role is not merely ceremonial. A good tamada composes toasts that are substantive, poetic, and fitted to the occasion and the company — to peace, to the homeland, to absent friends, to the departed, to the guests, to the hosts, to love, to Georgia, to the future. Each toast opens a theme the table then develops, so the supra at its best is a form of collective storytelling and social bonding, presided over by the tamada's judgment and verbal skill. (There are two kinds of supra: the keipi, the celebratory feast — a wedding, say — and the kelekhi, the gathering after a funeral; the tamada leads both, in very different keys.)

The horn the figure raises — the kantsi — is itself meaningful. Made from the horn of an ox or buffalo and, having no base, impossible to set down until it's empty, the kantsi enforces a particular social rhythm: when the horn is passed, it must be finished. That communal rhythm is part of the deliberate structure of the feast, not just a practical quirk. The raised kantsi in the statue's hand isn't only an attribute identifying the figure's role — it's a symbol of the whole framework of Georgian hospitality.

Finding it

The statue's small scale and its busy setting mean it rewards those who know to look for it. It stands at the start of Shardeni Street (and Bambis Rigi), by Sioni Cathedral — in the part of the Old Town where the layers of Tbilisi's social and cultural life are most compressed: the sulfur baths of Abanotubani a short walk away, the wine bars and terraces of Shardeni right alongside, Narikala on the ridge above.
