The tamada in the middle of it raises his horn in a gesture that connects the archaeological past to the living present with a directness larger and more imposing monuments rarely manage. It's a small thing, but a fitting emblem for a city — and a culture — built around the table.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: Start of Jan Shardeni Street (by Bambis Rigi and Sioni Street), Old Tbilisi.
  • Getting there: In the heart of the Old Town — a few minutes' walk from Sioni Cathedral, Shardeni's wine bars, the Bridge of Peace, and Abanotubani.
  • Hours: Open-air and accessible at all times.
  • Admission: Free.
  • Best time: Evening, when the surrounding streets and terraces come alive (it can be crowded then — early or late is quieter for a photo).
  • Tip: Rub it for luck, as locals and visitors do; then carry the spirit of it into a supra — the real thing is a Georgian feast with a real tamada.
  • Combine with: Shardeni Street, Sioni Cathedral, the Rezo Gabriadze clock tower and Anchiskhati, the Bridge of Peace, and Abanotubani — the whole Old Town cluster.

faq.title: What is the Tamada statue?
faq.content: It's a small bronze sculpture in Tbilisi's Old Town — at the start of Shardeni Street, near Sioni Cathedral — of a tamada, the toastmaster who leads the Georgian feast (the supra). Installed in 2006, it's an enlarged replica of a 7th-century-BC Colchian bronze figurine unearthed at Vani in western Georgia (the original is in the Georgian National Museum). The figure raises a kantsi, the traditional drinking horn, mid-toast. It's free, easy to fit into an Old Town walk, and one of the city's most direct symbols of the Georgian culture of hospitality and the shared table.

faq.title: Who or what is a tamada?
faq.content: The tamada is the toastmaster of a Georgian feast — the person the gathering recognizes to lead the meal through its toasts and set the table's tone. It's a role of real skill, not just ceremony: a good tamada composes toasts that are substantive and poetic, fitted to the occasion — to peace, the homeland, absent friends, the departed, the guests, love, Georgia. Each toast opens a theme the table develops, making the supra a kind of collective storytelling. The tamada presides over both kinds of supra — the joyful feast (keipi) and the gathering after a funeral (kelekhi).

faq.title: What's the horn the figure is holding?
faq.content: It's a kantsi, the traditional Georgian drinking horn, made from the horn of an ox or buffalo. Because a horn has no flat base, it can't be set down until it's empty — so when the kantsi is passed, it has to be finished, which sets a particular communal rhythm to the feast. In the statue it's both the attribute that identifies the figure as a tamada and a symbol of the whole framework of Georgian hospitality. You'll see kantsi sold as souvenirs across the country.

faq.title: How old is the original, and where is it?
faq.content: The statue is a modern (2006) enlarged copy; the original is a small bronze figurine from the 7th century BC, discovered at Vani — an ancient site of the Colchian civilization in western Georgia — and now held in the Georgian National Museum. It's one of the most reproduced images in Georgian culture, appearing on wine labels, logos, and tourist materials, precisely because it ties the modern feast tradition to a lineage stretching back two and a half millennia.

faq.title: Where is it, and is it worth a stop?
faq.content: It's at the start of Jan Shardeni Street (by Bambis Rigi and Sioni Street) in the heart of the Old Town, a few minutes from Sioni Cathedral, the Bridge of Peace, and Abanotubani. It's small and easy to miss, but worth a pause — both for the photo (locals and visitors rub it for luck) and for what it represents. It's free and open at any time; evenings are liveliest, though busier for photos. Really, it's best appreciated as a prelude to the real thing: a Georgian supra with an actual tamada.

tbilisiFunicular.heroTitle: Tbilisi Funicular: The Historic Climb up Mtatsminda
tbilisiFunicular.content:
The Tbilisi Funicular has carried passengers up the steep face of Mtatsminda — the "Holy Mountain" above Tbilisi, capital of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus) — since 1905, making it one of the older urban funiculars still running in the region and one of the most enduring fixtures of Tbilisi life. What began as a practical way to reach the forested mountain above the city has become, over more than a century, something closer to an institution — a ride generations of Tbilisians have taken for leisure, for the views, and for the particular pleasure of watching the city fall away below as the cabin climbs.

What is the Tbilisi Funicular?

The Tbilisi Funicular is a historic incline railway that climbs Mount Mtatsminda from near the city center to the summit park, opened in 1905 and fully renovated in 2012. The ride takes three to five minutes in glass-sided cabins, with a middle station at the Mtatsminda Pantheon — the hillside cemetery where many of Georgia's most celebrated writers and public figures are buried, around St. David's Church (Mamadaviti) — and an upper station at Mtatsminda Park, the city's best viewpoint. It runs daily from morning into the late evening, and is paid for with a dedicated Mtatsminda Park card (separate from the city's metro/bus card). Riding up and walking back down to the Pantheon is the classic plan.

The ride

The funicular was fully renovated and modernized in 2012, and the current cabins are comfortable and glass-sided, built to make the most of the views that are the main reason most people ride. The route, though, follows the same line it always has — a steep diagonal climb up the Mtatsminda hillside from the lower station near the city center (on Chonkadze Street) to the summit, with the middle station along the way.

The ascent takes three to five minutes, and the view changes continuously. From the lower station the cabin rises quickly above the rooftops of the streets below, and within the first minute the Mtkvari comes into view, cutting its characteristic bend through the city. As you climb, the panorama widens — the Old Town to the east, Narikala Fortress on its ridge, the Soviet-era residential districts filling the surrounding hills, and on clear days the Greater Caucasus on the northern horizon above the urban spread. By the upper station the full scale of Tbilisi is laid out below in a way no street-level vantage can match. The descent is just as worthwhile, giving a second perspective on the city from a slightly different angle.

The middle station and the Pantheon

The middle station serves the Mtatsminda Pantheon, the hillside cemetery — established in 1929, around St. David's Church (Mamadaviti) — where many of Georgia's most celebrated writers, poets, artists, and public figures are buried. The first to be interred here were the Russian writer Alexander Griboyedov and his Georgian wife Nino Chavchavadze (whose grave draws many visitors). Among the Georgians buried here are Ilia Chavchavadze, the 19th-century writer and national figure known as the "uncrowned king of Georgia" (his grave bears the "Grieving Georgia" sculpture by Iakob Nikoladze); the poet Akaki Tsereteli; the great 20th-century poet Galaktion Tabidze; Vazha-Pshavela; and many other major names in Georgian cultural history — set in forested terraces on the steep hillside, with views through the trees toward the city. The Pantheon (now closed to new burials) is worth a stop in its own right for anyone interested in Georgian literary and cultural history, and the middle station makes it easy to reach without going all the way to the summit. A common plan is to ride to the top for the views, then walk back down to the Pantheon.

Getting there and visiting

  • Lower station: 2 Chonkadze Street, central Tbilisi (a short walk uphill from Rustaveli).
  • Upper station: Mtatsminda Park summit (the city's highest point, ~770 m); middle station at the Mtatsminda Pantheon.
  • Hours: Daily, from morning into the late evening; hours vary by season (later in summer), and the funicular can pause for maintenance or weather. The Pantheon keeps its own, shorter daytime hours.
  • Tickets/payment: A dedicated Mtatsminda Park card is used for the funicular (and the park's rides) — this is the park's own rechargeable card, separate from the city's metro/bus transport card. Buy and load it at the lower station; a one-way ride is in the region of a few lari. (Entry to the park and the Pantheon is free; you pay for the funicular and the rides.)
  • Best time: Late afternoon into the evening, for the light over the city and the lit panorama at night.
  • Tip: Ride up for the summit views, then walk down to the Pantheon (or get off there on the way up). The Pantheon is a solemn, active cemetery — visit it quietly and respectfully.
  • Combine with: Mtatsminda Park at the top (the amusement park, the Ferris wheel, the view); the Pantheon and St. David's Church at the middle; a walk down through the Sololaki/Rustaveli streets at the bottom.

faq.title: What is the Tbilisi Funicular?
faq.content: It's a historic incline railway that climbs Mount Mtatsminda from near the city center to the summit park, opened in 1905 and fully renovated in 2012. The ride takes three to five minutes in glass-sided cabins, with a middle station at the Mtatsminda Pantheon (the writers' cemetery around St. David's Church) and an upper station at Mtatsminda Park — the city's best viewpoint. It runs daily from morning into the late evening. The classic plan is to ride up for the views and walk back down to the Pantheon.

faq.title: How do you pay, and how much is it?
faq.content: The funicular uses a dedicated Mtatsminda Park card — the park's own rechargeable card, which is separate from the city's metro and bus transport card (so it's not the same card you'd use elsewhere in Tbilisi). You buy and load it at the lower station, and a one-way ride costs in the region of a few lari. Entry to the park at the top and to the Pantheon is free; you pay for the funicular itself and for the amusement-park rides.

faq.title: What is the Mtatsminda Pantheon at the middle stop?
faq.content: It's the hillside cemetery, established in 1929 around St. David's Church (Mamadaviti), where many of Georgia's most celebrated writers, artists, and public figures are buried. The first interred here were the Russian writer Alexander Griboyedov and his Georgian wife Nino Chavchavadze; among the Georgians are Ilia Chavchavadze (the "uncrowned king of Georgia," with the famous "Grieving Georgia" sculpture on his grave), Akaki Tsereteli, Galaktion Tabidze, and Vazha-Pshavela. You can get off the funicular at the middle station to visit it; it's a solemn, much-respected place with fine views over the city.

faq.title: What are the views like from the ride?
faq.content: Excellent and continuously changing. As the cabin climbs, the Mtkvari River comes into view with its characteristic bend, then the Old Town, Narikala Fortress on its ridge, the surrounding hills, and on clear days the Greater Caucasus on the northern horizon. By the upper station the whole city is laid out below in a way no street-level viewpoint can match. The descent is just as good, from a slightly different angle — which is why many people ride both ways rather than walking down.

faq.title: What are the hours, and when's best?
faq.content: It runs daily from morning into the late evening, with hours varying by season (later in summer); it can occasionally pause for maintenance or weather, and the Pantheon keeps its own shorter daytime hours. The best time is late afternoon into the evening — for the light over the city and the lit panorama after dark. A good plan: ride up near sunset, take in the summit views, and come back down via the Pantheon.

jumaMosque.heroTitle: Tbilisi Juma Mosque: Where Sunni and Shia Pray Together
jumaMosque.content:
The Juma Mosque sits in the Abanotubani district of Tbilisi, capital of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus), its octagonal minaret rising above the sulfur-bath domes and the Old Town rooftops — in a skyline that also holds Orthodox church cupolas, the ruins of Narikala Fortress, and, in the distance, the golden dome of Sameba. The mosque has been part of this neighborhood for centuries, and its presence within a few hundred meters of Georgian Orthodox churches, an Armenian church, and a synagogue makes Abanotubani one of the most compact demonstrations anywhere in the South Caucasus of the multi-religious urban life Tbilisi has sustained — imperfectly but continuously — across its long history.

What is the Tbilisi Juma Mosque?

The Juma Mosque is the only surviving mosque in Tbilisi, in the Old Town's Abanotubani district at the foot of Narikala. It's known for something rare: Sunni and Shia Muslims pray together in the same space, without separation — one of the very few mosques in the world where the two branches of Islam share a single house of worship. That came about after Tbilisi's other mosque, the Shia "Blue Mosque," was demolished in the Soviet era; a curtain divided the two communities inside the Juma Mosque until 1996, when it was removed. The red-brick building, blending neo-Gothic and Islamic elements, dates in its current form to 1895. It's an active mosque, open to respectful visitors outside prayer times, and free.

A shared mosque

The mosque serves Tbilisi's Muslim community — primarily Azerbaijanis, alongside Georgia's broader Muslim population, and both Sunni and Shia. What makes it remarkable, and what is often cited as one of the more striking facts of religious life in Georgia, is that Sunni and Shia pray together in the same space. In most of the Islamic world the two maintain separate mosques; here they share one.

The reason is a particular piece of Tbilisi's history. The city once had more than one mosque — Sunnis worshipped at the Juma Mosque, Shia at the Blue Mosque nearby. Around 1950–51, the Soviet authorities demolished the Blue Mosque (during work on the Metekhi Bridge), leaving the Shia community with nowhere to pray — so the Juma Mosque took them in. For decades a black curtain divided the prayer hall between the two; in 1996 the curtain was removed, and since then the two branches have prayed side by side. The arrangement reflects both the pragmatic accommodation of a small community in a predominantly Christian country and a genuine coexistence that has few parallels. (The mosque is administered under the Mufti of Azerbaijan.)

History and the building

A mosque has stood at the foot of Narikala since the early 18th century: the Ottomans, who then held Tbilisi, built a Sunni mosque here around 1723–1735. The Persians destroyed it in the 1740s. It was rebuilt in 1846–1851 by the Italian architect Giovanni Scudieri, then rebuilt again in its current red-brick form in 1895, funded by the Azerbaijani philanthropist Zeynalabdin Taghiyev of Baku (who substantially reworked its eastern side); it was restored again in 1998.

The building is constructed of red brick and combines neo-Gothic and Islamic elements — the arched windows along its walls are unusual for a mosque, and an octagonal minaret marks it from a distance. It stands on a steep slope in the dense fabric of Abanotubani, with a madrasa (religious school) alongside. Inside, the arrangement is the standard one — a carpeted prayer hall, a mihrab indicating the direction of Mecca, largely unadorned walls — at an intimate, congregational scale rather than a monumental one. The atmosphere is one of functional devotion rather than architectural spectacle.

The neighborhood

The value of visiting the Juma Mosque is inseparable from where it stands. Walking from Sioni Cathedral — the historic heart of Georgian Orthodox Christianity — through the Old Town to Abanotubani and reaching the mosque within a few minutes makes the point about Tbilisi's religious geography more directly than any description. The sulfur-bath domes, the Orthodox churches, an Armenian church a short distance away, the synagogue, and the mosque's minaret share the same compact urban space, and have for centuries. This coexistence was not always harmonious — the region's history includes periods of conflict between these communities — but it was persistent, and the physical closeness of the religious buildings is the most direct evidence of that persistence.

The mosque is busiest at Friday prayers and during Ramadan and the Eid holidays, when the congregation is largest and the neighborhood takes on a particular character. Outside those times it is open to respectful visitors, and the quiet of a mid-afternoon visit — stepping inside briefly, then back out into the steam and cobblestones of Abanotubani — offers a stillness the busier parts of the Old Town rarely provide.

Visiting

  • Location: Botanikuri (Botanical) Street, Abanotubani, Old Tbilisi — at the foot of Narikala, by the sulfur baths.
  • Hours: Open daily; visitors are welcome outside prayer times. Friday midday prayers draw the largest congregation and may limit visitor access then.
  • Admission: Free.
  • Dress code: Modest dress for all visitors — shoulders and knees covered; women cover their heads; shoes are removed before entering the prayer hall. As an active mosque, visit quietly and respectfully, and avoid visiting during prayers unless you are there to pray.
  • Combine with: The Abanotubani sulfur baths, Sioni Cathedral, the Great Synagogue, Narikala (above), and the Old Town — the mosque is part of the multi-religious Old Town cluster.

faq.title: What is the Tbilisi Juma Mosque?
faq.content: It's the only surviving mosque in Tbilisi, in the Old Town's Abanotubani district at the foot of Narikala Fortress. It's known for a rarity: Sunni and Shia Muslims pray together in the same space — one of the very few mosques in the world where the two branches of Islam share a single house of worship. The red-brick building, blending neo-Gothic and Islamic elements with an octagonal minaret, dates in its current form to 1895. It's an active mosque, free, and open to respectful visitors outside prayer times.

faq.title: Why do Sunni and Shia pray together there?
faq.content: Because of Tbilisi's history. The city once had two mosques — Sunnis at the Juma Mosque, Shia at the nearby "Blue Mosque." Around 1950–51 the Soviet authorities demolished the Blue Mosque (during work on the Metekhi Bridge), leaving the Shia community with nowhere to pray, so the Juma Mosque took them in. For decades a black curtain divided the prayer hall between the two branches; in 1996 it was removed, and since then they have prayed side by side. It's a genuine and unusual example of coexistence.

faq.title: How old is it, and who built it?
faq.content: A mosque has stood here since the early 18th century, when the Ottomans built one around 1723–1735 at the foot of Narikala. The Persians destroyed it in the 1740s; it was rebuilt in 1846–1851 by the Italian architect Giovanni Scudieri, then rebuilt again in its current red-brick form in 1895, funded by the Azerbaijani philanthropist Zeynalabdin Taghiyev of Baku, and restored once more in 1998. So the building you see is essentially the 1895 mosque.

faq.title: Can non-Muslims visit, and what should I wear?
faq.content: Yes — it's open to respectful visitors outside prayer times. Dress modestly: shoulders and knees covered, women cover their heads, and shoes are removed before entering the prayer hall. It's busiest at Friday midday prayers and during Ramadan and the Eid holidays, when visitor access may be limited; a quiet mid-afternoon visit is the easiest. As an active place of worship, keep your visit quiet and unobtrusive.

faq.title: Where is it, and what's nearby?
faq.content: It's on Botanikuri (Botanical) Street in the Abanotubani district of the Old Town, at the foot of Narikala by the sulfur baths. That puts it at the heart of Tbilisi's multi-religious quarter: within a few minutes' walk are the sulfur baths, Sioni Cathedral and other Orthodox churches, an Armenian church, and the Great Synagogue — a cluster of faiths in one compact space that's one of the most direct expressions of Tbilisi's long multi-religious history.

tbilisiZoo.heroTitle: Tbilisi Zoo: Georgia's Oldest Zoological Park
tbilisiZoo.content:
The Tbilisi Zoo is the oldest and largest zoological park in Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus), founded in 1927 and set in the Vere River valley in central Tbilisi, within easy reach of the city center. It's primarily a destination for families with children and for travelers with a genuine interest in animals, rather than a standard stop on a first-time Tbilisi itinerary — and approaching it with that understanding gives the visit the right frame.

What is the Tbilisi Zoo?

The Tbilisi Zoo, founded in 1927, is Georgia's oldest and largest zoo, in the Vere River valley in central Tbilisi. It holds a range of species — Caucasian endemics alongside African and Asian animals, plus a marine aquarium (the first in the Caucasus, opened 2007) — across a green, hilly site. Its recent history is marked by the catastrophic 2015 Vere River flood, which killed three keepers, drowned or scattered much of the collection, and sent animals into the streets of Tbilisi; the zoo lost nearly half its animals and has been rebuilding since. It's an unpretentious, genuinely local place — where Tbilisi families spend weekend mornings — at a very modest cost, best suited to families and animal-lovers rather than as a marquee sight.

A long and uneven history

The zoo's history is long and uneven. At its Soviet-era peak it housed well over a thousand animals and drew hundreds of thousands of visitors a year, one of the more significant zoological institutions in the Caucasus. The post-Soviet economic collapse of the 1990s hit it hard, and the institution spent much of that decade in serious difficulty before a modest recovery in the 2000s brought improvements and new facilities — including the marine aquarium, the first in the Caucasus, in 2007.

A relocation has been discussed for years: in 2012 the city decided to move the zoo from its constrained, flood-prone central site to a larger new complex by the Tbilisi Sea on the city's eastern edge. Funding shortfalls repeatedly delayed it, and the zoo was still at its old location when the flood came. The move remains a long-planned but unresolved question; for now, the zoo is still in the Vere valley.

The 2015 flood

The defining event of the zoo's recent history came on 13–14 June 2015, when the Vere River — normally a small stream — flooded catastrophically after exceptionally heavy rain. The floodwaters tore through the low-lying zoo, killing many animals and destroying enclosures. Several large animals — a hippopotamus, big cats, bears, wolves — escaped into the streets of Tbilisi, in scenes that briefly drew worldwide attention; the hippo was cornered in a city square and tranquilized. Some animals were recaptured; others were shot by security forces during the emergency (a decision the zoo publicly questioned), and on 17 June a white tiger still on the loose killed a man before being shot.

The human cost was grave. Three zookeepers died in the flood — among them a keeper who had stayed to try to protect the animals — and the wider disaster killed around twenty people across the city. The zoo lost more than 300 animals, nearly half its population, and reopened about three months later in much-reduced form. Rebuilding has continued since, but the gap between the zoo's pre-2015 scale and its present operation remains real and visible.

One animal became a symbol of the recovery: Begi, the hippo photographed wandering the flooded streets, survived, was returned, and still lives at the zoo — an image that moved the city and now stands for its rebuilding.

The zoo today

The current collection covers a reasonable range across several departments — predators and primates, birds, ungulates, and an exotarium of reptiles, amphibians, and fish. Caucasian endemic species — mountain ungulates and regional birds — sit alongside African and Asian animals, giving the collection both local natural heritage and broader range; the aquarium remains one of its more distinctive features by regional standards.

The setting is one of its genuine strengths: the Vere-valley terrain gives the park a hilly, green character, with mature trees for shade in the warmer months and paths that wind through the landscape more like a natural park than a flat conventional zoo — though the usable site is now fairly compact, hemmed in by the city around it. The atmosphere is unpretentious and local: this is where Tbilisi families spend weekend mornings rather than a polished international attraction, and the absence of big tourist crowds gives it a relaxed quality.

Visit with honest expectations. This is an institution still in recovery and development rather than a showpiece, and the gap from its pre-2015 scale is real. But for travelers with children, or with a genuine interest in the animals — and in the Caucasian species in particular — a morning here is a pleasant, genuinely local experience at very modest cost.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: Kostava Street, in the Vere River valley, central Tbilisi (a short distance from the center; the Delisi or Station Square metro and a short taxi are easiest).
  • Hours: Longer in summer (roughly morning to evening) and shorter in winter; open daily. Check current seasonal hours before visiting.
  • Admission: Modest (low ticket price; reduced for children).
  • Website / email: zoo.ge / zoo@zoo.ge
  • Best for: families with children and animal-lovers; a relaxed half-morning rather than a headline sight.
  • Combine with: the nearby Vere/Vake green spaces and Mziuri Park (where there's now a monument to Begi the hippo), or a wider day in the Vake–Saburtalo side of the city.

faq.title: What is the Tbilisi Zoo?
faq.content: It's Georgia's oldest and largest zoological park, founded in 1927, in the Vere River valley in central Tbilisi. It holds a range of species — Caucasian endemics alongside African and Asian animals, plus a marine aquarium (the first in the Caucasus, opened in 2007) — across a green, hilly site. It's an unpretentious, local place, best suited to families with children and to travelers with a genuine interest in animals, rather than a marquee stop on a first Tbilisi itinerary.

faq.title: What happened in the 2015 flood?
faq.content: On 13–14 June 2015 the Vere River flooded catastrophically after heavy rain, tearing through the low-lying zoo. Many animals were killed, and several — a hippopotamus, big cats, bears, wolves — escaped into the streets of Tbilisi, drawing worldwide attention; some were recaptured, others were shot during the emergency. Three zookeepers died, and the wider flood killed around twenty people across the city. The zoo lost more than 300 animals — nearly half its population — and reopened about three months later in much-reduced form. It has been rebuilding since.

faq.title: Has the zoo moved, or is it being relocated?
faq.content: It's still at its original Vere-valley site in central Tbilisi. A relocation to a larger new complex by the Tbilisi Sea, on the city's eastern edge, has been planned since 2012 — partly because the central site is constrained and flood-prone — but funding shortfalls have repeatedly delayed it, and it remains unresolved. So for now, you'll find the zoo where it has always been.

faq.title: What animals are there, and what's the setting like?
faq.content: The collection spans predators and primates, birds, ungulates, and an exotarium of reptiles, amphibians, and fish, with Caucasian endemic species (mountain ungulates, regional birds) alongside African and Asian animals, plus the marine aquarium. The setting is a genuine strength — a hilly, green, tree-shaded valley that feels more like a natural park than a flat zoo, though the usable site is fairly compact now. One famous resident is Begi, the hippo who survived the 2015 flood and still lives there.

faq.title: Is it worth visiting, and who is it for?
faq.content: It's best for families with children and for animal-lovers, especially anyone interested in Caucasian wildlife — a relaxed, genuinely local half-morning at very modest cost, rather than a headline sight. Visit with honest expectations: it's an institution still recovering and rebuilding after 2015, not a showpiece facility. If you want a polished international zoo, this isn't that; if you want a green, unpretentious, local experience with kids, it works well.

georgianMuseumFineArts.heroTitle: Georgian Museum of Fine Arts: A Private Home for Georgian Art
georgianMuseumFineArts.content:
The Georgian Museum of Fine Arts on Rustaveli Avenue is one of the newest and most architecturally distinctive buildings on the main boulevard of Tbilisi, capital of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus) — and the story behind it is as interesting as the collection it holds. Opened in 2018, it is the only building in Georgia constructed specifically to house art exhibitions. It's a private institution, founded by Dr. George (Gia) Jokhtaberidze and Manana Shevardnadze — founders of Magticom, the country's largest telecommunications company — who began collecting Georgian art in the 1990s.

What is the Georgian Museum of Fine Arts?

The Georgian Museum of Fine Arts is a private art museum on Rustaveli Avenue, directly opposite the Parliament of Georgia, opened in 2018 — the only building in the country purpose-built for art. It houses the private family collection of its founders, Gia Jokhtaberidze and Manana Shevardnadze (founders of Magticom): over 3,500 works by almost 80 Georgian artists from the last 70 years, spanning the late Soviet era, independence, and the present. The contemporary glass-and-steel building, marked by a large eagle sculpture on its facade, sits on the site of the former Artists' House. It's one of the best places anywhere to see modern and contemporary Georgian art that the wider world rarely saw while it was being made.

The founding story

The impulse behind the museum shapes the whole character of the collection. Much of this art was made during the Soviet period, behind the Iron Curtain, and so was little known internationally. Jokhtaberidze set out to collect, restore, and preserve Georgian art that might otherwise have been scattered or lost. The original intention was to donate the collection to state museums, but the post-Soviet economic collapse left those institutions unable to take it on — and so the decision was made to build a dedicated home for it instead. The result functions at once as a cultural institution, a preservation project, and a statement about the value of Georgian artistic heritage. (Manana Shevardnadze is the daughter of Eduard Shevardnadze, the former Georgian president and Soviet foreign minister — one strand of the personal history behind this privately built national collection.)

The building

The complex occupies a prominent site directly opposite the Parliament of Georgia, on land where the Artists' House once stood before its destruction during the civil conflict of the early 1990s (an earlier National Gallery had opened on this spot in 1920). The facade is defined by a large eagle sculpture at its center — based on ancient miniature bronze eagle figurines (around the 2nd–1st century BC) found in the archaeological excavations at Vani in western Georgia — which gives the exterior a distinctive identity against the 19th-century architecture around it. Inside, the galleries are organized across several floors with 31 exhibition halls and about 7,000 square meters of exhibition space, shown in a stainless-steel-and-glass interior (the central glass staircase is a memorable, slightly vertiginous feature), giving the works room to breathe.

The architecture is contemporary and unambiguous about its modernity — glass, steel, and clean geometric forms that make no attempt to blend with the historic context. That's deliberate: it reflects the museum's identity as an institution oriented toward the present and future of Georgian art, and the contrast with the 19th-century buildings on either side is part of the statement.

The collection

The permanent collection holds over 3,500 artworks by almost 80 Georgian artists, spanning the last 70 years — roughly 1945 to the present, across the late Soviet era, the turbulent years of independence, and the contemporary moment. This focus means the collection documents a period of Georgian art that was largely invisible to the international art world while it was being made, produced under conditions — political constraint, material scarcity, cultural isolation — that shaped its character in ways now of real historical as well as aesthetic interest.

The Soviet-period works carry the particular quality of art made within strict ideological limits, by painters who were at once complying with those limits and finding ways to say something personal within them. The strategies Georgian artists used to keep their integrity under socialist realism — landscape, religious symbolism, formally conservative styles that carried more subversive content — run through the collection and reward attentive looking. The post-independence works show how quickly Georgian practice diversified once those constraints lifted, as artists engaged with international contemporary movements while keeping their connection to the specifically Georgian visual traditions that had sustained them. The collection takes in painting, sculpture, and graphic work, and a temporary-exhibition program brings further contemporary work into the galleries alongside it.

Location and surroundings

The museum sits at one of the most culturally dense points on Rustaveli Avenue — the Parliament of Georgia directly opposite, the Kashveti Church of St. George immediately adjacent, and the Rustaveli National Theatre a short walk east. The attached Art House complex includes restaurants, cafés, and other spaces that extend the visit beyond the galleries and make it a destination in its own right, whatever is showing.

Getting there and visiting

  • Address: 7 Rustaveli Avenue, central Tbilisi (Rustaveli metro; on Rustaveli Avenue opposite the Parliament).
  • Hours: Tuesday–Sunday, roughly late morning to early evening; closed Mondays. (Check current hours before visiting.)
  • Admission: A standard museum ticket (reduced rates typically available).
  • Phone: +995 591 680 000
  • Website: finearts.ge
  • Best for: anyone interested in modern and contemporary Georgian art, Soviet-era and post-independence painting, or distinctive contemporary architecture.
  • Combine with: a walk along Rustaveli Avenue — Kashveti Church next door, the Parliament opposite, the Rustaveli Theatre and the Opera nearby, and the Georgian National Museum further along.

faq.title: What is the Georgian Museum of Fine Arts?
faq.content: It's a private art museum on Rustaveli Avenue in Tbilisi, directly opposite the Parliament of Georgia, opened in 2018 — the only building in the country purpose-built to house art exhibitions. It holds the private family collection of its founders, Gia Jokhtaberidze and Manana Shevardnadze (founders of Magticom, Georgia's largest telecom): over 3,500 works by almost 80 Georgian artists from the last 70 years, spanning the late Soviet era, independence, and the present. It's one of the best places to see modern and contemporary Georgian art.

faq.title: Why was it built, and who founded it?
faq.content: It grew out of the private collection of Gia Jokhtaberidze and Manana Shevardnadze, who began collecting Georgian art in the 1990s — much of it Soviet-era work that was little known internationally because of the Iron Curtain. The aim was to collect, restore, and preserve art that might otherwise have been scattered. The collection was originally meant to be donated to state museums, but the post-Soviet economic collapse left them unable to accept it, so the founders built a dedicated home for it instead. (Manana Shevardnadze is the daughter of former Georgian president Eduard Shevardnadze.)

faq.title: What's the building like, and what's the eagle?
faq.content: It's a contemporary glass-and-steel building, deliberately modern against the 19th-century boulevard around it, with about 7,000 square meters of exhibition space across 31 halls and a striking central glass staircase. The large eagle sculpture on its facade — the museum's symbol — is based on ancient miniature bronze eagle figurines (around the 2nd–1st century BC) found in the archaeological excavations at Vani in western Georgia. It stands on the site of the former Artists' House, destroyed in the early-1990s civil conflict.

faq.title: What's in the collection?
faq.content: Over 3,500 works by almost 80 Georgian artists, spanning roughly 1945 to the present — painting, sculpture, and graphic art across the late Soviet period, the years around independence, and the contemporary moment. The Soviet-era works are especially interesting for the ways artists worked within (and quietly around) the constraints of socialist realism; the later works show Georgian art diversifying rapidly after independence. A temporary-exhibition program adds further contemporary work alongside the permanent collection.

faq.title: Where is it, and what's nearby?
faq.content: It's at 7 Rustaveli Avenue, directly opposite the Parliament of Georgia, with the Kashveti Church of St. George immediately next door and the Rustaveli National Theatre a short walk east — one of the densest cultural stretches of the boulevard. The attached Art House complex has restaurants and cafés. It's open Tuesday to Sunday (closed Mondays); check current hours before visiting. The Opera and the Georgian National Museum are both within an easy walk along Rustaveli.

nationalCentreOfManuscripts.heroTitle: Korneli Kekelidze Georgian National Centre of Manuscripts
nationalCentreOfManuscripts.content:
The Korneli Kekelidze Georgian National Centre of Manuscripts is one of the most significant cultural institutions in Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus) and one of the most important repositories of medieval written heritage in the Caucasus. In central Tbilisi, it holds tens of thousands of manuscripts, rare books, and archival documents spanning more than a millennium of Georgian literary, religious, and intellectual history. It is named in honor of Korneli Kekelidze (1879–1962), the philologist and literary historian whose work established the systematic study of the Georgian manuscript tradition as a scholarly discipline. The institution is at once a leading research center for Georgian medieval literature and paleography and a place where the public can encounter the physical reality of Georgia's written heritage — something no digital reproduction fully replaces.

What is the Centre of Manuscripts?

The Korneli Kekelidze Georgian National Centre of Manuscripts is Georgia's principal repository and research institute for manuscripts and early written heritage, in central Tbilisi. Founded in 1958 (and named for the philologist Korneli Kekelidze in 1962), it holds around 170,000 items — several thousand Georgian manuscripts dating from the 5th century onward, plus manuscripts in Greek, Arabic, Persian, Armenian, Hebrew and other languages, early printed books, and archives. Part of its holdings are inscribed on UNESCO's Memory of the World Register. It is primarily a research institution rather than a conventional museum, with rotating public exhibitions of selected treasures. For travelers interested in the history of writing, medieval Christian art, or the intellectual history of the Caucasus, it offers direct access to primary sources of real age and significance.

Why manuscripts matter in Georgia

To see why an institution devoted to manuscripts carries the weight it does here, it helps to understand the place of writing in Georgian history. The Georgian alphabet — often cited as one of only a small number (around fourteen) of alphabetic scripts in use in the world, and developed for the Georgian language rather than adapted from another — took shape around the 4th–5th century, almost certainly tied to the Christianization of the country and the need for religious texts in Georgian; the earliest surviving examples date to the 5th century. (The three historical Georgian scripts are together recognized by UNESCO as a living writing culture.) From then on, manuscript production became central to Georgian monastic life, and the monasteries — both within Georgia and in the Georgian communities established in Palestine, Sinai, Mount Athos, and elsewhere — were the primary sites of literary and intellectual work for the next thousand years.

These were not only religious texts, though religious writing forms a large part of the collection. They included historical chronicles, poetry, philosophy, science, medicine, and translations from Greek, Arabic, Persian, and Armenian — a record of Georgian scholars' engagement with the wider world of late-antique and medieval thought. Through Georgia's repeated invasions and upheavals, manuscripts were among the objects most deliberately preserved and most deliberately destroyed, and the survival of much of this heritage is partly luck and partly the effort communities put into protecting their written culture under extreme conditions. Many manuscripts now in the Centre came to Tbilisi from the remote mountain monasteries of Svaneti, Racha, and other highland regions, where they had been kept for centuries precisely because those places were hard for invaders to reach.

The collection

The Centre holds around 170,000 items in all — several thousand Georgian manuscripts alongside a substantial body of manuscripts in Greek, Arabic, Persian, Armenian, Hebrew, Syriac, and other languages, reflecting the multilingual character of Georgian intellectual culture. The Georgian manuscripts run from the earliest surviving examples of the script (5th century), through the illuminated productions of the Georgian Golden Age in the 12th–13th centuries, to later medieval and early-modern texts (a large number are palimpsests — reused parchment whose erased under-layers often preserve otherwise-lost texts).

The collection also includes landmark early printed books: the first printed Georgian book, a Georgian-Italian dictionary produced in Rome in 1629, and the first books printed in Georgia after King Vakhtang VI established a press in Tbilisi in 1709 (a Gospel in 1709, Psalms in 1711, and the first printed edition of Rustaveli's poem in 1712).

The illuminated manuscripts are among the most visually striking items — Georgian manuscript illumination developed its own aesthetic over the centuries, and the finest examples (decorated Gospel books, illustrated hagiographies, calligraphic Psalters) stand among the significant productions of medieval Christian art: the pigments, the gold leaf, the calligraphy, and the decorative programs all reflect the resources and ambition of the monastic scriptoria that made them.

Beyond the religious texts, the collection holds material central to the history of Georgian literature — manuscript copies of Shota Rustaveli's 12th-century epic The Knight in the Panther's Skin, the foundational text of Georgian literary culture, survive in various forms and dates, and comparing these copies and their variants is part of the philological work pursued at the Centre. Historical chronicles, medical and astronomical texts, legal documents, and the private archives of major Georgian writers (Ilia Chavchavadze, Vazha-Pshavela, and others) fill in the picture of what Georgian intellectual life actually encompassed.

A working research institution

The Centre functions first as a scholarly research institute: most of its work — conservation, cataloguing, digitization, and academic study — happens behind the scenes of any public visit. Georgian and international scholars use its reading rooms for research into medieval Georgian literature, history, paleography, and art history, and the institution publishes on the collection. That academic character gives it a particular atmosphere — quiet, purposeful, oriented toward the long work of preservation and understanding rather than toward visitor experience — that sets it apart from museums built primarily for the public.

A digitization program is gradually making parts of the collection available online, widening access for scholars who cannot travel to Tbilisi. For those who do come in person, though, being in the presence of the physical objects — the parchment a monk prepared and wrote on a thousand years ago, the pigments mixed and applied in a mountain scriptorium — carries a weight that digital access cannot match.

What visitors can see

Public access is built around the Centre's exhibition spaces, which show a rotating selection from the collection with context on the manuscripts, their origins, their content, and how they were made and preserved. The exhibitions are designed to make the collection's significance accessible without a specialist background — explaining the development of the Georgian script, the role of the monasteries, and the circumstances under which particular texts were created.

The illuminated manuscripts on display give the most immediate impact — their colors often still vivid after centuries, their calligraphy rewarding close attention. The scientific and medical manuscripts show how broad the monasteries' intellectual reach was: these were centers of learning in a comprehensive sense, not purely devotional institutions. The documentary materials — records, legal texts, correspondence — give a more concrete kind of access to the medieval world, closer to the daily reality of governance, commerce, and social life.

Because the Centre is primarily a research institution, check current exhibition arrangements and opening hours before visiting — these vary, and general access is not always available on a drop-in basis. Where a guided visit is available, it adds a great deal, supplying the scholarly context the manuscripts need to be fully understood.

Why it's worth it

For travelers interested in the history of writing, the development of alphabets, medieval Christian art, or the intellectual history of the Caucasus, the Centre offers something genuinely rare — direct access to primary sources of considerable age in a country whose written culture is less internationally known than it deserves. Georgia's manuscript tradition is not a footnote to the main story of medieval European or Middle Eastern intellectual history but a distinct, substantial contribution to it — which is exactly why parts of it sit on UNESCO's Memory of the World Register — and this is where that contribution is most fully represented and carefully kept.

It isn't a passive or entertainment-oriented visit; it asks for engagement and some prior curiosity. But for the right visitor — and there are more of them than the institution's low tourist profile suggests — an hour here gives a depth of perspective on Georgian civilization that the architecture and the wine culture, for all their appeal, can't provide on their own.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: Merab Aleksidze Street, Saburtalo, central Tbilisi (a short way from the center; metro plus a short walk or taxi).
  • Best for: travelers interested in the history of writing and alphabets, medieval manuscripts and illumination, and the intellectual history of Georgia and the Caucasus.
  • Tip: ask about a guided visit, which adds the scholarly context the manuscripts need.
  • Combine with: the Georgian National Museum and the other Rustaveli cultural institutions, or pair it thematically with the highland regions (Svaneti, Racha) and monasteries (Gelati) whose manuscripts the Centre preserves.

faq.title: What is the Georgian National Centre of Manuscripts?
faq.content: It's Georgia's principal repository and research institute for manuscripts and early written heritage, in central Tbilisi. Founded in 1958 (and named for the philologist Korneli Kekelidze in 1962), it holds around 170,000 items — several thousand Georgian manuscripts dating from the 5th century onward, plus manuscripts in Greek, Arabic, Persian, Armenian, Hebrew and other languages, early printed books, and archives. Part of its holdings are on UNESCO's Memory of the World Register. It's primarily a research institution, with rotating public exhibitions of selected treasures.

faq.title: Why is Georgia's manuscript tradition significant?
faq.content: Writing has been central to Georgian culture for over 1,500 years. The Georgian alphabet — often cited as one of only a small number of alphabetic scripts in use in the world, and made for the Georgian language rather than adapted from another — emerged around the 4th–5th century, tied to the country's Christianization. Manuscript production then became central to Georgian monastic life, both in Georgia and in Georgian communities abroad (Sinai, Mount Athos, Jerusalem). The result is a deep, multilingual written heritage — chronicles, poetry, philosophy, science, and translations — that preserves material lost in other languages, which is why parts of it are UNESCO-listed.

faq.title: What's in the collection?
faq.content: Around 170,000 items in all. The Georgian manuscripts run from the earliest surviving examples of the script (5th century), through the illuminated Gospels of the Golden Age (12th–13th centuries), to later texts — including many palimpsests, whose erased under-layers often preserve otherwise-lost works. There are also landmark early printed books: the first printed Georgian book (a Georgian-Italian dictionary, Rome, 1629) and the first books printed in Georgia after Vakhtang VI's press opened in Tbilisi in 1709. The collection includes manuscript copies of Rustaveli's The Knight in the Panther's Skin and the archives of major Georgian writers.

faq.title: Can I visit, and what will I see?
faq.content: Yes, but check first. Public access is built around rotating exhibitions of selected items, with context on the script, the monasteries, and how the manuscripts were made and preserved. The illuminated manuscripts give the most immediate visual impact — colors still vivid after centuries. Because the Centre is mainly a research institution, exhibition access and hours vary and drop-in access isn't guaranteed, so confirm current arrangements before going; a guided visit, where available, adds a great deal.

faq.title: Who is it for, and how is it different from other museums?
faq.content: It's for travelers genuinely interested in the history of writing and alphabets, medieval manuscripts and illumination, or the intellectual history of the Caucasus. It's not an entertainment-oriented attraction — it's quiet, scholarly, and asks for some curiosity and engagement. But for the right visitor it gives a depth of perspective on Georgian civilization that the famous architecture and wine culture can't provide alone — a direct encounter with primary sources a thousand years old.

museumHistoryGeorgianJews.heroTitle: Museum of History of Georgian Jews (David Baazov Museum)
museumHistoryGeorgianJews.content:
The Museum of History of Georgian Jews — named after David Baazov, one of the most significant figures in the modern history of the community — occupies a modest building in the Old Town of Tbilisi, capital of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus), and tells one of the more remarkable stories in the long history of the Jewish diaspora. The community it documents has lived in Georgia for around 2,600 years — one of the oldest Jewish communities anywhere outside the Land of Israel — and what sets its story apart is the long, largely peaceful coexistence of Georgian Jews with the Christian majority, relatively free of the systematic persecution that defined Jewish life in much of Europe and beyond. That makes the museum's story genuinely different from most Jewish-history museums elsewhere.

What is the David Baazov Museum?

The David Baazov Museum of History of Jews of Georgia is the principal museum of Jewish history and culture in the country, in the Old Town of Tbilisi (near Jerusalem Square), housed in a former dome-shaped synagogue. It tells the ~2,600-year story of the Georgian Jewish community — one of the world's oldest Jewish diasporas — and, above all, its remarkably peaceful relationship with the Georgian Christian majority. Through religious objects, ethnographic items, manuscripts, documents, and photographs, it traces the community from its ancient origins through the Soviet decades to the mass emigration to Israel. The museum is itself part of the story: founded in the 1930s, closed under Stalin, and reopened after independence. It's a modest but moving museum, best for travelers interested in Jewish history and the religious diversity of the Caucasus.

David Baazov

The museum is named after David Baazov (1883–1947), a rabbi and the leading figure of the Zionist movement in Georgia. He founded the first Georgian-Jewish newspaper (Ebraelis Khma, "The Voice of the Jew") in 1918, helped organize the All-Jewish Congress in Tbilisi, established Jewish schools across the country, and helped launch the first major wave of Aliyah — emigration to the Land of Israel — from Georgia.

His life ran straight through the upheavals of the early 20th century: the end of the Russian Empire, the brief Georgian independence of 1918–1921, and the Sovietization that followed. Under Soviet rule his work became dangerous. Jewish cultural institutions were shut down, and during the Great Purge of 1938 both his sons were arrested — one, the writer Gerzel (Herzl) Baazov, was executed. Baazov himself was then arrested and sentenced to death for "Zionist activities," a sentence commuted to exile in Siberia. He returned to Georgia in 1945 and died in 1947. The museum named for him is both a tribute to that life and the institutional home for the community he served.

The museum's own history

The museum's story mirrors the community's 20th century. It was founded in 1932–1933 as the Jewish Historic-Ethnographic Museum — one of the earliest museums of its kind anywhere — and through the 1930s its staff ran expeditions across Georgia to assemble a large collection of artifacts. Then, in 1951, amid the antisemitic campaigns of the late-Stalin Soviet Union, it was closed. It stayed closed for four decades, reopening only in 1992, after Georgian independence, when it was renamed for David Baazov; the building — a former early-20th-century dome-shaped synagogue — was renovated in 2013–14. That arc, from foundation to forced closure to reopening, is itself a compressed history of Jewish life in Soviet and post-Soviet Georgia.

The collection

The collection is modest in scale but genuine in character — religious objects, personal artifacts, documents, and ethnographic material that together build a picture of Jewish life in Georgia across the centuries. Manuscripts and texts, some of considerable age, reflect the community's own traditions: Georgian Jews are a distinct, ancient community with their own customs, who spoke Judeo-Georgian (a blend of Georgian and Hebrew) and developed practices that set them apart from the Ashkenazi world. Synagogue and ceremonial items give physical form to that religious life across periods.

The ethnographic material — traditional clothing, household objects, tools — documents the everyday life of Georgian Jewish families in the 19th and early 20th centuries, when the community was most numerous and widely spread across the country (it peaked at around 80,000 in the 1960s–70s). Photographs trace the community through the Soviet decades, when Jewish religious and cultural life faced the same suppression applied to all faiths in the USSR, and into the post-independence period, when emigration to Israel sharply reduced a community that had been present in Georgia for over two and a half thousand years. Among the museum's recurring themes is one that surprises many visitors: the role Georgian Jews played in the early spread of Christianity in Georgia (the Jewish community of Mtskheta features in the story of St. Nino and Georgia's conversion).

The broader story

The museum exists partly to make visible a history that gets relatively little attention in the standard narratives of Georgian culture. The Jewish community's long coexistence with the Christian majority is a genuine phenomenon — not free of all tension at every point, but marked by a degree of mutual tolerance and exchange that is historically unusual, and which the museum makes its central theme. Georgian Jews took on elements of Georgian language, culture, and custom while keeping their religious distinctiveness, producing a Georgian-Jewish identity that is culturally specific and historically significant. The museum documents this without romanticizing it, presenting the story with a modest, unhurried seriousness.

A visit suits travelers with a genuine interest in Jewish history, in the religious diversity of the Caucasus, or in Georgia's minority communities. It is not a large or technically sophisticated museum, and anyone expecting the polished experience of a major national institution will find it modest. Those who come with the right expectations will find something more valuable — a direct, authentic encounter with a history that is both distinctly Georgian and part of a much larger story of Jewish survival and adaptation.

Getting there and visiting

  • Address: 3 Anton Katalikosi Street, Old Tbilisi — near Jerusalem Square, in the historic Jewish quarter (a short walk from Liberty Square and the Great Synagogue).
  • Hours: Tuesday–Sunday (closed Mondays), roughly late morning to evening. (Check current hours before visiting.)
  • Admission: A small ticket fee (a few lari; reduced for students and children).
  • Phone: +995 32 298 59 92
  • Best for: travelers interested in Jewish history, Georgian-Jewish coexistence, and the multi-religious history of the Old Town.
  • Combine with: the Great Synagogue and the historic Jewish quarter nearby, and the wider multi-religious Old Town — the Juma Mosque, Sioni Cathedral, and the churches — within an easy walk.

faq.title: What is the David Baazov Museum?
faq.content: It's the principal museum of Jewish history and culture in Georgia, in the Old Town of Tbilisi (near Jerusalem Square), housed in a former dome-shaped synagogue. It tells the roughly 2,600-year story of the Georgian Jewish community — one of the world's oldest Jewish diasporas — and, above all, its remarkably peaceful relationship with the Georgian Christian majority. Through religious objects, ethnographic items, manuscripts, and photographs, it traces the community from its ancient origins through the Soviet decades to the mass emigration to Israel. It's a modest but moving museum.

faq.title: Who was David Baazov?
faq.content: A rabbi (1883–1947) and the leading figure of the Zionist movement in Georgia. He founded the first Georgian-Jewish newspaper in 1918, established Jewish schools, and helped launch the first major wave of emigration (Aliyah) from Georgia to the Land of Israel. Under Soviet rule his work became dangerous: during the Great Purge of 1938 both his sons were arrested and one was executed, and Baazov himself was sentenced to death for "Zionist activities," commuted to exile in Siberia. He returned to Georgia in 1945 and died in 1947. The museum is named in his honor.

faq.title: How old is the Georgian Jewish community?
faq.content: Around 2,600 years — one of the oldest Jewish communities anywhere outside the Land of Israel. Tradition traces it to the Babylonian exile (after 586 BC), with later waves arriving in the centuries that followed. What makes its history unusual is that Georgian Jews lived for most of those centuries largely free of the systematic antisemitism that marked Jewish life in much of Europe and the Middle East — a long, broadly peaceful coexistence with the Georgian Christian majority that is the museum's central theme. The community peaked at around 80,000 in the 1960s–70s before most emigrated to Israel.

faq.title: What's in the collection, and what's the museum's own history?
faq.content: The collection includes religious and ceremonial objects, manuscripts and documents, ethnographic material (traditional clothing, household items), and photographs documenting Georgian Jewish life across the centuries — including the distinctive customs and the Judeo-Georgian language of this community. The museum itself has a dramatic history: founded in 1932–33 as one of the earliest Jewish museums anywhere, closed in 1951 during the antisemitic campaigns of the late-Stalin USSR, and reopened in 1992 after Georgian independence, in a renovated former dome-shaped synagogue.

faq.title: Where is it, and what's nearby?
faq.content: It's at 3 Anton Katalikosi Street in the Old Town, near Jerusalem Square in the historic Jewish quarter, a short walk from Liberty Square. It's open Tuesday to Sunday (closed Mondays); check current hours before visiting. It pairs naturally with the nearby Great Synagogue and the historic Jewish quarter, and more broadly with the multi-religious Old Town — the Juma Mosque, Sioni Cathedral, and the churches are all within an easy walk.

georgianNationalMuseum.heroTitle: Georgian National Museum (Simon Janashia Museum): Where to Begin in Georgia
georgianNationalMuseum.content:
The Georgian National Museum on Rustaveli Avenue is the country's principal institution for history and culture, and for travelers arriving in Tbilisi, capital of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus), with limited prior knowledge of the country, it is the best place to begin. A few hours here, before heading out to the monasteries, the wine regions, or the mountains, gives everything else a context and depth that would otherwise take much longer to build.

What is the Georgian National Museum?

The Georgian National Museum is an umbrella organization (formed in 2004) of more than a dozen museums and institutions, and its main historical building is the Simon Janashia Museum of Georgia at 3 Rustaveli Avenue — the country's oldest museum, with roots back to 1852. Across several floors it tells Georgia's story from prehistory to the 20th century: the Dmanisi hominins (~1.8 million years old, the oldest human fossils outside Africa), the gold treasury of ancient Colchis and the Bronze Age, the medieval Golden Age, and a pointed Soviet Occupation exhibition. With over a million items in its collections, it's the single best overview of Georgian history and culture — and the ideal first stop in the country.

The building

The museum isn't a single collection but a network, with the main historical exhibitions in the Simon Janashia Museum of Georgia on Rustaveli Avenue. The institution traces back to 1852 (the museum of the Caucasus department of the Russian Imperial Geographic Society) — making it Georgia's oldest museum — and was named for the historian Simon Janashia in 1947. The present building dates to 1910, designed by the architect Nikolay Severov with medieval Georgian decorative motifs worked into it; its place on Tbilisi's main boulevard reflects the importance the institution carried from the start. (Much of the collection was evacuated to France in 1921 during the Soviet takeover and famously returned by the scholar Ekvtime Takaishvili in 1945.)

The collections

Archaeology and prehistory. The archaeological collection covers Georgia's prehistoric and ancient past with a thoroughness that reflects how long and continuously this land has been inhabited. The Dmanisi materials — from the site where ~1.8-million-year-old hominin fossils were found — set the starting point of the human story here in the most direct way possible. The Trialeti culture gold and silver vessels (~2000–1500 BC) that follow show the level of craftsmanship reached in the South Caucasus long before the classical Mediterranean civilizations peaked. One small but resonant object: a Neolithic vessel from Khramis Didi Gora (~6th millennium BC) decorated with grape clusters — part of the evidence behind Georgia's claim as one of the birthplaces of winemaking, some 8,000 years ago.

The Gold Treasury (a highlight). The treasury — a separately displayed, secured collection, often on a separate ticket — is the high point for many visitors. Its gold from ancient Colchis (the western-Georgian kingdom that Greek myth made the destination of the Argonauts and the Golden Fleece) is one of the finest assemblages of ancient goldwork in the Caucasus: jewelry and ornaments through to ceremonial pieces worked with granulation, filigree, and inlaid stones, at a level of technical mastery that consistently surprises visitors who didn't know how sophisticated early Georgian metallurgy was. It also holds material from the eastern-Georgian kingdom of Iberia (including the Akhalgori hoard, 5th c. BC) and from the Scythian and Sarmatian cultures of the Caucasus steppe. It isn't large in the number of pieces, but the quality is consistently high and the best items are extraordinary.

Medieval Georgia. The medieval section covers Georgia's Golden Age — the unified kingdom of the 11th–13th centuries — with manuscripts, ecclesiastical metalwork, and architectural fragments that give physical substance to a period otherwise known mainly through the monuments still standing in the landscape. The cloisonné enamel (minankari), a craft in which medieval Georgian artisans reached a refinement recognized across the Byzantine world, is especially worth attention.

The Soviet Occupation exhibition. Located within the museum (opened in 2006), this section takes a deliberately different tone from the archaeological displays. It documents the period the exhibition presents as the Soviet occupation of Georgia, 1921–1991 — the 1921 Red Army takeover, the Stalinist terror, deportations, the suppression of Georgian national culture, and the resistance movements — using documents (many from Security Service archives), photographs, testimonies, and reconstructed settings. It is, explicitly, an account framed from the Georgian national perspective rather than through Soviet or Russian historiography; the "occupation" framing is itself part of a contested debate between Georgia and Russia over how this history is characterized. For visitors who want to understand why the relationship between Georgia and Russia carries the weight it does, this section provides direct and essential context.

Ethnography. The ethnographic galleries present traditional material culture from across Georgia's regions — clothing, tools, household and agricultural objects, musical instruments — arranged to show how the country's different geographical zones produced distinct ways of living and making. The contrasts between highland and lowland, eastern and western Georgia, and the country's various communities become tangible through objects in a way a purely written narrative can't manage.

Getting there and visiting

  • Address: 3 Rustaveli Avenue, central Tbilisi (Rustaveli metro; on the main boulevard, near Freedom Square, the Parliament, and the Opera).
  • Hours: Tuesday–Sunday, roughly 10:00–18:00 (last entry ~17:30); closed Mondays. (Check current hours before visiting.)
  • Tickets: A standard museum ticket; the Gold Treasury has limited numbers and is usually a separate ticket bought at the entrance — see it first or book ahead at busy times. Guided tours (several languages) and audio guides are available; the audio guide adds a lot in the archaeological galleries.
  • Phone: +995 32 299 71 76
  • Best for: a first orientation to Georgian history, archaeology and the Colchis gold, medieval art, and 20th-century history — useful before heading to the regions.
  • Combine with: a walk along Rustaveli Avenue and into the Old Town — the Georgian Museum of Fine Arts, Kashveti Church, the Parliament, the Opera, and Freedom Square are all close. The museum shop carries Georgian history and archaeology titles hard to find elsewhere.

faq.title: What is the Georgian National Museum?
faq.content: It's the country's principal institution for history and culture — an umbrella organization (formed in 2004) of more than a dozen museums, whose main historical building is the Simon Janashia Museum of Georgia at 3 Rustaveli Avenue, the country's oldest museum (roots back to 1852). Across several floors it tells Georgia's story from prehistory to the 20th century, with over a million items in its collections. It's the single best overview of Georgian history and culture, and the ideal first stop on a trip.

faq.title: What are the highlights?
faq.content: The Dmanisi hominin fossils (~1.8 million years old — the oldest human remains outside Africa); the Gold Treasury of ancient Colchis (the Golden Fleece kingdom), one of the finest collections of ancient goldwork in the Caucasus; the Bronze Age Trialeti gold; a Neolithic vessel decorated with grape clusters, tied to Georgia's claim as a birthplace of wine; medieval Georgian art, especially the cloisonné enamel; and the Soviet Occupation exhibition. The Gold Treasury is the standout for many visitors.

faq.title: What is the Soviet Occupation exhibition?
faq.content: A section of the museum (opened in 2006) documenting the period it presents as the Soviet occupation of Georgia, 1921–1991 — the 1921 Red Army takeover, the Stalinist terror, deportations, the suppression of Georgian culture, and the resistance — through documents, photographs, and testimonies. It's framed explicitly from the Georgian national perspective (the "occupation" framing is itself part of a contested Georgia–Russia debate), and it offers direct context for why the relationship between the two countries carries the weight it does.

faq.title: How much time, tickets, and hours?
faq.content: Allow a couple of hours, more if you take it slowly. It's open Tuesday to Sunday, roughly 10:00–18:00 (last entry around 17:30), closed Mondays — check current hours before visiting. The main museum has a standard ticket; the Gold Treasury has limited numbers and usually a separate ticket bought at the entrance, so see it first or book ahead when it's busy. Guided tours (in several languages) and audio guides are available, and the audio guide adds a lot in the archaeological galleries.

faq.title: Where is it, and what's nearby?
faq.content: It's at 3 Rustaveli Avenue in central Tbilisi (Rustaveli metro), on the main boulevard within walking distance of most central accommodation. Nearby are the Georgian Museum of Fine Arts, Kashveti Church, the Parliament, the Opera, and Freedom Square — so a museum visit pairs naturally with a walk along Rustaveli and into the Old Town for a full morning or afternoon.

openAirMuseumEthnography.heroTitle: Tbilisi Open Air Museum of Ethnography (Giorgi Chitaia Museum)
openAirMuseumEthnography.content:
The Open Air Museum of Ethnography occupies a forested hillside above the Vake district of Tbilisi, capital of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus), just west of Turtle Lake and within easy reach of the center. It's the kind of place that sounds modest in description — a collection of old buildings moved from their original locations — but delivers far more in practice, especially for travelers who have spent time in different parts of Georgia and want to understand how the country's regional diversity actually shaped the way people built their homes and lived their daily lives.

What is the Open Air Museum of Ethnography?

The Giorgi Chitaia Open Air Museum of Ethnography is an outdoor museum of traditional Georgian architecture on a wooded hill above Vake, near Turtle Lake. Founded in 1966 by the ethnographer Giorgi Chitaia (and part of the Georgian National Museum since 2004), it gathers around 70 authentic buildings — relocated from across Georgia's regions and reassembled here — across some 52 hectares of forested paths, with more than 8,000 traditional objects inside them. Walking it is like visiting a dozen regions of Georgia in a few hours: the semi-underground darbazi houses of the east, the stone watchtowers of the mountains, the raised wooden houses of the humid west, and Kakhetian wine cellars. It's one of the best places to grasp Georgia's regional diversity made physical — and a quiet, green escape from the city.

What the museum shows

The museum was founded in 1966 by Giorgi Chitaia, who built the Georgian ethnographic school and made it his life's work to preserve traditional Georgian material culture (notably through the Soviet decades); it has been expanded since and is now part of the Georgian National Museum. It holds around 70 structures gathered from across Georgia's historical regions — Kartli, Kakheti, Imereti, Samegrelo, Guria, Adjara, Racha, Lechkhumi, Svaneti, Khevsureti, Samtskhe-Javakheti, and more — dismantled at their original sites, brought to Tbilisi, and reassembled with as much of their original fabric intact as the process allowed. This is not a reconstructed replica village but a genuine assemblage of authentic historical buildings, which gives the museum a material honesty that more theatrical heritage attractions often lack.

The most striking thing about walking through it is how fundamentally different the buildings from different regions are — not just in decorative detail but in structural logic, materials, spatial organization, and their relationship to the landscape they were built for.

The darbazi houses of eastern Georgia (Kartli, Kakheti) are among the most distinctive in the collection. Built of stone and timber and set partly into the slope, the darbazi is defined by a stepped wooden ceiling that rises in a corbelled dome toward a central skylight opening (which doubled as a smoke hole), supported on carved pillars. It evolved for the continental climate of the eastern lowlands, where winters are cold; the interior — dim, enclosed, organized around a central hearth beneath the skylight — is unlike any other domestic architecture in Georgia and gives an immediate physical sense of how climate and materials shape building culture.

The tower-houses of the highlands (Svaneti, Khevsureti, Pshavi) are the defensive end of the spectrum — thick stone walls and minimal openings reflecting an environment where clan conflict and raids were facts of daily life. Standing beside one here, stripped of the dramatic mountain backdrop that makes the towers of Svaneti so powerful, focuses attention on the craftsmanship itself: the dressing of the stone, the slight taper of the walls as they rise, the small openings placed to cover approaches rather than to admit light.

The wooden houses of western Georgia (Imereti, Samegrelo, Guria) take a completely different approach — raised above the ground on posts against the damp subtropical climate, with carved wooden detailing and the deep verandas characteristic of the wetter west. The contrast with the earthen darbazi or the stone towers makes the point about regional diversity more vividly than any text panel could.

Many buildings can be entered, their interiors furnished with period objects — tools, cooking implements, textiles, furniture. Seeing the actual layout of a traditional kitchen, the sleeping arrangements in a highland tower, or the wine cellar (marani) that was a standard feature of any Kakhetian farmhouse, connects the agricultural and wine culture travelers meet throughout Georgia to the physical spaces that produced it. The collection isn't only houses, either: it includes a relocated early-medieval Sioni basilica (5th–6th century) from Tianeti and old grave-stones, and at weekends some houses offer demonstrations and tastings of regional food.

The setting

The hillside setting adds a dimension a flat urban site couldn't. The buildings are spread along wooded paths that climb and descend through forest, the topography giving each cluster a degree of separation that approximates something of an original village layout. The forest is dense enough that the city below is largely out of sight from most of the museum, and the mix of traditional architecture and trees creates a genuine quiet unusual for a Tbilisi attraction. (A trail at the back leads to a viewpoint with a Svan tower and a view over the city.)

The full walk covers a few kilometers of path with moderate elevation change — comfortable for most visitors, but worth bearing in mind if mobility is limited. The upper sections give partial views through the trees toward the city and the surrounding hills, and the paths are pleasant for their own sake. In summer, the tree cover keeps it noticeably cooler than the exposed city — one of the better warm-weather options in Tbilisi; spring and autumn give the most comfortable walking and the nicest light through the canopy.

Getting there and visiting

  • Address: Turtle Lake Road, Vake district, central Tbilisi (on the hill west of Turtle Lake, above Vake).
  • Hours: Tuesday–Sunday, roughly 10:00–18:00 (last entry ~30 min before close); closed Mondays. (Check current hours before visiting.)
  • Admission: Modest (a small adult ticket; reduced for students and children — note the tourist price may differ from the local one). Allow 2–3 hours for a thorough visit, or about 90 minutes for the highlights.
  • Phone: +995 32 272 90 45
  • Best for: travelers interested in traditional architecture and rural life, anyone who has toured the regions and wants to see the contrasts side by side, and families; a green, walkable half-day.
  • Combine with: Turtle Lake (right nearby — the museum in the morning, then a walk around the lake and lunch at a lakeside café), the Vake area and Vake Park, or the cable car up to Turtle Lake from the city. If you're here in summer, the annual Art-Gene festival of traditional music and crafts is held at the museum.

faq.title: What is the Tbilisi Open Air Museum of Ethnography?
faq.content: It's an outdoor museum of traditional Georgian architecture — the Giorgi Chitaia Open Air Museum of Ethnography — on a forested hill above the Vake district, just west of Turtle Lake. Founded in 1966 by the ethnographer Giorgi Chitaia (and part of the Georgian National Museum since 2004), it gathers around 70 authentic buildings relocated from across Georgia's regions, with more than 8,000 traditional objects inside them, spread over some 52 hectares of wooded paths. Walking it is like visiting a dozen regions of Georgia in a few hours.

faq.title: What will I see there?
faq.content: The contrast between regions, made physical: the stone-and-timber darbazi houses of eastern Georgia, set into the slope with a stepped wooden dome rising to a central skylight; the defensive stone watchtowers of the mountains (Svaneti, Khevsureti); and the raised wooden houses of the humid western regions. Many can be entered and are furnished with period tools, textiles, and furniture, including the wine cellar (marani) standard to a Kakhetian farmhouse. There's also a relocated early-medieval Sioni basilica and, at weekends, regional food demonstrations in some houses.

faq.title: How long does it take, and how hard is the walking?
faq.content: Allow about 2–3 hours for a thorough visit, or roughly 90 minutes for the highlights. The buildings are spread along wooded paths that climb and descend through forest — a few kilometers in all, with moderate elevation change.
