It's comfortable for most visitors, but worth bearing in mind if you have limited mobility. The forest cover keeps it noticeably cooler than the city in summer, which makes it one of the better warm-weather options in Tbilisi.

faq.title: Where is it, and how do I combine it with other things?
faq.content: It's on the hill west of Turtle Lake, above the Vake district. It pairs naturally with Turtle Lake — the museum in the morning, then a walk around the lake and lunch at a lakeside café in the afternoon makes an easy half-day in the Vake area. It's open Tuesday to Sunday (closed Mondays); check current hours before visiting. In summer, the annual Art-Gene festival of traditional music and crafts is held here.

faq.title: Who is it best for?
faq.content: Anyone interested in traditional architecture and rural life, and especially travelers who have toured Georgia's regions and want to see the contrasts side by side — the eastern darbazi, the mountain towers, the western wooden houses, all within one walk. It's also a good, green, walkable outing for families. The setting itself — quiet forest paths above the city — is a draw regardless of how closely you study the buildings.

narikalaFortress.content:
Narikala has stood on the ridge above the Old Town of Tbilisi, capital of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus), since the 4th century. In that time it has been expanded by Arab emirs, rebuilt by Georgian kings, fought over by Mongols, Persians, and Ottomans, and finally left in its present state of atmospheric ruin by the explosion of a gunpowder magazine in 1827. The result isn't a tidily restored fortress with intact walls and explanatory panels but something more interesting — a layered accumulation of different building periods, some sections standing to near full height, others reduced to their foundations, spread across a ridge that offers the single best elevated view of Tbilisi from within the city.

What is Narikala Fortress?

Narikala is the ancient citadel on the Sololaki ridge above Tbilisi's Old Town — the city's defining fortress, often called its "heart and soul." First fortified in the 4th century (when it was known as Shuris-Tsikhe), it was expanded by Arab emirs in the 7th–8th centuries and rebuilt by Georgian kings, most notably David IV the Builder; the name "Narikala" came later, under the Mongols. Largely wrecked by an 1827 gunpowder explosion and never fully restored, it survives as a dramatic ruin of walls and towers across two sections of the ridge, with the (reconstructed) St. Nicholas Church in its lower court. It's free, open at all hours, reached most easily by cable car from Rike Park, and offers the best panoramic view of the Old Town in the city — beside it stands the Mother of Georgia statue.

A fortress in layers

The first fortifications here are usually dated to the 4th century, among the earliest layers of the city's history, when the citadel was known as Shuris-Tsikhe ("the Invidious," or "Enviable," Fort). Over the following centuries it was expanded and rebuilt many times. Arab rulers strengthened it considerably in the 7th–8th centuries, when Tbilisi was the seat of an Arab emirate, building the Emir's palace within its walls; and Georgian kings — most notably David IV the Builder, who retook Tbilisi in 1122 — rebuilt and enlarged it further as the city grew. By the medieval period Narikala was a substantial complex guarding the southern approaches to the city, and it remained central through later Persian and Ottoman struggles for control. The name Narikala (from Turkic/Mongol roots usually translated "little fortress") was given during the period of Mongol rule — though the structure it named was by then anything but little.

The fortress's military life ended abruptly. In 1827, during the Russian imperial period, a gunpowder magazine stored inside exploded (by several accounts after a lightning strike, its effect compared to an earthquake), wrecking much of the citadel; an earthquake is also cited among the 1827 damage. Narikala was never fully rebuilt, and it has stood as a ruin ever since — though its walls have been stabilized and partly restored in recent campaigns (in 2016–17 and again more recently), so what you see today is a consolidated ruin rather than one actively crumbling.

The ruins and what remains

The fortress occupies two sections of the ridge connected by a curtain wall — the upper citadel, with the most complete surviving walls and towers, and a lower section extending toward the Abanotubani district below. Walking through it means moving between stretches of wall standing six or eight meters high and stretches reduced to their lowest courses, passing through gaps where gates once stood and climbing to viewpoints the original garrison used to watch the valley.

In the lower court stands the small Church of St. Nicholas. It's important to be clear about what it is: the original 13th-century church on this spot was destroyed (in the 1827 disaster), and the church you see today is a reconstruction built in 1996–1997 on the old foundations, in the tradition of medieval Georgian church architecture — a cross-type church with doors on three sides. It's active and occasionally holds services, and its presence is a reminder that Narikala was never purely military but, like any medieval Georgian citadel, contained religious and residential buildings too.

The stonework throughout repays attention even without a particular interest in military architecture. The different building campaigns are legible in the masonry — the earlier sections using different coursing from the later Georgian work, and the more regular brick and mortar of the modern repairs used to stabilize the walls. Reading these layers in the stone is a more direct way of grasping the fortress's history than any descriptive text. (Among the surviving features are several towers, the remains of a bathhouse, and a reservoir.)

The views

The ridge gives panoramic views in several directions, and different sections of wall frame different compositions. From the upper citadel looking north, the full spread of the Old Town lies below — the sulfur-bath domes of Abanotubani immediately beneath, the wooden-balconied houses of the historic district reaching toward the center, the Mtkvari making its bend, and the newer districts on the hills beyond. From the walls facing east, the Metekhi Church on its cliff is directly across the river, with the Avlabari district and the golden dome of Sameba Cathedral behind it. The views at sunset, when the low western light catches the Old Town facades, are among the most celebrated in the city.

The Mother of Georgia statue stands at the northern end of the ridge, just beyond the fortress walls. The 20-meter aluminum figure — erected in 1958 to mark Tbilisi's 1,500th anniversary — holds a bowl of wine in her left hand (for friends) and a sword in her right (for enemies): a characteristically Georgian formulation of hospitality and martial readiness in a single gesture, and now so identified with the city that its absence from the skyline would feel like a real loss.

Getting there

The cable car from Rike Park is the easiest and most enjoyable approach, setting you down at the upper station beside the Mother of Georgia and the fortress walls. The walk up from the Old Town — from Meidan, or via the Betlemi Street stairs through Sololaki — is steeper and longer but passes through some of the most characterful residential lanes in the historic district, and is worth doing at least one way. Arriving by cable car and walking back down through the Old Town makes a natural circuit — up by air, down through the streets — taking in the fortress, the views, and the historic neighborhood in one continuous loop.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: Narikala Fortress, on the Sololaki ridge above the Old Town, Tbilisi.
  • Getting there: Cable car from Rike Park to the upper station (beside the Mother of Georgia); or on foot from Meidan / via the Betlemi Street stairs in Sololaki.
  • Hours: Open-air and freely accessible at all hours (the cable car runs on its own schedule, into the evening).
  • Admission: Free.
  • Best time: Late afternoon into sunset, for the light over the Old Town.
  • Tips: Wear sturdy shoes — the ground is uneven and the walls are unfenced in places; take care near the edges.
  • Combine with: The Mother of Georgia (right beside it), the cable car and Rike Park, the Leghvtakhevi gorge and Botanical Garden (the ridge continues that way), Abanotubani, and the Old Town below — the whole southeastern ridge-and-Old-Town cluster, with the Metekhi/Bridge of Peace riverfront in view across the water.

faq.title: What is Narikala Fortress?
faq.content: Narikala is the ancient citadel on the Sololaki ridge above Tbilisi's Old Town — the city's defining fortress, sometimes called its "heart and soul." First fortified in the 4th century (when it was known as Shuris-Tsikhe), it was expanded by Arab emirs and rebuilt by Georgian kings over the centuries, and given the name "Narikala" under the Mongols. Largely wrecked by a gunpowder explosion in 1827 and never fully rebuilt, it survives as a dramatic ruin of walls and towers across two sections of the ridge, with a (reconstructed) church in its lower court. It's free, open at all hours, easiest reached by cable car, and offers the best panoramic view of the Old Town in the city.

faq.title: How old is it, and what's its history?
faq.content: The first fortifications date to the 4th century, around the time the city was founded, when it was a Persian-era citadel called Shuris-Tsikhe ("the Invidious Fort"). Arab rulers expanded it considerably in the 7th–8th centuries (building the Emir's palace inside), and Georgian kings rebuilt and enlarged it, most notably David IV the Builder, who retook Tbilisi in 1122. The Mongols gave it the name Narikala. It was fought over by Persians and Ottomans, and in 1827 — during the Russian period — a gunpowder magazine stored inside exploded, wrecking much of it; it was never fully restored. The walls have been stabilized in recent restoration work, so it's now a consolidated ruin rather than a crumbling one.

faq.title: Is the church inside original?
faq.content: No — and this is worth knowing. The original St. Nicholas Church on the site, dating to the 13th century, was destroyed (in the 1827 disaster). The church standing in the lower court today is a reconstruction, built in 1996–1997 on the old foundations in the tradition of medieval Georgian architecture (a cross-type church with doors on three sides). It's an active church that occasionally holds services. So it looks medieval, but it's a faithful modern rebuild rather than a surviving original.

faq.title: What are the views like?
faq.content: They're the best elevated views of Tbilisi from within the city, and they change as you move around the walls. Looking north, the whole Old Town is laid out below — the sulfur-bath domes of Abanotubani, the balconied houses, the Mtkvari's bend. Looking east, the Metekhi Church on its cliff is directly across the river, with Sameba Cathedral's golden dome behind. Sunset, when the low light catches the Old Town facades, is the most celebrated time. The Mother of Georgia statue stands at the northern end of the ridge, just beyond the walls.

faq.title: How do you get there, and what should I know?
faq.content: The easiest and most enjoyable way is the cable car from Rike Park, which sets you down at the upper station beside the Mother of Georgia and the walls. You can also walk up from the Old Town (from Meidan, or via the Betlemi Street stairs in Sololaki) — steeper, but through characterful lanes; a popular plan is to ride up and walk down. It's open-air, free, and accessible at any hour. Wear sturdy shoes: the ground is uneven and the walls are unfenced in places, so take care near the edges, especially with children.

mtatsmindaPark.heroTitle: Mtatsminda Park: Tbilisi's Mountain-Top View and Funicular
mtatsmindaPark.content:
Mtatsminda — "Holy Mountain" in Georgian — is the forested height that rises directly above the Old Town of Tbilisi, capital of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus). Its summit is visible from much of the city and marked by a tall television tower that's one of the most reliable orientation points in the urban landscape. The park on its upper slopes has been a place of leisure for Tbilisi residents since the early 20th century, when the funicular was built to carry people up the steep hillside, and it remains one of the most popular recreational spots in the capital — a place where the city comes to breathe, look at itself from above, and spend an evening with far more air and space than the streets below allow.

What is Mtatsminda Park?

Mtatsminda Park is a large hilltop park at the summit of Mount Mtatsminda — at around 770 meters, the highest point in Tbilisi — overlooking the city. It combines the best elevated view over Tbilisi with a long-running amusement park (a big edge-of-the-mountain Ferris wheel, rides, a TV tower) and restaurants and cafés with open terraces. The classic way up is the historic Tbilisi Funicular, opened in 1905, which climbs from Chonkadze Street in the city center via a middle station at the Mtatsminda Pantheon — where many of Georgia's most celebrated figures are buried — to the summit. It's not a cultural sight in the way the Old Town is, but it gives a perspective on the city that more historically focused visits rarely include, and it's at its best in the early evening, for the view as the city lights come on.

The view

The panorama from Mtatsminda's upper terraces is the main reason most visitors make the trip. At around 770 meters above sea level — the highest point in Tbilisi — the summit looks out over the full spread of the city: the Mtkvari winding through the valley below, the Old Town clustered on the right bank, the 19th-century center reaching north along Rustaveli Avenue, the Soviet-era residential districts filling the surrounding hills, and the Caucasus foothills along the horizon in several directions. The scale of the view makes clear how geographically compressed Tbilisi actually is — a city of over a million packed into a relatively narrow river valley — and gives a spatial orientation to the city that's genuinely useful after time spent navigating its winding streets.

Evening is the best time for the view. As the sun drops behind the western hills, the city lights come on progressively across the valley, and the combination of the illuminated Old Town and the darkening Caucasus ridges beyond makes one of the more memorable urban panoramas in the South Caucasus. On clear nights visibility extends well beyond the city, and in winter, when the surrounding mountains carry snow, the contrast between the lit city below and the white peaks above is striking.

The funicular

The Tbilisi Funicular — which connects the lower station on Chonkadze Street, near the Rustaveli area of the center, to the Mtatsminda summit — has been running since 1905, making it one of the older urban funiculars in the region (it closed around 2000 and reopened in 2012 after a full renovation). The ride takes only a few minutes and passes through a middle station at the Mtatsminda Pantheon, the cemetery, around St. David's Church (Mamadaviti), where many of Georgia's most celebrated writers and public figures are buried — among them Ilia Chavchavadze, Akaki Tsereteli, and Vazha-Pshavela, as well as the Russian playwright Alexander Griboyedov and his Georgian wife Nino Chavchavadze. The ascent itself is a pleasure, the views over the rooftops of the Old Town and the Narikala ridge opening up as you climb — a different and equally good perspective from the one at the summit. (A popular plan is to ride up to the top and walk back down to the Pantheon to visit it.)

The funicular is the most convenient and atmospheric way to reach the park, especially on a first visit. A road climbs the back of the mountain for those arriving by taxi or car, and city buses also serve the area. Note that rides and the funicular are paid for with a rechargeable park card (bought and topped up at the stations or in the park) rather than cash per ride.

The park

The amusement park at the summit has been operating since the Soviet period — it was established in the 1930s (and once carried Stalin's name) — and has been progressively updated, most substantially in the early 2000s, though it keeps a character rooted more in the mid-20th-century public pleasure garden than the modern theme park. The Ferris wheel, set right on the edge of the plateau so its glass cabins lift riders out over the city for unobstructed views in every direction, is the most popular single attraction and worth the short queue on busy evenings. Other rides cater mainly to families with children, and the atmosphere is convivial and unpretentious — this is where Tbilisi comes to enjoy itself, not to impress.

Beyond the rides, the park has extensive walking areas, open terraces with benches and viewpoints, pine woods and meadows, and a cluster of restaurants and cafés whose main selling point is the view (though the food has improved considerably in recent years; the upper-station Funicular Restaurant is the long-established grand option). Spending an hour or two here in the early evening — arriving by funicular as the afternoon heat eases, walking the terraces as the sun sets, staying for a drink or a meal as the city lights up below — is one of the more pleasant ways to end a day in Tbilisi.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: Mount Mtatsminda, above the Old Town, Tbilisi — the city's highest point (~770 m).
  • Getting there: The Tbilisi Funicular from its lower station on Chonkadze Street (the classic route, with a stop at the Pantheon); or by taxi/car via the road up the back of the mountain; city buses also serve the area.
  • Hours: Daily, roughly late morning until late evening; hours vary by season (later in summer). The funicular runs on a similar schedule.
  • Tickets/payment: Rides and the funicular use a rechargeable park card (bought and topped up at the stations/park); the park itself is free to enter.
  • Best time: Early evening into sunset, for the view and the lit city.
  • Website: mtatsmindapark.ge
  • Combine with: The Mtatsminda Pantheon and St. David's Church (at the middle station); a walk down through the Sololaki/Rustaveli streets; the Mother of Georgia and Narikala are along the same broad ridge system (reached separately, via the Rike cable car). Trails also lead toward Turtle Lake.

faq.title: What is Mtatsminda Park?
faq.content: Mtatsminda Park is a large hilltop park at the summit of Mount Mtatsminda ("Holy Mountain"), at around 770 meters the highest point in Tbilisi, overlooking the whole city. It combines the best elevated view over Tbilisi with a long-running amusement park — a big Ferris wheel set on the edge of the mountain, rides, restaurants and cafés with terraces, and a TV tower. The classic way up is the historic Tbilisi Funicular. It's a relaxed, family-friendly, slightly old-fashioned pleasure park rather than a cultural sight, and it's most popular in the early evening, for the view as the city lights come on.

faq.title: How do you get up there?
faq.content: The most atmospheric way is the Tbilisi Funicular, which has run since 1905 (reopened in 2012 after renovation) from its lower station on Chonkadze Street in the city center. The ride takes only a few minutes and stops at a middle station by the Mtatsminda Pantheon. You can also reach the summit by taxi or car on the road up the back of the mountain, or by city bus. Note that the funicular and the park rides are paid for with a rechargeable park card (bought and topped up at the stations or in the park), not cash per ride; the park itself is free to enter.

faq.title: What is the Mtatsminda Pantheon?
faq.content: It's the cemetery at the middle funicular station, around St. David's Church (Mamadaviti), where many of Georgia's most celebrated writers, artists, and public figures are buried — among them Ilia Chavchavadze, Akaki Tsereteli, and Vazha-Pshavela, as well as the Russian playwright Alexander Griboyedov and his Georgian wife Nino Chavchavadze. A popular plan is to ride the funicular to the summit, then walk back down to the Pantheon to visit it before continuing to the city.

faq.title: When is the best time to visit?
faq.content: The early evening into sunset. The view is the main draw, and it's at its best as the sun drops behind the western hills and the city lights come on progressively across the valley — the lit Old Town against the darkening Caucasus ridges is one of the more memorable urban panoramas in the South Caucasus. In winter, with snow on the surrounding mountains, the contrast with the lit city is especially striking. Hours run roughly from late morning to late evening and vary by season (later in summer).

faq.title: Is it worth it, and what's there to do?
faq.content: Yes, especially for the view and for an easygoing few hours. Beyond the panorama, there's the amusement park (the edge-of-the-plateau Ferris wheel with glass cabins is the highlight, plus family rides), walking areas through pine woods and meadows, open terraces, and restaurants and cafés whose main draw is the view (the food has improved in recent years). It's not a cultural destination like the Old Town, but it gives you a perspective on the whole city — and a relaxed, local, family atmosphere — that more historically focused sightseeing misses.

motherOfGeorgia.heroTitle: Mother of Georgia (Kartlis Deda): Tbilisi's Guardian Statue
motherOfGeorgia.content:
The Mother of Georgia stands on the Sololaki ridge above the Old Town of Tbilisi, capital of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus) — a 20-meter aluminum figure visible across a wide arc of the city below. She has watched over Tbilisi since 1958, when the original statue was raised to mark the city's 1,500th anniversary, and she has become so thoroughly identified with the skyline that the city is now hard to picture without her. From the Old Town, from the river, from the Metekhi cliff across the water, the figure appears on the ridge beside the Narikala walls — always at the same elevation, always in the same posture, the sword and the wine bowl raised against the sky.

What is the Mother of Georgia?

The Mother of Georgia — Kartlis Deda, "Mother of Kartli" — is a 20-meter aluminum statue on the Sololaki ridge above Tbilisi's Old Town, beside the Narikala Fortress. Designed by the Georgian sculptor Elguja Amashukeli and first erected in 1958 for the city's 1,500th anniversary, she depicts a woman in Georgian national dress holding a bowl of wine in her left hand — for those who come as friends — and a sword in her right — for those who come as enemies. The two objects capture the Georgian self-image: openness and hospitality toward guests, readiness to defend the country against threats. She's one of Tbilisi's defining landmarks, reached by cable car from Rike Park, and the ridge beside her offers some of the best views in the city.

The symbolism

The two objects she holds are worth pausing on, because they capture something real about how Georgia has understood itself. The bowl of wine in her left hand is extended toward those who arrive in peace — an expression of the hospitality that is among the most deeply held values in Georgian culture: the tradition of the supra (feast) and the tamada (toastmaster), the near-sacred obligation to feed and shelter a guest that runs through Georgian social life at every level. The sword in her right hand faces those who come with hostile intent — a reference to the long military history of a small country that has spent much of its existence defending its territory and identity against larger neighbors. The combination of radical openness to friends and readiness to fight enemies isn't a contradiction in the Georgian self-image but a single coherent stance — and Kartlis Deda expresses it with a directness more sophisticated public art rarely achieves.

The statue and its setting

The figure was designed by the Georgian sculptor Elguja Amashukeli — who also created the equestrian statue of Vakhtang Gorgasali at Metekhi across the river — and built in the form of a traditional Georgian woman in national dress. The silhouette of the long tunic and the way the garments fall are recognizably Georgian rather than generically classical, which gives the monument a specificity that generic allegorical figures lack. (Amashukeli originally titled it simply "Capital"; the people renamed it Kartlis Deda, and he was awarded the Shota Rustaveli State Prize for it in 1966.)

At 20 meters she's large enough to be a genuine landmark without being so enormous that human proportion is lost. The aluminum surface, which weathers to a slightly matte silver-grey, catches different qualities of light through the day — bright and reflective in direct midday sun, warmer and more textured in late afternoon. Her history mirrors the city's care for her: the original 1958 figure was wooden, intended as a temporary decoration for the anniversary; it proved so popular that it was made permanent, clad in aluminum in 1963, and in 1997 replaced with a faithful new version that preserves the original image and proportions — the statue that stands today.

She sits immediately beside the Narikala Fortress walls, and the two — the medieval fortification and the 20th-century monument — share the same elevated position in a pairing less incongruous than it first seems. Both express the same basic idea: that this ridge above the river is where Tbilisi asserts itself, where the city places its most public symbols of identity and defense. The fortress was built to protect the city physically; the statue to represent what the city believes itself to be. That continuity of function, across some fifteen centuries, is part of what makes the Sololaki ridge one of the most historically layered sites in Tbilisi.

The views

The views from the ridge beside the statue are among the best from any accessible point in the city. Looking north over the Old Town, the full spread of the historic district lies below — the Mtkvari making its characteristic bend, the wooden-balconied houses climbing the hillside, the Metekhi Church on its cliff across the river, the Bridge of Peace and the newer left-bank developments beyond. On clear days the view reaches well past the city to the Greater Caucasus foothills on the northern horizon. Looking south and east, the forested Sololaki slopes and the deeper valleys beyond show how quickly the urban fabric gives way to natural terrain at the city's southern edge.

Sunset is the most popular time, for good reason — the low western light catches the Old Town facades from the ideal angle, the city lights come on progressively as the light fades, and the shift from afternoon to evening seen from this height is genuinely beautiful. The ridge is busy at that hour but large enough that a clear viewpoint is only a short walk along the walls away.

Getting there

The most enjoyable approach is the cable car from Rike Park to the upper station, which sets you down by the Narikala walls, a short (3–5 minute) walk along the ridge from the statue. The walk up from the Old Town through the streets below Abanotubani is steeper but passes through characterful residential lanes and takes around 20–30 minutes at a comfortable pace. A road behind the ridge gives car/taxi access. The most satisfying way to experience both the monument and its surroundings is to ride the cable car up and walk back down through the Old Town.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: Sololaki ridge, above the Old Town, Tbilisi — beside the Narikala Fortress.
  • Getting there: Cable car from Rike Park to the upper (Narikala) station, then a 3–5 minute walk along the ridge; or a 20–30 minute uphill walk from the Old Town; or by taxi via the road behind the ridge.
  • Hours: Open-air, freely accessible 24 hours.
  • Admission: Free.
  • Best time: Sunset, for the light over the Old Town and the city lights coming on.
  • Combine with: Narikala Fortress (right beside it), the cable car and Rike Park, the Leghvtakhevi gorge and Botanical Garden, Abanotubani, and the Old Town below — the whole southeastern ridge-and-Old-Town cluster.

faq.title: What is the Mother of Georgia statue?
faq.content: The Mother of Georgia — Kartlis Deda ("Mother of Kartli") — is a 20-meter aluminum statue on the Sololaki ridge above Tbilisi's Old Town, beside the Narikala Fortress. Designed by the Georgian sculptor Elguja Amashukeli and first raised in 1958 for the city's 1,500th anniversary, she depicts a woman in Georgian national dress holding a bowl of wine in her left hand (for those who come as friends) and a sword in her right (for those who come as enemies). She's one of Tbilisi's defining landmarks, visible across much of the city, and the ridge beside her gives some of the best views in Tbilisi.

faq.title: What do the wine bowl and sword mean?
faq.content: They capture the Georgian self-image. The bowl of wine, held out to friends, stands for the country's deep tradition of hospitality — the supra (feast), the tamada (toastmaster), the near-sacred duty to welcome a guest. The sword, raised toward enemies, stands for the readiness to defend the country, reflecting the long history of a small nation guarding its identity against larger neighbors. The two together — open to friends, ready to fight foes — aren't a contradiction but a single coherent stance, and the statue expresses it with unusual directness.

faq.title: Who built it, and when?
faq.content: It was designed by the prominent Georgian sculptor Elguja Amashukeli (who also made the Vakhtang Gorgasali statue at Metekhi) and first erected in 1958, the year Tbilisi celebrated its 1,500th anniversary. Amashukeli originally titled it "Capital," but locals called it Kartlis Deda and the name stuck; he won the Shota Rustaveli State Prize for it in 1966. The original 1958 figure was wooden, intended as temporary; it was made permanent and clad in aluminum in 1963, and in 1997 it was replaced with a faithful new version (preserving the original image and proportions) — the statue you see today.

faq.title: How do you get to it?
faq.content: The easiest and most scenic way is the cable car from Rike Park up to the Narikala station, from which it's a 3–5 minute walk along the ridge to the statue. You can also walk up from the Old Town (a steeper 20–30 minute climb through the residential lanes below Abanotubani), or come by taxi on the road behind the ridge. A popular plan is to ride the cable car up and walk back down through the Old Town. The site is open-air, free, and accessible at any hour.

faq.title: Is it worth visiting, and when's best?
faq.content: Yes — both for the statue itself and, just as much, for the setting. It stands right beside Narikala Fortress, so the two are visited together, and the ridge offers some of the best views in Tbilisi: north over the Old Town, the Mtkvari, the Metekhi cliff and the Bridge of Peace, with the Greater Caucasus foothills beyond on clear days. Sunset is the most popular time, when the low light catches the Old Town and the city lights come on — the ridge gets busy then, but there's room to find a clear spot along the walls.

metekhiChurch.content:
The Metekhi Church occupies one of the most dramatically positioned sites in Tbilisi, capital of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus) — a sheer cliff on the left bank of the Mtkvari, directly across the river from the Old Town, where the rock drops vertically to the water and the church appears, from most angles, to grow straight from the cliff edge. It is one of the defining images of the city, visible from the Bridge of Peace, from the Narikala ridge, and from the riverside streets of the Old Town opposite — so fundamental to the Tbilisi skyline that the city is hard to picture without it.

What is Metekhi Church?

Metekhi is a 13th-century Georgian Orthodox church on a cliff above the Mtkvari River, facing the Old Town of Tbilisi — one of the city's most recognizable landmarks. The site has been a royal and religious center since the 5th century, when Vakhtang Gorgasali, the founder of Tbilisi, is traditionally said to have built a church and palace here (the name Metekhi means "the area around the palace"). The present cross-dome church was built around 1278–1289 by King Demetrius II, after earlier structures were destroyed in the Mongol invasion of 1235. It is traditionally the burial place of the 5th-century martyr St. Shushanik and is associated with the martyrdom of St. Abo, the patron saint of Tbilisi. Beside it stands the famous equestrian statue of Vakhtang Gorgasali, and the cliff terrace offers one of the best views of the Old Town in the city. It's an active church, free to enter.

A cliff with deep history

The cliff has been significant for far longer than the present building. The Metekhi district — the name means "the area around the palace" — was the site of a royal residence from at least the 5th century, when King Vakhtang Gorgasali, the founder of Tbilisi, is traditionally said to have built his palace and a church here above the river. The strategic logic is obvious: the cliff commands the river crossing below and gives a naturally defensible position with clear views in several directions. For centuries this promontory was one of the political centers of the Kartlian kingdom, and the church that eventually stood here inherited both its physical prominence and its historical weight. By tradition, it was here that Vakhtang Gorgasali buried St. Shushanik, the 5th-century queen martyred for refusing to renounce Christianity — one of the earliest Georgian saints.

History

None of the earliest structures survived the Mongol invasion of 1235. The present Church of the Nativity of the Virgin was built around 1278–1289, during the reign of King Demetrius II — known in Georgian history as Demetrius the Self-Sacrificer (Demetre the Devoted), the title earned by his choice to surrender himself to the Mongol Ilkhanate, where he was executed in 1289, in the hope of sparing his people reprisals. The church was damaged and rebuilt several times in the following centuries through successive Persian and Ottoman raids on Tbilisi (King Erekle II repaired it in the 18th century, among others).

The site's hardest chapter came under foreign rule. In 1819, during the Russian imperial period, the adjacent fortifications were demolished and a prison was built in the churchyard — it operated until 1934, and among those held there over the years was the writer Maxim Gorky. Under the Soviet authorities the church itself was closed as a place of worship and used for a time as a theater. Its religious function was not restored until 1988 — after a public campaign led by Patriarch Ilia II, with the dissident (and future president) Zviad Gamsakhurdia going on a hunger strike in support. That a church of this age and centrality was shuttered and repurposed within living memory is part of the history the building carries, and it gives a visit a dimension beyond the architectural.

The building

Metekhi is a cross-dome (cross-cupola) church of the type characteristic of medieval Georgian architecture — a central dome on a drum rising above a cruciform plan — though a somewhat distinctive example, with three projecting apses and four free-standing columns supporting the dome. It's built of warm brownish stone, and the exterior is largely undecorated: its solidity and proportion do the work that ornamental carving does in more elaborate Georgian churches of the period. The effect is concentrated strength rather than elegance — a building that looks as though it belongs on its cliff, as though rock and church are continuous.

The interior is modest in scale and relatively austere; the original medieval frescoes have not survived, so the walls are plain rather than painted. But the candles burning before the icon stands give it the quality of active worship that distinguishes a living church from a monument, and several of its icons are of particular interest — including the "100,000 Martyrs of Metekhi," and icons of St. Shushanik and St. Abo of Tiflis, the two martyrs most closely tied to this site.

The Vakhtang Gorgasali statue

The bronze equestrian statue immediately outside the church — King Vakhtang Gorgasali on horseback, looking out across the river toward the Old Town he founded — was created by the sculptor Elguja Amashukeli (who also made the Mother of Georgia statue on the Narikala ridge) and erected in the 1960s. Gorgasali is shown in full armor, his horse posed above the cliff edge, and the combination of the figure, the cliff, and the river below makes one of the most photographed silhouettes in Tbilisi. The statue anchors the founding story of the city to this exact spot — the cliff where, by the chronicles, Gorgasali's palace stood and from which Tbilisi grew — giving the Metekhi visit a narrative dimension beyond the architecture.

The view

Standing on the cliff edge beside the church and looking back across the river toward the Old Town, you take in the full width of the historic district — the wooden-balconied houses climbing the hillside, Narikala Fortress on the ridge above, the sulfur-bath domes of Abanotubani to the south, the Bridge of Peace and Rike Park below, and the forested slope of Mtatsminda on the western skyline. It's one of the best ground-level views of the Old Town anywhere in the city, the elevated cliff giving it a breadth and depth that riverside viewpoints at water level can't match — and it's especially good in late-afternoon light and at sunset.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: Metekhi Rise, Avlabari district, Tbilisi — on the left bank of the Mtkvari, reached from the Old Town across the Metekhi Bridge.
  • Getting there: A short walk from the Old Town over the Metekhi Bridge, or from Avlabari metro; the cliff terrace is a few minutes' walk either way.
  • Hours: Open daily during daylight hours; as an active church, hours may vary around services.
  • Admission: Free.
  • Dress code: Modest dress — shoulders and knees covered (women may be asked to cover their heads); the usual courtesies of an active place of worship apply.
  • Best time: Late afternoon and sunset, for the light on the cliff and across the Old Town.
  • Combine with: The view across to Narikala, Abanotubani, and the Old Town; Rike Park, the cable car, and the Bridge of Peace just below; Avlabari and (uphill) Sameba Cathedral.

faq.title: What is Metekhi Church?
faq.content: Metekhi is a 13th-century Georgian Orthodox church standing on a cliff above the Mtkvari River, directly facing the Old Town of Tbilisi — one of the city's most recognizable landmarks. The site has been a royal and religious center since the 5th century, when Vakhtang Gorgasali, the founder of Tbilisi, is traditionally said to have built a palace and church here (the name Metekhi means "the area around the palace"). The present cross-dome church was built around 1278–1289 by King Demetrius II, after earlier structures were destroyed in the Mongol invasion of 1235. Beside it stands the famous equestrian statue of Vakhtang Gorgasali, and its cliff terrace gives one of the best views of the Old Town in the city.

faq.title: Who is buried or commemorated there?
faq.content: By tradition, the church is the burial place of St. Shushanik, a 5th-century queen martyred for refusing to renounce Christianity — one of the earliest Georgian saints, said to have been buried here by Vakhtang Gorgasali. The site is also associated with the martyrdom of St. Abo of Tiflis, the 8th-century patron saint of Tbilisi. Both are commemorated in icons inside the church, along with the "100,000 Martyrs of Metekhi." The church was rebuilt by King Demetrius II ("the Self-Sacrificer"), who surrendered himself to the Mongols and was executed in 1289 in the hope of sparing his people — one of the more poignant figures in Georgian history, though it's Shushanik who is traditionally buried here.

faq.title: Why is it historically important?
faq.content: Because the cliff has been one of Tbilisi's central places since the city's founding. It was the site of the royal residence of Vakhtang Gorgasali in the 5th century, a political center of the medieval Georgian kingdom, and the spot the city's founding story is tied to. The building itself has lived through much of Tbilisi's turbulent history: destroyed in the Mongol invasion of 1235, rebuilt in the 1270s–80s, damaged and repaired through Persian and Ottoman raids, used as a theater under the Soviets, and restored to worship only in 1988 (after a public campaign and a hunger strike by the dissident Zviad Gamsakhurdia). In 1819, under Russian rule, a prison was built beside it — held there, among others, was the writer Maxim Gorky.

faq.title: What's the building and interior like?
faq.content: It's a cross-dome (cross-cupola) church in the medieval Georgian tradition — a central dome on a drum over a cruciform plan, though a somewhat distinctive example with projecting apses and four free-standing columns supporting the dome. Built of warm brownish stone and largely undecorated outside, its effect is one of solidity and proportion rather than ornament — it looks as though it grows from the cliff. Inside it's relatively austere: the original medieval frescoes have not survived, so the walls are plain, but the candles and icons (including those of St. Shushanik and St. Abo) give it the feel of a living, active church.

faq.title: How do I visit, and what's the view like?
faq.content: It's on the cliff in Avlabari, on the left bank of the Mtkvari, reached from the Old Town across the Metekhi Bridge (or from Avlabari metro), and it's free and open during daylight hours; as an active church, dress modestly and be respectful around services. The reason many people come is the view: from the cliff terrace beside the church you look across the river to the full sweep of the Old Town — the balconied houses, Narikala Fortress on the ridge, the sulfur-bath domes of Abanotubani, the Bridge of Peace and Rike Park below, and Mtatsminda beyond. It's one of the best ground-level views in Tbilisi, and best of all in late-afternoon and sunset light.

leghvtakheviWaterfall.heroTitle: Leghvtakhevi Waterfall: A Hidden Gorge in the Heart of Tbilisi
leghvtakheviWaterfall.content:
A few minutes' walk from the sulfur-bath domes of Abanotubani, the dense fabric of Tbilisi's Old Town gives way, without warning, to a narrow basalt gorge. The canyon walls rise sharply on both sides, the street noise drops away almost at once, and a path runs along the stream bed — over wooden walkways and small bridges — toward a waterfall at the gorge's upper end. That this exists in the middle of the capital of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus), within five minutes of one of the busiest tourist districts in the country, is the kind of thing that takes a moment to register on first encounter.

What is the Leghvtakhevi Waterfall?

The Leghvtakhevi Waterfall is a roughly 22-meter waterfall at the head of a narrow basalt gorge in Tbilisi's Old Town, immediately behind the Abanotubani sulfur-bath district. The gorge is carved by the Tsavkisistsqali stream, which descends from the Mtatsminda range, runs through the National Botanical Garden, and drops over a basalt ledge as the waterfall before joining the Mtkvari. A short, easy path (about 15–20 minutes) leads from Abanotubani up the gorge to the falls, crossing the stream on small wooden bridges; the gorge's upper end connects to the Botanical Garden. The walk is free, open at all hours, and lit in the evening — an unexpected pocket of nature a few minutes from the heart of the Old Town. Its name comes from the Georgian leghvi, "fig," after the fig trees that once filled the gorge.

The gorge and the falls

The Leghvtakhevi gorge ("fig gorge") is carved by the Tsavkisistsqali stream — the same watercourse that runs through Abanotubani and is bound up with the natural hot springs that gave Tbilisi its name. The stream descends from the forested Mtatsminda slopes above the Old Town, passes through the National Botanical Garden, and where it drops over a basalt ledge before entering the narrower canyon it forms the waterfall — about 22 meters high — that gives this stretch its character. The falling water, the spray, the enclosing rock walls, and the vegetation clinging to the canyon sides create a microclimate that feels several degrees cooler and markedly quieter than the streets above. The gorge has a long history — historically it separated the old Kala district from the rest of the city, and the waterfall is mentioned in sources as far back as the 15th century — and the walkways and bridges that make today's walk so easy were renovated in 2012.

The walk

The approach from the Abanotubani end is straightforward and needs no particular fitness or preparation. The path follows the stream up the canyon, crossing it several times on small wooden bridges, the rock walls narrowing as you go deeper; it's a short walk — about 15–20 minutes of easy going — and well maintained and accessible for most visitors. In places the canyon walls are close enough to touch on both sides at once, which gives the walk a slightly enclosed, exploratory quality that contrasts nicely with the open spaces of the Old Town above. Most visitors spend around 30–40 minutes on the round walk with photos and a pause at the falls.

The seasons change the character of the place. In summer, as the stream runs lower, the falls are calmer — but the gorge still offers real relief from the city heat, the shade and moisture keeping it noticeably cooler than the exposed streets. In spring, with the stream swollen by rain and snowmelt off the upper slopes, the waterfall is at its most powerful and the sound carries the length of the gorge. In winter it can partly freeze. Continuing past the waterfall connects the walk to the National Botanical Garden, extending it into the broader natural landscape behind the Old Town.

An evening visit

The gorge and waterfall are lit after dark, and the mix of the lighting, the moving water, and the enclosed basalt walls makes for an atmosphere quite different from the daytime — slightly theatrical, the light catching the spray and the wet rock. Coming from the busy terraces of Shardeni Street or the Abanotubani baths into the sudden quiet of the canyon is one of the more unexpected contrasts available within a short walk in central Tbilisi.

Location and connections

The gorge links naturally to several of the most-visited parts of the Old Town. The lower entrance is beside Abanotubani, within a short walk of the Juma Mosque, Narikala Fortress, the Mother of Georgia, and the cable-car station. The upper end opens into the National Botanical Garden — so a combined visit (gorge, waterfall, and garden) makes a natural half-day through the southeastern corner of the Old Town and the landscape immediately behind it. The walk can be done in either direction, and pairing an upward route through the gorge with a return through the Old Town streets (or the reverse) makes the most of the contrast between the natural and the urban.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: Leghvtakhevi Gorge, behind the Abanotubani sulfur baths, Old Tbilisi — the entrance is accessible from the streets behind the bathhouses (well signposted once you're in Abanotubani).
  • Getting there: Walk from Abanotubani (the falls are at the end of the gorge path, past the Orbeliani/Chreli Abano bathhouse); about 15–20 minutes on foot from Liberty Square metro through the Old Town. Driving into the Old Town isn't advised — park centrally and walk.
  • Hours: Open at all times; the path is lit in the evening.
  • Admission: Free (the gorge walk; the Botanical Garden charges separately).
  • Tips: Wear comfortable shoes (the path can be uneven); allow ~30–40 minutes; combine with the sulfur baths and/or the Botanical Garden.
  • Combine with: Abanotubani and the sulfur baths, the Juma Mosque, Narikala Fortress and the Mother of Georgia, the cable car, and the National Botanical Garden — the whole southeastern Old Town cluster.

faq.title: What is the Leghvtakhevi Waterfall?
faq.content: It's a roughly 22-meter waterfall at the head of a narrow basalt gorge in Tbilisi's Old Town, right behind the Abanotubani sulfur-bath district. The gorge is carved by the Tsavkisistsqali stream, which flows down from the Mtatsminda range, through the National Botanical Garden, and over a basalt ledge as the falls before joining the Mtkvari. A short, easy, free path leads up the gorge from Abanotubani to the waterfall, crossing the stream on small wooden bridges — a surprising pocket of nature just a few minutes from the busiest part of the Old Town. Its name comes from the Georgian word for fig (leghvi), after the fig trees that once filled the gorge.

faq.title: How do you get there, and how long is the walk?
faq.content: The entrance is in the Abanotubani sulfur-bath district — follow the gorge path past the bathhouses (including the colorful Chreli Abano/Orbeliani Baths) and the waterfall is at the end. It's about a 15–20 minute walk from the Liberty Square metro station through the Old Town to Abanotubani, then a short, easy walk up the gorge itself; most visitors spend around 30–40 minutes there. The path is well maintained but can be uneven in places, so comfortable shoes help. Driving into the Old Town isn't advised — park centrally and walk.

faq.title: Is it worth visiting, and when is best?
faq.content: Yes — its appeal is the contrast: a quiet, cool, green gorge a few minutes from one of the busiest tourist districts in Georgia. In spring the falls are at their most powerful (swollen by snowmelt); in summer they're calmer, but the shaded, moist gorge is a genuine relief from the city heat; in winter the water can partly freeze. It's also worth seeing in the evening, when the gorge and waterfall are lit — a slightly theatrical, atmospheric experience quite different from the daytime walk.

faq.title: Is there an entrance fee?
faq.content: No — the gorge walk to the waterfall is free and open at all hours, with the path lit in the evening. (The National Botanical Garden at the upper end charges its own separate admission.)

faq.title: What can I combine it with?
faq.content: A lot, because it sits in the densest corner of the Old Town. The lower entrance is right by Abanotubani and its sulfur baths, within a short walk of the Juma Mosque, Narikala Fortress, the Mother of Georgia, and the cable-car station. The upper end opens into the National Botanical Garden. A combined gorge–waterfall–garden visit makes a natural half-day, and pairing it with a sulfur-bath soak in Abanotubani is one of the classic Old Town combinations.

holyTrinityCathedral.heroTitle: Holy Trinity Cathedral (Sameba): Tbilisi's Great Modern Cathedral
holyTrinityCathedral.content:
The Holy Trinity Cathedral — Tsminda Sameba in Georgian — stands on Elia Hill in the Avlabari district, on the left bank of the Mtkvari in Tbilisi, capital of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus). Its gold-covered dome rises around 84–87 meters above the city and is visible from an extraordinary range of vantage points across Tbilisi. It is the largest cathedral in Georgia, the seat of the Catholicos-Patriarch of All Georgia, and one of the tallest Orthodox Christian churches in the world. Consecrated in 2004 after roughly a decade of construction, it was built to mark the 1,500th anniversary of the autocephaly of the Georgian Orthodox Church and the 2,000th anniversary of Christianity — conceived not merely as a place of worship but as a physical statement about the continuity and post-Soviet revival of Georgian religious and national identity.

What is Sameba Cathedral?

Sameba (the Holy Trinity Cathedral) is the main cathedral of the Georgian Orthodox Church and the largest church in Georgia, standing on Elia Hill above the Avlabari district of Tbilisi. Designed by the architect Archil Mindiashvili and consecrated on 23 November 2004 (St. George's Day), it's a vast modern cathedral in a synthesis of historic Georgian styles, with a gold-covered central dome rising around 84–87 meters (and over 100 meters measured from its foundation in the hillside) — making it the tallest church in the country and among the tallest Orthodox churches in the world. It was built to commemorate 1,500 years of the Georgian Church's autocephaly and 2,000 years of Christianity, and funded largely by donations. It's an active cathedral, free to enter, and its golden dome is one of Tbilisi's defining landmarks.

A landmark unlike the city's others

Among Tbilisi's landmarks, Sameba occupies an unusual position. It's neither ancient nor architecturally experimental — it draws on the established vocabulary of medieval Georgian church architecture rather than attempting anything new — but its scale, hilltop position, and significance to the Georgian Orthodox community give it a presence and atmosphere that make it worth visiting, not just seeing from a distance. It was, notably, the one large-scale building project Georgia undertook during the 1990s — one of the darkest periods of the country's modern history, marked by civil war, economic collapse, and shortages of even electricity and running water — which says something about the symbolic weight invested in it.

Architecture and exterior

The cathedral consciously references the great medieval Georgian churches — the cruciform plan, the carved stone ornament, the conical drum and dome — while working at a scale medieval builders never attempted. Architecturally it's a cross-dome cathedral: a cruciform plan with a central dome carried on eight pillars and four smaller domes around it, synthesizing styles from different periods of Georgian church architecture with Byzantine undertones. What sets it apart from older Georgian churches is its pronounced verticality — the emphasis is upward, the effect closer to a single many-bulwarked tower than the compact, grounded form of the medieval churches it echoes. The result feels at once traditional and overwhelming, the familiar Georgian vocabulary amplified to dimensions that make even the largest medieval churches in the country look modest.

The exterior is clad in warm, light-toned stone in the Georgian tradition, which shifts through different registers with the light — pale and bright at midday, warm and saturated in late afternoon, almost amber at sunset when the low light catches the carved relief. The gold-covered dome and cross are the dominant feature from a distance and the element most visible across the city. Up close, the carved stone ornament repays examination — the decorative programs reference medieval Georgian motifs, executed by contemporary craftsmen working in a tradition over a thousand years old. Because the foundation is built into the hillside, the rear of the church sits partly underground, which is why its height measured from the foundation (over 100 meters) far exceeds its visible height above the courtyard.

The complex extends well beyond the main church: a bell tower, a lower/underground church, the Patriarch's residence, a seminary and monastery, ecclesiastical offices, gardens with viewpoints over the city, and a large paved courtyard for the substantial gatherings on major feast days — the whole precinct enclosed within walls on the hilltop.

A site with a contested history

The choice of site carries a difficult history that's worth knowing. Elia Hill was the location of Khojivank (Khojavank), a historic Armenian cemetery, and its Holy Mother of God (St. Astvatsatsin) church. The church and most of the cemetery had already been destroyed in the Soviet period (in the 1930s), and the area turned into a park; but graves remained, and during the cathedral's construction much of what was left was destroyed, with reports of unearthed remains and gravestones — which caused significant controversy and lasting hurt in the Armenian community. A small remnant survives nearby as the Armenian Pantheon of Tbilisi. The cathedral is venerated by many Georgians as a symbol of national and spiritual revival; for many Armenians the same site carries a painful loss. Both of those are true, and a visitor is better for knowing it.

Interior

The interior is vast, with the central space rising to a height that dwarfs the human figure — the vertical immensity that has been a deliberate effect of church architecture since the Byzantine period. The huge dome admits more light than older Georgian churches, making the central space notably brighter; the walls and dome carry fresco and mosaic work in the medieval Georgian iconographic tradition (by the artist Amiran Goglidze), and the gilded iconostasis catches the light from candles and high windows. The cathedral is relatively spare compared with some older churches, but it holds many of Georgia's most important icons.

The atmosphere is shaped as much by use as by architecture. Sameba is in continuous daily use, and at almost any hour people are present — some at services, some praying privately at the icon stands along the walls, some simply sitting in the quiet of the great space. That living worshipping community, rather than a museum-like stillness, is one of the more memorable aspects of a visit.

Visitors are expected to dress modestly — shoulders and knees covered, women with heads covered (headscarves are usually available at the entrance). Photography is generally permitted in the main interior but not during services, and the usual courtesies of an active place of worship apply throughout.

The surrounding area

The hilltop gives good views over the eastern parts of Tbilisi and across the city, though Narikala and Mtatsminda offer more sweeping panoramas. The approach passes through Avlabari — one of Tbilisi's older residential neighborhoods, historically Armenian in character (a connection that makes the Khojivank history all the more pointed) — and the streets around the cathedral's base are worth walking for their own interest: traditional houses, small churches, and ordinary neighborhood life continuing around the edges of the great ecclesiastical complex above.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: Elia Hill, Avlabari district, Tbilisi — left bank of the Mtkvari.
  • Getting there: About a 10–15 minute uphill walk from Avlabari metro station, or 15–20 minutes uphill from Rike Park; also reachable by taxi. The golden dome is visible from much of central Tbilisi, so it's easy to orient toward.
  • Hours: Open daily, roughly 08:00–20:00; services held through the day (visit outside service times for a fuller look, or quietly during them).
  • Admission: Free.
  • Dress code: Modest dress required — shoulders and knees covered; women cover their heads (scarves usually available at the entrance).
  • Combine with: Avlabari's streets and the Armenian Pantheon; Rike Park, the cable car, and the Bridge of Peace below; the Old Town across the river.

faq.title: What is Sameba Cathedral?
faq.content: Sameba — the Holy Trinity Cathedral (Tsminda Sameba) — is the main cathedral of the Georgian Orthodox Church and the largest church in Georgia, standing on Elia Hill above the Avlabari district of Tbilisi. Designed by the architect Archil Mindiashvili and consecrated in 2004, it's a vast modern cathedral with a gold-covered central dome rising around 84–87 meters, making it the tallest church in Georgia and one of the tallest Orthodox churches in the world. It was built to commemorate 1,500 years of the Georgian Church's autocephaly and 2,000 years of Christianity, and its golden dome is one of Tbilisi's defining landmarks, visible from across the city.

faq.title: When was it built, and why?
faq.content: Construction ran from 1995 to 2004 — the cathedral was consecrated on 23 November 2004, St. George's Day. It was conceived as a symbol of Georgian national and spiritual revival after the Soviet period, marking two great anniversaries: 1,500 years of the autocephaly (self-governance) of the Georgian Orthodox Church and 2,000 years since the birth of Christ. Notably, it was the one large-scale building project Georgia undertook during the 1990s — a decade of civil war, economic collapse, and severe shortages — which gives a sense of the symbolic weight placed on it. It was funded largely by donations.

faq.title: What's the architecture like?
faq.content: It deliberately draws on the medieval Georgian church tradition — a cruciform, cross-dome plan with a central dome carried on eight pillars and four smaller domes around it, in a synthesis of historic Georgian styles with Byzantine undertones — but at a scale far beyond anything medieval builders attempted, and with a pronounced upward, vertical emphasis uncommon in older Georgian churches. The exterior is clad in warm, light-toned stone with carved relief ornament, topped by a gold-covered dome and cross. Because the foundation is built into the hillside, the rear sits partly underground, so its height measured from the foundation exceeds 100 meters. Inside, the huge dome makes the central space brighter than most older Georgian churches; it holds frescoes, mosaics, a gilded iconostasis, and many of Georgia's most important icons.

faq.title: Is there any controversy about the site?
faq.content: Yes, and it's worth knowing. The cathedral stands on Elia Hill, the site of Khojivank (Khojavank), a historic Armenian cemetery and its church. Most of the cemetery and the church had already been destroyed in the Soviet period, but graves remained, and much of what was left was destroyed during the cathedral's construction, with reports of unearthed remains — which caused significant controversy and lasting hurt in the Armenian community. A small surviving remnant is preserved nearby as the Armenian Pantheon of Tbilisi. The cathedral is deeply venerated by many Georgians as a symbol of revival; for many Armenians the same site represents a painful loss. Both are part of its story.

faq.title: How do I visit, and what should I know?
faq.content: It's on Elia Hill in Avlabari — about a 10–15 minute uphill walk from Avlabari metro, or 15–20 minutes up from Rike Park, and easy to orient toward since the dome is visible from much of the city. It's open daily (roughly 08:00–20:00), with free entry; services run through the day, so visiting outside service times gives a fuller look. Dress modestly — shoulders and knees covered, and women cover their heads (scarves are usually available at the entrance). Photography is generally fine in the main interior but not during services. It's an active cathedral, so the usual respect for a place of worship applies.

greatSynagogue.heroTitle: The Great Synagogue of Tbilisi: Heart of a 2,500-Year-Old Community
greatSynagogue.content:
The Great Synagogue stands in the Old Town of Tbilisi, capital of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus), a short walk from the sulfur baths — its red-brick facade and arched windows marking it as a building of substance in a neighborhood already dense with the religious architecture of several traditions. Built in the early 20th century by Georgian Jews from Akhaltsikhe who had settled in the city — which is why it is also known as the "Synagogue of the Akhaltsikhe Jews" — it has served as a place of worship ever since, at the heart of a community whose roots in Georgia are among the deepest of any Jewish community in the world.

What is the Great Synagogue of Tbilisi?

The Great Synagogue (also called the Georgian Synagogue) is the largest synagogue in Georgia and the central house of worship for Tbilisi's Jewish community. It stands at 45–47 Kote Apkhazi Street (formerly Leselidze Street) in the Old Town, a red-brick, two-storey building in a restrained Neo-Moorish and Neo-Romanesque style, constructed in the early 20th century by Georgian Jews from Akhaltsikhe. It is the larger of Tbilisi's two active synagogues — the Georgian-Jewish (Sephardic-tradition) one — and remains an active religious institution, with two prayer halls, a women's gallery, and the ark oriented south toward Jerusalem. The Jewish presence in Georgia is one of the oldest continuous Jewish communities outside Israel, traditionally traced back some 2,500 years.

A community of remarkable antiquity

The Jewish presence in Georgia is traditionally dated to the 6th century BC, when Jewish exiles from the Babylonian captivity are said to have settled in the Caucasus. Whether or not that specific chronology holds in every detail, the documentary and archaeological evidence for a continuous Jewish presence in Georgia spanning at least two and a half millennia is substantial — making Georgian Jews one of the oldest uninterrupted Jewish communities outside the Land of Israel. (In 2014, Georgia marked 26 centuries of Georgian-Jewish coexistence.)

Over those centuries the community developed its own distinct traditions, its own liturgical customs, and a relationship with the surrounding Georgian Christian majority that was, by the standards of Jewish history elsewhere, remarkably stable. Georgian Jews were largely spared the systematic legal discrimination, the pogroms, and the expulsions that marked Jewish life across much of medieval and early-modern Europe. The relationship between the Jewish and Georgian Orthodox communities — not entirely without tension at various points — was generally one of tolerated coexistence that allowed the community to keep its institutions and its identity intact across the centuries. That long, relatively peaceful continuity is a genuine and distinctive part of the country's history.

The building

The Akhaltsikhe Jews who founded it settled in the Kvemo Kala quarter of the Old Town in the late 19th century; an earlier synagogue (a converted house) gave way, and the present building was constructed in the early 20th century — sources variously date the construction to between roughly 1895/1904 and 1910/1911, the work spanning several years and, by contemporary accounts, no small effort given its size.

It's a substantial red-brick, two-storey structure whose decoration draws, moderately, on Neo-Moorish and Neo-Romanesque elements — set back from the street behind a small forecourt, reached by stone steps, with its rear facade onto Tumanian Street. The architectural language is restrained rather than elaborate: large arched windows, symmetrical massing, a dignified solidity that gives the building a clear presence without competing with the visual drama of the Old Town's more ornate religious buildings. The impression is of a community confident enough in its position to build for permanence rather than display. The building was reconstructed and renovated in recent years (around 2009–2012).

Inside, it follows the Georgian-Jewish (Sephardic) tradition rather than the Ashkenazi plan — the building has two prayer halls, a lower and an upper, one on each floor, with a women's gallery and the ark, holding the Torah scrolls, set toward Jerusalem (south). The halls are lit by the large arched windows and decorated with painted plant and ornamental motifs; the ark is the most elaborate element. The synagogue holds Torah scrolls of considerable age, and the community's accumulated religious objects and furnishings give the interior a depth that the building's relatively recent date alone wouldn't suggest.

The community today

Georgia's Jewish population has declined sharply over the past several decades, as emigration to Israel — which began in earnest in the 1970s and accelerated after Georgian independence — reduced a community once numbering in the tens of thousands to a much smaller remainder. The Great Synagogue continues to serve that community, with services held regularly through the week and the largest gatherings on Shabbat (Friday evening and Saturday morning) and the Jewish holidays. The community maintains its Georgian Jewish customs and liturgy, which differ in various respects from both Ashkenazi and Sephardic practice and reflect its particular development in the Caucasus.

This is an active religious institution, not a museum, and visits are welcomed within the constraints of a working house of worship. The community is generally open to respectful visitors who come with appropriate awareness of the setting — and experiencing the synagogue as a living institution, with its own rhythms of prayer and community life continuing regardless of tourism, is much of what gives a visit its character. (Tbilisi has a second active synagogue nearby — the smaller Ashkenazi synagogue, Beit Rachel, run by Chabad — as well as the Historical-Ethnographic Museum of the Jews of Georgia, for those interested in the wider Jewish heritage of the city.)

Religious diversity in a single neighborhood

The synagogue's location places it within a short walk of Sioni Cathedral, the Juma Mosque, and several Armenian and Georgian Orthodox churches — making this compact part of the Old Town one of the most concentrated examples of multi-religious urban coexistence in the South Caucasus. The proximity isn't coincidental: it reflects the historical reality of Tbilisi as a trading crossroads where different communities maintained their religious and cultural identities in close physical proximity for centuries. Walking between these institutions in the course of an Old Town visit makes the point about Tbilisi's religious character more vividly than any single site can on its own.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: 45–47 Kote Apkhazi Street (formerly Leselidze Street), Old Tbilisi — a short walk from Meidan/Vakhtang Gorgasali Square and the sulfur baths.
  • Access: Generally open during daylight hours, around services; it's an active synagogue, so checking access before visiting is advisable, and visiting outside active service times is best.
  • Services: Daily, with Shabbat (Friday evening, Saturday morning) and Jewish holidays drawing the largest attendance.
  • Admission: Free; donations appreciated.
  • Dress code: Modest dress required. Men should cover their heads (kippot are usually available at the entrance); women should have shoulders and knees covered.
  • Etiquette: Approach as a place of worship — be discreet, ask before photographing, and respect any guidance from the community.
  • Combine with: Sioni Cathedral, Anchiskhati, the Juma Mosque, the Armenian and Georgian churches of the Old Town, Abanotubani, and the second (Ashkenazi) synagogue and the Jewish museum nearby — a compact multi-religious walk.

faq.title: What is the Great Synagogue of Tbilisi?
faq.content: The Great Synagogue (also called the Georgian Synagogue) is the largest synagogue in Georgia and the central house of worship for Tbilisi's Jewish community. It stands at 45–47 Kote Apkhazi Street (formerly Leselidze Street) in the Old Town — a red-brick, two-storey building in a restrained Neo-Moorish and Neo-Romanesque style, built in the early 20th century by Georgian Jews from Akhaltsikhe (which is why it's also known as the "Synagogue of the Akhaltsikhe Jews"). It's the larger of Tbilisi's two active synagogues, follows the Georgian-Jewish (Sephardic) tradition, and remains an active place of worship.

faq.title: How old is the Jewish community in Georgia?
faq.content: Among the oldest in the world. The Jewish presence in Georgia is traditionally traced to the 6th century BC, when exiles from the Babylonian captivity are said to have settled in the Caucasus. Whether or not that exact chronology holds, the evidence for a continuous Jewish presence spanning at least two and a half millennia is substantial — making Georgian Jews one of the oldest uninterrupted Jewish communities outside the Land of Israel (Georgia marked 26 centuries of Georgian-Jewish coexistence in 2014). Notably, the community was largely spared the systematic persecution, pogroms, and expulsions seen in much of Europe, living in generally tolerated coexistence with the Georgian Orthodox majority across the centuries.

faq.title: When was the synagogue built, and what's it like?
faq.content: It was built in the early 20th century — sources date the construction variously to between roughly 1895/1904 and 1910/1911, the work spanning several years. It's a substantial red-brick, two-storey building drawing moderately on Neo-Moorish and Neo-Romanesque styles, set back from the street behind a small forecourt and stone steps. Inside, following the Georgian-Jewish (Sephardic) tradition rather than the Ashkenazi plan, it has two prayer halls (a lower and an upper), a women's gallery, and the ark — holding Torah scrolls, some of considerable age — set toward Jerusalem. The interior is decorated with painted ornamental motifs and was reconstructed in recent years.

faq.title: Can visitors go inside?
faq.content: Yes, respectfully. It's an active synagogue rather than a museum, and the community generally welcomes visitors who come with appropriate awareness of the religious setting. It's best to visit outside active service times, and checking access beforehand is advisable. Modest dress is required: men should cover their heads (kippot are usually available at the entrance), and women should have shoulders and knees covered. Be discreet, ask before photographing, and follow any guidance from the community. Entry is free; donations are appreciated.

faq.title: What else is nearby?
faq.content: A great deal — which is part of the point. The synagogue sits in one of the most religiously diverse corners of the South Caucasus: within a short walk are Sioni Cathedral, the Juma Mosque, and several Armenian and Georgian Orthodox churches, plus Tbilisi's second (Ashkenazi) synagogue, Beit Rachel, and the Historical-Ethnographic Museum of the Jews of Georgia. It's also close to Abanotubani and the rest of the Old Town. Walking between these institutions makes the point about Tbilisi's long multi-religious coexistence more vividly than any single site could.

freedomSquare.heroTitle: Freedom Square: The Civic Heart of Tbilisi
freedomSquare.content:
Freedom Square sits where Rustaveli Avenue meets the edge of the Old Town, at the junction between the 19th-century city and the medieval core, between the main cultural boulevard and the winding streets of the historic district — a position that gives it a natural centrality in Tbilisi, capital of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus). It's at once a transit point, a civic space, a historical site, and the city's main orientation landmark, and most visitors pass through it several times without necessarily stopping to consider what it holds and what it has meant to the city. At its center stands the gilded St. George monument; around it, two centuries of Georgia's political transformations are written into the square's changing names and monuments.

What is Freedom Square?

Freedom Square (Tavisuplebis Moedani) is the central square of Tbilisi, at the eastern end of Rustaveli Avenue where the modern city meets the Old Town. At its center stands the Freedom Monument — a tall column topped by a gilded statue of St. George slaying the dragon, created by the Georgian sculptor Zurab Tsereteli and unveiled in 2006. The square has carried many names through Georgia's shifting history — Erivan Square under the Russian Empire, Freedom Square under the first Georgian republic of 1918, Beria and then Lenin Square in the Soviet period, and Freedom Square again since independence — and it has been the stage for major moments in the country's modern political life. For visitors, it's the most useful orientation point in central Tbilisi, with the Liberty Square metro station beneath it and the main central districts radiating outward within a short walk.

A square of many names

The square's current name is the most recent of several. Under the Russian Empire it was Erivan Square (after the city of Yerevan); earlier still it was a theatre square. It was first named Freedom Square in 1918, with the founding of the short-lived first Georgian republic. The Soviet administration renamed it — for a time Beria Square, then Lenin Square, placing a large statue of Lenin at its center (erected in 1956), as it did in central squares across the USSR. When the Lenin statue was removed around 1990–91 and Georgia regained independence, the square's name was restored to Freedom Square — a deliberate return to the 1918 name. The sequence of names and monuments is a compressed history of Georgia's political transformations over two centuries: the current dedication — freedom, St. George — reflects the self-understanding of independent Georgia as much as the Lenin statue once reflected the ideology of the Soviet state.

The St. George monument

The monument at the center of the square is the Freedom Monument: a tall column topped by a gilded figure of St. George on horseback, spear driven into the dragon below. It was designed by the Georgian sculptor Zurab Tsereteli (also responsible for the Chronicles of Georgia above the Tbilisi Sea) and unveiled on 23 November 2006 — St. George's day in the Georgian calendar — on the spot where Lenin had once stood (in the years between, the space held grass, a rose bed, and then a fountain). The column rises around 33–35 meters, the gilded statue about 5 meters, and the gold catches light from a distance — the glint above the surrounding buildings is visible from the upper reaches of Rustaveli Avenue.

St. George is the patron saint of Georgia. The country's name in Georgian, Sakartvelo, carries no etymological connection to the saint — but the identification between Georgia and George has been reinforced over centuries until it has become central to national iconography. The dragon-slayer carries several resonances at once: the Christian defeat of paganism, the nation's defense against its historical enemies, and the aspiration to freedom that the square's name invokes. Like the Bridge of Peace, the monument was not universally loved when it appeared — some admired its scale and symbolism, others felt it didn't suit the square — but it has become one of the city's defining images.

A stage for Georgia's modern history

Freedom Square and the avenue it anchors have been the setting for major public gatherings throughout Tbilisi's modern history, including moments of profound significance.

On 9 April 1989, Soviet troops violently dispersed a peaceful pro-independence demonstration on Rustaveli Avenue, at the Government House just up from the square, killing around twenty-one people — most of them women — and injuring many more. The April 9 tragedy became a turning point in Georgian public consciousness, and the date carries lasting weight: it is now marked as the Day of National Unity, and it was on 9 April 1991 that Georgia formally declared the restoration of its independence. It is commemorated every year.

In November 2003, the Rose Revolution — the peaceful protests that ended Eduard Shevardnadze's presidency and brought Mikheil Saakashvili to power — centered on Rustaveli Avenue and the Parliament building, with demonstrators famously carrying roses; Freedom Square was part of the same protest geography. These events give the square and its avenue a place in Georgian political memory well beyond their everyday function as a city-center crossroads.

Orientation and visiting
