For the visitor, Freedom Square is the most useful orientation point in central Tbilisi. Liberty Square metro station sits beneath it, connecting directly to Rustaveli and Avlabari stations, and it's the most central stop for both the Old Town and the Rustaveli corridor. The square is ringed by hotels, banks, the Tbilisi City Hall, and commercial buildings, and six streets radiate from it — Rustaveli Avenue west toward the cultural institutions; Pushkin, Leonidze, Shalva Dadiani, and Galaktion Tabidze streets; and the routes south and east into the Old Town. Begin a Tbilisi walking day here and a move in almost any direction brings you straight into the most historically and culturally dense parts of the city.

The square itself isn't a place designed for lingering — it's wide, traffic-bound on several sides, and lacks the intimacy of the Old Town streets or the greenery of Rike Park. Its interest is more historical and symbolic than experiential: the St. George monument, the surrounding institutions, and the sense of standing at the city's civic center are the reasons to pause here rather than simply pass through.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: Freedom Square (Tavisuplebis Moedani), central Tbilisi — the eastern end of Rustaveli Avenue, at the edge of the Old Town.
  • Metro: Liberty Square station (directly beneath the square).
  • What to see: The Freedom Monument (St. George column); the surrounding 19th-century and institutional architecture; the radiating historic streets.
  • Best use: As the starting point and orientation hub for a central Tbilisi walking day — Rustaveli Avenue one way, the Old Town the other.
  • Combine with: Rustaveli Avenue and its museums and theatres; the Old Town, Sioni and Anchiskhati, and Abanotubani; the Bridge of Peace and Rike Park — all within walking distance.

faq.title: What is Freedom Square in Tbilisi?
faq.content: Freedom Square (Tavisuplebis Moedani) is the central square of Tbilisi, at the eastern end of Rustaveli Avenue where the modern city meets the Old Town. At its center is the Freedom Monument — a tall column topped by a gilded statue of St. George slaying the dragon, by the Georgian sculptor Zurab Tsereteli, unveiled in 2006. The square is the city's main civic space and orientation point, ringed by hotels, banks, and the City Hall, with the Liberty Square metro station beneath it and the main central districts radiating out within a short walk. It has also been the stage for major moments in Georgia's modern history.

faq.title: Why has the square had so many names?
faq.content: Its changing names track Georgia's political history. Under the Russian Empire it was Erivan Square (after Yerevan); it was first named Freedom Square in 1918, under the short-lived first Georgian republic; in the Soviet period it became, for a time, Beria Square and then Lenin Square, with a large statue of Lenin at its center; and when Georgia regained independence around 1990–91, the name was restored to Freedom Square — a deliberate return to the 1918 name. So the current name isn't new — it's a revival, and the sequence of names and monuments is a compressed history of two centuries of Georgian political change.

faq.title: What is the statue in the middle?
faq.content: It's the Freedom Monument, commonly called the St. George Statue: a column around 33–35 meters tall topped by a roughly 5-meter gilded figure of St. George on horseback, spearing the dragon. It was created by the Georgian sculptor Zurab Tsereteli (who also made the Chronicles of Georgia monument) and unveiled on 23 November 2006, on the spot where a statue of Lenin once stood. St. George is the patron saint of Georgia, and the dragon-slayer stands for the defeat of evil, the defense of the nation, and the aspiration to freedom the square is named for. Like a number of modern Tbilisi monuments, it had a mixed reception when unveiled, but it's now one of the city's landmarks.

faq.title: What major historical events happened here?
faq.content: Freedom Square and Rustaveli Avenue have been the setting for pivotal moments in Georgia's modern history. On 9 April 1989, Soviet troops violently broke up a peaceful pro-independence demonstration on Rustaveli Avenue near the square, killing around twenty-one people, mostly women — the April 9 tragedy, now commemorated as the Day of National Unity (Georgia declared the restoration of its independence on the same date, 9 April 1991). And in November 2003, the peaceful Rose Revolution, which ended Eduard Shevardnadze's presidency and brought Mikheil Saakashvili to power, centered on Rustaveli Avenue and Parliament, with the square part of the same protest geography. These events give the square real weight in Georgian political memory.

faq.title: How do I get there, and is it worth stopping?
faq.content: It's in the center of Tbilisi, at the eastern end of Rustaveli Avenue, with the Liberty Square metro station directly beneath it — the most central stop for both the Old Town and Rustaveli. It's the single most useful orientation point in the city: six streets radiate from it into the main central districts, so it's the natural place to start a walking day. The square itself isn't somewhere to linger — it's wide and traffic-bound, without the intimacy of the Old Town or the greenery of Rike Park — but it's worth a pause for the St. George monument, the surrounding architecture, and the sense of standing at the city's civic center before heading off in any direction.

bridgeOfPeace.heroTitle: The Bridge of Peace: Tbilisi's Modern Landmark Crossing
bridgeOfPeace.content:
The Bridge of Peace opened in May 2010 and has become one of the most debated — and most photographed — structures in Tbilisi, capital of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus). A pedestrian crossing over the Mtkvari River, it provoked strong opinions when it was built, and has since settled into the city's visual identity in a way that now feels almost inevitable. Designed by the Italian architect Michele De Lucchi, it's a curved steel-and-glass canopy roughly 150 meters long, its translucent, wave-like roof arcing above the walkway and lit at night by thousands of LEDs programmed to send shifting patterns across the surface. It links the Old Town with Rike Park, and it's both a genuinely useful crossing and one of the reliable set-piece views of the city.

What is the Bridge of Peace?

The Bridge of Peace is a bow-shaped pedestrian bridge over the Mtkvari (Kura) River in central Tbilisi, opened in May 2010. Designed by Italian architect Michele De Lucchi, with lighting by French designer Philippe Martinaud, it's a roughly 150-meter steel-and-glass canopy lit by thousands of LEDs that switch on about 90 minutes before sunset. It connects the Old Town riverfront with Rike Park (and the cable car up to Narikala) on the opposite bank, making it both a scenic landmark and a practical crossing. Its sharply modern design against the medieval backdrop made it controversial when built; it's now one of the city's most recognizable sights — best seen at dusk.

A modern bridge in a historic setting

The Bridge of Peace occupies one of the most visually loaded spots in the city. Looking upstream from the bridge, the Metekhi Church stands on its cliff above the left bank with the Vakhtang Gorgasali statue beside it, Narikala Fortress rises on the ridge behind the Old Town, and the domes of Abanotubani show through the gap in the buildings to the south. The modern glass and steel in the foreground against the medieval and 19th-century architecture behind it produces the old-versus-new contrast that Tbilisi's recent development has created across the city — concentrated here in a single frame.

That contrast is exactly why the bridge was divisive. When it opened, a number of locals and public figures criticized its assertively contemporary design as out of place against the historic skyline (it picked up some unflattering nicknames along the way). Others welcomed it as a sign of a modernizing city. More than a decade on, the argument has largely subsided into familiarity — the bridge has become one of Tbilisi's defining images, whatever one's view of the aesthetics.

There's also a concept embedded in it. De Lucchi designed the LED lighting (realized by lighting designer Philippe Martinaud) to do more than shimmer: the lights pulse a message in Morse code — the names of the chemical elements that make up the human body — intended as, in the architect's words, a message of life and peace between people and nations. Hence the name: the Bridge of Peace. (The steel-and-glass structure itself was prefabricated in Italy and shipped to Tbilisi for assembly.)

The bridge and its setting

The southern end connects to the Old Town riverfront, where the streets into the historic district begin their climb through the traditional neighborhoods toward Abanotubani and Narikala. The northern end opens onto Rike Park, the long riverside park along the left bank, which runs to the cable car station at its eastern end (the cable car climbs to Narikala Fortress). This makes the Bridge of Peace not just a scenic crossing but a genuinely useful urban connector — the most direct pedestrian route between the Old Town, the park, the cable car, and the left-bank neighborhoods.

Crossing takes only a few minutes, but the views from the midpoint are worth a pause. Looking east toward the Old Town bend of the river, the Metekhi cliff, the water, and the historic buildings above make one of the more complete river-level panoramas of historic Tbilisi. Looking west toward the newer center, the glass facades of recent development against the forested hills behind give a sense of how rapidly — and unevenly — the city has been changing.

At night

The LED lighting is the bridge's most spectacular feature, and the reason most photographers time their visit for after dark. The lights cover the whole underside and structure of the canopy, switching on about 90 minutes before sunset, and are programmed into slowly shifting patterns that illuminate the bridge from within and reflect off the river below. In the surrounding darkness the lit structure seems to float above the water, and the combination of the glowing bridge, the floodlit Metekhi Church on its cliff upstream, and the wider nighttime skyline makes one of the most reliably photogenic compositions in Tbilisi. It's at its best in the hour after sunset, when enough light remains in the sky to give the surroundings depth and color while the LEDs are already fully active.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: Over the Mtkvari River, central Tbilisi — connecting the Old Town (south/right bank) with Rike Park (north/left bank).
  • Hours: Open 24 hours, freely accessible at all times; the lights come on about 90 minutes before sunset.
  • Admission: Free.
  • Best time: The hour around dusk (for the light and the sky together); good by day for the views in both directions.
  • Combine with: Rike Park and the cable car up to Narikala Fortress (on the Rike side); the Old Town, Abanotubani, Metekhi Church, and the Vakhtang Gorgasali statue (on the Old Town side). It's the natural link between the two riverbanks.

faq.title: What is the Bridge of Peace in Tbilisi?
faq.content: The Bridge of Peace is a bow-shaped pedestrian bridge over the Mtkvari (Kura) River in central Tbilisi, opened in May 2010. Designed by the Italian architect Michele De Lucchi, with lighting by French designer Philippe Martinaud, it's a roughly 150-meter curved steel-and-glass canopy lit at night by thousands of LEDs. It connects the Old Town riverfront with Rike Park on the opposite bank, and it's both a practical pedestrian crossing and one of the most recognizable and photographed landmarks in the city — especially after dark.

faq.title: Who designed it, and when did it open?
faq.content: It was designed by the Italian architect Michele De Lucchi — who also designed several other modern Tbilisi buildings, including the Ministry of Internal Affairs and the Presidential (Ceremonial) Palace — and officially opened on 6 May 2010. The LED lighting was created by the French lighting designer Philippe Martinaud, and the steel-and-glass structure was prefabricated in Italy and shipped to Tbilisi for assembly.

faq.title: Why is it called the "Bridge of Peace"?
faq.content: The name reflects a concept built into the lighting. The LEDs don't just shift in pattern — they pulse a message in Morse code spelling out the chemical elements that make up the human body, intended by De Lucchi as a message of life and peace shared by all people and nations. So the bridge's name and its light show are linked: it's meant as a small monument to what humans have in common.

faq.title: Was the bridge controversial?
faq.content: Yes. When it opened in 2010, its assertively modern glass-and-steel design drew strong reactions — some locals and public figures criticized it as out of place against the medieval and 19th-century architecture of the Old Town, while others saw it as a symbol of a modernizing city. More than a decade on, that debate has largely faded into familiarity, and the bridge has become one of Tbilisi's defining images. It's a clear example of the old-versus-new tension running through the city's recent development.

faq.title: When is the best time to visit?
faq.content: The bridge is open and free 24 hours, but it's at its best around dusk. The LED lights switch on about 90 minutes before sunset, so the hour just after sunset — when there's still color and depth in the sky while the lights are fully active — gives the most striking effect, with the illuminated bridge appearing to float above the river and the floodlit Metekhi Church on its cliff upstream. By day it's worth crossing for the views in both directions: the historic Old Town and Metekhi cliff upstream, the newer city center downstream.

chroniclesOfGeorgia.heroTitle: The Chronicles of Georgia: Tbilisi's Monumental History in Stone and Bronze
chroniclesOfGeorgia.content:
On a hill above the Tbilisi Sea in the northern outskirts of the city, the Chronicles of Georgia rise from the landscape at a scale that stops most visitors on first sight. Sixteen massive columns, each up to around 35 meters tall, are covered from base to summit in dense relief depicting the full sweep of Georgian history and Christian faith. Created by the celebrated Georgian sculptor Zurab Tsereteli (1934–2025), the monument was begun in 1985 and left unfinished when the Soviet Union collapsed — a circumstance that adds a layer of meaning to the site, as a work never formally completed or inaugurated, yet standing as one of the most powerful artistic statements in Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus). It's well off the usual tourist trail, and the combination of the monument and the wide views over the reservoir gives a visit here a real sense of discovery.

What are the Chronicles of Georgia?

The Chronicles of Georgia (also called the History Memorial of Georgia, and nicknamed the "Georgian Stonehenge") is a monumental sculpture on Keeni Hill above the Tbilisi Sea, in the northern outskirts of Tbilisi. Created by Zurab Tsereteli from 1985 and never fully finished, it consists of 16 huge columns, up to about 35 meters tall, clad in bronze and stone relief: the upper sections depict the kings, queens, and heroes of Georgia, the lower sections scenes from the life of Christ and Georgian Christian history. It was conceived to commemorate 3,000 years of Georgian statehood and 2,000 years of Christianity. Reached by a long flight of stairs and freely open at all hours, it's a dramatic, lesser-visited landmark with sweeping views — at its best at sunset.

The monument

The sixteen columns are arranged into facing groups around a central space, reached by a grand staircase. Each column carries two iconographic programs stacked vertically. The upper sections depict the kings, queens, and national heroes of Georgia — the great rulers whose reigns defined the country's political and cultural history. The lower sections carry scenes from the life of Christ and from Georgian Christian history, grounding the nation's political story in the religious identity that has been inseparable from Georgian statehood since the 4th century. The whole was conceived as a tribute to roughly three millennia of Georgian statehood and two millennia of Christianity — a striking choice of subject given that it was begun under the officially atheist Soviet system.

The carving is dense and assured, the individual faces specific enough to reward close looking. The columns are clad in monumental bronze relief (with stone), and the dark, near-black weathered surfaces give the reliefs a heaviness and permanence that lighter materials wouldn't; in low, angled light — especially late afternoon and evening — the carved surfaces take on a three-dimensionality that photographs struggle to convey. Between and around the columns, further figures and relief panels extend the narrative into the surrounding space, so the overall effect is of a whole landscape of stone-and-bronze figures — a compressed version of Georgian history made physically present at a scale that insists on being taken seriously.

A few additional elements reward attention. At the foot of the approach stairs stands a row of statues of the Thirteen Assyrian Fathers — the 6th-century monks who, by tradition, spread Christianity and founded monasteries across Georgia (also Tsereteli's work). Near the stairway is a cross representing St. Nino's grapevine cross — the cross of vine bound with her own hair, carried by the saint credited with converting Georgia. And behind the columns sits a small chapel (dedicated to the Annunciation), modest and frescoed, in the understated manner of Georgian churches.

An unbuilt Stalin, an unfinished monument

The site has its own layered backstory. By several accounts, the hilltop had originally been set aside in the earlier Soviet period for a planned, enormous (around 100-meter) statue of Stalin that was never built — the spot sat empty for decades until Tsereteli's project was assigned to it in the 1980s. There's an irony worth sitting with: a place once intended to glorify a Soviet leader instead became a vast monument to Georgian statehood and the Christian faith. And then it, too, was overtaken by history — work began in 1985, the Soviet Union collapsed before the monument could be completed, and Tsereteli (by then based in Moscow) continued only intermittently. It has never been formally finished or inaugurated. That unfinished, unofficial quality is part of what gives the site its particular, slightly haunted power.

The setting and views

The hill overlooks the Tbilisi Sea — a large artificial reservoir built in the early 1950s — and the views from the monument across the water and the surrounding landscape are among the more expansive anywhere near Tbilisi. The site sits well out from the center, in a part of the city most visitors never reach, and the combination of the monument and the open panorama gives the visit a quality of discovery that more central attractions rarely offer.

Sunset is the most dramatic time: the low western sun catches the reliefs, the reservoir below turns gold, the columns seem to glow against the darkening sky, and the full scale of the composition becomes apparent in a way midday light flattens. The site is open-air and freely accessible at any hour, so an early-morning or late-evening visit — without other people present — is entirely possible (it's sometimes illuminated at night).

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: Keeni Hill, above the Tbilisi Sea, northern outskirts of Tbilisi — well out from the center.
  • Getting there: It's a fair way from the city center and not on the main tourist routes; most visitors come by taxi or as part of a tour (public transport gets you only partway, then a walk/climb). Allow time for the journey.
  • Hours: Open-air, freely accessible 24 hours.
  • Admission: Free; no ticket office.
  • Best time: Sunset (or sunrise) for the light and the views; midday sun on the open hilltop can be harsh and hot.
  • Bring: Sun protection and water (little shade); comfortable shoes for the stairs.
  • Combine with: The Tbilisi Sea itself; it pairs as a half-day with a trip out of the center rather than with the Old Town sights.

faq.title: What are the Chronicles of Georgia?
faq.content: The Chronicles of Georgia (also called the History Memorial of Georgia, and nicknamed the "Georgian Stonehenge") is a monumental sculpture on a hill above the Tbilisi Sea, in the northern outskirts of Tbilisi. Created by the sculptor Zurab Tsereteli from 1985 and never fully finished, it's made up of 16 huge columns — up to about 35 meters tall — clad in bronze-and-stone relief depicting Georgian history and Christianity: the upper parts show the kings, queens, and heroes of Georgia, the lower parts scenes from the life of Christ and Georgian Christian history. It was conceived to commemorate 3,000 years of Georgian statehood and 2,000 years of Christianity, and it's reached by a long staircase, with sweeping views over the reservoir.

faq.title: Who built it, and why is it unfinished?
faq.content: It was created by Zurab Tsereteli (1934–2025), the celebrated Georgian sculptor also responsible for the St. George statue on Tbilisi's Freedom Square and many large-scale works around the world. He began the monument in 1985, but the collapse of the Soviet Union and the funding problems that followed left it incomplete; Tsereteli, based in Moscow, continued only intermittently, and it was never formally finished or inaugurated. That unfinished, unofficial state is part of the site's atmosphere. Interestingly, the hilltop had reportedly once been set aside for a giant statue of Stalin that was never built — so a spot once meant to glorify a Soviet leader became a monument to Georgian statehood and faith instead.

faq.title: Why is it called the "Georgian Stonehenge"?
faq.content: It's a nickname based on atmosphere rather than architecture — walking among the towering columns and looking up at the carved faces of kings and saints against the sky gives a sense of being dwarfed by something on a more-than-human scale, which reminds people of Stonehenge. But unlike Stonehenge, the Chronicles are densely narrative: every surface is covered with figures, scenes, and symbols telling specific stories from Georgian history and Christianity.

faq.title: What else is there to see at the site?
faq.content: Besides the main columns, look for the row of statues of the Thirteen Assyrian Fathers (the 6th-century monks who spread Christianity in Georgia) near the approach stairs, a cross representing St. Nino's grapevine cross, and a small frescoed chapel behind the columns. But the real draw alongside the monument is the setting: the hill overlooks the Tbilisi Sea, a large artificial reservoir from the early 1950s, with some of the widest views available near the city.

faq.title: How do you get there, and when should you go?
faq.content: It's well out from the center, in the northern outskirts on Keeni Hill, and not on the main tourist routes — most visitors come by taxi or as part of a tour (public transport only gets you partway, followed by a walk and a climb up the stairs). It's open-air, free, and accessible 24 hours. The best time to visit is sunset (or sunrise), when the low light catches the reliefs and the reservoir below turns gold; midday sun on the exposed hilltop can be harsh, so bring water, sun protection, and comfortable shoes.

tetnuldiSkiResort.heroTitle: Tetnuldi Ski Resort: Georgia's High-Alpine Freeride Mountain in Svaneti
tetnuldiSkiResort.content:
Tetnuldi is the most serious ski destination in Svaneti and one of the most compelling in all of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus), set on the high slopes of the Tetnuldi massif about 15 kilometers from Mestia, with lifts reaching around 3,165 meters. Georgia's second-largest resort after Gudauri and the main one in Svaneti, it skis in a landscape unlike anywhere else in the country — a world of largely open, above-treeline snowfields and glaciated peaks where the terrain feels genuinely alpine, and where the surrounding Caucasus panorama on clear days is among the finest from any ski resort in the region. For experienced skiers, especially freeriders, it's the standout ski mountain in the country.

What is Tetnuldi?

Tetnuldi is a high-altitude ski resort on the Tetnuldi massif, about 15 km from Mestia in Upper Svaneti, Georgia. Opened in 2016 (in use from 2015) and still developing, it runs between roughly 2,265 and 3,165 meters, served by a system of chairlifts (three POMA chairlifts plus a side lift), with several pistes — the longest about 9.5 km, among the longest runs in the South Caucasus — and, above all, extensive off-piste and freeride terrain that has made it the best-regarded freeride resort in Georgia. The skiing is mainly intermediate to advanced (not ideal for absolute beginners), the season runs December through April, and most visitors stay down in Mestia and drive up for the day. The combination of high-alpine terrain, deep Svaneti snow, and a Caucasus panorama is what sets it apart.

The terrain

Tetnuldi's defining quality is its openness and scale. Much of the resort is above the treeline, so the slopes spread in broad, uninterrupted snowfields from the lift stations down toward the valley — a spaciousness unusual even by high-altitude Alpine standards, giving the skiing a sense of freedom and scale that more enclosed mountains can't match. (Lower down there's some birch and fir woodland, which adds tree-line freeride options; higher up it's open alpine bowls, ridges, and couloirs.) On clear days the horizon is defined by the peaks of the main Caucasus ridge rather than by a treeline, and the sense of skiing in genuine high-mountain terrain rather than on a developed resort hill is constant.

The groomed pistes cover intermediate and a little easier terrain in the lower sections, with the more technical runs on the steeper upper faces — Tetnuldi suits intermediate and advanced skiers best, and isn't really a place for absolute beginners (the gentlest run feeds into an intermediate valley descent). But it's the freeride that has earned Tetnuldi its reputation: the open bowls, the high ridgelines, and the consistent, heavy snowfall of the Svaneti high country combine to create off-piste of a quality that in Western Europe would need a helicopter or hours of skinning to reach. The powder holds well after a storm thanks to the cold and the many north-facing aspects, and the resort has a genuine following among European and international freeride skiers who travel to Svaneti specifically for this terrain.

The upper sections approach the glacier zones of the massif and, in good snow, give the longest, most sustained descents. The flip side of all that open, high terrain is exposure: wind and weather can change conditions fast, and the same openness that's glorious in clear weather means visibility can drop suddenly and become disorienting. Skiing the upper mountain in deteriorating weather takes experience and judgment, and Tetnuldi's patrol and infrastructure are more limited than at big Alpine resorts — so self-sufficiency matters more here. For off-piste, carrying avalanche safety equipment and ideally going with a local guide is strongly advisable.

The surrounding landscape

The view from the upper lifts is one of the defining experiences Tetnuldi offers. The Caucasus ridge stretches both ways from the massif, with Shkhara — Georgia's highest peak at about 5,193 meters — in one direction and the distinctive twin summit of Ushba in another on clear days. (Svan legend has it that Ushba and Tetnuldi were lovers, separated and turned to mountains to gaze at each other forever — when one is sad, both are wrapped in cloud.) Below, the Svaneti valleys and the tower clusters of the ancient villages give a foreground of human scale against the mountain backdrop. On the exceptional days when the whole horizon is clear from the top stations, the panorama is among the finest from any ski resort in the Caucasus.

Practical considerations

The drive from Mestia to the base station takes roughly 30 minutes to an hour up the road through the Mulkhura valley, climbing through forest before reaching the open high-mountain environment of the resort. The road is maintained in winter but can be affected by heavy snow, and a 4WD vehicle (or the shuttle service) is needed in winter — check conditions before driving after a big snowfall. Accommodation at the resort itself is limited, so most visitors base in Mestia and travel up for the day.

The season typically runs December through April (sometimes into May), with January through March the most consistent. The high altitude gives good snow reliability, and the late season (March–April) can offer excellent spring skiing — stable weather, firm morning snow softening through the day.

Equipment rental and instruction are available at the base, and facilities improve each season as the resort develops — but they remain more limited than at established European destinations. Skiers coming from Western Europe should calibrate expectations for the hospitality and catering side accordingly: the skiing and the terrain are genuinely world-class; the supporting infrastructure is still maturing. (Svaneti's other, gentler resort, Hatsvali, just above Mestia, is the better choice for beginners and families, and an easy pairing.)

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: On the Tetnuldi massif, up the Mulkhura valley, about 15 km from Mestia, Upper Svaneti, Georgia.
  • Getting there: By car or taxi from Mestia, roughly 30 minutes to an hour to the base station; a 4WD or the shuttle is needed in winter.
  • Elevations: Base ~2,265 m; top lift ~3,165 m (vertical ~900 m). Longest run ~9.5 km.
  • Lifts / terrain: Chairlifts (POMA); intermediate-to-advanced pistes plus extensive freeride/off-piste — Georgia's premier freeride resort. Not ideal for absolute beginners.
  • Season: December–April (best January–March).
  • Hours: Lifts generally run roughly 09:00–16:00 in season, weather permitting.
  • Stay / combine with: Base in Mestia (the towers, the Svaneti Museum, the Margiani and Mikheil Khergiani house museums); pair with the gentler Hatsvali resort, and in summer the area's hikes.

faq.title: What is Tetnuldi Ski Resort?
faq.content: Tetnuldi is a high-altitude ski resort on the Tetnuldi massif, about 15 km from Mestia in Upper Svaneti, Georgia. Opened in 2016 and still developing, it runs between roughly 2,265 and 3,165 meters, served by chairlifts (three POMA chairlifts plus a side lift), with several pistes — the longest about 9.5 km, among the longest runs in the South Caucasus — and, above all, extensive freeride and off-piste terrain. It's Georgia's second-largest resort after Gudauri, the main one in Svaneti, and widely considered the best freeride mountain in the country. The skiing is mainly intermediate to advanced.

faq.title: Is Tetnuldi good for beginners?
faq.content: Not really — it's best for intermediate and advanced skiers, and especially for freeriders. The pistes lean intermediate-to-challenging, and even the gentlest run feeds into an intermediate valley descent, so absolute beginners aren't well served here. Beginners and families are much better off at Svaneti's other resort, Hatsvali, just above Mestia, which has gentle slopes and easy lift access. Many visitors combine the two — Hatsvali to learn or cruise, Tetnuldi for the serious terrain.

faq.title: Why is Tetnuldi known for freeriding?
faq.content: Because of the terrain and the snow. Much of the resort is above the treeline, with open bowls, high ridgelines, and couloirs, and the Svaneti high country gets consistent, heavy snowfall that holds well thanks to the cold and the many north-facing aspects. The result is off-piste of a quality that in Western Europe would require a helicopter or hours of climbing to reach — which has earned Tetnuldi a real following among European and international freeride skiers. If you ski off-piste, carry avalanche safety gear and ideally go with a local guide: the terrain is serious and patrol infrastructure is limited.

faq.title: How do you get to Tetnuldi, and where do you stay?
faq.content: Tetnuldi is about 15 km from Mestia, up the Mulkhura valley — roughly a 30-minute-to-one-hour drive, for which a 4WD (or the shuttle service) is needed in winter, as the mountain road can be affected by heavy snow. There's little accommodation at the resort itself, so almost everyone bases in Mestia, the Svaneti capital, which has plenty of guesthouses and hotels (and is a remarkable place in its own right) and travels up for the day.

faq.title: When is the ski season?
faq.content: Roughly December through April, sometimes into May, with January through March the most consistent. The high altitude gives good snow reliability through the season, and the late season (March–April) can produce excellent spring skiing — stable weather and firm morning snow that softens as the day warms. Whenever you go, the upper mountain is high and exposed, so be ready for fast weather changes and check conditions before heading up after heavy snow.

crossOverMestia.heroTitle: The Cross Over Mestia: Svaneti's Best Easy Viewpoint
crossOverMestia.content:
Above the rooftops and towers of Mestia, in the Svaneti region of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus), a large white cross stands on an open ridge that gives one of the most complete panoramas of Upper Svaneti reachable from anywhere near the town. The cross is an ordinary religious monument of the kind set on prominent hilltops all over Georgia, but its position — on a ridge (Tskhakazagari) rising sharply above Mestia, commanding a wide arc of the surrounding mountains — makes it far more significant as a viewpoint than as a shrine. For most visitors, the walk up to the cross is the most natural way to get above the valley floor and see the geography of the region from above rather than from within it. It's also the first, lower stage of the longer hike to the Koruldi Lakes, which makes it a good short option in its own right.

What is the Cross Over Mestia?

The Cross Over Mestia is a large white cross on the Tskhakazagari ridge above the town of Mestia, in Upper Svaneti, reached by a moderate hike of about 1.5–2.5 hours' ascent (or partway by car). At around 2,150 meters, it offers a wide, near-360° panorama: the whole town and its Svan towers laid out below, the Enguri valley, and an arc of Greater Caucasus peaks — above all the twin-peaked Mount Ushba — on the horizon. It's the most popular short viewpoint hike from Mestia, free and always open, and doubles as the first section of the route up to the Koruldi Lakes.

The hike

The trail starts in the town center and climbs through the upper residential streets of Mestia before entering the forest on the hillside above. The ascent is steady rather than technical — a clear path gaining height through the trees, firm enough to require some effort but within reach of anyone with reasonable fitness and proper footwear. The forest gives shade on the lower sections, and the gradual emergence from the trees onto the open upper ridge marks the point where the views start to open and the climb begins to feel worth it.

The round trip from the town center takes most walkers around 2 to 3 hours at a comfortable pace — roughly 1.5 to 2.5 hours up and faster down. The path is clear and well trodden, and navigation is easy, with the cross visible from various points on the way to give both orientation and motivation. A partial approach by car is possible on the road winding up the hillside, cutting the walking for those who'd rather limit the effort — though the full hike is more rewarding, and the forest section has its own pleasant character apart from the destination.

A note on footwear and water: grippy shoes help, since the trail can be muddy after rain (frequent in Svaneti) and the final open-ridge section gets slippery when wet. Carry water in warm weather — there are no facilities on the trail or at the viewpoint.

The view

The ridge just below the cross gives the first big views, and the panorama widens as you climb the last stretch to the monument. From the cross, the whole spread of Mestia lies below — the compact town center, the Svan towers rising above the houses in their distinctive clusters, the guesthouses and newer buildings spreading out from the historic core, and the Enguri valley running north and south beyond the town.

The mountain horizon is what makes it. Caucasus ridgelines rise on several sides, their upper reaches snow-covered through much of the year and glaciated higher up. Mount Ushba — its twin summits one of the most recognizable profiles in the whole Caucasus — stands out toward the north on clear days, the extra height of the viewpoint lifting the sightline above the intervening terrain so it reads more clearly than it often does from the valley floor. The Tetnuldi massif, the Chalaadi glacier valley, and more distant peaks of the main Caucasus ridge complete a horizon that, on a fully clear day, sweeps almost the whole way around.

Light matters here. Morning visits — especially the first hour after sunrise, when the light is directional and shadows pick out the topography of the mountain faces — give the landscape a depth that flat midday light flattens out. Sunset is the other prime moment, when the western light catches the tower clusters below and the peaks above at once, and the shift through gold and orange into the blue of the mountain evening is one of the quietly remarkable things Svaneti offers — with the cross silhouetted against the sky in a way photographers reliably find compelling.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: On the Tskhakazagari ridge above Mestia town center, Upper Svaneti, Georgia (~2,150 m). The trail starts from the upper residential area — asking locally for directions to the cross will get you to the trailhead quickly.
  • Access: On foot from Mestia (about 1.5–2.5 hours' ascent), or partway by car on the hillside road, leaving a shorter walk to the cross.
  • Hours / admission: Freely accessible at all times; free.
  • Bring: Grippy footwear (muddy/slippery when wet) and water in warm weather — no facilities on the trail or at the top.
  • Best time: Early morning for the clearest mountain light and the best chance of an unobstructed Ushba; late afternoon and sunset for atmosphere.
  • Combine with / extend to: The town's museums and towers, and — for a much longer day — continue past the cross up to the Koruldi Lakes (the cross is the first stage of that route). It pairs naturally with the Margiani and Mikheil Khergiani house museums, the Svaneti Museum, the Chalaadi Glacier, and Hatsvali/Zuruldi.

faq.title: What is the Cross Over Mestia?
faq.content: It's a large white cross on the Tskhakazagari ridge above the town of Mestia, in Upper Svaneti, Georgia — and one of the most popular short viewpoint hikes in the region. At around 2,150 meters it gives a wide, near-360° panorama: the whole town and its Svan towers below, the Enguri valley, and an arc of Greater Caucasus peaks on the horizon, with the twin-peaked Mount Ushba the standout. The cross is an ordinary hilltop religious monument of the kind found across Georgia, but its position makes it far more notable as a viewpoint. It's free and always open, and it's also the first stage of the longer hike up to the Koruldi Lakes.

faq.title: How hard is the hike to the Cross, and how long does it take?
faq.content: It's a moderate hike — steady but not technical. The round trip from Mestia town center takes most people about 2 to 3 hours: roughly 1.5 to 2.5 hours up through the residential streets and forest, and faster coming down. The path is clear and well trodden, with the cross visible from various points for orientation. Anyone with reasonable fitness and proper footwear can manage it. If you'd rather shorten it, a road winds partway up the hillside by car, leaving a short walk to the cross.

faq.title: What can you see from the Cross?
faq.content: A full sweep of Upper Svaneti. Directly below is Mestia — the town center, the clusters of Svan towers, and the Enguri valley running north and south. On the horizon, Caucasus ridgelines rise on several sides, snow-covered and glaciated up high, with Mount Ushba's twin summits the most recognizable feature (clearer from here than from the valley floor), plus the Tetnuldi massif and the Chalaadi glacier valley. On a fully clear day the mountain horizon wraps almost all the way around.

faq.title: When is the best time to go?
faq.content: Early morning — especially the first hour after sunrise — gives the most directional light, the best depth on the mountain faces, and the best chance of a clear, unobstructed Ushba before cloud builds. Sunset is the other prime time, when the low western light catches the towers below and the peaks above together and the cross is silhouetted against the sky. Either way, clear weather is what delivers the full view, so it's worth picking your moment.

faq.title: Is it free, and what should I bring?
faq.content: Yes — the viewpoint is freely accessible at all times and there's no charge. Bring footwear with good grip (the trail can be muddy after Svaneti's frequent rain, and the open final ridge is slippery when wet) and carry water in warm weather, as there are no facilities on the trail or at the cross.

shkharaGlacier.heroTitle: Shkhara Glacier: The Hike to Georgia's Highest Peak from Ushguli
shkharaGlacier.content:
The Shkhara Glacier descends from the southern face of Mount Shkhara — at about 5,193 meters, Georgia's highest peak — into the upper Enguri valley above Ushguli, in the Svaneti region of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus). Its terminus is reached on foot up the valley from the village, and it's one of the more accessible glacial environments in the Greater Caucasus, needing no technical equipment or mountaineering experience. The combination of the glacier, the immense mountain wall at the head of the valley, and the wide-open approach makes it one of the most rewarding walks from Ushguli — and for travelers who've already made the effort of reaching Ushguli, one of Europe's more remote inhabited places, it extends the high-Svaneti experience into terrain the village itself, for all its character, can't offer.

What is the Shkhara Glacier hike?

The Shkhara Glacier hike runs up the Enguri valley from Ushguli to the foot of the glacier beneath Mount Shkhara, Georgia's highest peak (~5,193 m), in Upper Svaneti. Along with the Chalaadi Glacier near Mestia, it's one of the only Caucasus glaciers reachable on an easy day hike — no technical skill required. The full walk is about 14–16 km round trip and takes roughly 5–6 hours, mostly flat along the valley floor with a short climb to the ice at the end (terminus around 2,390 m, where the Enguri River is born). It can be shortened to about 2–3 hours by taking a 4WD jeep or a horse part-way up the valley — the common choice for day-trippers from Mestia. The glacier is the dramatic source of the Enguri, and the walk is mostly about the experience of the valley and the great south wall of Shkhara at its head.

The mountain and the glacier

Mount Shkhara dominates the upper Enguri valley — its southern face a wall of rock, ice, and snow that fills the head of the valley and defines the northern horizon of Ushguli. It's part of the main divide of the Greater Caucasus, forming a stretch of the border between Georgia and Russia, with extensive glaciation across its summit plateau and ridges. Shkhara is one of the more technically demanding peaks in Georgia to summit — complex glacier terrain, serious objective hazard from ice and rockfall on the upper mountain, and the altitude challenges of any Caucasus 5,000-meter peak. The walk from Ushguli reaches nowhere near that world; it goes only to the glacier terminus, not into the serious mountaineering ground above. But the proximity of that world, visible overhead in clear weather, gives the hike a scale and context that lower walks elsewhere in Georgia can't match.

The Shkhara Glacier itself is one of the longer glaciers in the Georgian Caucasus, fed from the accumulation zones of the high plateau and descending through icefalls and flatter sections to its terminus in the valley above Ushguli. Like glaciers everywhere, it has retreated markedly over the past century — the moraines of earlier, more extensive positions are visible as ridges and mounds of debris well below the present front, a tangible record of how much ice has been lost. The current terminus is an active glacial front where the proglacial river emerges from beneath the ice, the grey-blue of glacier ice showing where the surface is exposed. The terminus position and the amount of visible ice vary with the season and recent weather, but in the main summer months the glacier is clearly visible and the approach brings you close enough to take in its scale and character.

The walk

The route follows the Enguri valley upstream from Ushguli, on a jeep track and paths along the valley floor, moving from the alpine meadows around the village (you'll pass grazing horses and cattle) through progressively more glacial terrain — boulder fields, moraine, the braided channels of the proglacial river — as the valley narrows and bends toward the glacier. It isn't a formally marked trail, but it's easy to follow in good visibility: the valley topography points you straight toward the head, and there's nowhere really to get lost (you'll cross a few small streams). The ground changes underfoot — grass and soft ground near the village giving way to rough moraine and river gravel near the ice — so footwear with grip and reasonable ankle support is worth having.

The full walk is about 14–16 km round trip and takes most people roughly 5–6 hours, with moderate, gradual elevation gain rather than steep climbing — the valley floor rises slowly, with a short climb to the ice at the end. Crucially, the walk can be substantially shortened: jeeps run part-way up the valley (parking at a point from which you continue on foot over the final moraine), and horseback trips are easy to arrange in Ushguli — either option cuts the glacier visit to roughly 2–3 hours, which is how most people doing it on a day trip from Mestia manage it. There's a small café/snack bar partway up the valley (by a bridge, where jeeps park) for a rest.

The views improve as the valley narrows and the surrounding peaks rise above the walls. Shkhara dominates the head of the valley from early on, but the full scale of its southern face — the ridges, the hanging glaciers, the ice cliffs where sections break away — only becomes comprehensible as you move deeper in and the angle opens upward. In clear conditions the blue sky against the white and grey of the upper mountain has a visual intensity hard to anticipate from photographs, and the silence of the upper valley — broken only by the river, the occasional crack of ice, and the wind — gives the walk a focused quality the busier sites of Georgia can't.

At the glacier

At the terminus, the shift from the living valley to the geological world of the ice is immediate. The proglacial river emerges cold and grey-green with glacial flour — the fine rock ground from the valley walls by the moving ice — and the air turns noticeably colder near the ice mass. The glacier's sounds are audible up close: meltwater moving, the occasional crack or groan of ice under its own weight, and in warm weather the steadier sound of surface melt running off the ice. Not the dramatic calving of a sea-front glacier, but the sounds of a living geological process, giving the terminus a quality of active presence the static grandeur of the mountain above doesn't convey in the same way.

A safety note for the terminus: as at any glacier front, keep a sensible distance and take care — rockfall is a real hazard here, with rocks of all sizes coming down across and from the glacier without warning. Don't climb onto the ice or linger directly beneath the front or the loose moraine slopes. The moraine you walk on for the final approach — rock, gravel, and debris left as the ice retreated — is stable enough but uneven, and slippery after rain; among it you'll find boulders faceted and scratched by their transit within the ice, a whole range of rock types carried down from across the mountain, like a geological collection assembled by the glacier.

Weather and conditions

The upper Enguri valley is subject to the rapid weather changes of the high Greater Caucasus, and conditions can shift significantly between leaving Ushguli and returning. Clear mornings often give way to cloud over the peaks by midday, and summer afternoon thunderstorms are common — bringing lightning, rain, and a sharp temperature drop to exposed valley terrain that offers no shelter. Start early — by 7 or 8 am, when conditions are usually most settled — for the best chance of reaching the glacier in clear weather and returning before the afternoon turns; and remember the full walk is a 5–6 hour commitment, so an early start matters. Turning back in deteriorating weather is always the right call, however close the glacier looks.

The glacier is reachable from roughly June through October, with July, August, and September the most reliable for clear conditions and snow-free terrain. Early June may have residual snow on the approach depending on the winter; October brings the first autumn snowfall (and beautiful autumn color) but can affect the route late in the month. Midsummer also shows the glacier at its clearest, once the winter snow that obscures the surface in spring has melted to expose the blue-grey ice.

Combining with Ushguli

The glacier walk fits naturally into a stay of a night or two in Ushguli — the visit structure that does most justice to both the village and the valley. With the Mestia–Ushguli road now paved (since 2024), cutting the drive to about 1.5–2 hours each way, and with the jeep/horse option to shorten the valley approach, a glacier visit is feasible on a longer day trip from Mestia — but it's tight, and an overnight is far better: it lets the glacier take one morning or afternoon while Ushguli's towers, the Ethnographic Museum, and Lamaria Church fill the rest, for a complete experience of the upper Enguri valley at its best.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: Up the Enguri valley above Ushguli, Mestia Municipality, Upper Svaneti, Georgia; terminus around 2,390 m, beneath Mount Shkhara (~5,193 m).
  • The walk: ~14–16 km round trip, ~5–6 hours, moderate/gradual; or ~2–3 hours if shortened by 4WD jeep or horse part-way up the valley.
  • Getting to Ushguli: From Mestia (~45 km), about 1.5–2 hours on the road paved in 2024, by shared marshrutka or private taxi; Ushguli is also the endpoint of the multi-day Mestia–Ushguli trek.
  • Essentials: Sturdy footwear, warm and waterproof layers, sun protection, food and water. Start early. Keep a safe distance from the glacier front (rockfall).
  • Best time: June–October (July–September most reliable).
  • Admission: Free.
  • Combine with: Ushguli itself — the Svan towers, Lamaria Church, the Ethnographic Museum, and the ruined Queen Tamar's Castle.

faq.title: What is the Shkhara Glacier hike?
faq.content: It's a walk up the Enguri valley from Ushguli to the foot of the Shkhara Glacier, beneath Mount Shkhara — Georgia's highest peak at about 5,193 meters — in Upper Svaneti. Along with the Chalaadi Glacier near Mestia, it's one of the only Caucasus glaciers reachable on an easy day hike, needing no technical skill. The glacier is the source of the Enguri River, and the walk is mostly along the open valley floor with the great south wall of Shkhara filling the head of the valley.

faq.title: How long is the Shkhara Glacier hike?
faq.content: The full walk is about 14–16 km round trip and takes most people roughly 5–6 hours — mostly flat along the valley with a short climb to the ice at the end (terminus around 2,390 m). It's not technical, but it's a real half-to-full-day walk, not a short stroll. You can shorten it considerably — to about 2–3 hours — by taking a 4WD jeep or a horse part-way up the valley, which is how most people visiting on a day trip from Mestia do it. Either way, start early and be off the exposed valley before the afternoon weather.

faq.title: Is the hike difficult, and is it safe?
faq.content: The walk itself is moderate and non-technical — the valley floor rises gradually — so it suits anyone with reasonable fitness in proper footwear. The main cautions are the weather and the glacier front. The upper valley is fully exposed, with frequent summer afternoon thunderstorms (lightning, rain, sharp cold) and no shelter, so start early and turn back if conditions deteriorate. At the glacier itself, keep a safe distance: rockfall is a real hazard at the front, with rocks coming down across the ice without warning, so don't climb onto the ice or stand directly beneath the front.

faq.title: Can you visit the Shkhara Glacier on a day trip from Mestia?
faq.content: It's possible now, though still tight. The Mestia–Ushguli road was paved in 2024, cutting the drive to about 1.5–2 hours each way, and you can shorten the valley approach to the glacier to 2–3 hours by jeep or horse — so a day trip taking in both Ushguli and the glacier is feasible. But it's a full, rushed day, and staying a night (or two) in Ushguli does both the village and the glacier far more justice: the glacier can take one morning or afternoon while the towers, Lamaria Church, and the Ethnographic Museum fill the rest.

faq.title: When is the best time to do it?
faq.content: June through October, with July, August, and September the most reliable for clear weather and snow-free terrain. Early June may still have snow on the approach depending on the winter; October brings the first autumn snow (and lovely autumn color) but can affect the route late in the month. Midsummer shows the glacier at its clearest, once the winter snow covering the surface has melted to reveal the blue-grey ice. Whenever you go, the valley is exposed to fast-changing mountain weather, so carry warm and waterproof layers and start early.

svanTowers.heroTitle: Svan Towers: The Medieval Stone Towers of Upper Svaneti
svanTowers.content:
The Svan towers are the most instantly recognizable feature of Upper Svaneti's villages, in the Svaneti region of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus) — stone structures rising 20 to 25 meters above the houses around them, their slightly tapering profiles and small defensive openings giving the skylines of Mestia, Ushguli, and the dozens of smaller settlements between them a silhouette with no close parallel anywhere in Georgia or the wider Caucasus. Between roughly the 9th and 12th centuries, Svan families built these towers in their hundreds across the upper Enguri valley and its tributaries, and enough survive in good condition that the landscape they created — clusters of towers above the stone houses, the glaciated Caucasus peaks behind — remains essentially intact as a visual and cultural environment. That preservation is why the historic villages of Upper Svaneti were inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1996.

What are the Svan towers?

The Svan towers (Georgian: koshki) are medieval defensive stone towers, built mostly in the 9th–12th centuries, that define the villages of Upper Svaneti, Georgia. Tall (up to about 25 m), square (roughly 5×5 m at the base), and tapering, they were dry-stone built without mortar, with a raised entrance reached by a removable ladder and several interior floors used as a family's refuge during clan feuds and raids — as well as a marker of a family's wealth and standing. Several hundred survive, concentrated in Mestia (the regional capital) and Ushguli (the cluster of villages at the head of the Enguri valley). The whole landscape is a UNESCO World Heritage Site (Upper Svaneti, inscribed 1996), and the towers are still part of living, inhabited villages rather than a museum.

Why the towers were built

The towers grew out of the specific conditions of medieval Svan life. Svaneti's isolation largely protected it from outside conquest, but not from internal conflict: the clan-based social structure produced feuds between families that could run across generations, and raids and attacks between neighboring clans were a constant of highland life. The tower was the ultimate refuge for a family under attack — a narrow entrance set several meters above the ground, reached only by a ladder that could be pulled up; thick stone walls able to absorb assault; upper levels from which to watch and respond on all sides; and storage for the food, water, and weapons to outlast a siege. (The family lived in the attached house, the machubi; the tower was for defense and storage, not everyday living.)

The towers were also status symbols. A family able to build and maintain one demonstrated its wealth, labor, and standing in terms every other family could read at a glance — the height (generally 20–25 m, some higher) signaling ambition and resource, the quality of the stonework reflecting the skill and materials a family could command. They were built without mortar, their stones fitted so precisely that the load is distributed across the full height of the structure — which is how the walls have held their integrity across eight or nine centuries. (The exact mix of purposes — defense, storage, status, even signaling — is still debated by scholars, but defense and prestige are the core of it.)

Architecture and construction

The structural logic is consistent across the region, even as individual towers vary in detail and quality. The base is the most massively built section, with walls up to about two meters thick giving a stable foundation; the structure tapers as it rises, the walls thinning to reduce weight while keeping the efficiency of the near-pyramidal form. Inside, three to five floors are connected by wooden ladders rather than fixed stairs — so the ladders could be pulled up between levels during a siege, stopping attackers who breached the ground floor from simply climbing.

The exterior is largely plain, the builders' ambition concentrated on the precision of the stonework rather than on carved ornament. The small openings — narrow at the upper levels, slightly wider lower down — let in light and allowed observation while minimizing exposure to projectiles. The crenellated, often machicolated parapet at the top of many towers served both defense and the visual effect that makes a Svan tower cluster so recognizable against the sky.

The towers in Mestia

Mestia, the administrative center of Svaneti and the main gateway for most visitors, has the largest number of towers accessible in a town setting — dozens, in various states of preservation, scattered through the residential neighborhoods (the Lekhtagi, Lagami, and Lanchvali districts have the densest groupings) alongside modern buildings and guesthouses. Walking through Mestia means moving through a streetscape where medieval defensive architecture and present-day Georgian provincial life sit side by side without apparent contradiction — a tower above a vegetable garden, another storing firewood, a third carefully kept as a monument. Several towers are open to visitors, independently or as part of compounds like the Margiani House Museum (whose tower you can climb) and the Mikheil Khergiani House Museum; the interior — low ceilings, darkness, the thick walls pressing in — gives a physical understanding of the defensive purpose that no outside view can.

The towers at Ushguli

The fullest, most dramatic expression of the tower landscape is at Ushguli, the cluster of villages at the head of the Enguri valley, about 45 km from Mestia. At around 2,100 meters — one of the highest continuously inhabited settlements in Europe — Ushguli has its towers rising above the stone houses against the wall of the Shkhara massif (Georgia's highest peak), which fills the eastern horizon. Its core village, Chazhashi, is so densely preserved that UNESCO records more than 200 medieval structures there — tower-houses, churches, and castles — making it the heart of the World Heritage site. The combination of the density of the towers, the antiquity of the surrounding architecture, and the mountain backdrop has made Ushguli one of the most photographed places in Georgia.

The villages that make up Ushguli — Chazhashi, Murqmeli, Chvibiani, and Zhibiani — are still inhabited year-round by a small permanent Svan community, and the towers here are features of a functioning settlement, not museum exhibits, woven into daily life across many centuries. Walking the lanes of Chazhashi, towers on every side and the Shkhara glacier closing off the head of the valley, is one of the more genuinely transporting experiences in Georgia.

Seeing the towers

You can see the towers freely from the streets of any tower village — they stand among ordinary houses, and viewing them costs nothing. Several individual towers are open for interior climbs for a small fee (often just a few lari), availability varying by tower and season; the Margiani House Museum in Mestia is the most reliable place to go inside one as part of a guided visit. Allow an hour or two to walk the main tower quarters of Mestia. The towers reward unhurried looking — and the best understanding comes from pairing the outside view with one interior climb and a stop at the Margiani or Svaneti museum for the social context.

Getting there and visiting

  • Where: Throughout Upper Svaneti, with the main concentrations in Mestia and Ushguli and tower villages scattered along the Enguri valley between them.
  • Access: Free to view from the streets of the villages. Some individual towers open for interior visits for a small fee — ask locally for current availability.
  • Ushguli: About 45 km from Mestia; the road was paved in 2024, cutting the drive to roughly 1.5–2 hours, by shared marshrutka or private taxi (still a mountain road that can close in deep winter). Ushguli is also the endpoint of the multi-day Mestia–Ushguli trek.
  • In Mestia: Freely accessible as part of exploring the town; the Margiani House Museum is the best place to climb a tower's interior.
  • Combine with: In Mestia — the Margiani and Mikheil Khergiani house museums and the Svaneti Museum of History and Ethnography; in Ushguli — Lamaria Church, the Ethnographic Museum, and the Shkhara Glacier hike.

faq.title: What are the Svan towers?
faq.content: The Svan towers (Georgian: koshki) are medieval defensive stone towers that define the villages of Upper Svaneti, in the mountainous northwest of Georgia. Built mostly between the 9th and 12th centuries, they're tall (up to about 25 m), square (roughly 5×5 m at the base), and slightly tapering, built dry-stone without mortar, with a raised entrance reached by a removable ladder and several interior floors. Each served as a family's refuge during clan feuds and raids — and as a visible marker of the family's wealth and standing. Several hundred survive, concentrated in Mestia and Ushguli, and the whole landscape is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

faq.title: Why were the Svan towers built?
faq.content: Mainly for defense and status. Svaneti's isolation protected it from outside conquest but not from internal conflict — the clan-based society produced long-running family feuds and frequent raids — so each family's tower was its last refuge: a high entrance reached by a ladder that could be pulled up, thick walls, upper floors for lookout and defense, and storage to outlast a siege. The towers were also status symbols, their height and the quality of their stonework signaling a family's wealth and standing. The family itself lived in the attached house (the machubi); the tower was for defense and storage, not everyday living.

faq.title: How old are the Svan towers, and how were they built?
faq.content: Most were built between the 9th and 12th centuries, during the Georgian Golden Age, though the tradition of tower-building in the region goes back much further. They were built dry-stone — without mortar — the stones fitted so precisely that the load is distributed across the full height, which is how they've survived eight or nine centuries. The base walls are up to about two meters thick, tapering as the tower rises, with three to five interior floors linked by wooden ladders (removable in a siege) and small defensive openings in the walls.

faq.title: Where can you see the Svan towers?
faq.content: Throughout Upper Svaneti, but the two main places are Mestia and Ushguli. Mestia, the regional capital, has dozens of towers among its houses and guesthouses (densest in the Lekhtagi, Lagami, and Lanchvali districts), with the Margiani House Museum the best place to climb inside one. Ushguli, the cluster of villages at the head of the Enguri valley about 45 km away, has the most dramatic concentration — its core village of Chazhashi preserves more than 200 medieval structures and is the heart of the UNESCO site, all set against the wall of Mount Shkhara, Georgia's highest peak.

faq.title: Can you go inside a Svan tower, and is there a fee?
faq.content: You can see the towers for free from the streets — they stand among ordinary houses in the villages. Several individual towers are open for interior climbs for a small fee (often just a few lari), though availability varies by tower and season. The most reliable place to go inside one is the Margiani House Museum in Mestia, where a tower is open as part of a guided visit of a traditional Svan compound — climbing the narrow, dark floors gives a physical sense of the towers' defensive purpose that looking from outside can't.

lamariaChurch.heroTitle: Lamaria Church: Ushguli's Ancient Church beneath Shkhara
lamariaChurch.content:
Lamaria Church stands on a hill at the upper edge of Zhibiani, the highest of the four hamlets that make up Ushguli, in the Upper Svaneti region of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus), looking directly toward the Shkhara massif — at about 5,193 meters, Georgia's highest peak — its glaciers and rock faces filling the eastern horizon at a distance that feels impossibly close. The church is small and built from the same dark local stone as the medieval towers around it, and on its own its plain exterior might pass almost unnoticed. What gives the site its weight is everything around it: the cluster of Svan towers, the old cemetery with its carved stone markers on the slope below, and the glacier-hung peaks rising above — a setting that has made Lamaria framed against Shkhara one of the most photographed scenes in the entire Caucasus.

What is Lamaria Church?

Lamaria is a 9th–10th-century Georgian Orthodox church on a hill above Zhibiani, the uppermost village of Ushguli, in Upper Svaneti — a UNESCO World Heritage site since 1996. A small single-nave stone church with a defensive Svan tower and a fortified wall, dedicated to the Dormition of the Mother of God, it's one of the most revered sacred sites in Svaneti, holding medieval frescoes in several layers (10th to 13th century) and a deep place in Svan religious life. Its name reflects a distinctively Svan blend of the veneration of the Virgin Mary with an older highland tradition. But it's the setting that draws most visitors: the church framed against Mount Shkhara, Georgia's highest peak, is among the iconic images of the Caucasus.

Lamaria and Svan faith

The dedication connects the church to one of the most distinctive aspects of Svan religious culture. The name "Lamaria" derives from the Svan veneration of the Virgin Mary — but it blends Orthodox devotion with an older, pre-Christian Svan tradition: Lamaria was also a female deity associated with motherhood, fertility, and the hearth, and in highland belief she is held as a protector of Svaneti and of Svan women in particular. The church (dedicated to the Dormition of the Mother of God) has been a center of local devotion for over a thousand years, in a mountain community whose faith long carried this syncretic character — absorbing Christianity while keeping older ritual forms alongside it. The observances held at Lamaria on certain feast days, combining Orthodox liturgy with Svan ceremonial tradition, are among the more distinctively regional religious events still practiced in Georgia. It remains an active church, and visitors may find a quiet local service underway.

History, frescoes, and the Tamar association

The church dates to the 9th or 10th century, from the early medieval period when Christianity was consolidating its hold on Georgia's highland regions, among the last to fully convert. Architecturally it's a modest single-nave hall church with a projecting apse, decorative blind arches, and an ambulatory along the south and west sides, built of neatly hewn stone and enclosed by a defensive wall that ends in a Svan tower on the west.

Inside, medieval frescoes survive in several layers, fragmentary now after centuries of exposure to this extreme high-altitude climate. The earliest belong to the 10th century — including a Crucifixion on the north wall with an unusual detail, the Virgin touching her head rather than her cheek in mourning, and faces in the characteristic Svan manner (low forehead, aquiline nose, wide expressive eyes). A later phase, around the 12th century, includes two equestrian warrior saints, St. George and St. Theodore, facing each other on the west wall, with some elements thought to date as late as the 13th century. The paintings are faded — the candles that light the dark interior have not helped their preservation — but legible enough to convey the devotional art of this remote highland church across the centuries. A small collection of icons, crosses, and liturgical objects accumulated through the community's long worship is kept inside. (Unusually for a Georgian church, photography is generally permitted here.)

As at dozens of major Georgian sites, local tradition links the church to Queen Tamar (whose reign, 1184–1213, marked the height of medieval Georgia), in the form of attributed patronage. Whether Tamar specifically funded work here or the association simply reflects the common tendency to tie the greatest Georgian ruler to the country's important monuments, the church's significance to Ushguli long predates and outlasts any single royal connection. (A separate ruined fortress above the nearby hamlet of Chazhashi is known locally as Queen Tamar's Castle — a distinct site from the church.)

The setting and the approach

Reaching Lamaria from the center of Ushguli is a short walk — ten minutes or so — up through the lanes, past tower compounds and the cemetery where generations of Svan families lie beneath the region's carved stone markers. The approach is as much a part of the experience as the church: the building reveals itself gradually within its frame of towers, graves, and mountains, building toward the view from the church terrace rather than handing it over at once.

From the low wall at the edge of the grounds, the view east toward Shkhara is direct and unobstructed — the glacier descending from the summit above the rocky ridgeline, the lower glacier's moraines in the middle distance, and the upper Enguri valley running between the church and the mountain's base. The combination — the stone church behind you, the towers of Ushguli to either side, Shkhara filling the horizon — is the composition that has made Lamaria one of the most reproduced images in Georgian landscape photography. By most accounts the reality, reached after the walk up through the village, matches and exceeds the photographs.

Ushguli and getting there

Lamaria is inseparable from Ushguli itself — a visit to the church is part of experiencing one of the most remarkable inhabited landscapes in Europe, not a standalone stop. Ushguli's four hamlets (Murqmeli, Chazhashi, Chvibiani, and Zhibiani) spread across the upper Enguri valley at around 2,100–2,200 meters, among the highest continuously inhabited settlements in Europe, and the whole of Upper Svaneti was inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 1996 for its medieval tower-houses and living mountain culture.

The journey from Mestia to Ushguli — about 45 kilometers up the upper Enguri valley — was for years a rough, slow drive on an unpaved road, but the road was fully paved in 2024, cutting the trip to roughly 1.5 to 2 hours and opening it to ordinary vehicles in good conditions. It's still a mountain road through high terrain and a string of Svan villages, with extraordinary scenery the whole way; in deep winter or heavy snow it can still close, so check conditions seasonally. From Mestia you can take a shared marshrutka or hire a private taxi, and Ushguli is also the endpoint of the celebrated multi-day Mestia–Ushguli trek.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: On a hill at the edge of Zhibiani, Ushguli, Mestia Municipality, Upper Svaneti, Georgia — about 45 km from Mestia, at roughly 2,100 m, beneath Mount Shkhara.
  • Getting there: From Mestia by paved road (since 2024), about 1.5–2 hours, by shared marshrutka or private taxi; then a short (~10-minute) walk up from the village. Ushguli is also the end of the Mestia–Ushguli trek.
  • Hours / admission: The grounds are freely accessible at all times; entry is free. Interior access depends on a key-holder in the village — visiting by day and asking locally usually secures it.
  • Dress: As an active church, dress modestly — shoulders and knees covered; head coverings for women expected.
  • Combine with: The rest of Ushguli (the towers, the Ethnographic Museum, the ruined Queen Tamar's Castle above Chazhashi), the Shkhara Glacier hike up the valley, and the wider Svaneti sights around Mestia.

faq.title: What is Lamaria Church?
faq.content: Lamaria is a small 9th–10th-century Georgian Orthodox church on a hill above Zhibiani, the highest of the four hamlets of Ushguli, in Upper Svaneti — a UNESCO World Heritage site. A single-nave stone church with a defensive Svan tower and a fortified wall, dedicated to the Dormition of the Mother of God, it holds medieval frescoes in several layers (10th to 13th century) and is one of the most revered sacred sites in Svaneti. Its real fame, though, is its setting: the church framed against Mount Shkhara, Georgia's highest peak, is one of the most photographed scenes in the Caucasus.

faq.title: What does the name "Lamaria" mean?
faq.content: "Lamaria" comes from the Svan veneration of the Virgin Mary, but it carries a distinctively Svan blend of Christian and older highland belief: Lamaria was also a pre-Christian female deity associated with motherhood, fertility, and the hearth, and in Svan tradition she is regarded as a protector of Svaneti and especially of Svan women. The church is dedicated to the Dormition of the Mother of God, and its observances on certain feast days combine Orthodox liturgy with Svan ceremonial traditions — among the more distinctively regional religious practices still alive in Georgia.

faq.title: How old is the church, and what are the frescoes?
faq.content: It dates to the 9th or 10th century. Inside are medieval frescoes in several layers: the earliest from the 10th century (including a Crucifixion on the north wall with the Virgin touching her head in mourning, painted in the characteristic Svan style), a 12th-century phase with the warrior saints George and Theodore on horseback on the west wall, and some elements thought to be as late as the 13th century. They're faded after centuries of exposure and candle smoke, but legible. Unusually for a Georgian church, photography inside is generally allowed.

faq.title: Is the church really connected to Queen Tamar?
faq.content: Local tradition links the church to Queen Tamar (reigned 1184–1213) through attributed patronage — as it does at dozens of important Georgian sites. Whether she funded work here specifically or the connection reflects the broader habit of tying Georgia's greatest medieval ruler to its major monuments, the church mattered to Ushguli long before and after any royal link. Note that the ruined fortress above the nearby hamlet of Chazhashi, known as Queen Tamar's Castle, is a separate site from the church.

faq.title: How do you get to Lamaria and Ushguli?
faq.content: Ushguli is about 45 km from Mestia. The road was fully paved in 2024, cutting the drive to roughly 1.5–2 hours and opening it to ordinary cars in good conditions (though it's still a mountain road that can close in deep winter, so check seasonally). From Mestia you can take a shared marshrutka or a private taxi, and Ushguli is also the end point of the multi-day Mestia–Ushguli trek. The church is a short (~10-minute) walk up from the village; the grounds are free and always open, while interior access depends on finding the key-holder, so visit by day and ask locally. As an active church, dress modestly.

margianiHouseMuseum.heroTitle: Margiani House Museum: Inside a Traditional Svan Home in Mestia
margianiHouseMuseum.content:
The Margiani House Museum is a preserved traditional Svan family compound in Mestia, in the Svaneti region of Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus), and the most direct encounter you can have with how the people of this highland region actually lived through the centuries of their relative isolation. It isn't a reconstruction or a curated interpretation but the genuine, largely intact home of one Svan family — the Margiani — kept as a museum with the physical reality of the living space intact: a home designed and used for survival in a mountain environment where self-sufficiency wasn't an aspiration but a necessity. Probably the most famous Svan house-museum in Mestia, it gives the towers you see all over the village the human reality they sheltered.

What is the Margiani House Museum?

The Margiani House Museum is the historic residential compound of the Margiani family — one of Mestia's most powerful Svan clans — preserved as a museum in the Lanchvali district of the town, in Upper Svaneti. The complex centers on the machubi, the traditional Svan communal living hall built around an open hearth, alongside a tall defensive tower, a cellar and storage rooms, and a courtyard. The family was powerful enough to once own eight towers, of which four survive — their number and height a measure of the clan's standing. Associated with the wider Svaneti Museum of History and Ethnography, it's one of Mestia's most visited sights, and it gives visitors a spatial, sensory understanding of Svan domestic life that no description can match. Entry is paid, usually with a guide.

The machubi

The machubi is where most visitors spend most of their time. It's a large, low-ceilinged hall built around a central open hearth, with a smoke hole in the roof above as the only ventilation for the fire that was the room's main heat through the long Svan winters. The darkness is immediately striking — small windows admitting little light, walls and ceiling blackened by centuries of hearth smoke — and the sense of enclosure is a direct experience of the conditions in which Svan families lived, worked, cooked, ate, and slept for months at a stretch when the snow outside made extended outdoor life impossible.
