Rabati was comprehensively (and controversially) reconstructed in 2012 into a polished, visitor-friendly complex; Sapara has been restored with far more discretion and keeps its authentic, secluded, lived-in atmosphere — a genuinely old monastery deep in a quiet forest gorge, with original medieval architecture and frescoes. Many visitors find the contrast worthwhile: the grand, illuminated showpiece of Rabati in the city, and the hidden, atmospheric, far less crowded Sapara in the forest above. They pair very well in a single Akhaltsikhe visit.

faq.title: How do you get to Sapara Monastery?
faq.content: It's about 12 km northeast of Akhaltsikhe, roughly 20–25 minutes by car up a forested mountain road; a taxi from Akhaltsikhe is the easy option if you don't have a vehicle (some visitors even walk it). The road is paved but narrow in places and rougher after rain or in early-spring snowmelt, when higher ground clearance helps. Entry is free and it's open during daylight hours, though access may be limited during services. As an active monastery, dress modestly (shoulders and knees covered; head coverings for women). It combines naturally with Rabati Fortress and the rest of the Akhaltsikhe area.

rabatiFortress.heroTitle: Rabati Fortress: Akhaltsikhe's Restored Citadel of Many Cultures
rabatiFortress.content:
Rabati Fortress dominates the center of Akhaltsikhe — the main city of southern Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus) — from a long hillside ridge above the Potskhovi River, its restored walls, towers, mosque minaret, and church cupola forming a skyline visible from most of the town below and from the road approaching the city from any direction. It is the defining landmark of the Samtskhe-Javakheti region and one of the most visited historical sites in Georgia: a complex whose scale, its mixture of architectural traditions from different centuries and cultures, and the drama of its illuminated evening appearance make it one of the more immediately impressive monuments in the country. It's also the natural hub for exploring southern Georgia — the gateway to Vardzia, Khertvisi, the Abastumani Observatory, and Borjomi.

What is Rabati Fortress?

Rabati Fortress is a large hilltop fortress complex in the center of Akhaltsikhe, in the Samtskhe-Javakheti region of southern Georgia. Originally a medieval Georgian castle of the Jakeli dynasty, it was expanded under more than two centuries of Ottoman rule (after 1578) into a provincial capital with a mosque, caravanserai, and citadel, before passing to the Russian Empire in 1829. Comprehensively restored (and substantially reconstructed) in 2012, it now blends a Georgian Orthodox church, an Ottoman mosque, a museum, gardens, and an upper citadel within one walled complex — a vivid, if debated, image of the city's multicultural past. Spectacularly lit at night, it's southern Georgia's defining landmark and the base for visiting Vardzia and the wider region.

The name and the layered history

The name Rabati comes from the Arabic rabat, meaning a fortified settlement that grows up around a citadel — the district that developed around the original medieval fortress as the town expanded beyond its first defensive walls. The site's history spans many centuries and political orders: medieval Georgian principality, Ottoman provincial capital, Russian imperial city, and now a restored heritage complex that has drawn major attention and tourism since the early 2010s.

The fortification of this ridge dates to the medieval Georgian period, when Akhaltsikhe was the seat of the Samtskhe principality under the Jakeli (Jaqeli) dynasty, who developed the castle and its settlement through the 13th–14th centuries. The complex was significant enough to figure in the long struggles between the fragmented Georgian kingdoms and the rising Ottoman power that shaped the southern Caucasus from the 15th century on.

Ottoman forces captured Akhaltsikhe in 1578, and the city — known to the Ottomans as Ahıska — became the center of the Childir (Akhaltsikhe) Eyalet, one of the larger Ottoman provinces in the region, spanning much of what is now southern Georgia and northeastern Turkey. Under Ottoman rule, lasting some 250 years, the fortress was expanded and substantially rebuilt, with a mosque, caravanserai, and the urban infrastructure of a provincial capital added to the medieval Georgian core. The city's population through this period mixed Georgians, Armenians, Turks, and Jews, and the physical complexity of the Rabati complex reflects that layering.

Russian forces took Akhaltsikhe in 1828, during the Russo-Turkish War, and the city passed to the Russian Empire under the Treaty of Adrianople in 1829. The following decades brought significant demographic change — much of the Muslim population departed or was displaced — and the city's character shifted again. The fortress gradually fell into disrepair through the 19th and 20th centuries until the comprehensive restoration of the early 2010s remade it in its current form.

The restoration — and the debate

The restoration completed in 2012 was one of the most ambitious — and most debated — heritage projects in recent Georgian history. It went well beyond conserving what survived: it involved the substantial reconstruction of buildings that had been partly or entirely lost, new construction in historical styles, and the installation of modern facilities (including a hotel, shops, and event spaces) within the precinct. The result is visually coherent and impressively organized for visitors, but it has prompted genuine professional debate about the line between restoration and reconstruction — much of what you see today reflects what the fortress may have looked like at its Ottoman-era peak, rather than preserving what actually survived.

This is a real and worthwhile tension: whether an ambitious reconstruction that makes a monument accessible and legible is preferable to preserving fragmentary but more authentic ruins is one of the central debates in heritage conservation worldwide, and Rabati is one of Georgia's most prominent examples of the "accessibility and completeness" side of it. Both positions have legitimate arguments, and knowing the restoration context adds a thoughtful dimension to the visit. (It's worth saying plainly: much of Rabati is a modern reconstruction — visitors expecting an untouched ancient ruin should adjust expectations, while those who enjoy a vivid, walkable evocation of the past will find it rewarding.)

The complex

Rabati divides into two main zones: the lower town — with the mosque, the church, the caravanserai, the museum, and the gardens — and the upper citadel, which preserves the oldest surviving fortress elements and the best viewpoints.

The mosque, with its slender minaret and gilded dome catching the afternoon light, is the most visually striking single element and the clearest expression of the Ottoman centuries. It stands close to the Georgian Orthodox church, the two separated by a courtyard — an arrangement that mirrors the city's historical reality as a place where different religious communities lived within one urban space (the city's Jewish heritage, the old synagogue quarter west of the fortress, is part of the same story). The museum within the complex presents the history and archaeology of Samtskhe-Javakheti in a well-organized permanent exhibition that usefully frames both the fortress and the wider region. The caravanserai recalls Akhaltsikhe's role as a station on the trade routes linking Anatolia with the South Caucasus, and the gardens and public spaces are kept to a high standard, giving the lower town a pleasant quality for unhurried walking that purely archaeological sites rarely offer.

The upper citadel, reached by climbing through the gate structures from the lower town, holds the most historically genuine portions — sections of the original medieval walls and towers predating both the Ottoman expansion and the modern restoration, their age visible in the character of the masonry. The views from the upper walls over Akhaltsikhe and the Potskhovi valley are among the better elevated panoramas in southern Georgia, and the layering of medieval towers, Ottoman religious architecture below, and forested hills beyond rewards a slow look.

Visiting in the evening

Rabati is lit after dark in a way that turns its considerable daytime presence into something theatrical. The illuminated minaret, church tower, and citadel walls, and the wash of warm light across the reconstructed facades, make one of the most visually impressive illuminated historical sites in Georgia — and one that photographs beautifully, the lit complex against the dark sky with the town below producing images that have made Rabati widely recognized. A late-afternoon arrival that allows time to explore in the warm light before staying for the illumination is the most complete way to experience the site.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: Center of Akhaltsikhe, Samtskhe-Javakheti, southern Georgia, on the ridge above the Potskhovi River; easily walkable from the town center.
  • Getting there: Akhaltsikhe is the regional hub, reached by road from Tbilisi (about 3–3.5 hours) via Borjomi; well connected by marshrutka. The fortress is in the middle of town.
  • Hours / admission: The fortress grounds are open daily into the late evening (to enjoy the illumination); the museum keeps shorter daytime hours. Entry to the grounds is paid at the main gate, with a separate fee for the museum.
  • Best time: Late afternoon into the evening, for both the warm daylight and the illumination.
  • Combine with: The Akhaltsikhe Synagogue and old Jewish quarter, Sapara Monastery (nearby), and the wider southern-Georgia circuit that Akhaltsikhe anchors — Vardzia and Khertvisi to the south, the Abastumani Observatory, Borjomi, and the Javakheti lakes (Paravani).

faq.title: What is Rabati Fortress?
faq.content: Rabati Fortress is a large hilltop fortress complex in the center of Akhaltsikhe, the main city of the Samtskhe-Javakheti region in southern Georgia. Originally a medieval Georgian castle of the Jakeli dynasty, it was expanded over more than two centuries of Ottoman rule (from 1578) into a provincial capital with a mosque, caravanserai, and citadel, then passed to the Russian Empire in 1829. Comprehensively restored in 2012, it now combines a Georgian Orthodox church, an Ottoman mosque, a museum, gardens, and an upper citadel within one walled complex — a vivid image of the city's multicultural past, and one of Georgia's most visited sites.

faq.title: Is Rabati Fortress original or reconstructed?
faq.content: Both — and this is worth knowing before you go. The site is genuinely ancient (medieval Georgian and then Ottoman), but the complex you see today is largely the result of an ambitious 2012 restoration that involved substantial reconstruction: rebuilding lost structures, new construction in historical styles, and modern facilities within the walls. Much of it reflects how the fortress may have looked at its Ottoman peak rather than what physically survived, which has prompted real debate among heritage professionals. The most genuinely old parts are in the upper citadel (sections of medieval wall and tower). If you arrive expecting an untouched ruin you may be surprised; if you enjoy a vivid, walkable evocation of the past, it's very rewarding.

faq.title: What is there to see at Rabati?
faq.content: The complex has two zones. The lower town holds the Ottoman mosque (with its gilded dome and slender minaret), the Georgian Orthodox church beside it, a caravanserai, a regional history-and-archaeology museum, and well-kept gardens. The upper citadel, reached by climbing through the gates, preserves the oldest medieval walls and towers and offers the best views over Akhaltsikhe and the Potskhovi valley. The juxtaposition of mosque and church within one courtyard captures the city's long history as a meeting point of Georgian, Ottoman, Armenian, and Jewish communities.

faq.title: When is the best time to visit Rabati Fortress?
faq.content: Late afternoon into the evening. The complex is dramatically illuminated after dark — the lit minaret, church tower, and citadel walls make one of the most striking night-time historical sights in Georgia and photograph beautifully. Arriving in the late afternoon lets you explore in warm daylight and then stay for the illumination, which is the most complete way to experience it. The grounds stay open into the late evening for this; the museum keeps shorter daytime hours.

faq.title: How do you get to Rabati, and what else is nearby?
faq.content: Rabati is in the center of Akhaltsikhe, walkable from the town, and Akhaltsikhe is the hub of southern Georgia — about 3–3.5 hours from Tbilisi by road via Borjomi, and well served by marshrutka. The fortress pairs naturally with the city's other heritage (the Akhaltsikhe Synagogue and old Jewish quarter, and nearby Sapara Monastery), and Akhaltsikhe is the base for the region's headline sights: Vardzia and Khertvisi to the south, the Abastumani Observatory, Borjomi, and the Javakheti lakes.

pokaNunnery.heroTitle: Poka Nunnery (St. Nino Monastery of Poka): A Highland Convent of Enamel and Cheese
pokaNunnery.content:
The St. Nino Monastery of Poka sits on the open volcanic plateau of the Javakheti highlands, on the shore of Lake Paravani, in a landscape of wide skies and sparse highland vegetation about as far as possible from the forested gorges where most Georgian monasteries are found. It is an active convent, maintained by a community of nuns whose daily religious life continues quietly alongside the visitors who stop here — and it has earned a reputation across Georgia that reaches well beyond the usual circle of pilgrims, thanks above all to the things the community makes: its renowned cloisonné enamel, its cheeses, and its other handmade products. It stands, too, at one of the foundational sites of Georgian Christianity, where St. Nino is said to have rested after entering the country.

What is the Poka Nunnery?

The Poka Nunnery (St. Nino Monastery of Poka) is an active Georgian Orthodox convent on the shore of Lake Paravani, on the Javakheti Plateau in the Samtskhe-Javakheti region of southern Georgia. Built around a medieval (11th-century) church and revived as a nunnery in 1992, it marks the spot where St. Nino is said to have rested after bringing Christianity into Georgia through Javakheti. It's famous across the country for the products the nuns make — especially cloisonné enamel (minankari) icons and artworks, and a range of cheeses — and for its social work: the nuns run a parish school and a free clinic for the local, largely Armenian-speaking community. A welcoming, living monastery in a stark, beautiful highland setting.

St. Nino and the founding

The monastery's dedication ties it directly to St. Nino, the 4th-century Cappadocian missionary who brought Christianity to Georgia and whose influence on the country's religious and cultural identity runs deeper than that of any other figure. By tradition, when St. Nino entered Georgia from the south through Javakheti, she rested for two days here at Poka, by Lake Paravani, where she received a vision directing her on to Mtskheta — so this quiet plateau spot stands near the very beginning of Georgian Christianity. A memorial obelisk marks where she is said to have first set foot, and the day of her arrival is celebrated here each 1 June, with baptisms in the lake.

The site itself is old: the church dates to the 11th century and is counted among the finer medieval monuments of Javakheti for its carved ornamentation and surviving fragments of ancient artwork, modest in size though it is. The modern community is recent. In 1988, Patriarch Ilia II visited Poka — and, because the area was then closed, bought a house by the church and registered as a resident to gain access; a monastery was established in 1989 and the nunnery in 1992, under Mother Superior Elisabeth (Meskhishvili). The community built both a genuine religious life and a productive working one, the two supporting each other in the way the best monastic communities have always managed.

A community that gives back

Part of what makes Poka distinctive is the nuns' role beyond the convent walls. For years the monastery has been the only Georgian-speaking enclave in a predominantly Armenian-speaking corner of the country, and rather than standing apart, the community has put itself at the service of its neighbors. The nuns run a parish school that teaches Georgian and English to local children — most of them from Armenian-speaking families — which is genuinely valuable for village young people who need those languages to pass Georgia's national exams. They also keep a small free clinic offering basic medical help to locals. This quiet, practical generosity, alongside the missionary and devotional life, gives the monastery a warmth and purpose that visitors notice.

The products

Poka's handmade goods are well known across Georgia, recommended by travelers and locals alike with the enthusiasm reserved for things that are both authentic and reliably good.

The signature craft is cloisonné enamel (minankari) — the nunnery has run an enamel studio for well over a decade, producing icons, holy vessels, and artworks of real quality, sold both at the monastery and at the "Pokani" shop in Tbilisi. It's some of the finest enamelwork made anywhere in Georgia, and the reason many people know Poka's name.

The cheeses are the other standout: the community has spent years blending Georgian tradition with French and European cheesemaking technique to produce an unusually wide range of distinctive cheeses (more than a dozen varieties), sold in the monastery shop. Beyond these, the nuns make chocolates and confectionery (truffles and the like), honey from the highland wildflowers of the plateau — with a flavor complexity that sets it apart from lowland honey — and jams and preserves, herbal teas, candles, porcelain, and natural cosmetics. Together they give the shop a genuine quality and variety that makes a stop here worthwhile on its own terms, not merely as charitable support.

The nuns who run the shop do so with the direct, warm manner of the best Georgian hospitality, and buying from them directly supports the community's religious, educational, and charitable work. Poka's products make some of the more distinctive and carefully made souvenirs available anywhere in Samtskhe-Javakheti.

The monastery and its setting

The church and monastic buildings are modest in architectural ambition — this is a living religious community whose buildings serve the practical needs of monastic life, not the patronage of powerful rulers. The medieval church interior has the simple, candlelit character of an active women's monastery — icons, incense, the quiet of a space in continuous devotional use — and the grounds are kept with the care a resident community gives its home rather than the institutional upkeep of a heritage site. Visitors who approach with respect for the monastic context (and the nuns are genuinely welcoming to respectful visitors) encounter Georgian religious life in its living rather than its historical dimension — a more immediate experience than the great medieval monasteries offer.

The setting is part of the appeal. Poka lies in the open volcanic grassland of the Javakheti Plateau, with wide horizons and dramatic, fast-changing weather, and Lake Paravani — Georgia's largest lake — right alongside. The unobstructed plateau sky, unframed by the mountains or forest that enclose most Georgian religious sites, gives the monastery an exposed, almost windswept character that is part of what makes it feel different. The plateau in autumn, when the grassland turns gold and dramatic cloud builds over the lake, is the most visually compelling time to visit; summer is the most comfortable and the greenest; and winter closes the plateau to most visitors, the monastery turning inward to the reduced rhythm of a community focused on its religious life.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: Poka village, Ninotsminda municipality, Samtskhe-Javakheti, southern Georgia, on the shore of Lake Paravani (~2,080 m); roughly 20–28 km from Akhalkalaki/Ninotsminda, about 3.5 hours from Tbilisi.
  • Getting there: By car, about 20–25 minutes from Akhalkalaki; the plateau road is generally fine for a standard vehicle in the main season (roughly May–October). Winter access depends on conditions, which can be severe at plateau elevation.
  • Hours / admission: Open daily during daylight hours; free. The shop runs during the main visiting hours — mornings and early afternoons are most reliable. As an active convent, access may be limited during services.
  • Dress: Modest clothing required — shoulders and knees covered; head coverings for women expected (wraps are often available).
  • Combine with: Lake Paravani, the other Javakheti lakes and the Javakheti Protected Areas, Akhalkalaki and Ninotsminda, and — back north — Vardzia, Khertvisi, and Akhaltsikhe.

faq.title: What is the Poka Nunnery?
faq.content: The Poka Nunnery (St. Nino Monastery of Poka) is an active Georgian Orthodox convent on the shore of Lake Paravani, on the Javakheti Plateau in the Samtskhe-Javakheti region of southern Georgia. It's built around a medieval (11th-century) church and was revived as a nunnery in 1992, on the spot where St. Nino is said to have rested after bringing Christianity into Georgia through Javakheti. It's known across the country for the products the nuns make — especially cloisonné enamel (minankari) icons and artworks and a wide range of cheeses — and for its social work, including a parish school and a free clinic for the local community.

faq.title: What can you buy at the Poka Nunnery?
faq.content: The nunnery's most famous product is cloisonné enamel (minankari) — handmade icons, holy vessels, and artworks of high quality, sold both on-site and at the "Pokani" shop in Tbilisi. The other standout is cheese: the nuns make more than a dozen distinctive varieties, blending Georgian tradition with French and European technique. They also produce chocolates and confectionery, highland wildflower honey, jams and preserves, herbal teas, candles, porcelain, and natural cosmetics. Buying from the shop directly supports the community's religious, educational, and charitable work, and the goods make some of the most distinctive souvenirs in the region.

faq.title: What is the connection to St. Nino?
faq.content: St. Nino was the 4th-century Cappadocian missionary who brought Christianity to Georgia. By tradition, when she entered the country from the south through Javakheti, she rested for two days here at Poka, by Lake Paravani, where she received a vision directing her on to Mtskheta — so the site stands near the very beginning of Georgian Christianity. A memorial obelisk marks where she is said to have first set foot, and the day of her arrival is celebrated at Poka each year on 1 June, with baptisms in the lake.

faq.title: Is the Poka Nunnery worth visiting beyond shopping?
faq.content: Yes. It's a genuinely living women's monastery in a striking highland setting on the shore of Georgia's largest lake, so it offers an encounter with Georgian religious life in its active rather than its museum form — and the nuns are welcoming to respectful visitors. The community is also notable for its generosity: as long the only Georgian-speaking enclave in a largely Armenian-speaking area, the nuns run a parish school teaching Georgian and English to local children and a small free clinic. That mix of devotion, craft, and quiet service gives the place a warmth that many grander monuments lack.

faq.title: How do you get to the Poka Nunnery, and when should you go?
faq.content: It's in Poka village, Ninotsminda municipality, on the shore of Lake Paravani — about 20–25 minutes by car from Akhalkalaki and roughly 3.5 hours from Tbilisi. The plateau road is generally fine for a standard vehicle from about May to October; winter access depends on conditions, which can be severe at this altitude. Summer is the most comfortable time and autumn the most beautiful (golden grassland, dramatic skies over the lake). It's open daily in daylight hours, entry is free, and you should dress modestly (shoulders and knees covered; head coverings for women). It pairs naturally with Lake Paravani and the wider Javakheti circuit.

lakeParavani.heroTitle: Lake Paravani: Georgia's Largest Lake, on the Javakheti Plateau
lakeParavani.content:
Lake Paravani sits on the Javakheti volcanic plateau in southern Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — at about 2,073 meters, covering roughly 37 square kilometers of open highland between Akhalkalaki and the Armenian border. It is the largest lake in Georgia (by surface area) and one of the highest substantial bodies of water in the South Caucasus, and the landscape around it is unlike almost anywhere else in the country: a wide, open, treeless volcanic plateau where the horizons run far, the sky fills more of the view than the ground, and the terrain feels closer to the Armenian and Anatolian highlands than to the forested Caucasus most people picture when they think of Georgia.

What is Lake Paravani?

Lake Paravani is the largest lake in Georgia by area (about 37 km²), a high-altitude volcanic lake on the Javakheti Plateau in the Samtskhe-Javakheti region of southern Georgia, at around 2,073 m. Broad but shallow (only about 3 m deep), it freezes solid every winter and sits in a stark, treeless volcanic landscape between the Javakheti and Abul-Samsari ranges. It's a major birdwatching site — part of the Javakheti Protected Areas, the one place in Georgia where flamingos are occasionally seen — the source of the Paravani River, and the site of a submerged Bronze Age find. About 1.5 hours from Akhaltsikhe, it's the centerpiece of the wider Javakheti highland landscape.

The lake and the plateau

The Javakheti plateau is a product of the volcanic geology that shaped much of southern Georgia and extends across the border into northern Armenia. The basalt and volcanic tuff beneath the surface give the landscape its coloring — dark rock, brown and grey soil, the sparse vegetation of high-altitude grassland — and the lake basin was formed by volcanic and tectonic processes, not by the glacial or river erosion behind most of Georgia's other significant water bodies. At this elevation the plateau supports grasses and low herbaceous plants rather than forest, and that openness is what gives Paravani its quality of exposure.

The lake is large but strikingly shallow — only about 3 meters at its deepest — which is why its water is often clouded and why it freezes solid each winter (the ice can reach over 70 cm, thick enough for ice fishing). Its surface is highly reflective in calm conditions, the sky and surrounding hills mirrored in the water in a way that amplifies the scale of the landscape and the quality of the light. The plateau weather changes fast — cloud can cross the lake in minutes, the water shifting from deep blue to grey to silver as the light moves overhead — and the photographic possibilities in variable light are exceptional for anyone willing to wait for the right moment. The lake is fed by small streams, snowmelt, and springs, and is the source of the Paravani River, a major tributary of the Mtkvari (Kura).

A curiosity adds to the lake's character: underwater research (a Tbilisi State University expedition) found a submerged Bronze Age site at the bottom of the lake — by interpretation an ancient structure or burial, from which vessels and household items have been recovered — giving Paravani a genuine archaeological mystery beneath its muddy surface. By tradition, the lake also carries an Orthodox association: St. Nino, who brought Christianity to Georgia, is said to have entered the country through Javakheti, passing this way.

Birdlife

Lake Paravani and the surrounding wetlands and grasslands of the Javakheti plateau are among the most significant areas for birds in Georgia. The plateau's lakes and marshes form part of the Javakheti Protected Areas (and Javakheti National Park) and are recognized internationally as an Important Bird Area, with over 260 bird species recorded across the wetland system. The lake and its margins provide habitat for large numbers of waterbirds on migration — waders, ducks and geese, terns, and other wetland species pass through or pause here in spring and autumn — while the open grassland of the surrounding plateau supports raptors and ground-nesting birds adapted to the sparse highland terrain.

For dedicated birdwatchers, the spring passage (April–May) and the autumn passage (September–October) are the most species-rich times, when the combination of lake, wetland, and grassland concentrates migrating birds in numbers that can be impressive on the right days. Among the more striking birds recorded on the plateau are pelicans, various herons, eagles and vultures, and — remarkably for a 2,000-meter Georgian lake — flamingos: the Javakheti Plateau is the one place in Georgia where flamingos are occasionally seen, though as rare migratory visitors rather than a reliable sighting. (The endangered Dalmatian pelican is more associated with the nearby Madatapa Lake, a Ramsar-designated site on the plateau.)

The wider Javakheti landscape

Paravani is best understood as part of the broader Javakheti highland rather than an isolated destination. The plateau holds several other lakes — Tabatskuri, Kartsakhi, Madatapa, Saghamo, Khanchali, and more — and the roads across it pass through a succession of volcanic terrain, open grassland, and small settlements with the consistent character of remote highland habitation. The towns of Akhalkalaki and Ninotsminda, the administrative centers of the Javakheti districts, are predominantly Armenian-speaking communities with their own distinct cultural and culinary character, and the meeting of the highland Georgian landscape with the Armenian cultural life of the plateau adds a dimension to travel here that purely natural tourism doesn't capture.

The road from Akhaltsikhe south to Akhalkalaki and then around the plateau toward Ninotsminda and the Armenian border is one of the more interesting drives in southern Georgia — the landscape opening progressively as the elevation rises, with Paravani the largest and most compelling of the lake basins along the way. Around the lake there's scope for more than a roadside stop: walking and cycling on the plateau, a marked hike up nearby Shaori Mountain (with megalithic ruins) from Tambovka village on the northwest shore, and the small lakeside settlements (Poka, with its St. Nino convent; Tambovka; Aspara).

Practical considerations

The Javakheti plateau has a continental mountain climate harsher than any other inhabited region of Georgia — indeed it's often the coldest place in the country in winter. Winters are long, cold, and snowy; the lake freezes reliably each year, and plateau roads can be impassable for spells from December through March. Summers are short and cool relative to the lowlands, and even summer days can end with sharply dropping temperatures and strong winds across the exposed plateau. The weather can change fast enough to catch the unprepared off guard, so warm and waterproof layers are essential regardless of the morning conditions.

  • Best time: June–September for comfort and access (July–August warmest); April–May and September–October for bird migration. Autumn brings dramatic skies, possible early snow on the volcanic hills, and the autumn migration — the most visually and ornithologically interesting season, for those who can handle the increasingly uncertain weather. Winter access is unreliable and conditions can be severe.
  • Location: Javakheti Plateau, Akhalkalaki municipality, Samtskhe-Javakheti, southern Georgia (~2,073 m); about 1.5 hours from Akhaltsikhe via Akhalkalaki, ~3.5 hours from Tbilisi.
  • Getting there: The main plateau roads are paved; secondary tracks to the shoreline vary. A standard vehicle reaches the main viewpoints easily; remoter shoreline sections may need higher ground clearance.
  • Access / admission: Open at all times, freely accessible.
  • Combine with: The other Javakheti lakes and the Javakheti Protected Areas, Akhalkalaki and Ninotsminda, and — back north — Vardzia, Khertvisi, Akhaltsikhe (Rabati), and the Tsalka/Dashbashi area toward Tbilisi.

faq.title: What is Lake Paravani?
faq.content: Lake Paravani is the largest lake in Georgia by surface area (about 37 km²), a high-altitude volcanic lake on the Javakheti Plateau in the Samtskhe-Javakheti region of southern Georgia, at around 2,073 m. It's broad but very shallow (only about 3 m deep), freezes solid every winter, and sits in a stark, treeless volcanic landscape between the Javakheti and Abul-Samsari ranges. It's a major birdwatching site within the Javakheti Protected Areas, the source of the Paravani River, and even has a submerged Bronze Age archaeological find at its bottom. It's about 1.5 hours from Akhaltsikhe and the centerpiece of the wider Javakheti highland.

faq.title: Is Lake Paravani really the largest lake in Georgia?
faq.content: Yes — by surface area, at roughly 37–38 km², Paravani is the largest lake in Georgia. It's also one of the highest substantial lakes in the country, at about 2,073 m on the Javakheti Plateau. Interestingly, despite its size it's extremely shallow — only around 3 meters at its deepest — which is why its water is usually clouded and why it freezes completely each winter (the ice gets thick enough for ice fishing). It's not Georgia's deepest lake (that's Ritsa, in Abkhazia), but in sheer surface area Paravani is the biggest.

faq.title: Can you see flamingos at Lake Paravani?
faq.content: Remarkably, yes — sometimes. The Javakheti Plateau is the one place in Georgia where flamingos have been recorded, though only as rare migratory visitors, so it's a lucky sighting rather than something you can count on. What you're more likely to see is the plateau's broader birdlife: the Javakheti lakes and wetlands form an Important Bird Area with over 260 recorded species, including ducks, geese, waders, terns, herons, eagles, and vultures, plus the endangered Dalmatian pelican (more associated with the nearby Madatapa Lake). The spring (April–May) and autumn (September–October) migrations are the richest times.

faq.title: When is the best time to visit Lake Paravani, and what should I bring?
faq.content: June through September is the most comfortable and reliably accessible window (July–August warmest), while April–May and September–October are best for the bird migrations; autumn adds dramatic skies and possible early snow on the hills. Winter is severe — the plateau is often the coldest place in Georgia, the lake freezes, and roads can be impassable. The weather changes fast and the plateau is windy and exposed, so bring warm and waterproof layers whatever the morning looks like, plus sun protection in summer.

faq.title: How do you get to Lake Paravani?
faq.content: It's on the Javakheti Plateau in Akhalkalaki municipality, about 1.5 hours from Akhaltsikhe (via Akhalkalaki) and roughly 3.5 hours from Tbilisi. The main plateau roads are paved and a standard car reaches the main viewpoints easily, though some secondary tracks to the shore are rougher and better with higher ground clearance. Access is free and unrestricted. The lake pairs naturally with the other Javakheti lakes and protected areas, the towns of Akhalkalaki and Ninotsminda, and — back toward the north — Vardzia, Khertvisi, and Akhaltsikhe.

khertvisiFortress.heroTitle: Khertvisi Fortress: One of Georgia's Oldest Strongholds, on the Road to Vardzia
khertvisiFortress.content:
Khertvisi Fortress occupies a triangular promontory of bare rock above the point where the Paravani River joins the Mtkvari, about 40 kilometers south of Akhaltsikhe on the main road to Vardzia, in the Samtskhe-Javakheti region of southern Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus. The confluence below — two rivers meeting in a dry, rocky gorge at the base of a sheer cliff — gives the site its geographical logic and its drama. The walls follow the edges of the promontory so closely that, from some angles, stone construction and natural rock face appear continuous: a single vertical mass rising from the river junction to the battlements. It is one of those fortifications where builders and landscape worked together, the architecture amplifying a defensibility geology had already provided. And it is genuinely ancient — by tradition one of the oldest fortresses in Georgia, with origins reaching back to antiquity and a legend that ties it to Alexander the Great himself.

What is Khertvisi Fortress?

Khertvisi Fortress is one of the oldest and largest fortresses in Georgia, set on a rocky promontory above the confluence of the Mtkvari and Paravani rivers in Samtskhe-Javakheti, southern Georgia, about 40 km south of Akhaltsikhe and ~11 km before Vardzia. Tradition dates its origins to the 2nd century BC, and a famous legend links it to Alexander the Great; the church within dates to 985 and the main walls to 1354. A largely unrestored medieval fortress with sweeping views over the river gorges, it sits on the UNESCO Tentative List (with Vardzia) and is an almost unmissable stop on the drive to the cave city.

A very old fortress

Khertvisi is among the oldest fortified sites in Georgia. Tradition and the medieval Georgian chronicles place a fortification at this confluence in deep antiquity — the 2nd century BC is the date usually given, and the 11th-century chronicler Leonti Mroveli (in Kartlis Tskhovreba) refers to Khertvisi even earlier still. While the structures visible today are mostly medieval, the strategic logic of the position — commanding the passage between the Mtkvari gorge below and the southern plateau above, holding both river valleys at once from an essentially unassailable cliff — would have been obvious to any commander in any age, and the fortress was rebuilt and strengthened repeatedly as successive rulers recognized its value on the routes linking the Georgian heartland with the south and, beyond, with Anatolia.

The legend of Alexander the Great. Khertvisi's signature story is that it was attacked — in some versions destroyed, in others never subdued — by Alexander the Great during his eastern campaign in the 4th century BC. Modern historians treat this as folklore (they doubt Macedonian troops reached the region) rather than history, but the legend is part of the fortress's identity and a measure of how deep its antiquity is felt to run.

History

Little is recorded of Khertvisi in the early medieval period, but it revived in the 10th–11th centuries, when a new fortress was built and a church established in 985; from then it grew into the center of the Meskheti (Samtskhe) region, attaining the status of a town by the 12th century. Its fortunes then turned: a devastating earthquake at the end of the 13th century brought down the walls, and the Mongol invasions left it in ruins. Significant reconstruction followed around 1354–1356 under Zakari Kamkamishvili (an inscription records his work) — the present main walls date from this campaign — though the fortress never fully regained its former standing. Through the 14th–15th centuries it was held by the Jaqeli lords of Meskheti.

In the 16th century the Ottomans took the southern region, and Khertvisi remained in their sphere for some three hundred years, with brief Georgian recaptures by Giorgi Saakadze (1624) and King Erekle (Heraclius) II (1771). The region was freed from Ottoman control and annexed by Russia in the late 1820s (1828–29). The fortress later served briefly as a Russian (and Georgian) military base before losing all military function — the strategic conditions that had made a medieval hilltop stronghold essential were irrelevant in the age of modern artillery and fixed state borders — and it passed into its present role as a historic monument.

The fortress today

Khertvisi today is a largely unrestored medieval fortress — sections of wall standing to near-full height beside sections collapsed to their foundations, the overall circuit readable but punctuated by gaps and tumbled stone. (Some consolidation and reconstruction has been done in recent years, including work on the outer walls, so it is not a wholly untouched ruin, but it retains a raw, authentic character.) The walls follow the irregular perimeter of the promontory rather than any geometric plan, tracing the cliff edges and using the natural rock as part of the defense wherever the topography allows, with towers placed at the angles and along the walls where extra height and firepower were needed.

The surviving masonry represents several construction campaigns, legible in the size and dressing of the stone, the mortar, and the details of openings and battlements — the oldest sections with the large, plainly laid blocks of early medieval Georgian military work, the later additions reflecting each period's standards. Within the walls are a court church (the 985 building), utility rooms, and — a notable feature — two tunnels leading down to the river from the fortress, one for water supply and one for communication, allowing the garrison to hold out under siege. The interior is an irregular plateau of exposed rock and debris, the foundations of internal structures visible in places but the overall layout more suggested than fully legible. The highest point, at the northern tip above the confluence, gives the most complete view and the clearest sense of why Khertvisi was worth fighting over.

The views

The views from the walls are exceptional and are one of the main reasons to make the short climb. The Mtkvari gorge runs upstream toward Akhaltsikhe and downstream toward Vardzia, the river tracing a pale course through the dry, rocky highlands; the Paravani valley opens east toward the Javakheti plateau; and the confluence directly below makes the strategic logic of the site instantly comprehensible. The surrounding landscape — dry, rocky, spare in vegetation — gives the views an openness and distance quite unlike the enclosed forests of northern Georgia, and the combination of the medieval ruin in the foreground and the river-gorge landscape stretching in several directions is generously rewarding for photographers.

On the road to Vardzia

Khertvisi sits on the main Akhaltsikhe–Vardzia road, roughly halfway and about 11 km before Vardzia, and virtually every vehicle making the journey passes directly beneath it. The first sight of the fortress from the road — walls and towers rising from the cliff above the river junction — is one of the more striking roadside encounters in southern Georgia, and even a twenty-minute stop gives a direct experience of medieval military architecture in its original landscape that no amount of reading can match. The two sites are jointly recognized — Khertvisi and Vardzia were placed together on the UNESCO World Heritage Tentative List in 2007 ("Vardzia–Khertvisi") — and combining them, the fortress on the way south and the cave monastery at the end of the road, makes a natural and satisfying southern-Georgia day.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: Above the Mtkvari–Paravani confluence, near Khertvisi village, Aspindza municipality, Samtskhe-Javakheti, southern Georgia — about 40 km south of Akhaltsikhe on the Vardzia road, ~11 km before Vardzia.
  • Getting there: Directly on, and visible from, the main Akhaltsikhe–Vardzia road; no special vehicle needed. Reaching the walls involves a short, steep climb up the hillside.
  • Hours / admission: Open during daylight hours; the base is freely accessible from the road.
  • What to bring: Comfortable, sturdy footwear for the climb and the uneven rocky interior; sun protection and water in summer (the site is exposed and hot).
  • Best time: Spring and autumn for mild weather; year-round access (summer is hot and exposed).
  • Combine with: Vardzia (the cave city, ~11 km on), Vanis Kvabebi cave monastery, Tmogvi Fortress, and — back toward Akhaltsikhe — Rabati Castle, Sapara Monastery, and the wider region (Borjomi, Abastumani).

faq.title: What is Khertvisi Fortress?
faq.content: Khertvisi is one of the oldest and largest fortresses in Georgia, standing on a rocky promontory above the confluence of the Mtkvari and Paravani rivers in the Samtskhe-Javakheti region of southern Georgia, about 40 km south of Akhaltsikhe and roughly 11 km before Vardzia. Tradition dates its origins to the 2nd century BC, and a famous legend links it to Alexander the Great; the church inside dates to 985 and the main walls to 1354. A largely unrestored medieval fortress with sweeping views over the river gorges, it's on the UNESCO Tentative List (together with Vardzia) and is one of the most striking stops on the road to the cave city.

faq.title: How old is Khertvisi Fortress?
faq.content: Very old — it's traditionally counted among the oldest fortresses in Georgia, with origins usually placed in the 2nd century BC (the medieval chronicler Leonti Mroveli refers to it even earlier). The structures you see today are mostly medieval: the court church dates to 985, and the present main walls to a reconstruction of 1354–1356 under Zakari Kamkamishvili, after an earthquake and the Mongol invasions had wrecked the earlier fortress. So while the visible fortress is medieval, the fortified site itself reaches back into antiquity.

faq.title: Did Alexander the Great really attack Khertvisi?
faq.content: That's the legend — a well-known story, recorded in the medieval Georgian chronicles, that Alexander the Great attacked (in some versions destroyed, in others failed to capture) Khertvisi during his eastern campaign in the 4th century BC. Modern historians treat it as folklore rather than fact, since they doubt Macedonian forces actually reached this region. But the legend is part of the fortress's identity and reflects just how ancient the site is felt to be — and it's a good story to have in mind as you look up at the walls.

faq.title: Is Khertvisi worth stopping at on the way to Vardzia?
faq.content: Yes — it's almost unmissable. The fortress sits right on the Akhaltsikhe–Vardzia road, roughly halfway and about 11 km before Vardzia, and nearly every vehicle to the cave city passes directly beneath it. The first sight of the walls and towers rising from the cliff above the river junction is one of southern Georgia's most striking roadside views, and even a 20-minute stop gives a real sense of medieval military architecture in its setting. The two sites are linked — they were submitted together to the UNESCO Tentative List in 2007 — so pairing the fortress with the cave monastery makes a natural southern-Georgia day.

faq.title: How do you visit Khertvisi, and is there a climb?
faq.content: The fortress is directly on the main Akhaltsikhe–Vardzia road and visible from it; no special vehicle is needed. The base is freely accessible, and reaching the walls involves a short but steep climb up the hillside, with uneven rocky ground inside — so wear sturdy, comfortable footwear, and bring sun protection and water in summer, as the site is exposed and gets hot. It's open during daylight hours. Combine it with Vardzia, the Vanis Kvabebi cave monastery, and Tmogvi Fortress, or with Rabati Castle and Sapara Monastery back toward Akhaltsikhe.

greenMonastery.heroTitle: The Green Monastery (Mtsvane Monastery): A Forest Sanctuary Above Borjomi
greenMonastery.content:
The Green Monastery — Mtsvane Monasteri in Georgian, officially the Chitakhevi Church of St. George — sits in a deep forest valley about 8–10 kilometers from Borjomi, in the Samtskhe-Javakheti region of southern Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus), at the end of a road that follows a mountain stream up into the hills. It is so thoroughly embedded in its setting that, on first encounter, the line between the built and the natural is genuinely hard to read: moss covers the stone, trees press against the church, and the stream is audible from within the precinct. The name is doubly earned — for the dense, damp green of the forest around it, and for the green-hued stone the church is built from. Founded in the 9th century and carrying a profound martyrs' history, it is unlike any other religious site in the Borjomi region: a small, quiet, atmospheric place whose power comes from the meeting of ancient faith and living forest.

What is the Green Monastery?

The Green Monastery (Mtsvane Monastery), officially the Chitakhevi Church of St. George, is a small medieval Georgian Orthodox monastery in a forested gorge about 8–10 km from Borjomi, within the Borjomi-Kharagauli National Park. Founded in the 9th century by Christopher and Theodore, disciples of St. Grigol Khandzteli, it's a modest three-nave stone basilica with a later bell tower, built of green-tinged stone and wrapped in deep Colchic forest. It's known for two things: its extraordinary natural setting, and a tragic 16th-century history — when invading forces martyred its monks, a tradition the reddish "bloody stones" in the stream are said to commemorate. Abandoned for some 200 years, it was restored and its monastic life revived in the early 2000s.

History

The monastery's origins lie in the 9th century (some sources say the 9th–10th), when its foundation is credited to Christopher and Theodore (Kristepore and Tevdore), disciples of St. Grigol Khandzteli — the towering figure of Georgian monasticism who founded numerous monasteries in the Tao-Klarjeti region. The two monks arrived in the Borjomi gorge (the historical province of Tori) around the 830s and established monastic communities here, of which the Chitakhevi foundation was one. The forested gorge — sheltered, well-watered, remote — was exactly the kind of place Georgian monks sought for the separation from secular life their calling required.

The monastery's defining moment, however, was a tragic one. In the 16th century, during the invasion of Shah Tahmasp of Persia, the monastery was captured and looted, and its monks were tortured and killed — dozens of them, by tradition. It is from this massacre that the monastery draws its deepest significance as a place of martyrdom (see below). The region then fell under Ottoman control from 1578, and the monastery was eventually abandoned in the 18th century, standing empty for roughly two hundred years. Restoration began in the late 20th century (from the late 1970s–1980s), and monastic life was revived around 2002–2003; today a small community of monks is again in residence. The complex bears the layered evidence of this long history — the 9th-century church, a bell tower of the 15th–17th centuries, fragments of fresco dated to the 12th–13th centuries, and the moss-grown older walls beside cleaner, more recent repair.

The martyrs and the "bloody stones"

The Green Monastery's most powerful and best-known feature is bound up with the 16th-century massacre. Tradition holds that the reddish-hued stones found in the bed of the Chitakhevi stream are stained with the blood of the monks killed here — the "bloody stones." They are scattered along much of the stream within the monastery grounds, and one has been brought into the church and displayed. The monks regard the stones' enduring color as miraculous, and Orthodox pilgrims come to pray among them and to honor the martyrs. By tradition, bones and remains of the slain are still occasionally found on the grounds and given proper burial by the community.

Whatever one makes of the tradition, the story gives the site a depth that its modest architecture alone would not convey: this is, for Georgian Orthodox visitors, a place of martyrdom and pilgrimage, and the quiet of the forest valley carries that weight. Visitors are asked to treat the stones and the site with the respect due a place of remembrance.

The setting

The dominant experience here is environmental as much as architectural, and anyone expecting a monument on the scale of Gelati or Bodbe will find something quite different — a modest complex whose power comes from the relationship between the human construction and the forest around it. The woodland is the Colchic mixed type of the Borjomi gorge — beech, hornbeam, chestnut, and alder, with an understory of rhododendron and ferns, sustained by the high rainfall of the Borjomi microclimate. The trees are large and the canopy dense, and the filtered, soft, occasionally shaft-like light reaching the forest floor is the kind that makes photographers slow down. (The monastery sits within the southwestern edge of the Borjomi-Kharagauli National Park, so the surrounding forest is protected wilderness; deer are sometimes seen, though larger wildlife is unlikely near the site.)

The mountain stream beside and through the precinct is the acoustic center of the place — its sound present on the approach road, in the grounds, and from inside the church when the door is open. Combined with birdsong and the complete absence of mechanical noise, it gives the site a genuine quietness increasingly rare within easy reach of any Georgian town. The moss-covered rocks of the streambed and walls have the luminous deep green of stream-side humid forest, softening the edges of the stone so that the built environment seems to be returning to the natural.

The complex

The main church is a three-nave basilica of rough-hewn stone in the Georgian medieval tradition, almost without decoration, its weathered, moss-covered surfaces making precise dating difficult by eye alone. The interior is simply furnished, lit largely by candles, with fragments of fresco (dated to the 12th–13th centuries) surviving in places. The atmosphere is one of accumulated devotion — the quiet of a space prayed in across centuries — rather than artistic spectacle, and the modest scale concentrates rather than disperses the spiritual quality.

A separate two-storey bell tower (15th–17th century), with a small chapel below and a belfry above, and the associated monastic buildings complete a compact complex that fits the irregular forest clearing it occupies. The stone walls and pathways are moss-covered and uneven, so walking the grounds needs the attention to footing any ancient stone surface demands. The overall impression is of a place not aggressively restored or "presented" — maintained by its small resident community with the means available to a working monastery, rather than polished into a heritage showpiece.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: Near Chitakhevi village, Borjomi municipality, Samtskhe-Javakheti, southern Georgia, about 8–10 km from Borjomi, within the Borjomi-Kharagauli National Park.
  • Getting there: By car, about 15–20 minutes from Borjomi, following the forest stream up the gorge. The final section is unpaved and can be rough after rain — a standard vehicle manages in dry conditions, but a vehicle with better ground clearance is more comfortable when wet. A taxi from Borjomi is the practical option without your own vehicle. The drive itself is lovely, the forest close on both sides and the stream alongside throughout.
  • Hours / admission: Open daily during daylight hours; free. As an active monastery, access may be limited during services.
  • Dress: Modest clothing required — shoulders and knees covered; head coverings for women are expected (wraps are often available at the entrance).
  • Respect: This is a place of martyrdom and pilgrimage as well as a beauty spot; please treat the church, the grounds, and the "bloody stones" accordingly, and keep noise low.
  • Combine with: Borjomi (the Central Park and mineral spring), the Borjomi-Kharagauli National Park, and the wider region — Likani, Bakuriani, and, further on, Akhaltsikhe (Rabati) and Abastumani.

faq.title: What is the Green Monastery near Borjomi?
faq.content: The Green Monastery (Mtsvane Monastery), officially the Chitakhevi Church of St. George, is a small medieval Georgian Orthodox monastery in a forested gorge about 8–10 km from Borjomi, within the Borjomi-Kharagauli National Park. Founded in the 9th century by Christopher and Theodore, disciples of St. Grigol Khandzteli, it's a modest three-nave stone basilica with a later bell tower, built of green-tinged stone and surrounded by deep forest. It's known both for its extraordinary natural setting and for a tragic 16th-century history of martyrdom commemorated by the reddish "bloody stones" in the nearby stream.

faq.title: Why is it called the Green Monastery?
faq.content: For two reasons, and sources give both: the monastery is hidden in dense, deep-green forest in the well-watered Borjomi gorge, and the church itself is built from a green-hued stone. Together they make "green" an unusually fitting name — the natural setting and the building material echo each other. Its official name is the Chitakhevi Church of St. George, after the nearby village of Chitakhevi and the saint to whom the church is dedicated.

faq.title: What is the story of the "bloody stones"?
faq.content: In the 16th century, during the invasion of Shah Tahmasp of Persia, the monastery was captured and its monks were tortured and killed. By tradition, the reddish stones in the bed of the Chitakhevi stream that runs through the grounds are stained with the blood of those martyred monks — the "bloody stones." They're found along much of the stream, one is displayed inside the church, and the monks regard their lasting color as miraculous. Orthodox pilgrims come to pray among them and honor the martyrs, so the stones and the site should be treated with respect as a place of remembrance.

faq.title: How old is the Green Monastery, and is it still active?
faq.content: It dates to the 9th century (some sources say 9th–10th), founded by two disciples of the great monastic figure St. Grigol Khandzteli. After the 16th-century massacre and the region's later fall under Ottoman control, the monastery was abandoned in the 18th century and stood empty for around 200 years. It was restored from the late 20th century, and monastic life was revived around 2002–2003 — so today it's once again an active monastery with a small resident community, alongside the medieval church, a 15th–17th-century bell tower, and fragments of 12th–13th-century frescoes.

faq.title: How do you visit the Green Monastery?
faq.content: It's about 8–10 km from Borjomi (15–20 minutes by car), reached by a road that follows a forest stream up the gorge; the final stretch is unpaved and best with a higher-clearance vehicle after rain, and a taxi from Borjomi works well if you don't have a car. Entry is free and it's open during daylight hours, though access may be limited during services. As an active monastery and a place of martyrdom and pilgrimage, dress modestly (shoulders and knees covered; head coverings for women expected) and treat the site quietly and respectfully. It pairs naturally with Borjomi's Central Park and the national park.

borjomiKharagauliNationalPark.heroTitle: Borjomi-Kharagauli National Park: One of Europe's Great Wildernesses
borjomiKharagauliNationalPark.content:
The Borjomi-Kharagauli National Park covers roughly 85,000 hectares of mountain forest and alpine terrain in the Lesser Caucasus of central Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — stretching from the Borjomi gorge in the east to the town of Kharagauli in the west, across parts of three regions (Imereti, Samtskhe-Javakheti, and Shida Kartli). It is one of the largest national parks in Europe, and one of the most ecologically significant protected forests in the Caucasus — a continuous expanse of largely undisturbed temperate and subalpine forest that works as a genuine wildlife corridor rather than an isolated island in a developed landscape. For travelers with a serious interest in hiking, wildlife, and wild country, it is Georgia's single most important destination of its kind.

What is Borjomi-Kharagauli National Park?

Borjomi-Kharagauli National Park is one of the largest national parks in Europe — about 85,000 hectares of forest and alpine ridge in the Lesser Caucasus of central Georgia, between the spa town of Borjomi and the town of Kharagauli. Created in 1995 (with WWF and German support) and opened to visitors in 2001, it was the first national park in the Caucasus built to international standards. It protects species-rich Caucasian and Colchic forest, brown bear, lynx, wolf, and Caucasian grouse, and rare relic yew groves, and offers Georgia's most developed network of marked day-hikes and multi-day trekking routes, with two visitor centers (Borjomi and Kharagauli) and overnight trail shelters.

History and significance

The park was created in 1995, with the support of WWF and the German government, and opened to visitors in 2001 — the first national park in the Caucasus established to international standards, though the forests it protects had been under some conservation management since the Soviet period, and before that served for centuries as royal and noble hunting grounds. Its creation reflected both the ecological value of the forest — the Caucasian and Colchic mixed-forest types here are among the most species-rich temperate woodland in Europe — and the ambition to build a Georgian eco-tourism infrastructure capable of drawing international hikers. The result is a park whose trail network and visitor infrastructure are more developed than most protected areas in the South Caucasus, while remaining considerably wilder and less organized than the major parks of Western Europe. (Together with the adjacent Borjomi State Nature Reserve, the wider protected area reaches around 851 km², more than 1% of Georgia's territory.)

The landscape

The terrain rises from the forested lower valleys around Borjomi, at roughly 800 meters, through a sequence of ecological zones — mixed deciduous forest of oriental beech, hornbeam, and oak at lower and middle elevations, giving way to fir and spruce conifer forest higher up, then to open subalpine meadow and rocky terrain on the upper ridges above 2,000 meters. The highest point reaches about 2,642 meters (Mt. Sametskhvario), and the contrast between the enclosed green forest valleys below and the open, windswept alpine ridges above is dramatic enough to feel like different countries.

The rivers and streams that drain the park are fast, cold, and exceptionally clear, fed by snowmelt and rain through the summer — a continuous element of sound and movement through the forested valleys. The Borjomula, the Gujaretistskali, and their tributaries rise within or near the park, and following a valley stream up through the forest into the open terrain above is one of the most consistently satisfying hikes the park offers.

Wildlife and forest

The park protects significant populations of the characteristic Caucasian mountain-forest species — brown bear, wolf, lynx, wild boar, roe deer, chamois, and red deer — along with many smaller mammals and a rich bird community. The forest is extensive and connected enough to support viable breeding populations of the larger predators, and the park's management explicitly prioritizes this ecological integrity over maximum visitor access. Wildlife sightings are genuinely possible on multi-day routes into the remoter sections, but the dense forest and the animals' wariness mean encounters are never guaranteed and are best treated as welcome bonuses, not expected highlights.

The birdlife is a particular draw for naturalists — Caucasian (black) grouse, raptors including short-toed snake eagle and lesser spotted eagle, and a range of old-growth-forest birds that are declining across much of Europe but remain relatively intact here. The flora is notable too: the park preserves rare relic groves of yew (Taxus baccata), a "Red List" threatened tree, in a region where such ancient yew stands have survived as almost nowhere else.

The trail network

The marked, mapped trail system is one of the park's most developed features and the main reason most international visitors come — described in materials from the visitor centers in Borjomi and Kharagauli, with around a dozen routes ranging from short half-day walks to multi-day traverses linking the two centers.

The Panorama Trail is the celebrated high-ridge route, following the main ridge system through open subalpine terrain with wide views over the surrounding forest and, on clear days, north to the Greater Caucasus and south across southern Georgia. The combination of the ridge terrain, the open views, and the sense of crossing a genuinely wild landscape makes it one of the more memorable high-mountain hikes in Georgia.

Among the multi-day routes:

  • The St. Andrew Trail — about 54 km, up to four days, the most demanding route — descends from the high ridge into the lower forest valleys, combining open alpine sections with atmospheric forest hiking, giving it more ecological variety than a purely high-ridge walk (often preferred by those with botanical or wildlife interests).
  • The Nikoloz Romanov Trail — about 43 km over three days, partly on foot and partly on horseback — follows a historic route through the former Romanov royal estate (from the Likani valley through the Lomismta mountains to the Marelisi station), tying the park's natural heritage to the imperial history of Borjomi below.

Shorter routes from both entrances let visitors without the time or gear for multi-day treks experience the real character of the park — the Footprint Trail from the Borjomi/Likani side (about 13–15 km, a day route past rare yew trees and the Mariamtsminda Church) and several loops from Kharagauli among them.

Infrastructure and preparation

The visitor centers in Borjomi and Kharagauli are the main access points, registration facilities, and information resources. Registration before any multi-day route is required — it gives the park authorities the information to respond to emergencies, and gives hikers the latest on trail and shelter conditions. Maps and route descriptions are available at the centers and online.

The overnight shelters along the main trails are simple wooden structures — roof and walls rather than furnished accommodation — so a sleeping bag is essential on any overnight route, as is enough food for the whole hike, since there are no resupply points on the main network. Weather in the high sections can change fast and hard, so carry clothing for cold, rain, and wind regardless of conditions at the trailhead.

Water is generally available from the many streams and springs, and quality in the upper sections (well away from grazing) is typically very good, though treating it is a sensible precaution. The park has a genuine brown bear population, so basic food-storage precautions are appropriate on remote overnight routes — hanging food away from and above sleeping areas is standard practice.

Getting there and access points

  • Eastern entrance — Borjomi: the most-used start, about 2.5–3 hours from Tbilisi by car or marshrutka; the visitor center and trailhead are a short way from Borjomi town center.
  • Western entrance — Kharagauli: on the main Tbilisi–Batumi railway, reachable by train from either direction without a car — handy for hikers doing the full east-to-west crossing and exiting at Kharagauli rather than returning to the start.
  • Some remoter trailheads (e.g. Marelisi, Atskuri) may need a vehicle, possibly 4WD depending on the road and weather.
  • Visitor centers: Borjomi (about 1 km from the central park entrance) and Kharagauli (town center).
  • Season: roughly May/June through October for hiking; the high ridge trails are generally snow-free from late May/June to early/mid-October, with July–August the most stable. Autumn (September–October) offers excellent conditions, fewer people, and the forest at its most colorful. High trails are snow-closed roughly November to late spring; winter access to the high routes is limited — discuss with the visitor center before planning.

faq.title: What is Borjomi-Kharagauli National Park?
faq.content: It's one of the largest national parks in Europe — about 85,000 hectares of forest and alpine ridge in the Lesser Caucasus of central Georgia, stretching from the spa town of Borjomi to the town of Kharagauli across three regions. Created in 1995 (with WWF and German support) and opened to visitors in 2001, it was the first national park in the Caucasus built to international standards. It protects species-rich Caucasian and Colchic forest, large mammals like brown bear, lynx, and wolf, the Caucasian grouse, and rare relic yew groves, and offers Georgia's most developed network of marked hiking and trekking trails, with two visitor centers and overnight trail shelters.

faq.title: How big is the park, and is it really one of the largest in Europe?
faq.content: The national park itself covers about 85,000 hectares (roughly 850 km²); together with the adjacent Borjomi State Nature Reserve, the wider protected area reaches around 851 km² — more than 1% of Georgia's entire territory. It's widely described as one of the largest national parks in Europe, and it was the first in the Caucasus region created to international conservation standards. The terrain ranges from about 800 m in the forested valleys up to around 2,642 m at Mt. Sametskhvario, spanning everything from dense deciduous and conifer forest to open subalpine meadow.

faq.title: What are the best hikes in Borjomi-Kharagauli?
faq.content: There are around a dozen marked routes, from half-day walks to multi-day traverses. The celebrated high route is the Panorama Trail, following the main ridge through open subalpine terrain with sweeping views (to the Greater Caucasus on clear days). For multi-day trekking, the St. Andrew Trail (about 54 km, up to four days) is the most demanding, descending from ridge to forest valley; the Nikoloz Romanov Trail (about 43 km over three days, partly on horseback) follows the former Romanov royal estate. For a day hike, the Footprint Trail from the Likani/Borjomi side (about 13–15 km, past rare yew trees and a hilltop church) is a good choice, along with shorter loops from Kharagauli.

faq.title: What do I need to know before hiking the multi-day trails?
faq.content: Register at a visitor center (Borjomi or Kharagauli) before any multi-day route — it's required, and it lets the park respond in an emergency and give you current trail and shelter information. Overnight shelters are simple wooden structures (roof and walls only), so bring a sleeping bag and all your food, as there are no resupply points. Carry clothing for cold, rain, and wind even if the trailhead is warm, since high-altitude weather changes fast. Water is widely available from streams (treating it is a sensible precaution), and because the park has a real brown-bear population, store food properly — hung away from and above sleeping areas — on remote overnight routes.

faq.title: When is the best time to visit, and how do you get there?
faq.content: The hiking season runs roughly May/June through October, with the high ridge trails generally snow-free from late May/June to early/mid-October and July–August the most settled; autumn (September–October) is excellent, with fewer people and colorful forest. High trails are snow-closed in winter. The eastern entrance at Borjomi is the most used, about 2.5–3 hours from Tbilisi by road; the western entrance at Kharagauli sits on the Tbilisi–Batumi railway, so you can reach it by train and even do the full east-to-west crossing without returning to your start.

borjomiSulphurPools.heroTitle: Borjomi Sulphur Pools: Forest Hot Springs Above the Spa Town
borjomiSulphurPools.content:
The Borjomi Sulphur Pools are a small set of open-air thermal pools fed by warm, mineral-rich spring water in the forested hills above Borjomi — the mineral-water spa town in the Samtskhe-Javakheti region of southern Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus). Reached on foot by a forest trail up the river valley from Borjomi Central Park, or by vehicle along a rough track, they are one of the more genuinely restorative outings in the Borjomi area: informal, set entirely within the forest, and far enough up the gorge that the bustle of the town below feels comfortably distant by the time you arrive. The pools — sometimes called the "Tsars' baths" for their imperial-era origins — are about the natural setting as much as the water: warm mineral bathing in a clearing beside a mountain stream, with the forest all around.

What are the Borjomi Sulphur Pools?

The Borjomi Sulphur Pools are open-air natural thermal pools in the forest about 3 km upstream from Borjomi Central Park, in southern Georgia. The warm, sulfurous mineral water collects in a few basic stone-and-concrete basins in a streamside clearing, surrounded by dense gorge forest. Originally arranged in the Romanov era (the "Tsars' baths") and restored in the mid-2000s, they're reached by a 30–45 minute forest walk from the Central Park or by a rough vehicle track. The appeal is the natural setting and the simple pleasure of warm mineral bathing outdoors — the facilities are very basic, so it's best approached as a rustic forest soak rather than a polished spa.

The water

The water comes from the same broad geothermal system that feeds the famous Borjomi mineral springs in the Central Park, but it differs from the cold, strongly carbonated drinking water down in the town: here it emerges warm and sulfurous, collected into a series of open basins. The temperature is moderate rather than hot — pleasantly warm, varying between the pools and with the season, and some visitors find it cooler than they expect, so set expectations toward "comfortable warm soak" rather than "hot spring." The sulfur gives the water the characteristic faint hydrogen-sulfide odor common to sulfur springs everywhere; it fades as you acclimatize and is simply a natural feature of the water chemistry, not a sign of any contamination.

Sulfur thermal waters like these have a long association with bathing for skin, joint, and circulatory complaints, and local tradition has attributed restorative properties to these pools for as long as they've been known — the imperial and Soviet spa culture of Borjomi was built on exactly that balneological tradition. Whether specific health claims hold up is a matter for the medical and balneological literature, and a soak here is no substitute for medical advice; but the plain pleasure of lying in warm mineral water in a mountain forest needs no scientific justification.

Getting there

The most rewarding approach is on foot from Borjomi Central Park, following the trail that climbs upstream along the river valley for roughly 2.5–3 km — about 30–45 minutes at an easy pace. The path runs through the increasingly dense and beautiful forest of the gorge above the town, the stream alongside throughout and the canopy giving shade in warm weather, and the walk gives arrival at the pools the quality of earned relaxation rather than a simple transfer. (Note that part of the lower route runs through the Central Park, where a small entry fee applies.)

Vehicle access is possible via a forest track branching off near Borjomi, which needs a vehicle with reasonable ground clearance on the unpaved section; a local taxi driver who knows the route is the practical choice for anyone who'd rather not walk both ways. Walking back down through the forest after bathing, with the town gradually reappearing through the trees as the valley widens, makes a satisfying end to the trip.

Seasonal character

The pools are open and enjoyable year-round, but the most atmospheric conditions come in autumn and winter. In autumn the gorge forest turns color, and the warm water amid the changing trees makes a beautiful setting. In winter, when the air is cold and the trees bare or snow-covered, the warm water sends up clouds of steam into the cold forest — a genuinely dramatic sight, and the contrast between warm water and cold air is at its most invigorating. Reaching the pools through a quiet, snow-covered forest is Borjomi at its most atmospheric. Summer is pleasant for the forest walk and the soak, though with the air warm the temperature contrast — and the refreshing thermal effect — is least pronounced, and the pools can get busy.

A note on expectations: this is a rustic, popular spot, not a managed spa. At peak times the small pools can be crowded, and the basic, open-air changing arrangements suit some visitors better than others. Approached for what it is — a simple forest hot spring — it's a lovely experience; approached expecting spa comfort, it can disappoint.

What to bring

  • Swimwear and a towel — essential.
  • Sandals or waterproof footwear for moving between the pools and over the surrounding ground — strongly recommended.
  • A warm layer for afterward — important in cooler seasons, as the move from warm water to mountain air cools you quickly, especially in autumn and winter.
  • Changing facilities are basic and open-air; calibrate expectations to a natural outdoor bathing spot rather than an organized spa.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: Forested valley above Borjomi, about 2.5–3 km upstream from Borjomi Central Park, Samtskhe-Javakheti, southern Georgia.
  • Getting there: On foot via the forest trail from the Central Park (30–45 min), or by the rough forest vehicle track (local taxi recommended without a suitable vehicle).
  • Hours: Accessible in daylight; no fixed closing time.
  • Admission: A small fee may apply at the pools, and the Central Park (which the walking route passes through) charges its own small entry fee.
  • Best time: Autumn and winter for the most atmospheric bathing (steam in the cold air); year-round otherwise.
  • Combine with: Borjomi Central Park and the mineral spring, the cable car, the Borjomi-Kharagauli National Park, and the wider region — Likani (Romanov) Palace, Bakuriani, Akhaltsikhe, Abastumani, and Vardzia.

faq.title: What are the Borjomi Sulphur Pools?
faq.content: The Borjomi Sulphur Pools are open-air natural thermal pools in the forest above Borjomi, the mineral-water spa town in southern Georgia, about 3 km upstream from Borjomi Central Park. Warm, sulfurous mineral water collects in a few basic stone-and-concrete basins in a clearing beside a mountain stream, surrounded by dense gorge forest. Originally arranged in the Romanov era (the "Tsars' baths") and restored in the mid-2000s, they're reached by a 30–45 minute forest walk from the Central Park or by a rough vehicle track. The appeal is the natural setting and the simple pleasure of an outdoor warm soak — the facilities are very basic.

faq.title: How hot is the water at the Borjomi Sulphur Pools?
faq.content: It's warm rather than hot — a comfortable soaking temperature that varies between the pools and with the season, but milder than many people expect from a "hot spring," so it's best to set expectations toward a pleasant warm bath rather than a steaming one. The water has the faint sulfur (hydrogen-sulfide) smell typical of sulfur springs worldwide, which fades as you get used to it and is just part of the natural water chemistry. The temperature contrast is most enjoyable in cold weather, when the warm water steams against the winter air.

faq.title: Are the Borjomi Sulphur Pools good for your health?
