Khvamli — the limestone massif that dominates central Lechkhumi, feeds the valley’s karst springs and waterfalls (including the Verdzistava fall with its ram’s-head legend), and carries the region’s richest body of myth — was the spiritual anchor of the area, and the fortress’s donation to its Church of St. George ties the stronghold to that tradition. The Zubi area also reaches deeper still: like much of Lechkhumi, it was part of the ancient Colchian metalworking landscape, with evidence of Bronze/Iron-Age ore-working near the village, and it sits within the Usakhelouri wine microzone (Zubi-Okureshi-Isunderi) that produces one of Georgia’s rarest and most prized wines.

The fortress structure

The fortress is laid out across the rocky promontory to exploit the terrain to maximum effect. With cliff faces and the river gorge defending three sides, the builders concentrated their effort on the single approachable side — an efficient use of resources rather than an all-round defensive circuit. The complex steps up from the approach through lower defensive terraces and walls that close off the accessible side, to a citadel on the highest, most defensible part of the cape, with towers at the key points and elevated watch positions commanding the valley in both directions. It’s compact — the scale of a regional stronghold and ducal residence, defensible by a modest garrison, rather than a royal fortress.

The surviving masonry varies across the site: the heaviest, best-built sections retain the most height and give the clearest reading of the defensive circuit, while lighter sections have been worn down by time and the forest. The overall state is one of partial survival — enough to make the defensive logic intelligible, with the atmospheric quality of a place substantially returned to nature.

The setting and views

The views from the promontory are among the most dramatic from any accessible point in Lechkhumi. The fortress ruins in the foreground, the Tskhenistskali valley below, and the forested ridges of the broader landscape in every direction reward time spent at the viewpoints rather than a quick circuit. The gorge directly beneath the cliffs — the natural feature that made the site defensible with a small garrison — adds a vertical dimension that gives the fortress its characteristic sense of elevation and exposure.

The site photographs especially well in autumn, when the deciduous forest on the surrounding slopes turns gold and orange against the evergreen conifers, and in the misty conditions common in the wetter seasons, which lend the ruins an atmospheric isolation that clear weather can’t match. The relationship between the promontory, the gorge, and the valley reads most fully from above.

The approach

The fortress is reached from Zubi village by an uphill walk of about 20–40 minutes, depending on the starting point and pace, through forest and across rocky sections, with valley views that improve as you climb. The difficulty is easy-to-moderate — sustained enough to need reasonable footwear and basic fitness, but not technically demanding like the serious mountain approaches of Svaneti or Kazbegi. Footwear with good grip is genuinely recommended, given the rocky upper approach and the cliff-edge areas within the fortress precinct.

Zubi village and the fortress approach are also the eastern trailhead for the Askhi Massif trek — the multi-day limestone-plateau crossing that is the most ambitious hike in Lechkhumi. Trekkers can fold the fortress visit into their approach day before starting the plateau ascent, giving the trek a historical opening that links the region’s cultural and natural sides in one journey.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: Near Zubi village, on a promontory above the Tskhenistskali River, Tsageri municipality, Lechkhumi (Racha-Lechkhumi and Kvemo Svaneti region), western Georgia.
  • Getting there: From Tsageri (the regional center, ~5 hours from Tbilisi via Kutaisi) by the Tskhenistskali valley road network; a vehicle with reasonable ground clearance is advisable, especially after rain. From Zubi village, a 20–40-minute uphill walk to the fortress.
  • Time needed: About 1–1.5 hours for the ruins, viewpoints, and approach.
  • Access / admission: Freely accessible, no fee or visitor infrastructure.
  • Best time: Spring through autumn; autumn for forest color, and misty days for atmosphere. Wear footwear with grip.
  • Combine with: The Lechkhumi circuit — the Kulbaki lakes, the Ghvirishi, Rachkha, and Verdzistava waterfalls, Khvamli Mountain, the Tvishi and Usakhelouri wine villages, the Dekhviri and Orbeli fortresses, and the Tsageri History Museum — or as the historical opening to an Askhi Massif trek.

faq.title: What is Zubi Fortress?
faq.content: Zubi Fortress is a ruined medieval stronghold on a rocky promontory above the Tskhenistskali River near the village of Zubi, in the Lechkhumi region of western Georgia. Sited on a cape with natural cliff defenses on three sides, it controlled the main medieval road between the Imereti lowlands and Lower Svaneti. It’s identified with the historic Takveri Fortress described by the 18th-century scholar Vakhushti Batonishvili, and was likely a residence of the dukes (eristavi) of Takveri — the old name for the Lechkhumi region. Reached by a short uphill walk from Zubi village, it offers dramatic views over the gorge and valley.

faq.title: Why is Zubi Fortress historically important?
faq.content: It carries an unusually well-documented identity for a regional Georgian fortress. The 18th-century geographer-prince Vakhushti Batonishvili described the Takveri Fortress as standing on a cape on the banks of the Tskhenistskali at the foot of the Caucasus — a description that matches Zubi exactly — and Takveri was the old name of the Lechkhumi region itself, so this was effectively the stronghold of the historic duchy. It guarded the strategic Imereti–Lower Svaneti road and likely served as a ducal residence. In the late 17th century, Grigol Tsagereli donated it to the Khvamli Church of St. George, tying it to the region’s most important religious tradition.

faq.title: What can you see at the fortress?
faq.content: The fortress occupies the promontory in a layout that uses the terrain’s natural defenses — cliffs and gorge on three sides — with built defenses concentrated on the single approachable side: lower terraces and walls stepping up to a citadel on the highest point, with towers and watch positions commanding the valley. The masonry survives in varying condition, enough to read the defensive plan. But the views are as much the reward as the ruins: the gorge directly below and the Tskhenistskali valley and forested ridges beyond make the promontory one of the more dramatic viewpoints in Lechkhumi, especially in autumn color or atmospheric mist.

faq.title: How hard is the walk to Zubi Fortress?
faq.content: It’s an easy-to-moderate uphill walk of about 20 to 40 minutes from Zubi village, through forest and across some rocky sections, with valley views that improve as you climb. It’s not technically demanding like the serious mountain hikes of Svaneti or Kazbegi, but it’s sustained enough to need basic fitness and proper footwear — and grippy shoes are genuinely worth having, given the rocky upper approach and the cliff edges within the fortress itself. Allow about 1–1.5 hours for the whole visit including the walk, the ruins, and time at the viewpoints.

faq.title: How do you get to Zubi Fortress, and what can you combine it with?
faq.content: Zubi village is reached from Tsageri, the regional center of Lechkhumi (about 5 hours from Tbilisi via Kutaisi), along the Tskhenistskali valley roads; a vehicle with reasonable ground clearance helps, especially after rain. The site is free, with no visitor infrastructure. It combines naturally with the wider Lechkhumi circuit — the Kulbaki lakes, the Ghvirishi, Rachkha, and Verdzistava waterfalls, Khvamli Mountain, the Tvishi and Usakhelouri wine villages, and the Dekhviri and Orbeli fortresses. Zubi is also the eastern trailhead for the multi-day Askhi Massif trek, so it makes a natural historical opening to that hike.

orbeliFortress.heroTitle: Orbeli Fortress: A Medieval Stronghold Above the Lajanuri Valley
orbeliFortress.content:
Orbeli Fortress is a medieval stronghold ruin in the Lechkhumi region of western Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — on a rocky bluff near the village of Orbeli, on the Orbeli pass above the Lajanuri River valley, in the Tsageri municipality. In its day it was regarded as one of the most impressive fortifications in Lechkhumi — valued in the historical record for its architecture, its style, and its strategic command of the valley routes. Centuries of abandonment have reduced it to ruins, but the qualities that earned its reputation remain legible: the commanding elevated position, the scale of the original construction, and the combination of walls, towers, church, and burial ground that made it a genuine center of regional life rather than a mere outpost. For travelers exploring the under-visited heritage of Lechkhumi, Orbeli is one of the more substantial and well-documented medieval sites in the region — and the views from its pass are among the finest in the area.

What is Orbeli Fortress?

Orbeli Fortress is a ruined 16th–17th-century stronghold on a rocky bluff near Orbeli village, on the Orbeli pass above the Lajanuri valley, in the Tsageri municipality of Lechkhumi, western Georgia. In its prime it was considered one of the region's most impressive fortifications, enclosed by an unusual oval wall about 50 by 30 meters, with three towers (the main one, facing north, holding living quarters), a church, and a graveyard. Its recovered treasures — including a crucifixion sculpture, an archangel icon, and the Orbeli Church Gospel — are now in the Kutaisi State Historical Museum. Freely accessible and rarely visited, it rewards a short stop with genuine history, an atmospheric old cemetery, and sweeping views over two river canyons.

Historical background

The fortress dates, in its main surviving form, to the 16th–17th centuries, though some accounts suggest a defensive structure stood on the site earlier — the kind of multi-period building and rebuilding common to Georgian highland fortifications, where later phases obscure earlier work and precise dating is hard without systematic excavation. What the sources are clearer about is its function and standing: it guarded the strategic routes through the Lajanuri valley and the wider Lechkhumi corridor, part of the feudal defensive network that controlled movement through this part of western Georgia in the era of the fragmented kingdoms that followed the unified Georgian state.

Contemporaries regarded Orbeli as one of the strongest and most architecturally accomplished fortifications in Lechkhumi — a reputation that hints at its original scale and quality relative to the region's other strongholds. The combination of the naturally defensible rocky elevation above the valley with a built circuit of oval walls and towers produced a fortress that would have been genuinely formidable by the standards of regional highland military architecture.

The church and burial ground within the complex give it the dual character — military stronghold and religious and residential center — typical of major Georgian noble fortifications of the medieval and early-modern periods. The church was not a mere pious addition but a functional and symbolic part of the complex: the liturgical heart of the community living within and around the walls, and an expression of the standing of the noble family or institution that held the site. The burial ground confirms the fortress was a place of enough status to warrant the interment of locally important people — a center of regional life, not just a defensive installation.

The structure

The fortress was enclosed within an oval defensive wall — an unusual plan for Georgian highland fortifications, which more often follow the rectangular or irregular forms dictated by their terrain — measuring roughly 50 meters long by 30 meters wide. This compact footprint, set on naturally defensible ground, made a fortress that a modest garrison could hold effectively while housing the residential and religious functions the complex required.

Within the oval circuit, the fortress is recorded as having had three towers, the main tower facing north and containing the living quarters, along with the church serving the community's spiritual life. The elevated lookout points commanded the Lajanuri valley in both directions, giving early warning of approach from either the lower valley toward Imereti or the upper routes toward Upper Lechkhumi and, ultimately, Svaneti.

The ruins today preserve this organization in fragmentary form. The most substantial surviving sections — the wall foundations and the lower courses of the towers — let a visitor familiar with medieval Georgian architecture read the original plan, while the more perishable elements have weathered down to a level that asks for some interpretive imagination. The church remains and burial ground are part of the site's archaeological record, and the artifacts recovered here are unusually fine for so small a site: an ancient sculpture of the crucifixion, an icon of an archangel, and the Orbeli Fortress Church Gospel — all now preserved in the Kutaisi State Historical Museum, where they contribute to the documentation of Lechkhumi's medieval heritage. Traces of painted decoration survive on the ruins themselves, and archaeological digs have uncovered a tomb within the complex.

The old cemetery

One detail makes Orbeli's burial ground especially worth lingering over: the gravestones carry folk bas-relief carvings depicting the trades and interests of the people buried beneath them — a guitar, a sewing machine, the tools and emblems of individual lives. It's a quietly moving folk-art tradition, turning the cemetery into a kind of portrait of the village's past inhabitants, and the sort of human, specific touch that rewards an unhurried visit far more than the fortress walls alone.

The setting and views

Orbeli sits on the Orbeli mountain pass, and the views are among the qualities that make it worth visiting in their own right. From the high point of the pass, the panorama opens onto two river canyons at once — the Tskhenistskali on one side and the Lajanuri on the other — a genuinely commanding vantage that explains at a glance why the site was chosen. The Lajanuri valley has its own character within Lechkhumi: narrower and more deeply forested than the main Tskhenistskali corridor, the slopes pressing close on the valley floor. From the fortress, this enclosed valley landscape — forested ridges on both sides, agricultural patches on the floor, the rural Lechkhumi country reaching toward the distant mountains — connects the fortress's historical purpose directly to the geography it was built to watch.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: Near Orbeli village, on the Orbeli pass above the Lajanuri valley, Tsageri municipality, Lechkhumi (Racha-Lechkhumi and Kvemo Svaneti), western Georgia.
  • Getting there: From Tsageri (the Lechkhumi regional center, ~5 hours from Tbilisi via Kutaisi), by the secondary roads of the Lajanuri valley / Orbeli pass. A vehicle with reasonable ground clearance is advisable, especially after rain. Drive with care on the pass road — it is prone to falling rocks and occasional landslide damage; obey any posted cautions.
  • Time needed: About 30–60 minutes for the ruins, the cemetery, and the viewpoints.
  • Access: Freely accessible, no entrance fee or visitor infrastructure; bring water, as facilities are limited.
  • Combine with: The wider Lechkhumi circuit — the nearby Muri Fortress (the historic seat of Lechkhumi, with the tomb of St. Maximus the Confessor), the Dekhviri fortress, the Tvishi wine villages, the Kulbaki lakes, and Khvamli Mountain (reachable on foot from the Orbeli area for hikers). Orbeli is also the home terroir of the red Orbeluri Ojaleshi wine.

faq.title: What is Orbeli Fortress?
faq.content: Orbeli Fortress is a ruined medieval stronghold on a rocky bluff near Orbeli village, on the Orbeli pass above the Lajanuri valley, in the Tsageri municipality of Lechkhumi, western Georgia. Dating in its main form to the 16th–17th centuries, it was considered one of the region's most impressive fortifications, enclosed by an unusual oval wall about 50 by 30 meters with three towers, a church, and a graveyard. It guarded the strategic routes through the Lajanuri valley. Freely accessible and rarely visited today, it rewards a short stop with genuine history, an atmospheric old cemetery, and sweeping views.

faq.title: How old is Orbeli Fortress, and why was it important?
faq.content: Its main surviving form dates to the 16th–17th centuries, though a defensive structure may have stood on the site earlier. During those centuries it was regarded as one of the strongest and most architecturally accomplished fortifications in Lechkhumi, valued for its architecture, style, and strategic command of the Lajanuri valley routes within the feudal defensive network of western Georgia. With its church and burial ground, it functioned as a center of regional life — a noble stronghold and religious-residential complex — rather than just a military outpost.

faq.title: What can you see at Orbeli Fortress today?
faq.content: The ruins preserve the oval wall foundations and the lower courses of the towers — enough to read the original plan — along with the remains of a church and an old graveyard, and traces of painted decoration on the stonework. A standout detail is the cemetery: its gravestones carry folk bas-relief carvings depicting the trades and interests of those buried there (a guitar, a sewing machine, and more), a quietly moving portrait of the village's past. The site's finest recovered artifacts — a crucifixion sculpture, an archangel icon, and the Orbeli Church Gospel — are now kept in the Kutaisi State Historical Museum.

faq.title: Are the views from Orbeli Fortress good?
faq.content: Yes — they're among the best in the area, and a reason to visit in their own right. The fortress sits on the Orbeli mountain pass, from which the panorama opens onto two river canyons at once: the Tskhenistskali on one side and the Lajanuri on the other. The Lajanuri valley itself is narrow and deeply forested, the slopes pressing close, and from the elevated fortress you get the full sense of the enclosed valley landscape the stronghold was built to command. The drive up to the pass is scenic, though it requires care for falling rocks.

faq.title: How do you get to Orbeli Fortress?
faq.content: It's near Orbeli village on the Orbeli pass, in the Tsageri municipality of Lechkhumi, reached from Tsageri (the regional center, about 5 hours from Tbilisi via Kutaisi) by the secondary roads of the Lajanuri valley. A vehicle with reasonable ground clearance is advisable, especially after rain, and you should drive carefully on the pass road, which is prone to falling rocks and occasional landslides. The site is free with no infrastructure, so bring water. It combines well with the nearby Muri Fortress, the Dekhviri fortress, the Tvishi wines, the Kulbaki lakes, and Khvamli Mountain.

lailashiPool.heroTitle: The Lailashi Pool (Okronishi): An Infinity Pool in a Historic Village
lailashiPool.content:
In the mountain village of Lailashi in the Lechkhumi highlands of western Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — a simple stone pool fed by a natural spring, set on a hillside high above the Lajanuri reservoir, has become one of the most widely shared images of rural Georgia online. The Okronishi pool — now widely called the Lailashi Secret Pool — is not an engineered resort infinity pool. It is a modest, functional spring-fed pool, built long ago by the village's Jewish community, that happens to sit at the edge of a hillside with a sweeping view over the forested valley and reservoir below; the spring water overflowing its edge creates, in photos and in person, the illusion of water meeting the valley horizon like a luxury infinity pool. The gap between the simplicity of the thing and the drama of what it appears to be is part of its appeal — but the pool is only the most photographed corner of a village with a genuinely remarkable history.

What is the Lailashi (Okronishi) Pool?

The Lailashi Pool, or Okronishi, is a small spring-fed stone pool in the village of Lailashi, in the Tsageri municipality of Lechkhumi, western Georgia, at about 850 meters elevation. Continuously fed by cold mountain spring water that overflows its edge, it produces a natural "infinity pool" effect against a panoramic view of the Lajanuri valley and reservoir below — which is why it has become a viral photo spot, nicknamed "Georgian Thailand" for its views. You can swim in the cold water in summer. But Lailashi is also one of Georgia's most historic multi-ethnic villages — long home to Georgians, Jews, Greeks, and Armenians together — with an old synagogue and a famous medieval manuscript, the Lailashi Bible, behind its name. It's reached by a steep mountain road from Tsageri.

The pool

The pool is stone-built and fed continuously by cold spring water flowing from the hillside above. The water is genuinely cold whatever the season — mountain spring temperature here rarely rises above what most would call bracing rather than comfortable — and swimming in summer is entirely possible for those willing to commit to the chill. In warm weather the cold plunge is a real draw after the heat of the drive up, and the combination of the cold water and the panoramic view from the pool's edge makes the stop more than just a photograph. (The spring is locally valued, and the water is traditionally regarded as beneficial — though it's best enjoyed simply as a fresh mountain spring rather than as any kind of proven remedy.)

The overflow that creates the infinity effect is a natural result of the continuous spring feed rather than a designed feature, and it varies with the season and the spring's flow. In spring and early summer, when the mountain water table is highest, the overflow is most pronounced and the effect most complete; in drier late summer the flow may ease, though the pool itself stays full.

Lailashi: a village of remarkable history

The pool is the village's most photographed feature, but Lailashi's deeper story is what makes it memorable. Set at around 850 meters above the Lajanuri valley, Lailashi was for centuries a genuinely multi-ethnic community — Georgians, Jews, Greeks, and Armenians living side by side — a rare and notable example of long interfaith coexistence in the Georgian highlands, commemorated today by a Friendship Obelisk raised in the village in 2013.

Its Jewish heritage is especially significant. Lailashi was home to one of Georgia's notable Jewish communities — by some accounts well over a thousand Jewish residents in the early 20th century, still dozens of families into the 1970s — and the Lailashi synagogue, built in the 1860s and active until the 1970s, still stands (now largely disused, a listed cultural monument, and the subject of recent restoration efforts by Georgian-Jewish supporters). The Okronishi pool itself was built by this community. Most remarkably, the village gives its name to the Lailashi Bible (the Lailashi Codex) — a medieval Hebrew biblical manuscript of great scholarly value, among only a handful of its kind known in the world, discovered here and now preserved at Georgia's National Centre of Manuscripts in Tbilisi. How it came to this small mountain village remains, intriguingly, unknown.

The village also holds an old Armenian church (now ruined), a hall-type church and bell tower in its cemetery, and an active Jewish graveyard with readable inscriptions nearby — and its deep past reaches further still: a hoard of ancient bronze axes found at Lailashi (now in the Hermitage Museum) ties the village, like much of Lechkhumi, to the Bronze-Age Colchian metalworking culture. In August 2025 the Tsageri municipality held a festival celebrating Lailashi's century-long multi-ethnic coexistence, drawing Georgian Jews back from Jerusalem for the occasion — a sign that this heritage is being actively remembered and revived. For travelers, this means the pool is best understood not as an isolated photo stop but as the gateway to a village whose layered history rewards a slower, more curious visit.

Getting there

The road to Lailashi from the main valley road ends with a steep, unpaved final section of roughly 3–4 kilometers, best suited to a vehicle with good ground clearance and four-wheel drive. Its condition varies with the season — spring snowmelt and autumn rain make it considerably harder — so drivers without a suitable vehicle should either arrange a local taxi from Tsageri (or Ambrolauri) with a driver who knows the road, or park lower down and walk the final stretch (around 30–40 minutes on foot). Once at village level, the pool is a very short walk from the nearest parking — no real hiking is needed once the road section is managed. The drive up, along the mountainside with the reservoir opening up below, is part of the experience.

Atmosphere and seasons

Lailashi has the quality of a long-storied place only recently "discovered" by the wider travelling world — the village keeps its authentic rural character and the pool sits in its original functional context, but the photos circulating online have brought visitors who would not otherwise have known it existed. The site does not yet have developed tourism infrastructure, so arriving still feels like finding something rather than visiting something — a quality that grows rarer as Georgia's natural sites become better known. (It's worth visiting with awareness that this is a living village with a sensitive heritage, not a staged attraction — the synagogue and graveyards in particular deserve respect.)

Summer brings green landscapes and the possibility of swimming, the most popular season. Autumn is the most visually dramatic, when the forested valley turns through its full range of color and the low October light sets off reflections in the reservoir below. Winter closes the village road for extended periods and the pool surface may partly freeze — striking, with ice-edged stone against the snow-covered valley, but access is unreliable and should never be attempted without local confirmation of current road conditions.

Practical information

  • Location: Lailashi village, Tsageri municipality, Lechkhumi (Racha-Lechkhumi and Kvemo Svaneti region), western Georgia; ~850 m elevation, above the Lajanuri reservoir. About 2.5–3 hours from Kutaisi; ~40 km from Ambrolauri. Tsageri is the nearest regional center and practical base.
  • Access: The final 3–4 km of road need a 4x4 or high-clearance vehicle; standard cars aren't recommended for this section. A taxi from Tsageri or Ambrolauri with a driver who knows the road is a practical alternative, or park below and walk up (~30–40 min).
  • Hours / Admission: Freely accessible; no fee.
  • Best time: June–September for swimming and green landscapes; October for autumn color; winter access is unreliable — check road conditions locally before attempting the drive.
  • Combine with: The wider Lechkhumi circuit — the Ghvirishi Waterfall, the Kulbaki lakes, Khvamli Mountain, the Tvishi and Usakhelouri wine villages, and Tsageri (with its museum and the St. Maxime the Confessor church).

faq.title: What is the Lailashi Secret Pool (Okronishi)?
faq.content: It's a small spring-fed stone pool in the village of Lailashi, in the Tsageri municipality of Lechkhumi, western Georgia, at about 850 meters elevation. Cold mountain spring water continuously overflows its edge, creating a natural "infinity pool" effect against a sweeping view of the Lajanuri valley and reservoir below — which has made it a viral photo spot, nicknamed "Georgian Thailand" for its views. Originally built by the village's Jewish community, it's a modest, functional village pool rather than a resort feature — and the historic village around it is as much a reason to visit as the pool itself.

faq.title: Can you swim in the Lailashi Pool?
faq.content: Yes. The pool is fed by cold mountain spring water and you can swim in it in summer, though the water stays genuinely cold year-round — bracing rather than warm — so it's a refreshing plunge rather than a leisurely swim. In warm weather the cold water is a real draw after the hot drive up, and the combination of the swim and the panoramic view over the valley is the whole appeal. The spring is locally valued and its water traditionally regarded as beneficial, but it's best enjoyed simply as fresh mountain spring water.

faq.title: Why is Lailashi village historically important?
faq.content: Lailashi is one of Georgia's notable multi-ethnic villages — for centuries home to Georgians, Jews, Greeks, and Armenians living together, an example of long interfaith coexistence commemorated by a Friendship Obelisk raised in 2013. Its Jewish heritage is especially significant: it had a large Jewish community (over a thousand people in the early 20th century), and its 1860s synagogue still stands. The village gives its name to the Lailashi Bible (Lailashi Codex), a medieval Hebrew manuscript of great scholarly value — one of only a handful of its kind in the world — discovered here and now kept at Georgia's National Centre of Manuscripts in Tbilisi.

faq.title: How do you get to the Lailashi Pool, and is the road difficult?
faq.content: Lailashi is in the Tsageri municipality of Lechkhumi, about 2.5–3 hours from Kutaisi (and roughly 40 km from Ambrolauri). The final 3–4 km of road up to the village are steep and unpaved, requiring a 4x4 or high-clearance vehicle — standard cars aren't recommended for that section, and it gets harder after rain or snowmelt. If you don't have a suitable vehicle, hire a local taxi from Tsageri or Ambrolauri with a driver who knows the road, or park below and walk up (about 30–40 minutes). Once in the village, the pool is a very short walk from parking.

faq.title: When is the best time to visit Lailashi?
faq.content: June through September is best for swimming and green landscapes, and is the most popular season. October brings the most dramatic visuals, when the forested valley turns autumn colors and the low light sets off the reservoir below. Winter closes the village road for long stretches and the pool can partly freeze — visually striking, but access is unreliable and should never be attempted without checking current road conditions locally first. Whenever you go, remember Lailashi is a living village with a sensitive heritage — the synagogue and graveyards deserve respectful, quiet visits.

kulbakiLakes.heroTitle: The Kulbaki Lakes: Forest Lakes in Hidden Lechkhumi
kulbakiLakes.content:
The Kulbaki Lakes are a pair of small lakes in the Lechkhumi region of western Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — set in forested mountain terrain at around 955–990 meters above sea level, near the town of Tsageri. Lechkhumi is one of the least-visited regions in Georgia — a small highland area wedged between Racha to the east, Svaneti to the north, and Imereti to the south, with its own distinct character and a landscape of forested valleys that draws a fraction of the attention given its more famous neighbors. The Kulbaki Lakes have become one of the more accessible and rewarding natural destinations in this overlooked region: a pair of forest lakes with an unhurried, peaceful atmosphere that suits the slow-travel character of Lechkhumi, and a genuine nature retreat within reach of travelers passing through the western Georgian highlands.

What are the Kulbaki Lakes?

The Kulbaki Lakes are two small forest lakes in the Lechkhumi highlands of western Georgia, near Tsageri at about 955–990 m elevation: the larger Green Lake (Mtsvane Tba), with a calm, reflective surface and a small trout farm, and the smaller, more seasonal Babushkino Lake. Set in a wooded hollow, they're a quiet, easy nature destination with a cool forest climate — and a base for more serious hiking up into the Askhi plateau and the alpine Lebarde lakes above. Reached by car from Tsageri (about 4.5–5 hours from Tbilisi via Kutaisi), they're best visited May–October as part of a slow Lechkhumi itinerary.

The two lakes

The two lakes that give the destination its name sit only a few hundred meters apart in the same forested hollow, but they have distinctly different characters that make seeing both worthwhile rather than stopping at the more obvious one.

The Green Lake — Mtsvane Tba in Georgian — is the larger, more developed, and more visually striking of the pair. Its name reflects the green-tinged color the water takes on, the surrounding forest reflecting in the surface as the depth absorbs the light. Its origin is unusual: by local accounts the lake was created from marshland, when a resident built a small dam and developed a trout-farming operation, turning previously unproductive wet ground into a lake of real scenic appeal. That trout-farm heritage is still evident in the lake's fish population — a draw for local anglers — and the modest infrastructure that grew up around it (basic facilities, access paths, a cottage) gives it a degree of comfort and accessibility that purely wild lakes lack.

The calm, reflective surface, ringed by forested hills that come down close to the waterline, produces the mirror-effect conditions that reward both the casual visitor and the photographer arriving with a composition in mind. The atmosphere is unhurried and genuinely peaceful — the kind of place where sitting a long while with nothing particular to do feels rewarding rather than dull.

Babushkino Lake is smaller, shallower, and more seasonal — in dry spells and hot summers the level drops and the lake can partly dry out, revealing the more temporary, naturalistic character of a body of water that depends on the season's rainfall. That variability is part of what distinguishes it from the more stable Green Lake, and visiting both at different times of year gives genuinely different experiences of the same place. Babushkino's wilder, less-managed presentation appeals to visitors who find the Green Lake's modest development at odds with what drew them to Lechkhumi — and together the two offer a range that neither alone could.

Hiking and the broader landscape

The Kulbaki Lakes work not only as a destination but as a gateway to the mountain terrain above the Lechkhumi valley, which gives the area an outdoor depth that lakes-only spots lack. The hiking routes from Kulbaki reach up into the higher terrain of the Askhi massif and the alpine lake country around Lebarde — destinations that ask more time and fitness than the lakes themselves, but that open up the high-mountain character of Lechkhumi the valley-level lakes can't provide.

The Lebarde alpine lakes, reached on a longer route from the Kulbaki area, are among the more significant high-altitude destinations in Lechkhumi — small mountain lakes in open alpine terrain above the treeline, with the panoramas the forested lower ground around Kulbaki doesn't offer. Pairing an overnight at Kulbaki with a full-day ascent toward Lebarde (or onward across the Askhi plateau) makes a Lechkhumi itinerary that covers both the accessible, contemplative lakes and the more demanding reward of the alpine zone above.

Horseback riding through the surrounding forest and mountain terrain is available and offers a different pace and perspective than walking — the forest paths and mountain approaches take on a different quality from the saddle, and the tradition of mountain horses in this part of the Caucasus gives the activity an authenticity that more resort-style riding elsewhere lacks.

The Lechkhumi context

The Kulbaki Lakes make more sense understood within their region. Lechkhumi is a small highland area with its own deeply rooted wine tradition, its own architecture, and a way of life that has changed more slowly than in the country's more developed parts. Its wines are a genuine, if little-known, highlight: the Tvishi appellation — a Protected Designation of Origin producing a naturally semi-sweet white from the Tsolikouri grape, grown on the steep Rioni-valley terraces around the villages of Tvishi and Alpana — is among the most refined of Georgia's semi-sweet whites. Lechkhumi is also home to the rare and highly prized Usakhelouri (from the Zubi-Okureshi area, one of the most valuable grapes in the region) and the red Orbeluri Ojaleshi — a wine culture that punches well above the region's size. The regional center of Tsageri, the nearest town to the lakes, provides the practical services and accommodation base, and the wider Lechkhumi valley drive — through small villages, past traditional houses, along the rivers draining westward toward Imereti — gives the lakes the regional context that makes them feel part of a specific place rather than just a scenic stop.

Getting there and when to go

  • Location: Forested highlands near Tsageri, Lechkhumi (Racha-Lechkhumi and Kvemo Svaneti region), western Georgia; ~955–990 m elevation.
  • Getting there: By car from Tsageri, the approach road climbing through forest to the lakes; conditions vary with season and weather, and while a standard vehicle manages in dry summer conditions, a vehicle with reasonable ground clearance is advisable for the final stages, especially after rain. From Tbilisi via Kutaisi and Tsageri, allow about 4.5–5 hours.
  • Time needed: A relaxed half-day at the lakes; longer (with an overnight) if combining with the Askhi/Lebarde hiking above.
  • When to go: May–October, with June–September the most reliable for accessible roads, full lake levels, and the cool forest climate that makes Kulbaki a fine summer retreat from the lowland heat. October brings autumn color to the deciduous forest — visually among the best times — though days shorten and roads grow less reliable toward winter.
  • Combine with: A wider Lechkhumi and western-Georgia trip — Racha to the east, Svaneti to the north via the Lechkhumi–Svaneti road, the Imereti sites around Kutaisi to the southwest, and the rest of the Lechkhumi cluster (the Ghvirishi Waterfall, Khvamli Mountain, the Tvishi wine villages, Dekhviri).

faq.title: What are the Kulbaki Lakes?
faq.content: The Kulbaki Lakes are two small forest lakes in the Lechkhumi region of western Georgia, near the town of Tsageri at about 955–990 meters elevation. The larger Green Lake (Mtsvane Tba) has a calm, reflective surface and a small trout farm; the smaller Babushkino Lake is shallower and more seasonal. Set in a wooded mountain hollow, they're a quiet, accessible nature destination with a cool forest climate, and they also serve as a base for hiking up into the Askhi plateau and the alpine Lebarde lakes above. They're one of the more rewarding stops in the little-visited Lechkhumi region.

faq.title: What's the difference between the two lakes?
faq.content: The Green Lake is the larger, more developed, and more photogenic — created, by local accounts, from former marshland by a resident who built a dam and a trout farm, so it has a stable water level, a fish population, and some basic facilities (and a cottage). Its still, forest-reflecting surface makes it the more visually striking of the two. Babushkino Lake is smaller, shallower, and seasonal — it can partly dry out in hot, dry summers — and feels wilder and less managed. Seeing both gives a fuller experience: the calm, accessible Green Lake and the more naturalistic Babushkino.

faq.title: Can you hike from the Kulbaki Lakes?
faq.content: Yes — this is a big part of their appeal. The lakes sit at the foot of more serious mountain terrain, and trails from Kulbaki lead up into the Askhi massif (a remote limestone plateau) and toward the alpine Lebarde lakes above the treeline, which offer the high-mountain panoramas the forested lakes themselves don't. These are demanding routes for fit hikers (often with an overnight), quite different from the easy lakeside visit, so they're best done with proper preparation or a guide. Horseback riding through the surrounding forest is also available for a different pace.

faq.title: What is Lechkhumi known for besides the lakes?
faq.content: Wine, above all. Despite its small size, Lechkhumi is one of Georgia's notable wine regions: the Tvishi appellation (a Protected Designation of Origin) makes a refined, naturally semi-sweet white from the Tsolikouri grape on the steep Rioni-valley terraces around Tvishi and Alpana, and the region is also home to the rare, highly prized Usakhelouri and the red Orbeluri Ojaleshi. Beyond wine, Lechkhumi offers the dramatic Ghvirishi Waterfall, the mythic Khvamli Mountain, the Dekhviri fortress, and a slow, traditional way of life — making the Kulbaki Lakes one stop in a rewarding off-the-beaten-path region.

faq.title: How do you get to the Kulbaki Lakes, and when should you go?
faq.content: The lakes are reached by car from Tsageri, the Lechkhumi regional center; from Tbilisi via Kutaisi and Tsageri, allow about 4.5–5 hours. The approach road climbs through forest, and while a standard car manages in dry summer conditions, a vehicle with reasonable ground clearance is advisable for the final stretch, especially after rain. The season is May–October — June to September for the most reliable roads, full lake levels, and the cool forest climate that makes it a pleasant summer retreat, and October for autumn forest color (though days shorten and roads worsen toward winter).

choleviLake.heroTitle: Cholevi Lake (Lake of Love): A Mountain Lake in Racha
choleviLake.content:
Cholevi Lake — also known as Lake Mravaldzali, and popularly as the “Lake of Love” — is a mountain lake in the Oni municipality of Racha, in the Racha-Lechkhumi and Kvemo Svaneti region of western Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus). It sits at around 1,840 meters above sea level near the village of Mravaldzali, ringed by dense forest and mountain peaks, its greenish water set in one of the quieter corners of Upper Racha. The lake is best known for its distinctive shape, which has earned it the “Lake of Love” nickname and made it a favorite of photographers, and for the calm, scenic setting that rewards a relaxed visit — picnicking, fishing, a boat on the water, or simply the walk around the shore. It’s one of the more rewarding natural stops in a region that still sees far fewer visitors than Kakheti, Svaneti, or Georgia’s headline mountain destinations.

What is Cholevi Lake?

Cholevi Lake (also called Lake Mravaldzali, or the “Lake of Love”) is a forest-ringed mountain lake at about 1,840 meters elevation near the village of Mravaldzali in the Oni municipality of Racha, western Georgia. Surrounded by dense forest and mountain peaks, with greenish water and a distinctive shape that gives it its romantic nickname, it’s a calm, scenic spot popular for picnics, fishing, and boat rides, with the finest panorama looking down from nearby Mount Khikhati. Less famous than Racha’s headline sights, it’s a peaceful, accessible lake set in the quiet beauty of Upper Racha — especially striking in autumn.

The setting

Cholevi Lake sits in the forested mountain terrain of Upper Racha, where the southern slopes of the Greater Caucasus carry dense mixed forest up into the highlands. At around 1,840 meters, it’s a genuine mountain lake — but a forested one, ringed by trees and peaks rather than the bare rock and snow of the highest alpine basins, which gives it an enclosed, sheltered character. The water carries greenish shades, and on still days its calm surface reflects the surrounding forest and sky in a composition that shifts through the day with the light and the wind. The most expansive view of the lake opens from above, on Mount Khikhati, which looks down over the water and out across the surrounding Rachan mountains.

The seasons change the lake’s character considerably. Spring brings the rapid greening of the deciduous forest and the wildflowers — the lake’s surroundings are known for primroses, among the first flowers to bloom after winter. Summer is the fullest green, the canopy at its densest and the water reflecting deep greens. Autumn is the most striking season, when the mixed forest turns through gold, orange, and red and the lake mirrors the colored canopy — the time most photographers aim for. Winter brings a quiet isolation, the bare deciduous trees opening views that the summer canopy hides and the snow giving the setting a stark, stripped-down character.

What to do at the lake

Despite its out-of-the-way location, Cholevi Lake is a place set up for relaxed enjoyment rather than a wilderness you simply look at. The shore has picnic areas, the lake offers good fishing, and there’s the option of a boat ride on the water — so a visit can be a leisurely few hours rather than a quick photo stop. Walking around the shoreline, settling in for a picnic, watching the light change on the water, or climbing toward Mount Khikhati for the panorama are the natural ways to spend time here. Its appeal is the combination of a scenic, distinctively shaped mountain lake with a calm, unhurried atmosphere — the quiet that Racha still offers and the busier parts of Georgia increasingly don’t.

Mravaldzali village

The nearby village of Mravaldzali is part of the experience. It has the handsome stone houses characteristic of Racha and wide views of the Caucasus, and it’s home to an 11th-century hall-type Church of St. George, which preserves inscriptions and fragments of art from the 11th century — a reminder that this quiet corner of Racha has deep historical roots alongside its natural appeal. The lake and the village together make a rewarding pairing of nature and heritage.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: Near Mravaldzali village, Oni municipality, Upper Racha, in the Racha-Lechkhumi and Kvemo Svaneti region of western Georgia; ~1,840 m elevation.
  • Getting there: Racha is reached from Tbilisi via Kutaisi and Ambrolauri — roughly a half-day drive (around 4–5 hours to the region). A car is the practical way to reach the lake area, given limited public transport, and local guesthouses are the best source of current road and access information.
  • What’s there: Picnic areas, fishing, and boat rides; no major tourism infrastructure beyond this.
  • Best time: Late spring through autumn — summer for green and warmth (the lake is best for boating/fishing rather than swimming), and autumn for the forest color. Winter access is unreliable.
  • Combine with: Other Racha sights — the Shaori Reservoir (the lake lies on the Shkmeri–Shaori hiking route over Mount Khikhati), Nikortsminda cathedral, Ambrolauri and the Khvanchkara wine villages, and Upper Racha’s mountain country around Oni and Shovi.

faq.title: What is Cholevi Lake?
faq.content: Cholevi Lake — also known as Lake Mravaldzali, or popularly the “Lake of Love” — is a forest-ringed mountain lake at about 1,840 meters elevation near the village of Mravaldzali in the Oni municipality of Racha, western Georgia. Surrounded by dense forest and mountain peaks, with greenish water and a distinctive shape that earned its romantic nickname, it’s a calm, scenic spot popular with photographers and visitors, with picnic areas, fishing, and the option of a boat ride. Less famous than Racha’s headline sights, it’s one of the more rewarding quiet stops in this overlooked highland region.

faq.title: Why is it called the “Lake of Love”?
faq.content: The “Lake of Love” (Sikvaruli) nickname comes from the lake’s distinctive shape, which has made it a popular spot for photographers and couples. Its other names are Cholevi Lake and Lake Mravaldzali, after the nearby village of Mravaldzali. The combination of the romantic name, the scenic forest-and-mountain setting, and the calm water has given it a quiet fame within Racha, though it remains far less visited than Georgia’s better-known lakes.

faq.title: What can you do at Cholevi Lake?
faq.content: It’s set up for relaxed enjoyment rather than just looking. The shore has picnic areas, the lake offers good fishing, and you can take a boat out on the water, so a visit can easily be a leisurely few hours. You can walk around the shoreline, picnic with the forest-and-mountain views, or climb toward nearby Mount Khikhati for the finest panorama down over the lake and across the Rachan mountains. It’s most beautiful in autumn, when the surrounding forest turns gold and orange and the colors reflect in the water.

faq.title: Where is Cholevi Lake, and how do you get there?
faq.content: It’s near the village of Mravaldzali in the Oni municipality of Upper Racha, in the Racha-Lechkhumi and Kvemo Svaneti region of western Georgia, at about 1,840 meters. Racha is reached from Tbilisi via Kutaisi and Ambrolauri — roughly a half-day drive. A car is the practical way to reach the lake area, as public transport is limited, and local guesthouses are the best source of current road and access details. It lies on the hiking route between Shkmeri village and the Shaori Reservoir, over Mount Khikhati.

faq.title: What else is near Cholevi Lake?
faq.content: The nearby village of Mravaldzali has handsome traditional Rachan stone houses, Caucasus views, and an 11th-century hall-type Church of St. George with original inscriptions and art fragments. Further afield, the lake pairs well with Racha’s other attractions: the large Shaori Reservoir, the richly carved Nikortsminda cathedral, the town of Ambrolauri and the famous Khvanchkara wine villages, and the high mountain country of Upper Racha around Oni and Shovi. Together they make Racha a rewarding region for travelers who prefer quiet highland landscapes and wine to the busy main circuit.

khvanchkaraMonument.heroTitle: The Khvanchkara Wine Monument: Georgia's Giant Wine Bottle
khvanchkaraMonument.content:
The Khvanchkara Wine Monument is a landmark in Ambrolauri, the regional center of Racha in northwestern Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — in the heart of the region that produces one of the country’s most celebrated wines. It takes the form of a giant wine bottle, a large and unmissable sculpture standing in the town that serves as the gateway to the Khvanchkara wine zone — the small stretch of the Rioni valley where the indigenous Aleksandrouli and Mujuretuli grapes ripen into the naturally semi-sweet red wine that carries the village’s name. More than a simple marker, the monument is a deliberate civic tribute: a statement of how completely this quiet mountain region identifies with its famous wine. For travelers passing through Ambrolauri on a Racha trip, it’s a quick, free, and characterful stop that anchors the abstract fame of Khvanchkara to a real place in the landscape.

What is the Khvanchkara Wine Monument?

The Khvanchkara Wine Monument is a large sculpture in the shape of a wine bottle, standing in a square in the town of Ambrolauri, the center of the Racha region in western Georgia. It honors Khvanchkara — Georgia’s celebrated naturally semi-sweet red wine, made from the Aleksandrouli and Mujuretuli grapes grown in a small microzone around the nearby village of Khvanchkara. A prominent, photogenic town landmark rather than a remote marker, it’s an easy free stop on a Racha itinerary and a fitting symbol of a region whose identity is bound up with one of Georgia’s most famous wines.

Khvanchkara wine

The wine the monument honors is among the most distinctively Georgian of all Georgian wines — not the most exported, but among the most deeply tied to a single place and a particular set of cultural associations. Khvanchkara is a naturally semi-sweet red made from two indigenous grapes, Aleksandrouli and Mujuretuli, grown in a microzone of very limited extent on the right bank of the Rioni valley in Racha (on the slopes of the Likhi ridge, roughly 450–800 meters up). The area that can legally carry the Khvanchkara designation is small — around 900 hectares — so total production is modest by any commercial standard, which has long given the wine a prestige and scarcity within Georgia beyond what quality alone would explain. It is one of Georgia’s oldest Protected Designations of Origin, recognized as a PDO since 1932.

The semi-sweetness is natural, not the result of arrested fermentation or added sugar: the specific microclimate lets the grapes accumulate enough sugar that fermentation stops naturally before all of it converts to alcohol, leaving residual sweetness. That site-dependent process is exactly why the designation is geographically restricted — the same grapes grown elsewhere in Racha or beyond don’t reliably produce the same result. Indeed, Aleksandrouli and Mujuretuli are so particular to this corner of Racha-Lechkhumi that attempts to grow them elsewhere change their character entirely, which is part of why Khvanchkara cannot be reproduced anywhere else.

A wine with a story

Khvanchkara’s history is richer than its fame as a sweet red suggests. The blend was created in the late 19th century by the local noblemen Luarsab and Dimitri Kipiani, and was originally known as “Sakipiano” wine (the Kipianis’ wine). In 1907 it won the Grand Prix at an international exposition in Belgium, sent there by the wine merchants Nestor Tsereteli and Varlam Papuashvili — an early mark of international recognition. When the Soviet regime industrialized wine production, it renamed the wine “Khvanchkara” after the village where the best Aleksandrouli and Mujuretuli grew, in part to erase the Kipiani name from public memory. The wine is also widely said to have been a favorite of Joseph Stalin — himself a Georgian — an association often repeated as part of its Soviet-era celebrity, though, like much Stalin lore, it’s better treated as part of the wine’s legend than as documented fact.

Made by serious producers from genuine-microzone grapes, Khvanchkara is a wine of real character — its berry-rich, raspberry-toned sweetness balanced by the natural acidity of mountain-grown fruit and the structure of the Aleksandrouli grape, giving it a complexity that simple sweetness alone could never produce.

The monument and its setting

The monument stands in Ambrolauri, the small regional capital of Racha, set among the forested hills and the vineyard country of the lower Rioni valley. Ambrolauri is a quiet town, undisturbed by the kind of wine-tourism infrastructure that fills Georgia’s more developed wine regions, and the giant-bottle monument gives the town an immediate, slightly playful identity — an unambiguous statement of what this place is known for. A stop here is brief — five to ten minutes for photographs and a moment to register the geography of one of Georgia’s most famous wine designations — and its value is as a symbol that anchors the idea of Khvanchkara to a real place, giving the wine a geographical reality that tasting it elsewhere cannot.

Combining with the wider Racha itinerary

The monument fits naturally into a Racha trip built around Ambrolauri as a base. From here, the family wine producers of the Khvanchkara microzone are within easy reach — the small cellars in the surrounding villages (Khvanchkara, Akhalsopeli, and others) where the wine is made in qvevri by traditional methods and where tastings are often wonderfully informal: a knock at the gate frequently earns a glass filled to the brim and a plate of local food, more than any formal appointment. The wider Racha circuit pairs the monument and the cellars with the richly carved Nikortsminda Cathedral, the Shaori Reservoir, the Cholevi (Lake of Love) and other Racha lakes, and the high mountain country of Upper Racha around Oni and Shovi. For anyone interested in Georgian wine, this short, free roadside stop carries a symbolic weight out of all proportion to the few minutes it takes.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: In a town square in Ambrolauri, the regional center of Racha, in the Racha-Lechkhumi and Kvemo Svaneti region of western Georgia.
  • Getting there: Ambrolauri is reached from Tbilisi via Kutaisi — roughly a half-day drive (the E60 to Kutaisi, then up the Rioni valley). Daily marshrutkas run from Kutaisi to Ambrolauri; a hired car gives more freedom (and lets you stop at the Nakerala Pass viewpoint en route). The monument is in the town itself, impossible to miss.
  • Time needed: 5–10 minutes; longer if you base in Ambrolauri to explore the wine villages.
  • Admission: Free.
  • Combine with: The Khvanchkara wine cellars, Nikortsminda Cathedral, the Shaori Reservoir, Cholevi Lake, and Upper Racha.

faq.title: What is the Khvanchkara Wine Monument?
faq.content: It’s a large sculpture in the shape of a wine bottle, standing in a square in the town of Ambrolauri, the center of the Racha region in western Georgia. It honors Khvanchkara — Georgia’s celebrated naturally semi-sweet red wine, made from the Aleksandrouli and Mujuretuli grapes grown in a small microzone around the nearby village of Khvanchkara. A prominent, photogenic town landmark, it’s an easy free stop on a Racha trip and a fitting symbol of a region whose identity is bound up with one of Georgia’s most famous wines.

faq.title: What is Khvanchkara wine?
faq.content: Khvanchkara is a naturally semi-sweet red Georgian wine made from two indigenous grapes, Aleksandrouli and Mujuretuli, grown in a small microzone on the right bank of the Rioni valley in Racha. Its sweetness is natural — the local microclimate lets the grapes ripen enough that fermentation stops on its own, leaving residual sugar — which is why the same grapes grown elsewhere can’t reproduce it. One of Georgia’s oldest Protected Designations of Origin (a PDO since 1932), it’s prized for its berry-rich, raspberry-toned flavor balanced by mountain-grown acidity, and it’s made in only modest quantities, which adds to its prestige within Georgia.

faq.title: Why is Khvanchkara wine so famous?
faq.content: Several reasons. It’s a naturally semi-sweet red that genuinely can’t be reproduced outside its tiny Racha microzone, which gives it real rarity. It has a notable history: the blend was created in the late 19th century by the noblemen Luarsab and Dimitri Kipiani (originally called “Sakipiano” wine) and won the Grand Prix at a 1907 exposition in Belgium. The Soviet regime renamed it after the village of Khvanchkara, and it’s also widely said to have been a favorite of Stalin — a piece of lore that, true or not, added to its fame across the USSR. Today it’s a point of national pride in Georgian winemaking.

faq.title: Are Aleksandrouli and Mujuretuli grown anywhere else?
faq.content: Essentially no — and that’s central to Khvanchkara’s identity. Both grapes are particular to the Racha-Lechkhumi area, and Aleksandrouli in particular changes character dramatically when grown elsewhere, so attempts to cultivate it in other regions don’t produce comparable wine. By legal definition, true Khvanchkara can only be made from Aleksandrouli and Mujuretuli grown in the Khvanchkara microzone. This is why the wine, unlike many semi-sweet wines, genuinely cannot be replicated anywhere else in the world.

faq.title: How do you visit the monument, and what else is nearby?
faq.content: The monument is in the town of Ambrolauri, the regional center of Racha, reached from Tbilisi via Kutaisi (about a half-day drive) — daily marshrutkas run from Kutaisi, or a hired car gives more freedom. It’s right in the town and impossible to miss, a quick free photo stop. The real reward is using Ambrolauri as a base to visit the family wine cellars of the Khvanchkara villages, where tastings are charmingly informal, alongside the carved Nikortsminda Cathedral, the Shaori Reservoir, Cholevi Lake, and the mountain country of Upper Racha.

loveWaterfall.heroTitle: The Love Waterfall (Sikvaruli): Racha's Heart-Shaped Cascade
loveWaterfall.content:
The Love Waterfall — Sikvaruli in Georgian, often “the Waterfall of Love” — is a forest waterfall in the Racha region of northwestern Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus), near the village of Utsera in Upper Racha. It drops roughly 30 meters over rock in a wooded mountain setting, reached by an easy half-hour walk through the forest. Its name comes from a genuine local feature: a heart-shaped rock by the falls, which has made it a romantic destination — a Valentine’s spot of sorts — and given it a small fame in a region otherwise known for wine and mountains. Modest enough to be an easy, relaxed stop rather than a hiking objective, it rewards the short walk with a quiet, characterful corner of the Racha forest, and a second, taller waterfall just a few minutes further on.

What is the Love Waterfall?

The Love Waterfall (Sikvaruli) is a roughly 30-meter forest cascade near the village of Utsera in Upper Racha, western Georgia, reached by an easy ~30-minute walk through the forest. It takes its name from a heart-shaped rock by the falls, which has made it a popular romantic spot. The flow is strongest in spring, during the snowmelt. A short, easy walk just a few minutes from a second and even taller waterfall, it’s a quiet, scenic stop best combined with the mountain resorts and mineral springs of Upper Racha (Shovi, Utsera, Oni).

The setting

The waterfall sits in the wooded mountain terrain of Upper Racha, in the kind of mid-elevation forest that defines this part of the western Georgian highlands — moss-covered rock, dense green vegetation in the warm months, and the filtered light and enclosed quiet that forest waterfalls in this environment produce. The water falls some 30 meters over the rock face into the stream below; the setting is intimate rather than grand, the composition of falling water against mossy stone and forest giving it a coherence and a natural calm that larger waterfalls hemmed in by visitor infrastructure rarely keep.

The flow is at its strongest in spring, when the snowmelt from the higher mountains feeds the maximum volume — the most powerful and rewarding time to see it. Through summer the flow eases as the snowmelt fades, and autumn brings the color change of the surrounding forest.

The heart-shaped rock — and why it’s the “Love” waterfall

The name is not just sentiment: by the falls there is a heart-shaped rock, a natural formation that gives the place its romantic identity and its name. That detail has made the Love Waterfall a destination for couples and a Valentine’s-season favorite — the kind of spot that acquires affection and a name of its own in local usage precisely because it has a concrete, memorable feature. The enclosed forest setting, the sound of the water, and the sense of being a little apart from the road all reinforce the association, but the heart-shaped rock is the literal heart of it.

A second waterfall just beyond

The Love Waterfall comes with a bonus: if you continue about five minutes past it, a path on the left leads to a second waterfall — higher, and by most accounts even more impressive than the first. Most visitors who make the easy walk to the Love Waterfall continue to the second fall, so the short outing gives two waterfalls rather than one, with little extra effort. Seeing both makes the modest stop a more complete experience of this corner of the Racha forest.

The visit

Reaching the Love Waterfall is an easy walk of about 30 minutes through the forest from the nearest road access — a quick scenic outing rather than a hiking objective. There’s no extended trail system, no sequence of viewpoints, and no visitor infrastructure: simply the walk in, the time at the falls (and the second waterfall beyond), and the walk back. Allow an hour or so for the round trip with both falls and some time to enjoy them. The path is straightforward, though, as with any forest trail here, it can be slippery after rain, so footwear with grip is sensible.

This easy, low-commitment character is exactly what makes the Love Waterfall useful within an Upper Racha day — a quiet natural pause that adds a different texture to a trip otherwise focused on the mountain resorts, mineral springs, and high scenery of the region, without the time or effort the area’s longer hikes demand.

In the context of a Racha visit

The Love Waterfall sits in Upper Racha, near Utsera, so it combines most naturally with the upper-region sights: the mineral-spring resort of Utsera itself, the mountain resort of Shovi (about 20 minutes away, with its famous mineral springs), and the town of Oni. It makes an easy add to a day exploring this high, forested end of Racha. Travelers doing a fuller Racha loop can pair it with the Lower Racha highlights around Ambrolauri — the Nikortsminda Cathedral, the Khvanchkara wine villages and monument, and the Shaori Reservoir — though those lie a drive away in the lower part of the region, so it’s worth planning the geography rather than assuming everything is close together.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: Near Utsera village, Upper Racha, in the Racha-Lechkhumi and Kvemo Svaneti region of western Georgia (near Oni and Shovi).
  • Getting there: Racha is reached from Tbilisi via Kutaisi — roughly a half-day drive (around 4–5 hours to the region); Utsera is in the upper region near Oni. A car is the practical way to reach the trailhead. From the road, an easy ~30-minute forest walk to the falls.
  • Time needed: About an hour for the round trip, including the second waterfall.
  • Best time: Spring for the strongest flow (snowmelt); summer and autumn for the forest setting (autumn for color). Wear shoes with grip.
  • Access / admission: Freely accessible, no fee or infrastructure.
  • Combine with: Utsera and Shovi mineral springs, Oni, and — on a fuller Racha trip — Nikortsminda, the Khvanchkara wine villages, and the Shaori Reservoir.

faq.title: What is the Love Waterfall?
faq.content: The Love Waterfall (Sikvaruli, or the “Waterfall of Love”) is a roughly 30-meter forest cascade near the village of Utsera in Upper Racha, western Georgia, reached by an easy half-hour walk through the forest. It’s named for a heart-shaped rock by the falls, which has made it a romantic destination popular with couples. Modest and easy to reach rather than a hiking objective, it’s a quiet, scenic stop — and a second, taller waterfall lies just a few minutes beyond it.

faq.title: Why is it called the Love Waterfall?
faq.content: Because of a genuine natural feature: there’s a heart-shaped rock by the waterfall, which gives the spot its name and its romantic identity. That detail has made it a destination for couples and a Valentine’s-season favorite. The enclosed forest setting and the sound of the water add to the atmosphere, but it’s the heart-shaped rock that’s the literal reason for the name — not just sentiment.

faq.title: Is there really a second waterfall?
faq.content: Yes. If you continue about five minutes past the Love Waterfall, a path on the left leads to a second waterfall that’s higher and, by most accounts, even more impressive than the first. Most visitors who walk to the Love Waterfall continue to the second one, so the short, easy outing actually gives you two waterfalls for little extra effort — a good reason to allow a bit more time than the first falls alone would need.

faq.title: How hard is the walk, and when should you go?
faq.content: It’s easy — about a 30-minute walk through the forest from the nearest road, suitable for ordinary fitness, not a demanding hike. Allow around an hour for the round trip if you include the second waterfall. The flow is strongest in spring, during the mountain snowmelt, which is the most rewarding time to visit; summer and autumn are quieter (autumn adds forest color). The forest path can be slippery after rain, so wear shoes with good grip.

faq.title: Where is the Love Waterfall, and what's nearby?
faq.content: It’s near the village of Utsera in Upper Racha, in the Racha-Lechkhumi and Kvemo Svaneti region of western Georgia, close to Oni and the mountain resort of Shovi (about 20 minutes away). Racha is reached from Tbilisi via Kutaisi, roughly a half-day drive, and a car is the practical way to get to the trailhead. It combines most naturally with the upper-region sights — the mineral springs of Utsera and Shovi and the town of Oni — and, on a fuller Racha trip, with the Lower Racha highlights around Ambrolauri (Nikortsminda, the Khvanchkara wines, the Shaori Reservoir), which lie a drive away.

mindaFortress.heroTitle: Minda Fortress: The “I Want” Castle of Racha
mindaFortress.content:
Minda Fortress is a medieval stronghold ruin in the Racha region of northwestern Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — perched on a high rocky cape near the village of Tsesi, in the Ambrolauri municipality, above the confluence of the Rioni and Lukhuni rivers. It is the largest fortress in Racha, and was once a key defensive hub linking the upper and lower valleys of the region with neighboring Svaneti. Its name, in Georgian, means “I want,” and it stands directly across the road from the celebrated Barakoni Church — the two together forming one of Lower Racha’s most rewarding stops. Built to be all but unreachable, on a cape so steep it is reckoned among the hardest fortresses in Georgia to climb to, Minda rewards the effort with genuine, unrestored medieval stonework and a commanding view over the whole Rioni valley.

What is Minda Fortress?

Minda Fortress (the “I Want” castle) is the largest medieval fortress in Racha, western Georgia, set on a high rocky cape near the village of Tsesi in the Ambrolauri municipality, above the meeting of the Rioni and Lukhuni rivers and across the road from the Barakoni Church. Thought to date to at least the 10th century, it became an eristavi (governor’s) residence in the 11th, and in the 1530s King Bagrat III of Imereti granted it to the Chkhetidze family. A stone wall, three towers, and a belltower survive. Reached by a steep footpath, it offers unrestored medieval ruins and sweeping views over the Rioni valley — a strong stop on a Lower Racha itinerary.

History

Minda’s history is better documented than its remote setting might suggest. The fortress is thought to have existed by the 10th century, built on its near-inaccessible cape precisely so a local garrison could hold out against attack in a region far from any major city. In the 11th century, the residence of the eristavi (the governor — the title combines the Georgian eri, “nation/clan,” and tavi, “head”) was moved here, marking its rise as a seat of regional authority. In the 1530s, King Bagrat III of Imereti granted Minda to the Chkhetidze family, along with the honor of being royal standard-bearer — the Chkhetidze being the family that held the title of eristavi (duke) of Racha. The fortress controlled the strategic junction of the upper and lower Racha valleys and the routes toward Svaneti, making it a critical link in the region’s medieval defensive network.

The name itself carries a small story: “Minda” means “I want” in Georgian — a name that has invited various local explanations, and one that sets the fortress apart from the more prosaically named strongholds of the region.

The ruins

Of the original fortress, a stone perimeter wall, three towers, and a belltower survive; another chamber is thought to remain but is inaccessible because of rockfall. The surviving sections are genuine, unrestored medieval stonework — the masonry, wall thickness, and the placement of the structures on the cape all reflecting medieval Caucasian defensive practice — in the vegetation-colonized condition of a ruin left largely to itself. Walking among them gives the kind of direct, unmediated encounter with medieval building that restored and managed sites cannot, and the scale of the original fortification is readable from what remains.

Two details bring the place alive. By tradition, the fortress once held an enormous eight-ton qvevri — the great clay vessel Georgians use to ferment and store wine — though no trace of it remains today, a reminder that even a mountaintop stronghold was bound up with Racha’s wine culture. And in the Soviet period, the image of Minda Fortress was used on the label of “Barakoni” wine, named for the church below — so the fortress was, for a time, the face of a Racha wine.

The setting and views

Minda sits on a high, rocky cape above the confluence of the Rioni and Lukhuni rivers, in the forested mountain landscape characteristic of Racha — dense, green, and enclosed. The fortress was deliberately built to be hard to reach, and it remains so: a narrow, steep path leads up, and it is counted among the more difficult fortresses in Georgia to climb to. The reward is the view — a commanding perspective over the whole Rioni valley and the surrounding ridgelines that makes the strategic logic of the site immediately clear, and that the valley road and villages below cannot offer. Because few visitors make the climb, the viewpoint has a quiet, contemplative quality.

The fortress is most famously seen from below, from the Barakoni Church directly across the road — an 18th-century church (built 1753 by Rostom, eristavi of Racha) renowned for its carved decoration, from which the ruins of Minda and the river confluence make a memorable view. The two sites are visited together as a natural pair.

Access and practical information

  • Location: On a rocky cape near the village of Tsesi, Ambrolauri municipality, Lower Racha, at the confluence of the Rioni and Lukhuni rivers, western Georgia (across the road from the Barakoni Church).
  • Getting there: Racha is reached from Tbilisi via Kutaisi — roughly a half-day drive; the fortress is near Tsesi, not far from Ambrolauri. From the road, the climb to the fortress is on foot up a steep, narrow path — short in distance but genuinely demanding, and not clearly signposted. Sturdy footwear is essential, and care is needed on the steep, exposed sections.
  • Time needed: About 20–40 minutes at the ruins and viewpoints, plus the climb.
  • Access / admission: Freely accessible, no fee or staffing.
  • A caution: This is one of the harder fortress climbs in Georgia, on a steep cape — those uncomfortable with steep, unprotected terrain should be aware before setting out.
  • Combine with: The Barakoni Church (directly opposite), and the wider Racha circuit — Nikortsminda Cathedral, the Khvanchkara wine villages and monument, the Shaori Reservoir, and the town of Ambrolauri; Upper Racha (Oni, Shovi, Utsera) lies further on.

faq.title: What is Minda Fortress?
faq.content: Minda Fortress is the largest medieval fortress in the Racha region of western Georgia, set on a high rocky cape near the village of Tsesi in the Ambrolauri municipality, above the confluence of the Rioni and Lukhuni rivers and directly across the road from the Barakoni Church. Its name means “I want” in Georgian. Thought to date to at least the 10th century, it became a governor’s (eristavi) residence in the 11th and was granted to the Chkhetidze family by King Bagrat III of Imereti in the 1530s. A stone wall, three towers, and a belltower survive, reached by a steep footpath with commanding views over the Rioni valley.

faq.title: Why is it called the “I Want” castle?
faq.content: “Minda” means “I want” in Georgian, which has made the fortress’s name a small point of curiosity and given rise to various local explanations. Beyond the name, Minda has a substantial history: it was an important enough stronghold to become the seat of the eristavi (the duke/governor) of Racha in the 11th century, and in the 1530s the King of Imereti granted it, along with the honor of royal standard-bearer, to the Chkhetidze family who held the dukedom of Racha. It was the largest and one of the most strategically important fortresses in the region.

faq.title: What can you see at Minda Fortress?
faq.content: A stone perimeter wall, three towers, and a belltower survive, in genuine unrestored condition; another chamber is thought to remain but is blocked by rockfall. The ruins sit on a dramatic rocky cape with sweeping views over the Rioni valley and its meeting with the Lukhuni. Two stories add character: the fortress is said to have once held an enormous eight-ton qvevri (a clay wine vessel), now vanished, and in Soviet times the image of Minda was used on the label of “Barakoni” wine. It’s also best photographed from the Barakoni Church across the road.

faq.title: How hard is it to reach Minda Fortress?
faq.content: It’s one of the more demanding fortress climbs in Georgia. The fortress was deliberately built on a steep, near-inaccessible cape, and reaching it means a climb on foot up a narrow, steep path that isn’t clearly signposted. The distance is short, but the terrain is genuinely steep and exposed in places, so sturdy footwear is essential and anyone uncomfortable with steep, unprotected ground should be cautious. Allow time for the climb on top of the 20–40 minutes you’ll want at the ruins and viewpoints.

faq.title: Where is Minda Fortress, and what’s nearby?
faq.content: It’s near the village of Tsesi in the Ambrolauri municipality of Lower Racha, western Georgia, at the confluence of the Rioni and Lukhuni rivers — reached from Tbilisi via Kutaisi (about a half-day drive). Its natural companion is the Barakoni Church directly across the road, a renowned 18th-century church from which the fortress is best seen. Further afield, it pairs with the rest of the Racha circuit: the carved Nikortsminda Cathedral, the Khvanchkara wine villages and monument, the Shaori Reservoir, and the town of Ambrolauri, with Upper Racha (Oni, Shovi, Utsera) beyond.

nikortsmindaCathedral.heroTitle: Nikortsminda Cathedral: A Masterpiece of Carved Stone in Racha
nikortsmindaCathedral.content:
Nikortsminda Cathedral stands in the village of the same name in the Racha region of northwestern Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — about 12 kilometers east of Ambrolauri, and is widely considered one of the finest achievements of medieval Georgian church architecture. Built in the early 11th century (1010–1014) under King Bagrat III, the first king of a united Georgia, and dedicated to St. Nicholas (the name “Nikortsminda” means “St. Nicholas”), it represents the mature Georgian cross-domed church form carried to a rare height of decorative ambition. The relief carving covering its facades is among the most refined and densely programmed ornamental work in the entire Georgian medieval canon, and the interior holds 16th–17th-century frescoes that add a second major layer of artistry. For travelers making the trip into Racha — a region that sees a fraction of the visitors flowing through Kakheti or the headline mountain destinations — Nikortsminda is the cultural monument that most justifies the journey on its own terms: a building of international artistic significance sitting quietly in a rural landscape.

What is Nikortsminda Cathedral?

Nikortsminda is an 11th-century Georgian Orthodox cathedral in the Racha region of western Georgia, about 12 km from Ambrolauri, regarded as one of the masterpieces of medieval Georgian architecture. Built in 1010–1014 under King Bagrat III, the first king of united Georgia, and dedicated to St. Nicholas, it is famous above all for the extraordinarily rich relief carving covering its exterior, and for the vivid 16th–17th-century frescoes inside.
