The cheese section is the most specifically Imeretian part of the market, and the one most worth seeking out for anyone interested in Georgian dairy. Imeretian cheese (imeruli kveli) — the fresh, mildly salty cow's-milk cheese that is the region's defining dairy product, and the ingredient without which Imeretian khachapuri wouldn't exist — is sold in large rounds or cut to order, and its quality and character vary between producers in ways vendors are usually happy to show through samples. Alongside it, sulguni appears throughout the market in its various forms — fresh, lightly aged, and smoked — along with regional varieties that are less widely known but equally worth trying.

The spice section carries the standard vocabulary of Georgian cooking — dried herbs, pepper blends, khmeli suneli in its regional formulations, dried fenugreek (utskho suneli), coriander seed — alongside the specific Imeretian preparations of the western Georgian tradition. Walnut-based products appear in several forms: whole walnuts in season, walnut paste for the sauces and stuffings that run through the Imeretian kitchen, and churchkhela in the pale, grape-must-coated form that is the characteristic western Georgian version of the sweet.

The produce section tracks the Georgian seasons with a directness that rewards visits at different times of year. Spring brings the first fresh herbs — bundles of tarragon, coriander, and purple basil, as fundamental to the Georgian table as any spice — with young vegetables and early fruit. Summer brings the tomatoes, peppers, aubergines, and cucumbers that form the backbone of Georgian summer cooking. Autumn brings the grape harvest, with fresh fruit and the first pressed juice appearing alongside the quinces, persimmons, and pomegranates of the Georgian autumn.

The experience

The Green Bazaar is busiest and most rewarding in the morning, when the produce is freshest and the vendors most active. It slows through the afternoon, with most stalls packing up by early afternoon, so a mid-to-late-morning arrival — roughly between nine and eleven — is the sweet spot for the full experience. The mix of produce displays, vendor interactions, and the general sensory character of a busy food market is as informative about Imeretian daily life as any museum exhibit.

Vendors are generally open to questions and happy to offer a taste before you buy, in the Georgian tradition of informal generosity that operates somewhat independently of commercial calculation. A few practical notes: bring cash (lari) in small denominations, as it's the only practical payment method and large notes can be hard to change; a few words of Georgian or Russian help, though pointing and goodwill go a long way; and as in any busy market, keep an eye on your belongings. It's an easy, rewarding stop to fold into a walk around central Kutaisi.

Practical information

  • Location: Central Kutaisi; the market occupies a covered and open-air area near the center — asking locally for "Mtsvane Bazari" (the Green Bazaar) will point you straight there.
  • Hours: Daily, from early morning until early afternoon; most active roughly 08:00–12:00. Come in the morning for the freshest produce and the fullest atmosphere.
  • Admission: Free.
  • Payment: Cash only (Georgian lari); bring small denominations.
  • What to look for: Imeretian cheese, sulguni, walnuts and walnut paste, churchkhela, fresh seasonal herbs and produce, local honey.

faq.title: What is the Green Bazaar in Kutaisi?
faq.content: The Green Bazaar (Mtsvane Bazari in Georgian) is the central food market of Kutaisi, the main city of the Imereti region in western Georgia. It's a busy, working local market — partly covered, partly open-air — selling fresh produce, cheeses, walnuts, honey, spices, and other staples, mostly from the farms and villages around the city. It's not a tourist site as such, which is exactly its appeal: it's one of the best places to experience everyday Imeretian food culture firsthand.

faq.title: What should you buy or try at the market?
faq.content: The standout is Imeretian cheese (imeruli kveli), the fresh, mildly salty cow's-milk cheese at the heart of the region's cooking and of Imeretian khachapuri — vendors will usually let you taste before buying. Also look for sulguni (fresh, aged, and smoked), walnuts and walnut paste, churchkhela (the western Georgian grape-must sweet), local honey, Georgian spice blends like khmeli suneli, and whatever fresh herbs, fruit, and vegetables are in season.

faq.title: When is the best time to visit, and how do you pay?
faq.content: Go in the morning — the market is busiest and freshest then, and most stalls start packing up by early afternoon, so arriving roughly between 9:00 and 11:00 is ideal. Bring cash in Georgian lari (small denominations are best, as it's cash only and big notes can be hard to change). Entry is free.

faq.title: Where is the Green Bazaar and is it worth visiting?
faq.content: It's in central Kutaisi, in a covered and open-air area near the city center — just ask locally for "Mtsvane Bazari." It's well worth an hour, especially for anyone interested in food: it gives a more direct sense of Imeretian daily life and cuisine than any restaurant, the vendors are welcoming, and it folds easily into a walk around the city center. Treat it as a cultural experience as much as a shopping stop.

faq.title: Is it a tourist market or a real local one?
faq.content: A real local one. There are no visitor routes, signage, or tourist concessions — it's where Kutaisi residents actually shop. That authenticity is the point. Approach it as a respectful visitor to a working market: a smile and a little curiosity go a long way, vendors are generally generous with samples and conversation, and it's a genuine window into the region rather than a staged experience.

gelatiMonastery.heroTitle: Gelati Monastery: Georgia's "Second Jerusalem" near Kutaisi
gelatiMonastery.content:
Gelati Monastery stands on a forested hillside about nine kilometers northeast of Kutaisi, in Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — overlooking the valley of the Tskaltsitela River in a setting that combines natural beauty with the atmosphere of a place that has been continuously significant for nine centuries. Founded in 1106 by King David IV — David the Builder — it was conceived from the outset not merely as a religious house but as an institution to express the cultural and intellectual ambitions of a unified Georgian kingdom at the height of its confidence. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1994, Gelati is one of the foundational monuments of Georgian civilization — a place where the religious, intellectual, and artistic dimensions of medieval Georgian culture reached their fullest expression.

What is Gelati Monastery?

Gelati Monastery is a 12th-century Georgian Orthodox monastery and former medieval academy near Kutaisi, in the Imereti region of western Georgia, and a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Founded in 1106 by King David IV "the Builder," it was both a great religious center and a renowned academy of theology, philosophy, and the sciences, and it served as a royal burial place — David himself is buried at the gate. Its greatest treasure is a magnificent 12th-century mosaic of the Virgin and Child in the apse of its main cathedral, among the finest Byzantine mosaics in the Caucasus. It remains an active monastery, in a forested hillside setting about 9 km from Kutaisi.

David the Builder and the foundation

The context of Gelati's founding matters. David IV became king of a fragmented, militarily weakened Georgia in 1089, when much of the country was under Seljuk Turkic domination and cities including Tbilisi were occupied. Over three decades he conducted a systematic campaign to reunify the Georgian kingdoms, drive out the Seljuks, recapture Tbilisi in 1122, and make Georgia the dominant power in the South Caucasus — the beginning of the Georgian "golden age" that ran through to the reign of Queen Tamar. Gelati was founded in 1106, in the middle of that campaign, and the decision to invest in a major monastery and academy while consolidation was still ongoing reflects David's conviction that cultural and religious institutions were as essential to Georgian statehood as military victory.

David's ambition for Gelati was explicit: he described it as a "second Jerusalem" for Georgia, a phrase that captures both the religious seriousness of the foundation and the national pride invested in it. The monastery was to be a royal burial place, a major religious center, and an academy where Georgian scholars would study at a level comparable to the great intellectual centers of the Byzantine world. David himself was buried at Gelati in 1125, his tomb a stone slab set into the threshold of the main gate — by tradition placed there at his own wish, so that all who entered would step over it, a gesture of humility long associated with this remarkable king. Set into the same gateway are the iron gates of Ganja, a war trophy brought back from the city of Ganja (in present-day Azerbaijan) — a striking emblem of the era's military confidence beside the founder's grave. Gelati became the burial place of many later Georgian and Imeretian kings as well.

The Academy

The Gelati Academy that David established became one of the most significant intellectual institutions of medieval Georgia, drawing scholars from across the Caucasus and the wider Orthodox world — it's sometimes called a "new Athens" of the East. The philosopher and theologian Ioane Petritsi, who translated Proclus and other Neoplatonic philosophers into Georgian and produced sophisticated original work, was associated with Gelati in the early 12th century. The academy maintained manuscript collections, conducted translation work, and produced scholarship in theology, philosophy, mathematics, and astronomy — the full curriculum of medieval learning — at a level reflecting the seriousness of David's conception and the resources royal patronage provided.

The academy declined after the Mongol invasions of the 13th century weakened the kingdom and reduced the patronage available for intellectual institutions, and the monastery later suffered looting and damage from Ottoman and Persian incursions in the 16th–18th centuries. But it continued as a major religious center through the medieval and early modern periods, and its accumulated collections of manuscripts and religious objects remain significant.

The architecture and art

The complex contains three churches built in the 12th and early 13th centuries, of which the Cathedral of the Virgin (the main church, completed in 1130 under David's son and successor Demetre) is the largest and most important. Its exterior follows the medieval Georgian cross-in-square plan, with careful proportions and high-quality stonework but relatively restrained facade decoration compared with some Georgian churches of the period. The real achievement of Gelati is concentrated inside.

The apse of the Cathedral of the Virgin holds a 12th-century mosaic of the Virgin and Child that is among the finest surviving Byzantine mosaics in the entire Caucasus. The tesserae — the small colored glass and stone pieces of Byzantine mosaic work — cover the semi-dome of the apse in a composition of considerable sophistication, the gold ground and precise rendering of the figures reflecting the highest Byzantine standards of the period. Its survival in good condition across nine centuries is remarkable, and its quality is immediately apparent even to visitors without specialist knowledge.

The cathedral walls carry frescoes from multiple periods — the most significant from the 12th and 13th centuries, alongside later additions from the 16th and 17th — including portraits of Georgian royalty. The layering of successive campaigns of decoration gives the interior a historical depth that single-period decoration cannot, and the combination of the apse mosaic, the wall frescoes, the carved stone capitals, and the quality of the stonework makes an interior of sustained artistic distinction. The smaller, simpler churches of St. George and St. Nicholas, and the gate chapel with the threshold stone, complete the precinct. (A practical caution: Gelati has been under long-running restoration, and scaffolding has at times obscured the mosaic and frescoes — see the access note below, as this affects what you can actually see.)

The monastery today and visiting

Gelati remains an active monastery with a community in residence and services in the cathedral and subsidiary churches, which gives the site a living presence that purely archaeological monuments lack. The forested hillside setting, with views across the valley, adds natural beauty to the historical and artistic interest, and the walk from the parking area to the buildings is short with only modest elevation change.

One important practical point: Gelati has undergone extensive, long-running restoration, and access and opening hours have been inconsistent and subject to change as the work proceeds — at times the scaffolding has blocked much of the interior art, and entry has sometimes been limited to restricted days and hours. Because this is exactly the kind of arrangement that changes, confirm current opening days, hours, and what's currently visible before planning a visit. Modest dress is required (shoulders and knees covered; head coverings for women, usually available at the entrance), and entry is free.

Practical information

  • Location: Gelati village, about 9–11 km northeast of Kutaisi, Imereti region, western Georgia; on a wooded hill above the Tskaltsitela River.
  • Getting there: By taxi, private transfer, or organized tour from Kutaisi (the simplest); local bus/marshrutka options also exist. Often combined with the nearby Motsameta Monastery.
  • Hours/Access: Subject to ongoing restoration — opening days and hours have been limited and changeable, and parts of the interior art may be under scaffolding; confirm before visiting.
  • Admission: Free.
  • Dress code: Modest dress required; shoulders and knees covered, head covering for women (wraps usually available at the entrance).

faq.title: What is Gelati Monastery?
faq.content: Gelati Monastery is a 12th-century Georgian Orthodox monastery near Kutaisi, in the Imereti region of western Georgia, and a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Founded in 1106 by King David IV "the Builder," it was both a great religious center and a famous medieval academy of theology, philosophy, and the sciences, and a royal burial place. Its most celebrated feature is a superb 12th-century mosaic of the Virgin and Child in the apse of its main cathedral. It remains an active monastery in a forested hillside setting.

faq.title: Who founded Gelati and who is buried there?
faq.content: It was founded in 1106 by King David IV, known as "the Builder," the king who reunified Georgia and drove out the Seljuk Turks, beginning the Georgian golden age. He envisioned Gelati as a "second Jerusalem" for Georgia. David was buried at the monastery in 1125 — by tradition, his tomb slab was set into the threshold of the main gate at his own wish, so all who entered would step over it. Many later Georgian and Imeretian kings were also buried at Gelati, and the famous iron gates of Ganja, a war trophy, stand in the same gateway.

faq.title: What is special about the art and the academy at Gelati?
faq.content: Two things above all. The art: the apse of the main Cathedral of the Virgin holds a 12th-century mosaic of the Virgin and Child that is among the finest Byzantine mosaics in the Caucasus, alongside frescoes from the 12th to 17th centuries including royal portraits. The academy: David established a renowned center of learning here — sometimes called a "new Athens" — where scholars like the philosopher Ioane Petritsi studied and translated Greek philosophy, producing work in theology, philosophy, mathematics, and astronomy.

faq.title: Can you visit Gelati Monastery right now, and what are the hours?
faq.content: You generally can, but with an important caveat: Gelati has been under long-running restoration, so opening days and hours have been inconsistent and parts of the interior art have at times been hidden by scaffolding. Access has sometimes been limited to certain days and hours. Because this changes as the work proceeds, it's essential to check the current situation before you go. Entry is free, and modest dress is required (shoulders and knees covered; head covering for women, usually available at the door).

faq.title: How do you get to Gelati from Kutaisi?
faq.content: Gelati is about 9–11 km northeast of Kutaisi. The easiest way is by taxi, private transfer, or an organized tour from the city; local bus or marshrutka options also exist. It's very commonly combined with the nearby Motsameta Monastery, and sometimes with other Kutaisi-area sights, as a half-day or day trip. The walk from the parking area to the buildings is short with only modest uphill.

colchisFountain.heroTitle: Colchis Fountain, Kutaisi: Golden Treasures of Ancient Colchis
colchisFountain.content:
The Colchis Fountain occupies the central square of Kutaisi, in Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — its multiple tiers and dozens of golden animal sculptures making it the most immediately striking public monument in the city and the visual reference point around which the center of western Georgia's largest city organizes itself. Installed around 2011, as part of the broad program of public-space development that reshaped many Georgian cities in that period, it has become the defining image of modern Kutaisi in much the way the Narikala Fortress defines Tbilisi or the Alphabetic Tower defines Batumi. It draws directly on the city's deepest historical identity: ancient Colchis, the kingdom of the Golden Fleece.

What is the Colchis Fountain?

The Colchis Fountain is a large tiered fountain in the central square of Kutaisi, Georgia, installed around 2011 and designed by the Georgian architect David Gogichaishvili. It's crowned and surrounded by around 30 gilded animal sculptures — most famously a pair of golden horses on top — which are enlarged replicas of ancient gold and bronze treasures excavated at Vani, a major site of the ancient Kingdom of Colchis nearby. It celebrates Kutaisi's identity as a center of Colchis, the land of the Golden Fleece in Greek myth, and is especially striking when illuminated at night. It sits on David Agmashenebeli Square in the city center.

The golden sculptures and Vani

The fountain's design draws on the archaeological heritage of ancient Colchis — the kingdom that occupied what is now western Georgia and won enduring fame through the Greek myth of the Golden Fleece and the Argonauts. The roughly thirty gilded sculptures across the fountain's tiers are large-scale reproductions of artifacts recovered from Colchic archaeological sites, above all the extraordinary gold and bronze objects found at Vani, the ancient Colchic city (a short distance from Kutaisi) whose excavations — including major finds in 1982 — produced some of the finest metalwork of the pre-Christian Caucasus. The originals are held in Georgian museums, principally the Georgian National Museum.

The animals that dominate the program — horses, deer, lions, rams, sheep, mountain goats, birds — reflect the zoomorphic vocabulary of Colchic decorative art and the creatures the Colchians held sacred, and a pair of golden horses crowns the highest tier (popularly associated with the legendary King Aeëtes of Colchis). Reproduced in gold-finished metal against the white stone and moving water, the effect is deliberately theatrical and pointedly referential to a specific ancient culture rather than a generic decorative fountain. The figures derive from real jewelry and bronze pieces — earrings, ornaments, and figurines of the kind Colchis was renowned for — which gives the spectacle a genuine archaeological grounding.

The Colchis connection

The choice of Colchic heritage for Kutaisi's central fountain is not arbitrary. Kutaisi is traditionally identified with the ancient Kingdom of Colchis — the Aea (Aia) of Greek mythology, the realm of King Aeëtes, where the Golden Fleece was kept and from which Medea fled with Jason and the Argonauts. Whether or not the mythological geography maps precisely onto the historical, the connection between Kutaisi and Colchis is ancient and deeply embedded in the city's sense of itself. The fountain makes that identity visible and central, placing the deepest layer of local historical consciousness at the literal center of contemporary urban life.

This same Golden Fleece and Argonaut heritage threads through western Georgia: the Medea statue in Batumi's Europe Square, the legends attached to Gonio Fortress on the Adjara coast, and the wetlands of Kolkheti National Park near Poti — the literal land of Colchis. Kutaisi's fountain is the inland, urban expression of that shared mythology.

A note on the square itself: David Agmashenebeli Square is named for King David IV "the Builder," and its centerpiece has changed repeatedly over the past century — from a cathedral, to Soviet-era monuments, to a statue of David the Builder, which was relocated (to the Kutaisi railway station area) when the fountain was installed. The fountain is the latest chapter in a long-contested civic space.

The square and evening experience

The fountain sits within a busy traffic roundabout, which makes it most visible from the surrounding streets and the café terraces on the square's perimeter rather than from directly beside it — and crossing to it on foot means navigating heavy traffic, so caution is needed. The civic prominence comes at the cost of close-up access; the best approach is to find a viewing point on the square's edge (the cafés, the Drama Theatre steps) from which the whole composition — the tiers, the sculptures, the water — reads as a unified spectacle rather than as individual details.

The evening illumination transforms it. The gold surfaces catch the artificial light and reflect it with a warmth that neutral daylight doesn't produce, and the combination of lit water and glowing golden figures is the most dramatic version of the monument — it's frequently cited as the best time to see and photograph it. It's at its most photogenic in the transition between late afternoon and full dark, when some residual light in the sky gives the background depth while the fountain's lighting is already active. Most walking tours of central Kutaisi begin here, which makes it a natural orientation point as well as a sight.

Practical information

  • Location: David Agmashenebeli Square (the central square), Kutaisi, Imereti region, western Georgia.
  • Getting there: In the heart of Kutaisi, walkable from the center; it sits in a busy roundabout, so take care crossing to it.
  • Hours/Admission: Freely accessible at all times; free. The fountain and its evening illumination run daily (illumination from dusk into the late evening).
  • Best time: Dusk to early evening, when the illumination is on and there's still some light in the sky — the most photogenic window.
  • Combine with: Kutaisi's other central sights; it's a common starting point for a walking tour of the city.

faq.title: What is the Colchis Fountain in Kutaisi?
faq.content: It's a large tiered fountain in the central square of Kutaisi, the main city of western Georgia, installed around 2011 and designed by the Georgian architect David Gogichaishvili. It's covered with about thirty gilded animal sculptures — most famously a pair of golden horses on top — that are enlarged replicas of ancient gold and bronze treasures from the Kingdom of Colchis. It's the city's defining modern landmark and is especially dramatic when lit at night.

faq.title: What do the golden animals represent?
faq.content: They're large-scale reproductions of real archaeological treasures from ancient Colchis, the kingdom that occupied western Georgia. Most come from the famous gold and bronze objects excavated at Vani, a major Colchic site near Kutaisi, with the originals now in Georgian museums. The animals — horses, deer, lions, rams, sheep, mountain goats — reflect the creatures sacred to the Colchians and the zoomorphic style of their renowned metalwork. The two horses crowning the fountain are popularly linked to the legendary King Aeëtes of Colchis.

faq.title: Why is Kutaisi connected to the Golden Fleece?
faq.content: Kutaisi is traditionally identified with ancient Colchis — the Aea of Greek mythology, the realm of King Aeëtes, where the Golden Fleece was kept and from which Medea fled with Jason and the Argonauts. That deep identity is exactly what the fountain celebrates. The same Golden Fleece heritage appears elsewhere in western Georgia too — the Medea statue in Batumi's Europe Square, the legends of Gonio Fortress, and the Colchis wetlands of Kolkheti National Park.

faq.title: When is the best time to see the Colchis Fountain?
faq.content: In the evening, when it's illuminated — the lights bring out the gold and the water, and it's at its most photogenic in the transition from late afternoon to full dark, with some glow still in the sky. By day it's still a striking sight, but the night lighting is the highlight. Note it sits in a busy traffic roundabout, so the best views are from the café terraces and sidewalks around the square rather than right beside it.

faq.title: How do you visit, and is it worth it?
faq.content: It's free and in the heart of Kutaisi, walkable from the center, and most city walking tours start here, so it doubles as a natural orientation point. It's a modern monument rather than an ancient site, but a genuinely impressive and meaningful one — it makes the city's Colchian, Golden Fleece identity visible at its very center. Take care crossing the busy roundabout, and view it from the perimeter for the best overall composition.

bagratiCathedral.heroTitle: Bagrati Cathedral: Kutaisi's Hilltop Symbol and Its Controversial Rebuilding
bagratiCathedral.content:
Bagrati Cathedral stands on Ukimerioni Hill above Kutaisi, in Georgia, the country in the South Caucasus, its silhouette visible from much of the city below and its elevated position giving some of the best views over the Rioni valley available anywhere in the area. The floor was laid in 1003, and the cathedral was built in the early 11th century under King Bagrat III — the ruler who completed the unification of the principal Georgian kingdoms under a single crown, and who chose this hilltop for a cathedral that would be both a religious center and a physical statement of the new unified state's confidence. Today it's at once an impressive, much-loved Kutaisi landmark and the subject of one of the most significant heritage-conservation controversies in the country, having been removed from the UNESCO World Heritage List in 2017 after a reconstruction that divided opinion.

What is Bagrati Cathedral?

Bagrati Cathedral (also called the Cathedral of the Dormition) is a large early-11th-century Georgian Orthodox cathedral on Ukimerioni Hill in Kutaisi, in the Imereti region of western Georgia. Built under King Bagrat III to mark the unification of Georgia (its floor laid in 1003), it stood for nearly seven centuries until an Ottoman gunpowder explosion in 1691 collapsed its dome, leaving it a celebrated ruin. It was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1994, jointly with Gelati Monastery, but a controversial reconstruction completed in 2012 led UNESCO to delist it in 2017 — a rare loss of status. The rebuilt cathedral is now an active church, a landmark of Kutaisi, and a superb viewpoint over the Rioni valley.

A monument to a unified Georgia

For the first seven centuries of its existence, Bagrati was among the most important religious buildings in Georgia — a large, carefully proportioned cross-dome cathedral with a high drum and dome, built to a scale that expressed the aspirations of a kingdom newly consolidated after generations of fragmentation. Bagrat III conceived it as a national church, and medieval chronicles record that he gathered nobles and clergy from across Georgia, and guests from abroad, for its consecration. Its standing in Georgian history runs deep: David IV "the Builder" — the king who would found Gelati Monastery nearby and lead Georgia into its golden age — was crowned at Bagrati.

Ottoman forces damaged the structure severely in 1691, detonating a gunpowder magazine within the cathedral and collapsing the dome and much of the upper structure. The cathedral then stood in ruins for roughly three centuries, and the atmospheric quality of those ruins — substantial walls and arches open to the sky on their prominent hilltop — became, in some respects, as much a part of the site's identity as the original building had been. Conservation and archaeological work began in the 1950s (led for decades by the architect Vakhtang Tsintsadze), and in 1994 the ruins were inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site jointly with the Gelati Monastery.

The reconstruction and its consequences

A full reconstruction, begun in 2010 (under the Italian architect Andrea Bruno) and with major works completed in 2012, rebuilt the dome and large parts of the upper structure, returning the building to use as an active church — while fundamentally altering the character of the site. The work combined modern materials and methods with salvaged original stone, and the result is a building that is visually complete but materially mixed — part ancient, part contemporary — in a way the ruins were not.

UNESCO had already placed the site on its List of World Heritage in Danger in 2010, in response to the reconstruction plans, citing concerns about the authenticity of the intervention. In 2017 it took a decisive step rarely seen in its history: it removed Bagrati Cathedral from the World Heritage List entirely — one of only a handful of sites ever delisted — while at the same time keeping Gelati Monastery on the list and removing Gelati from the In-Danger list, renominating it on its own. In effect the joint 1994 inscription was split: Gelati retained its status, and Bagrati lost it.

The debate reflects a genuine disagreement about the purpose and ethics of heritage conservation — whether a ruined building is better preserved as a ruin, keeping its historical authenticity at the cost of its function, or whether rebuilding serves the living religious and cultural community the building was made for. The Georgian government and the Georgian Orthodox Church supported the reconstruction; many architects, historians, and conservation specialists (and UNESCO's advisory bodies) opposed it. Both positions reflect real values, and the visitor today encounters the consequence of that disagreement in a cathedral that is simultaneously functional, impressive, and contested.

The cathedral today

Whatever view one takes on the restoration, the rebuilt cathedral is a significant and visually powerful building. The interior is active for worship, and the combination of the reconstructed medieval architecture with the frescoes and furnishings of an operating cathedral gives the space a warmth and vitality a purely preserved ruin cannot have. Seen from a distance and from the approach roads up the hill, the exterior reads as a complete medieval cathedral — the dome and drum restored to their original proportions — and the relationship between the building and its hilltop site is restored in a way the ruins could not achieve.

The grounds preserve sections of the original medieval wall and foundation remains that show the building's pre-restoration character, and walking the perimeter — along with the remains of the inner citadel on the hill behind — gives a fuller sense of the scale and complexity of the original construction than the reconstructed interior alone.

The views

The hilltop position is one of Bagrati's most consistently rewarding qualities, whatever one's view on the restoration. The panorama from the grounds takes in the sweep of the Rioni valley — the river below, Kutaisi spreading across both banks, the forested hills rising on all sides, and on clear days the peaks of the Greater Caucasus and the Racha range on the northern horizon. The view at sunset, when the low light catches the city and the river takes on the warm colors of the evening sky, is one of the better elevated panoramas in western Georgia.

Practical information

  • Location: Ukimerioni Hill, Kutaisi, Imereti region, western Georgia — accessible by car via the hill road, or on foot from the city center (a moderate uphill walk).
  • Getting there: A short taxi/Bolt ride from central Kutaisi, or a walk up from the center; easily combined with the city's other central sights.
  • Hours: Daily, generally early morning to evening; as an active cathedral, hours vary around services.
  • Admission: Free.
  • Dress code: Modest dress required; shoulders and knees covered, head covering for women (wraps usually available at the entrance).
  • Best time: Late afternoon to sunset for the light and the views.

faq.title: What is Bagrati Cathedral?
faq.content: Bagrati Cathedral (also called the Cathedral of the Dormition) is a large early-11th-century Georgian Orthodox cathedral on Ukimerioni Hill in Kutaisi, in the Imereti region of western Georgia. Built under King Bagrat III to commemorate the unification of Georgia — its floor was laid in 1003 — it's one of the most important medieval Georgian cathedrals and a defining symbol of Kutaisi. After centuries as a ruin and a controversial modern reconstruction, it's once again an active church and a superb hilltop viewpoint over the city and the Rioni valley.

faq.title: Why was Bagrati Cathedral removed from the UNESCO World Heritage List?
faq.content: Bagrati was inscribed in 1994 jointly with Gelati Monastery, but a full reconstruction (completed in 2012) used modern materials and methods that UNESCO judged to compromise the cathedral's authenticity and integrity. UNESCO had placed it on the List of World Heritage in Danger in 2010 over the reconstruction plans, and in 2017 it removed Bagrati from the World Heritage List entirely — one of only a few sites ever delisted. At the same time, Gelati was kept on the list and taken off the In-Danger list, so the original joint inscription was effectively split.

faq.title: Was the reconstruction a good thing?
faq.content: That's exactly the debate. Supporters — including the Georgian government and the Georgian Orthodox Church — argued that rebuilding returned a national cathedral to living religious use and protected it from further decay. Critics — many architects, historians, and conservation experts, along with UNESCO's advisory bodies — argued that mixing modern construction with the original fabric destroyed the historical authenticity that made the ruin valuable. Both sides reflect genuine values, and the cathedral you see today is the visible result of that unresolved disagreement.

faq.title: How old is Bagrati Cathedral and who built it?
faq.content: It was built in the early 11th century under King Bagrat III, the first king of a united Georgia, with an inscription recording that the floor was laid in 1003. It was conceived as a national church marking the unification of the Georgian kingdoms, and David IV "the Builder" was later crowned here. An Ottoman gunpowder explosion in 1691 collapsed its dome, after which it stood in ruins for about three centuries until restoration work, begun in the 1950s and completed in 2012, returned it to its present form.

faq.title: Can you visit Bagrati Cathedral, and is it worth it?
faq.content: Yes — it's free, an active cathedral open daily (hours vary around services), on Ukimerioni Hill just above central Kutaisi, reachable by a short taxi ride or a moderate uphill walk. It's well worth visiting: the rebuilt cathedral is genuinely impressive, the interior is a living place of worship, and the hilltop views over Kutaisi and the Rioni valley — especially at sunset — are among the best in the area. Dress modestly (shoulders and knees covered; head covering for women). It pairs naturally with the nearby Gelati and Motsameta monasteries.

chiaturaCableCars.heroTitle: Chiatura Cable Cars: The Soviet Ropeway Town of Imereti
chiaturaCableCars.content:
The cable car network of Chiatura is one of the more genuinely unusual transport systems anywhere — a collection of aerial tramlines first built across a deep river canyon in the 1950s to move manganese miners between their homes on the canyon rim and the industrial facilities on the valley floor. For decades the cable car was not a leisure attraction but simply how Chiatura worked: the canyon topography made it the most practical way to get from one part of town to another. That ordinariness — that this was just daily life — is still the most interesting thing about the place. What's changed, importantly, is the system itself: the original Soviet cabins no longer run, and the network today is a mix of modern rebuilt lines and a handful of restored historic ones, set against the photogenic relics of the old system.

What are the Chiatura cable cars?

The Chiatura cable cars are an aerial ropeway network in the manganese-mining town of Chiatura, in the Imereti region of western Georgia, about 75 km (1.5 hours) from Kutaisi. First built in the 1950s to carry miners across the steep Kvirila canyon, the original Soviet cabins — nicknamed "metal coffins" — were shut down for safety around 2018. Since 2021 the core network has been rebuilt with four modern two-way gondola lines from a central station, and a few historic lines have since been restored; much of the old network survives as abandoned, photogenic relics. It remains a working local transport system and one of Georgia's most distinctive Soviet-industrial sights.

Why the cable cars exist

Chiatura's geography created the problem the cable cars were built to solve. The town grew in a narrow canyon carved by the Kvirila River, with the manganese deposits that gave it its reason for existence in the hills above the canyon walls, and homes and offices spread across both the valley floor and the rim above. The canyon walls are too steep for easy road connections between the levels, and before the cable cars the only routes were lengthy detours around the canyon edges. The Soviet administration, which prized Chiatura as a strategic manganese source during the industrialization of the USSR, commissioned the network in the mid-1950s purely as productive infrastructure — built to move workers efficiently, not for comfort or aesthetics. Over the following decades the system grew to as many as 17 lines, some carrying ore as well as people.

The old "metal coffins" — now retired

The original Soviet cabins — small metal boxes suspended from cables, with minimal ventilation and no engineering beyond the functional minimum — earned the local nickname "metal coffins" (sometimes "flying coffins"), a description more evocative than alarming, but one that captured something real about their character. They ran, remarkably, for over sixty years. By the early 2000s the network was in serious decline, and after years of emergency stoppages and limited service the last of the original lines were shut down for safety reasons around 2018.

This is the most important thing to know before visiting: you can no longer ride the original Soviet cabins. Many of the old stations are barricaded, and rusting gondolas still hang motionless on their lines or sit parked beside the canyon — which makes them a compelling sight for anyone interested in Soviet industrial heritage, urban exploration, or photography, but as relics to look at, not transport to ride. Some original stations have been demolished; others stand abandoned; the old network as a whole is now treated as an endangered heritage site.

The new and restored network

Since the old lines closed, Chiatura has rebuilt its ropeway. In September 2021 a modernization project opened four new two-way lines running from a central station, with enclosed, insulated modern gondolas (around 15-person capacity) and newly built stations — smoother, quieter, and safer than the old cabins, and once again serving residents as everyday transport. Beyond the four modern lines, a few of the historic lines have since been restored for heritage and continued use — for example, an older line reopened in rehabilitated form in late 2024, and one notably steep line (with an incline around 48 degrees, said to be the steepest passenger cable car in the post-Soviet space, more like a vertical lift) operates again and is reportedly free to ride. The number of working lines has been growing, with further restorations underway. Because exactly which lines are running changes as the program continues, it's worth checking locally on arrival.

The rides and the views

Each ride is short — a few minutes from a lower station to an upper one, or across the canyon between neighborhoods — but the views during the crossing give the experience its impact. The canyon is deep enough that the river is visible far below the cable, and the industrial landscape of the valley floor — mining infrastructure, Soviet residential blocks, the angular geometry of the town's working buildings — spreads out in a panorama unlike any conventional tourism view. It isn't beautiful in the usual sense, but it's visually compelling in the way authentic industrial geography often is, and the mix of height, motion, and the view over Chiatura's particular character makes each crossing memorable. The steep line in particular, with its near-vertical ascent, is a genuine thrill.

The upper stations, on the canyon rim, reach the residential neighborhoods above the valley and viewpoints that are among the best for understanding Chiatura's topography. Walking between stations along the canyon edge, riding several lines, and spending time in the neighborhoods at both levels gives a far fuller picture of the town than a single ride from the most obvious starting point.

Chiatura beyond the cable cars

The ropeways are why most visitors come, but the town rewards a little more time. The Soviet industrial architecture of the valley floor — apartment blocks, cultural and administrative buildings, and surviving Soviet mosaics from the peak manganese years — is among the more intact examples of Soviet urban planning in Georgia, relatively unmodified by post-independence development. The Mghvimevi Monastery, a medieval rock-cut religious complex a short distance from town, provides a historical counterpoint to the industrial landscape, and the Katskhi Pillar — the church atop a natural limestone column — is within easy driving distance, making the two a natural combined day trip from Kutaisi.

Practical information

  • Location: Chiatura, Imereti region, western Georgia; about 75 km (around 1.5 hours) northeast of Kutaisi.
  • Getting there: By car, private transfer, or marshrutka from Kutaisi (~1.5–2 hours); a seasonal train also runs from Kutaisi (not in winter). Most often visited as a day trip from Kutaisi, frequently combined with the Katskhi Pillar.
  • The cable cars now: Four modern lines from a central station plus a few restored historic lines; the original Soviet "metal coffins" are retired and cannot be ridden (they remain as relics to view/photograph).
  • Hours: Roughly early morning to early evening (one source cites ~8:00–20:00 with a midday break); varies by line and season — check locally on arrival.
  • Cost: Minimal — around 50 tetri per ride on the modern lines; some restored lines are free.

faq.title: What are the Chiatura cable cars?
faq.content: They're an aerial ropeway network in Chiatura, a manganese-mining town in the Imereti region of western Georgia. First built in the 1950s to carry miners across the steep Kvirila canyon between their homes and the mines, the system grew to as many as 17 lines and became the town's everyday transport. It's one of the most distinctive Soviet-industrial sights in Georgia — though the system has changed a lot in recent years (see below).

faq.title: Can you still ride the old Soviet "flying coffins"?
faq.content: No — this is the key thing to know. The original Soviet cabins (nicknamed "metal coffins") were shut down for safety reasons around 2018 and are no longer in service. Many old stations are barricaded and rusting cabins still hang on the lines, so they're a great sight for photography and Soviet-heritage interest, but you can't ride them. Since 2021 the town has rebuilt its network with four modern gondola lines, and a few historic lines have been restored, so there are working cable cars to ride — just not the original ones.

faq.title: Which cable cars are running now?
faq.content: As of recent reporting, four modern two-way lines run from a central station with enclosed 15-person gondolas, and a handful of historic lines have been restored — including one reopened in late 2024 and a famously steep line (around a 48-degree incline) that operates again and is said to be free. More restorations are underway, so the exact number changes; it's best to check locally on arrival. The modern lines charge a minimal fare (around 50 tetri); some restored lines are free.

faq.title: How do you get to Chiatura from Kutaisi?
faq.content: Chiatura is about 75 km (roughly 1.5 hours) northeast of Kutaisi in the Imereti region. You can go by car, private transfer, or marshrutka (around 1.5–2 hours), and there's also a seasonal train from Kutaisi (it doesn't run in winter). Most people visit on a day trip from Kutaisi, very often combined with the nearby Katskhi Pillar, and sometimes the Mghvimevi Monastery.

faq.title: Is Chiatura worth visiting now that the old cable cars are gone?
faq.content: Yes, if you're interested in Soviet history, industrial landscapes, and unusual places — though set expectations accordingly. You come now for a working modern ropeway over a dramatic canyon, the atmospheric relics of the old "metal coffin" network, intact Soviet architecture and mosaics, and the views over the town's striking topography, rather than to ride the original cabins. Paired with the Katskhi Pillar, it makes a genuinely distinctive day trip from Kutaisi.

urekiBeach.heroTitle: Ureki Beach: Georgia's Magnetic Black-Sand Coast
urekiBeach.content:
Ureki Beach occupies a stretch of the Black Sea coast in the Guria region of Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — about 50 kilometers north of Batumi, and it's immediately distinguishable from any other beach on the Georgian coast by the color of its sand. The beach at Ureki is dark — almost black in some light — its sand composed largely of magnetite and other iron-bearing minerals eroded from the volcanic highlands of the Caucasus and carried to the coast by the rivers draining into this section of the sea. The magnetic properties are real and measurable: a magnet drawn across the surface picks up visible quantities of the dark mineral, and the magnetite content here is among the highest of any beach in the world. That geological peculiarity, combined with the calm, shallow sea along this stretch, has given Ureki its particular identity as a wellness and family destination within Georgian coastal tourism — it's the country's best-known stretch of true sandy (rather than pebble) beach.

What is Ureki Beach?

Ureki Beach is a roughly 5-kilometer stretch of dark, fine, magnetite-rich "magnetic sand" on Georgia's Black Sea coast in the Guria region, about 50 km north of Batumi. Unlike the pebble beaches of Batumi and Kobuleti, it's true soft sand, and its shallow, calm water makes it one of the most family-friendly beaches in the country. Long a Soviet-era health resort, it's associated with traditional therapeutic claims about the magnetic sand (more folk tradition than proven medicine). It's quieter, more local, and cheaper than Batumi, with a pine forest behind the beach, and is easy to reach by train.

The magnetic sand

The sand at Ureki is soft underfoot — its fine-grained volcanic-mineral composition (magnetite, ilmenite, and other iron- and titanium-bearing minerals) producing a texture quite unlike the coarse pebble beaches that dominate much of the Georgian coast, including the well-known stretches at Batumi and Kobuleti. This softness is one of the beach's most immediately noticed advantages, particularly for families with young children, for whom pebble beaches are an obvious discomfort. A fun, genuinely real party trick: run a magnet through the sand and it comes up coated in dark grains. One practical note — the dark sand absorbs heat readily, so the surface can get considerably hotter than the air in direct summer sun; footwear for crossing the beach at midday is wise.

The beach has been associated with therapeutic traditions since the Soviet period, when Ureki was developed as a sanatorium resort for conditions — cardiovascular problems, musculoskeletal and joint disorders, disorders of the peripheral nervous system — for which the magnetic sand was believed beneficial (sand baths, lying in the warm sand). It's worth being clear-eyed about this: the medical case for specific therapeutic effects from the magnetic sand is not established clinical consensus — scientific evidence is limited, and the claims are best understood as a deeply embedded Georgian wellness tradition rather than proven medicine. That said, the tradition genuinely draws visitors who come for the perceived benefits, and the combination of warm summer sun, soft dark sand, calm sea, and pine-forest air is restorative for most people regardless of the specific mechanisms.

The beach and the sea

The sea at Ureki is generally calm and shallow for a good distance from shore, which makes it among the most child-friendly swimming environments on the Georgian coast — the flat, gently shelving bottom means shallow water extends far out, reassuring for families and non-swimmers. The Black Sea here warms to comfortable swimming temperature by June and holds it through September, and the reduced wave action compared with more exposed stretches makes the water pleasant throughout the summer without particular caution in normal conditions.

The beach runs for several kilometers in both directions from the main resort center, and the full length is walkable — a long beach walk with the pine forest behind the sand and the sea to the horizon is one of the more pleasant simple pleasures here. The pine trees lining the back of the beach provide shade within a short walk of the waterline, and the mix of beach, forest edge, and sea gives the environment a variety that open beaches lack.

The resort town

Ureki is a modest Black Sea resort town without the scale, architectural ambition, or international infrastructure of Batumi to the south. Accommodation ranges from basic guesthouses and small hotels to larger sanatorium-style facilities reflecting the town's therapeutic heritage, and the restaurants and cafés along the strip serve the standard range of Georgian coastal cooking — fresh fish, the full Georgian menu, seasonal produce — at prices generally lower than Batumi (family guesthouses are notably affordable). The atmosphere is local and family-oriented rather than cosmopolitan; in peak summer it's busy and a bit chaotic, with vendors selling inflatables and churchkhela along the front. Visitors expecting Batumi's energy and variety will find something much more relaxed and much less curated — which is exactly the right expectation, since Ureki's appeal is its simplicity and its specific beach quality rather than urban entertainment.

The pine forest behind the beach is a distinctive feature most resorts of this size lack. It provides shade for walking and for escaping the exposed beach in the hottest part of the day, and the mix of salt air and resinous pine gives Ureki an atmosphere of freshness that is a genuine pleasure beyond the beach itself.

Getting there

Ureki is about 50–55 kilometers north of Batumi on the main coastal road and railway; the drive from Batumi takes around an hour. The train is the standout option — Ureki has its own station on the Tbilisi–Batumi mainline, and the trip from Batumi is roughly 45 minutes, making it one of the easier Georgian coastal destinations to reach without a car (it works well as a day trip from Batumi, with several trains back). From Tbilisi the journey is around four to five hours by train. Marshrutkas on the coastal route between Batumi and Kutaisi also stop at Ureki. The Shekvetili attractions (the Dendrological Park, Musicians Park, and Tsitsinatela) are just to the south, easily combined with an Ureki stay.

Practical information

  • Location: Ureki, Ozurgeti Municipality, Guria, on the Black Sea coast about 50–55 km north of Batumi.
  • Getting there: ~1 hour by car from Batumi; ~45 minutes by train (Ureki has its own station on the Tbilisi–Batumi line); marshrutkas on the Batumi–Kutaisi coastal route also stop here.
  • Beach: ~5 km of soft, dark magnetite "magnetic sand"; calm, shallow, family-friendly sea; pine forest behind.
  • Hours/Admission: Beach freely accessible at all times; free. Sun-lounger rental, facilities, and accommodation charged separately. Main season June–September.
  • Good to know: Sand gets hot underfoot at midday (bring footwear); busy in peak summer; quieter and pleasant in early September–October with the sea still warm.

faq.title: What is special about Ureki Beach?
faq.content: Ureki is famous for its dark, fine "magnetic sand" — sand rich in magnetite and other iron minerals, with a magnetic content among the highest of any beach in the world (run a magnet through it and dark grains stick to it). It's one of the few genuinely sandy beaches on Georgia's mostly pebbly Black Sea coast, with soft sand and calm, shallow water that make it especially family-friendly. It's about 50 km north of Batumi in the Guria region, and has a long history as a health resort.

faq.title: Is the magnetic sand at Ureki actually therapeutic?
faq.content: The magnetism is real and measurable, but the medical claims are another matter. Since the Soviet era, Ureki has been a health resort where the warm magnetic sand was used to treat cardiovascular, joint, and nervous-system conditions, and many Georgians hold strong beliefs about its benefits. However, rigorous scientific evidence for specific therapeutic effects is limited — it's best understood as a long-standing wellness tradition rather than proven medicine. Many visitors find the experience restorative regardless.

faq.title: Is Ureki good for families with children?
faq.content: Yes — it's one of the most family-friendly beaches in Georgia. The sand is soft (far more comfortable than the pebbles at Batumi or Kobuleti), and the sea is calm and shallow, with a flat bottom that keeps the water shallow a long way out — reassuring for young children and non-swimmers. The town is relaxed, local, and affordable, and there's pine-forest shade behind the beach. Note the dark sand gets hot at midday, so bring footwear.

faq.title: How do you get to Ureki from Batumi?
faq.content: It's about 50–55 km north of Batumi. The easiest way is the train — Ureki has its own station on the Tbilisi–Batumi line and the trip from Batumi takes around 45 minutes, so it works well even as a day trip. By car it's about an hour, and marshrutkas on the coastal route between Batumi and Kutaisi also stop at Ureki. From Tbilisi, the train takes roughly four to five hours.

faq.title: When is the best time to visit Ureki?
faq.content: The main season is June through September, when the sea is warm (it's said to be among the warmest on the Black Sea) and the town is fully active — though July and August are busy and can feel crowded. Early September into October is a rewarding quieter window, with the sea still warm and pleasant temperatures. The beach itself is free and open year-round, but the resort facilities operate mainly in summer.

shemokmediMonastery.heroTitle: Shemokmedi Monastery: Guria's Medieval Religious Heart near Ozurgeti
shemokmediMonastery.content:
Shemokmedi Monastery is the most significant medieval religious complex in the Guria region of western Georgia, in the country in the South Caucasus, set on a forested hill above the Bzhuzhi River about five kilometers east of Ozurgeti, the regional center of Guria. Founded in the 15th century, it served for centuries as the principal ecclesiastical center of the Gurian principality — the seat of the Bishop of Shemokmedi and the burial ground of the Gurieli dynasty, the focus of religious and cultural life in a region that kept its own distinct political identity within the Georgian world through much of the medieval and early modern periods. It isn't a site that draws many international visitors — Guria is among the less-traveled parts of western Georgia — but the quality of the complex, its hilltop setting, and its historical depth give it a significance its modest tourist profile doesn't reflect.

What is Shemokmedi Monastery?

Shemokmedi Monastery is a 15th-century Georgian Orthodox complex near Ozurgeti in the Guria region of western Georgia. It was one of the three bishoprics of the principality of Guria and the principal burial ground of the ruling Gurieli dynasty, and it became one of Georgia's richest monasteries — a treasury of manuscripts and church objects gathered from across the country. The complex has two churches (the Church of the Redeemer and the domed Church of the Transfiguration, built to house a rescued 9th-century icon) and a bell tower, on a forested hill above the Bzhuzhi River. It's an active monastery, free to visit, with sweeping views over the Gurian landscape.

History and ecclesiastical importance

Shemokmedi grew in importance as Guria developed as a semi-autonomous principality within the fragmented political landscape that followed the breakup of the unified Georgian kingdom in the 15th century. As one of the three bishoprics of Guria (alongside Jumati and Khino), it was the religious and cultural center of this distinct political entity — where the Gurian ruling family, the Gurieli dynasty, maintained its relationship with the Georgian Orthodox Church and expressed its legitimacy through religious patronage. The local prelates held the rank of archbishop or metropolitan and the title Shemokmedeli.

The monastery was the principal dynastic necropolis of the Gurieli, and the surviving tombs include those of Rostom Gurieli (died 1564) — a major figure who fought the Ottomans and expanded Gurian territory — and Mamia III Gurieli (died 1714). This connection to the ruling family, as patrons, donors, and the buried dead, gives Shemokmedi the dynastic and aristocratic associations that characterize Georgia's major regional monasteries, whose development was shaped by noble patronage over centuries: the great families expressed both piety and political authority through investment in religious foundations, and the quality and scale of the buildings reflect that investment.

Shemokmedi was also one of Georgia's richest monasteries and an important repository of cultural heritage — over the centuries it accumulated an extensive collection of manuscripts, icons, and church treasures gathered from monasteries across Georgia, preserved within the relative security of the fortified complex through the Ottoman expansion and the conflicts of the 17th and 18th centuries. Parts of that collection, which survived 19th-century thefts, are now displayed in Georgia's museums. This custodial role — preserving written and material heritage in difficult centuries — was one that Georgia's major monasteries played repeatedly, and Shemokmedi's contribution to Gurian cultural survival gives it an importance beyond its architecture and liturgy. The monastery's history was not always peaceful: after a Gurieli-led insurrection against the Russian Empire in 1820, Russian troops stormed Shemokmedi, demolished its fortifications, and devastated its surroundings.

The complex and its churches

The complex occupies its hilltop within a walled precinct that gives it a defined, contained character, the buildings and open spaces organized around its two churches. The larger is the Church of the Redeemer, a three-nave stone basilica lined with bluestone, its interior once entirely frescoed — surviving fragments depict Gurieli princes, which is how the frescoes are dated to the early 17th century. The smaller Church of the Transfiguration (also called "Zarzma") is a single-nave, octagonal-domed church built in the late 1570s under Prince Vakhtang I Gurieli for a specific purpose: to house a venerated 9th-century icon of the Transfiguration that the Gurieli had rescued from Zarzma Monastery when the Ottomans occupied the principality of Samtskhe — hence the church's second name. Surviving inscriptions and a fresco of the first Bishop of Shemokmedi, Besarion Machutadze, remain on its walls.

The bell tower, originally built in the 16th century (and renovated in 1831), stands on the enclosure wall and provides the vertical accent that many Georgian monastery complexes instead achieve through the elevation of the main church. All the structures show traces of multiple reconstructions across the monastery's long life of continuous use. The broader complex includes the ancillary monastic buildings, courtyards, and enclosure walls of a functioning monastery, and the community in residence gives Shemokmedi the quality of a place still working to its original purpose rather than existing only as a heritage monument.

The setting

The forested hillside setting is a real part of the appeal, and distinguishes Shemokmedi from the more exposed, open-terrain monasteries of Kakheti and central Georgia. The trees around the precinct give the approach a quality of progressive enclosure and discovery — the monastery appearing through the forest before the precinct is entered — and the greenness and moisture of the Gurian landscape at this elevation, shaped by the nearby Black Sea and the region's high rainfall, give it a lushness the drier eastern sites lack.

The views from the elevated precinct over the Bzhuzhi valley and the surrounding hills set the monastery in its landscape — Mount Jumati to the southwest, the Adjara mountains to the east — and among the best from any accessible point in the Ozurgeti area. Guria's landscape character — forested, rolling, humid, intensely green — is distinct from both the open wine country of Kakheti and the dramatic high mountains of Svaneti or Kazbegi, and Shemokmedi's position within it suits the monastery's intimate, regionally focused character.

Visiting

The monastery is a short drive from Ozurgeti, reached by road climbing through the forested hills (about 5 km southeast of the town center). Entry is free, the site is open during daylight hours, the active monastic community is present, and the churches are open to visitors outside service times. Modest dress is required, as at all active Georgian Orthodox sites. Allow roughly twenty to forty minutes for a thorough look at the two churches, the bell tower, the wider complex, and the hillside viewpoints.

Shemokmedi works naturally as part of an Ozurgeti-area itinerary combining the monastery with the Gomismta mountain destination, the wider Gurian landscape, and the coast of the Guria–Adjara borderzone. For travelers passing through western Georgia on a route taking in Batumi, Kutaisi, and the Imereti sites, a half-day around Ozurgeti — Shemokmedi plus the approach to Gomismta — adds a genuinely distinctive regional character to an itinerary that might otherwise follow the standard western-Georgia circuit without engaging with what Guria specifically offers.

Practical information

  • Location: Village of Shemokmedi, about 5 km southeast of Ozurgeti, Guria, western Georgia; on a hill above the Bzhuzhi River.
  • Getting there: A short drive from Ozurgeti by car or taxi on a paved road; Ozurgeti is about an hour from Batumi. No regular public transport to the monastery itself.
  • Hours/Admission: Open during daylight hours; free. Active monastery — churches open to visitors outside services.
  • Dress code: Modest dress required; women may need a head covering and skirt (wraps are sometimes available at the entrance).
  • Time needed: About 20–40 minutes; combine with Gomismta and the Ozurgeti area.

faq.title: What is Shemokmedi Monastery?
faq.content: Shemokmedi Monastery is a 15th-century Georgian Orthodox complex on a forested hill near Ozurgeti, in the Guria region of western Georgia. It was one of the three bishoprics of the principality of Guria and the principal burial ground of the ruling Gurieli dynasty, and it became one of Georgia's richest monasteries — a treasury of manuscripts and church objects gathered from across the country. The complex has two churches and a bell tower, and it remains an active monastery.

faq.title: Who is buried at Shemokmedi, and why does it matter?
faq.content: Shemokmedi was the dynastic necropolis of the Gurieli, the ruling family of the principality of Guria. The surviving tombs include Rostom Gurieli (died 1564), a prominent prince who fought the Ottomans and expanded Gurian territory, and Mamia III Gurieli (died 1714). The monastery was where the Gurieli expressed their piety and political legitimacy through religious patronage, which is why it became the cultural and religious heart of the principality.

faq.title: What is there to see at Shemokmedi Monastery?
faq.content: Two churches and a bell tower. The Church of the Redeemer is a three-nave stone basilica, once fully frescoed (surviving fragments show Gurieli princes). The smaller domed Church of the Transfiguration — also called "Zarzma" — was built in the late 1570s to house a 9th-century icon rescued from Zarzma Monastery when the Ottomans took Samtskhe. The 16th-century bell tower stands on the enclosure wall. The hilltop also gives some of the best views in the Ozurgeti area.

faq.title: Where is Shemokmedi and how do you get there?
faq.content: It's in the village of Shemokmedi, about 5 km southeast of Ozurgeti, the main town of Guria, on a hill above the Bzhuzhi River. It's a short drive from Ozurgeti by car or taxi on a paved road (Ozurgeti is about an hour from Batumi); there's no regular public transport to the monastery itself. Entry is free, and it's open during daylight hours.

faq.title: Is Shemokmedi worth visiting?
faq.content: For travelers interested in Georgian history and Orthodox heritage, yes — it's the most important medieval monument in Guria, with two historic churches, the Gurieli tombs, a remarkable history as a treasury of Georgian church heritage, and a lush forested hilltop setting with fine views. It's a modest, intimate site rather than a major monument on the scale of Mtskheta or Gelati, so it rewards interest in regional history; it pairs well with Gomismta and the Gurian landscape for a distinctive half-day around Ozurgeti.

dendrologicalPark.heroTitle: Shekvetili Dendrological Park: Georgia's Park of Giant Trees
dendrologicalPark.content:
The Shekvetili Dendrological Park occupies a large stretch of the Black Sea coastal zone in the Guria region of Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — between the resort areas of Ureki and Kobuleti. It's one of the more unexpected attractions on Georgia's western coast: a large, carefully designed botanical and wildlife park combining an extraordinary collection of mature trees with landscaped lakes, wooden walkways, and a range of birds and animals, giving it a character somewhere between a botanical garden, a wildlife park, and a leisure destination. Opened in 2020, it's among the most ambitious public park projects completed in Georgia in recent years and has quickly become one of the most visited non-beach attractions on the Black Sea coast.

What is the Shekvetili Dendrological Park?

The Shekvetili Dendrological Park is a roughly 60-hectare arboretum on Georgia's Black Sea coast in Guria, opened in 2020 and created by the Georgian billionaire Bidzina Ivanishvili. Its signature feature is its giant mature trees — hundreds of them, some centuries old, transplanted from across Georgia and abroad rather than grown from saplings — alongside 1,000+ plant species, bamboo groves, landscaped lakes, and aviaries of exotic birds including flamingos. It's free to enter, takes about two hours to walk, and is a popular family and nature stop near Ureki and Kobuleti, about 45–70 km north of Batumi. (Note: closed Mondays and Thursdays.)

The tree collection

The dendrological collection is the core of the park's identity and what sets it apart from the ordinary recreational parks of most Georgian coastal towns. The most striking thing on entry is the scale and age of the trees: many specimens are enormous, their trunks suggesting centuries of growth (some are said to be over 300 years old), and the decision to relocate mature trees of this scale — rather than plant saplings and wait decades — gives Shekvetili a visual maturity that new parks lack. Roughly 18 hectares are devoted to giant trees endemic to Georgia (200-plus giants across some 29–39 species — eucalyptus, sequoia, cypress, cedar, magnolia, oak, beech), transplanted from different regions of the country; the remaining ~42 hectares hold thousands of exotic species from five continents. Bamboo groves occupy sections of the park, their dense growth and distinctive sound in the coastal breeze creating microenvironments of particular atmospheric quality.

The oldest and largest specimens are worth seeking out specifically — trees of this scale are genuinely impressive as individual organisms, and the sense of being small beside a very large tree is one of those physical experiences description can't fully convey. The diversity of bark textures, leaf forms, and branching structures makes a continuously engaging environment for anyone who looks at trees as individual subjects rather than background.

It's worth knowing how the park came to be: the giant trees were transplanted from across Georgia in a large, much-discussed (and, among environmentalists, controversial) operation in the years before the park opened to the public in 2020. The result is undeniably impressive, and the story is part of what makes the place distinctive — though it remains a debated chapter in recent Georgian environmental history.

The lakes and water features

The landscaped lakes are among the park's most visually successful elements, the still water reflecting the tree canopy and providing habitat for the waterfowl that have become one of its most popular features. Wooden bridges cross the lakes and link different sections, and the views from the decks — the reflections, the moving birds, the trees above — are the kind of calm, compositionally complete scenes that make the park especially rewarding for photography.

The flamingos around the lakes are among the most unexpected and consistently delighting features — their improbable pink against the green vegetation and dark water gives the lake sections a mild visual surprise that never quite wears off. Ducks, swans, and other waterbirds complete the lake community, and the park keeps aviaries of exotic birds and animals (around 58 bird species, many rare or endangered, plus animals such as lemurs and parrots), so the wildlife element is a genuine part of the visit, not an afterthought.

The experience

The park is designed for unhurried exploration rather than purposeful transit — the paths wander through the tree collection and around the lakes in a way that invites stopping, observing, and doubling back rather than following a straight line from entrance to exit. Plan on about two hours to see it properly. The well-maintained, relatively flat paths make it accessible to visitors of most ages and mobility levels, and there are benches, shaded spots, drinking fountains, and picnic areas throughout. There are no cafés inside, so bring snacks and water, especially in summer; the beach isn't directly accessible from the park (it's about a 20-minute walk around).

One practical caveat to know in advance: the park posts a minimum age of 12 for entry (in practice this is reportedly applied leniently, and families with younger children have been admitted, but it's worth being prepared in case it's enforced). So while the trees, birds, and open space genuinely appeal to children, the official rule is worth checking if you're traveling with young kids.

The Shekvetili cluster

The Dendrological Park is the anchor of a cluster of attractions in the Shekvetili area. Close by — though a separate attraction rather than part of the same site — is the Musicians Park, an open-air sculpture walk where statues of Georgian and international musicians play their music as you approach; combining the two makes a natural Shekvetili outing, covering botanical and wildlife interest plus the cultural, interactive character of the sculpture walk. Also in the area are the Georgia in Miniatures Park (scale models of Georgia's monuments), the Tsitsinatela amusement park, and the Black Sea Arena concert venue. (These are clustered together in Shekvetili but are individually separate attractions, even though tours sometimes bundle them.)

Getting there and context

Shekvetili is on the main Black Sea coastal road between Batumi and Kutaisi, roughly 45–70 km north of Batumi (about an hour by car from the Adjarian capital). Coastal marshrutkas between Batumi and Kobuleti pass the area, though some take the bypass highway, so confirm the route. The park sits between Ureki — the magnetic black-sand beach resort — and Kobuleti, making it an easy addition to any western-Georgia coastal itinerary combining beach time with the natural and cultural sites of Guria.

Practical information

  • Location: Shekvetili, Ozurgeti Municipality, Guria, on the Black Sea coast between Ureki and Kobuleti.
  • Getting there: By car on the coastal route between Batumi and Kutaisi, ~45–70 km (about an hour) north of Batumi; coastal marshrutkas pass the area (confirm the route).
  • Hours: Daily from 10:00 (closing varies seasonally), closed Mondays and Thursdays.
  • Admission: Free.
  • Good to know: Allow ~2 hours; no cafés inside (bring snacks/water; drinking fountains available); beach ~20 min walk away; posted minimum entry age 12 (reportedly applied leniently).

faq.title: What is the Shekvetili Dendrological Park?
faq.content: It's a roughly 60-hectare arboretum on Georgia's Black Sea coast in the Guria region, opened in 2020 and created by the Georgian billionaire Bidzina Ivanishvili. Its signature feature is its giant mature trees — hundreds of them, some centuries old, transplanted from across Georgia and abroad — alongside more than 1,000 plant species, bamboo groves, landscaped lakes, and aviaries of exotic birds including flamingos. It's free to enter and one of the most popular non-beach attractions on the western coast.

faq.title: Why are the trees so big — and why is the park controversial?
faq.content: Rather than planting saplings, the park's creator transplanted huge mature trees (some over 300 years old) from around Georgia, giving it instant visual maturity. That operation — moving giant trees long distances, reportedly with major logistical and state involvement — drew significant criticism from environmentalists before the park opened in 2020. The result is genuinely impressive, but the way it was created remains a debated chapter in recent Georgian environmental history.

faq.title: What is there to see at the park?
faq.content: The giant trees are the headline, alongside bamboo groves, themed gardens, and more than 1,000 plant species from Georgia and five continents. The landscaped lakes, crossed by wooden bridges, are home to waterbirds — the flamingos are a particular favorite — and the park keeps aviaries of exotic birds and animals (around 58 bird species, plus lemurs and parrots). It's a relaxed, photogenic place for an unhurried two-hour walk.

faq.title: Is it good for families and children?
faq.content: The trees, birds, and open green space genuinely appeal to children, but there's a catch: the park posts a minimum entry age of 12. In practice this is reportedly applied leniently and families with younger children have been admitted, but it's worth being aware of and checking before you go if you're traveling with young kids. There are picnic areas and drinking fountains, but no cafés, so bring food and water.

faq.title: Where is it, when is it open, and how do you get there?
faq.content: It's in Shekvetili, on the Black Sea coast between Ureki and Kobuleti, about 45–70 km (roughly an hour) north of Batumi on the coastal road; coastal marshrutkas pass the area. Entry is free and it's open daily from 10:00, but importantly it's closed on Mondays and Thursdays, so plan around that. Allow about two hours. It pairs naturally with the nearby Musicians Park and the other Shekvetili attractions.

paliastomiKolkheti.heroTitle: Paliastomi Lake & Kolkheti National Park: Georgia's Black Sea Wetland
paliastomiKolkheti.content:
Paliastomi Lake is the largest lake in the Kolkheti National Park and the hydrological heart of the most significant wetland ecosystem on Georgia's Black Sea coast, in the country in the South Caucasus. It lies near the port city of Poti, on the boundary of the Samegrelo-Zemo Svaneti and Guria regions of western Georgia, roughly 300 kilometers west of Tbilisi. The lake covers around 17–18 square kilometers and connects to the Black Sea through a channel, making a semi-enclosed coastal lagoon whose ecology reflects that dual influence — fresh water from the rivers draining the Colchic lowlands mixing with the brackish influence of the sea to produce a habitat of exceptional biological productivity. It's the ecological centerpiece of Kolkheti National Park, established in 1998–1999 to protect what remains of the ancient Colchic wetland system that once covered far more of the Black Sea coastal plain — and the part of the park that most directly expresses what makes this corner of Georgia ecologically unique.

What is Paliastomi Lake / Kolkheti National Park?

Paliastomi Lake is a large coastal lagoon (about 17–18 km²) near Poti, the centerpiece of Kolkheti National Park in western Georgia. The park (around 28,940 hectares, established 1998–1999) protects the remnant Colchic wetlands and forests of the historical land of Colchis, and since 2021 it has been part of the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Colchic Rainforests and Wetlands" — the only natural UNESCO site in the Caucasus. It's a wetland rather than a mountain park, explored by boat from Paliastomi Lake up the reed-lined Pichori River, and it's one of the most important bird habitats in the region, home to Dalmatian pelicans and hundreds of thousands of migrating waterbirds.

A UNESCO Colchic wetland

The wetland system around Paliastomi Lake is part of the remnant Colchic landscape — the ancient ecosystem of the eastern Black Sea coast that appears in Greek sources as the land of Colchis, progressively reduced by drainage, agriculture, and development over the past two centuries to a fraction of its former extent.
