What remains within the park is internationally significant: in 2021 Kolkheti National Park was inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List as part of the "Colchic Rainforests and Wetlands" — together with Mtirala, Kintrishi, and the Kobuleti (Ispani) protected areas — and it's noted as the only natural-category World Heritage site in the entire Caucasus. The Colchic forest here is a relict of the subtropical forests that covered southern Europe before the Ice Ages, surviving in the mild, wet coastal climate; the park holds more than a thousand species of vascular plants. The wetlands are also a Ramsar site of international importance and incorporate the former Kolkheti State Nature Reserve (1947).

There's a resonant cultural layer too: this is the historical Colchis of Greek myth — the destination of Jason and the Argonauts in their quest for the Golden Fleece, sometimes called "the rim of the world" in the ancient imagination. The same Argonaut and Golden Fleece story runs through the coast to the south, commemorated by the Medea statue in Batumi's Europe Square and the legends attached to Gonio Fortress.

The birdlife

The lake's position on the eastern Black Sea coast places it on one of the major bird-migration routes linking the breeding grounds of northern and eastern Europe with the wintering areas of Africa and the Middle East, and the surrounding wetlands provide critical stopover and wintering habitat for huge numbers of birds moving through this corridor each year. The productivity of the lake and its marshes, reed beds, and riparian forest supports an extraordinary concentration of waterbird life through the migration seasons and breeding populations of herons, egrets, cormorants, and ducks through the warmer months. Around 190–200 bird species have been recorded. Globally threatened species give the park specific ornithological weight: Dalmatian pelicans winter on the lagoon, the rare pygmy cormorant breeds in the reed beds, and white-tailed eagles and black storks are among the notable birds of the area. For birdwatchers, this is one of the most important sites in Georgia.

The aquatic ecosystem — fish, invertebrates, aquatic vegetation — reflects the productivity of a nutrient-rich coastal lagoon, supporting both the wildlife that feeds on it and a traditional fishery that has been part of the Poti economy for centuries (sport fishing is permitted in the park). The semi-enclosed lagoon, with the sea connection providing salinity influence while the freshwater rivers keep the lake predominantly fresh, creates the kind of transitional habitat that is among the most biologically productive on earth.

The boat experience

The most rewarding — and essentially the only — way to engage with Paliastomi Lake and the Kolkheti wetlands is by boat. There are no hiking trails through the wetland; the park is explored on boat tours organized through the park's visitor infrastructure, which reach habitat and landscape entirely inaccessible on foot. Small boats run from Paliastomi Lake up the network of channels and the reed-lined Pichori River, giving a perspective no shore-based viewpoint can match — reed walls rising on both sides, the water at boat level, the sounds of the wetland all around, the sky reduced to a strip above the channel. The transition between the enclosed channel and the open lake is one of the more dramatic shifts of spatial character in Georgian nature tourism, the confinement of the reed bed giving way to the wide open water with a suddenness that makes the lake's scale especially striking. (The park offers several routes from Poti — a short Paliastomi Lake tour, a standard Paliastomi–Pichori tour, and a fuller route — plus a separate boat route on the Churia River near Zugdidi.)

Wildlife observation from the boat is the most productive approach to birdwatching here, since a small boat moving quietly through the channels disturbs waterbirds far less than a walker approaching from shore. Herons at the channel edge, ducks moving between reed-bed openings, and the larger species feeding on the open lake are most approachable by water, and the park guides' knowledge of current wildlife distribution and seasonal patterns turns simple observation into informed engagement with the lake's ecology.

Poti and access

The lake and park are most easily reached from Poti, the main port city of western Georgia and the largest settlement by the park. Poti is a working port rather than a tourist destination — its character defined by the commercial harbor, the largest deep-water port on the Georgian Black Sea coast — but it provides the services, accommodation, and transport that make it the logical base for a Kolkheti and Paliastomi visit, and the park's visitor center (in the Maltakva district near Poti) handles boat-tour bookings and park access. Poti also has long black-sand beaches, with cleaner stretches south toward Maltakva and Grigoleti.

Poti connects to Kutaisi by road in about an hour and a half and to Tbilisi in roughly four hours, and the park is also reachable from Zugdidi to the north and Batumi to the south, so travelers on a wider western-Georgia itinerary can include it from several directions without much backtracking. Boat tours run year-round by reservation (and are suspended in bad weather for safety), so advance booking is advisable, especially in the peak spring and autumn migration seasons.

When to visit

Spring (April through early June) is the most ecologically rewarding season, combining the peak spring bird migration with the breeding activity of resident species and the full flowering of the Colchic vegetation — the lake and reed beds are at their most active, and the mix of migrating and breeding species gives the widest variety of wildlife at any one time. Autumn (September–October) brings the second migration peak, with a different species mix and the particular quality of autumn light over the open water. Summer is warm and fully accessible for boat tours, with breeding birds still active in the earlier weeks. Winter brings wintering waterfowl — including the Dalmatian pelicans — and a quieter, more austere wetland under the subdued light and cloud of the western Georgian winter, a very different atmosphere from the green warmth of the main season.

Practical information

  • Location: Paliastomi Lake, Kolkheti National Park, near Poti, on the Samegrelo–Guria boundary, western Georgia; visitor center in the Maltakva district near Poti.
  • Getting there: Poti is ~1.5 hours from Kutaisi, ~4 hours from Tbilisi, and reachable from Zugdidi (north) and Batumi (south); boat tours depart from the Poti/Paliastomi area.
  • How you visit: By boat only (no hiking trails through the wetland) — book tours through the park visitor center; sport fishing permitted. Tours run year-round by reservation, weather permitting.
  • Best time: Spring (April–early June) and autumn (September–October) for migration and the richest birdwatching.
  • Bring: Binoculars, sun and rain protection, insect repellent (it's a wetland).

faq.title: What is Paliastomi Lake / Kolkheti National Park?
faq.content: Paliastomi Lake is a large coastal lagoon (about 17–18 km²) near Poti in western Georgia, the centerpiece of Kolkheti National Park. The park (around 28,940 hectares, established 1998–1999) protects the remnant Colchic wetlands and forests of the historical land of Colchis, and since 2021 it has been part of the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Colchic Rainforests and Wetlands" — the only natural UNESCO site in the Caucasus. It's a flat coastal wetland, explored by boat, and one of the most important bird habitats in the region.

faq.title: What is there to do at Kolkheti National Park?
faq.content: The main activity is a boat tour. There are no hiking trails through the wetland — instead, small boats run from Paliastomi Lake up the reed-lined channels and the Pichori River, which is the best way to see the birdlife and the waterside Colchic forest. The park offers several boat routes from the Poti area (from a short lake tour to a fuller Paliastomi–Pichori trip), plus a separate route on the Churia River near Zugdidi. Sport fishing is also permitted. It's especially rewarding for birdwatchers.

faq.title: What birds can you see at Paliastomi Lake?
faq.content: It's one of Georgia's premier birding sites, on a major Black Sea migration route, with around 190–200 species recorded. The headline species is the globally threatened Dalmatian pelican, which winters on the lagoon; the rare pygmy cormorant breeds in the reed beds, and white-tailed eagles, black storks, herons, egrets, cormorants, and many ducks and waders use the wetlands. Spring and autumn migration bring the greatest numbers and variety, while winter brings the wintering waterfowl.

faq.title: How do you get to Kolkheti National Park?
faq.content: It's reached from Poti, the western Georgian port city beside the park, where the visitor center (in the Maltakva district) handles boat-tour bookings. Poti is about 1.5 hours from Kutaisi and 4 hours from Tbilisi by road, and is also reachable from Zugdidi to the north and Batumi to the south, so it fits into a wider western-Georgia route. Boat tours run year-round by reservation (weather permitting), so it's best to book ahead, especially in the spring and autumn migration seasons.

faq.title: Why is Kolkheti connected to the Golden Fleece?
faq.content: Because this is the historical region of Colchis — the destination of Jason and the Argonauts in Greek myth, who sailed here to win the Golden Fleece. The Colchic lowland was, in the ancient imagination, almost the edge of the known world. That same Argonaut and Golden Fleece heritage runs down the coast to the south, where it's commemorated by the Medea statue in Batumi's Europe Square and the legends attached to Gonio Fortress, making Kolkheti part of a wider Colchis story across western Georgia.

dadianiPalaceMuseum.heroTitle: The Dadiani Palace Museum: Zugdidi's Princely Estate and Its Napoleon Mask
dadianiPalaceMuseum.content:
The Dadiani Palace Museum is the most significant historical site in Zugdidi and one of the more remarkable cultural institutions in western Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — occupying a walled estate in the center of the city that combines 19th-century princely palaces with an extensive botanical garden. It was the seat of the Dadiani dynasty, the rulers of the Principality of Samegrelo (Mingrelia), and the museum founded here in the mid-19th century is among the oldest in Georgia, holding a collection of around 45,000 items. Its two most famous treasures speak to the family's extraordinary reach: a bronze death mask of Napoleon Bonaparte, one of only a few in the world, and a garment traditionally believed to have belonged to the Virgin Mary. For travelers passing through Zugdidi to or from Svaneti, the palace is by far the city's most rewarding stop.

What is the Dadiani Palace Museum?

The Dadiani Palace Museum is a complex of 19th-century princely palaces, a court church, and a large botanical garden in central Zugdidi, the former seat of the Dadiani dynasty who ruled the Principality of Samegrelo. Founded as a museum in the mid-19th century by Prince David Dadiani, it holds around 45,000 items spanning the history of Samegrelo and the Dadiani court. Its headline treasures are a bronze death mask of Napoleon Bonaparte — which reached Zugdidi through the family's marriage into Napoleon's relatives, the Murats — and a robe traditionally venerated as the Virgin Mary's. It is the most important cultural site in the Samegrelo region and the best stop in Zugdidi.

The Dadiani family and Samegrelo

The Dadiani dynasty ruled Samegrelo as a semi-autonomous principality for centuries, their power rooted in the fertile lowlands and forested highlands of the region, their position squeezed between the Ottoman Empire to the west and the wider Georgian and, later, Russian imperial worlds to the east. They held their rule through military capability, strategic marriage alliances, and the defensibility of a land of forests and rivers. Under Russian suzerainty, as western Georgia was absorbed into the empire through the early 19th century, the Dadianis kept a degree of formal authority and social prestige — enough to continue developing their residence and collections even as their independence waned.

Russian administration took direct control of the principality in 1857, and the principality was formally abolished in 1867, ending Dadiani rule. By then the family had woven itself into both the Russian imperial aristocracy and, through marriage, the European nobility — and it is exactly this dual character, a Georgian regional dynasty connected to the wider European aristocratic world, that gives the Zugdidi palace and its collection their distinctive flavor.

The palaces and the estate

The estate is not a single building but a complex of palaces within a walled park. The main residence is the palace of Princess Ekaterine Chavchavadze-Dadiani — daughter of the poet and nobleman Alexander Chavchavadze of Tsinandali (and sister of Nino, the widow of the Russian writer Griboedov) — who reconstructed it in the 1860s in a Neo-Gothic style, working with a European architect and blending Gothic elements with Georgian, Russian, and other influences. Nearby stands the palace of her son, Prince Niko Dadiani, the last ruler of Mingrelia, which holds what is said to be the largest ballroom in Georgia. A court church and the botanical garden complete the ensemble.

The palaces have the character of prosperous 19th-century European aristocratic residences rather than a grand imperial palace, which gives them an approachable, human scale. The decorated halls, ceilings, and surviving period furnishings convey how the Dadiani household lived at the height of its prosperity and cultural ambition — and the walled estate, with its private park set between the palaces and the city, is itself an expression of aristocratic separation from the town outside.

The collection

The museum's collection is far richer than its modest tourism profile suggests — around 45,000 items spanning thousands of years, from the history of the Dadiani family and Samegrelo to archaeology, fine and applied art, weapons, manuscripts, and sacred objects. It includes archaeological material from Nokalakevi (the ancient site of Archaeopolis in Samegrelo), medieval European armor and weapons, goldwork, numismatics, and a notable portrait and painting collection. But two pieces draw visitors above all others.

The death mask of Napoleon Bonaparte is the museum's most discussed treasure — a bronze mask, one of only a few known to exist (the others held in France and England). Its presence in a provincial western Georgian palace is no accident of "Paris contacts" but a genuine inheritance: Salome Dadiani — daughter of David Dadiani and Ekaterine Chavchavadze — married Achille Murat, a descendant of Napoleon's family (the Murats, into whom Napoleon's sister had married). Through that marriage the Dadianis inherited a remarkable trove of Napoleonic relics by right of succession — the death mask, personal effects and furniture of the Emperor, and even part of Napoleon's personal library, still held by the museum. It is a thoroughly improbable Napoleonic collection in the western Caucasus, and it speaks eloquently to the European reach of the 19th-century Georgian aristocracy.

The second great treasure is a garment traditionally believed to have been worn by the Virgin Mary — a robe (or shroud) venerated as a Christian relic. By tradition it was brought to western Georgia after the fall of Constantinople in 1453, among other sacred objects carried out of the Byzantine world, and it has long been associated with the Dadiani family. It is kept in storage (in the so-called Virgin's Tower) and, by tradition, shown to the public once a year, in August.

Beyond these, the weapons collection — more than 200 swords, pistols, and firearms, much of it Georgian, Ottoman, Persian, and European, gathered as war booty or received as gifts from the Russian tsars — reflects the military and diplomatic life of a principality at the crossroads of competing powers, with craftsmanship of real quality in the decorated pieces. The portraits of the Dadianis and associated figures put faces to the history the documents and objects record.

The park and botanical garden

The estate's park is one of the more pleasant green spaces in Zugdidi — now the Zugdidi Botanical Garden, around 26 hectares, laid out from the mid-19th century at the initiative of the Dadianis (Ekaterine Chavchavadze-Dadiani is credited with founding it, bringing in European gardeners and rare plants from around the world). Some of the oldest trees date from the Dadiani period and have reached a size and presence that gives the garden a settled depth younger plantings lack.

The garden is especially rewarding in spring, when the flowering trees and shrubs peak, and in early autumn, when mild weather and turning leaves make the walks particularly pleasant; in summer the mature canopy gives welcome shade, and the lushness of the Samegrelo lowland climate keeps the estate green through most of the year. It also functions as a public green space for Zugdidi's residents, and the presence of local families using it gives the estate a lived quality that purely tourist sites lack — making the Dadiani estate a place of several registers at once: cultural, historical, botanical, and simply recreational.

Visiting

  • Location: Central Zugdidi (Samegrelo–Zemo Svaneti region), western Georgia, walkable from the city's main streets.
  • Time needed: About 1–1.5 hours for the palace interior and the garden together; longer if the garden or the collection holds you.
  • Hours / admission: The palace museum is open Tuesday to Sunday, 10:00–18:00, and closed on Mondays; the botanical garden is generally freely accessible. Admission to the museum is a minor, modest fee — small enough that it needn't factor into your planning, and as it may change it is best checked locally if you want the current figure. Note that the Virgin's robe is, by tradition, displayed only once a year (in August).
  • Best time: Spring through autumn for the full experience of palace and grounds; the collection is open year-round.
  • Getting there: Zugdidi is the main city of Samegrelo and the gateway to Svaneti, reached by train and marshrutka from Tbilisi, Kutaisi, and Batumi; the palace is central.
  • Combine with: A Svaneti itinerary (Zugdidi is the staging point for Mestia), the nearby Rukhi Fortress, and the Samegrelo lowlands; an ideal substantive stop the evening before, or on return from, the mountains.

faq.title: What is the Dadiani Palace Museum?
faq.content: The Dadiani Palace Museum is a complex of 19th-century princely palaces, a court church, and a large botanical garden in central Zugdidi, western Georgia — the former seat of the Dadiani dynasty, who ruled the Principality of Samegrelo (Mingrelia). Founded as a museum in the mid-19th century by Prince David Dadiani, it's among the oldest museums in Georgia and holds around 45,000 items spanning the history of Samegrelo and the Dadiani court. Its most famous treasures are a bronze death mask of Napoleon Bonaparte and a garment traditionally believed to have belonged to the Virgin Mary. It's the most important cultural site in the Samegrelo region.

faq.title: Why is there a Napoleon death mask in Zugdidi?
faq.content: Through marriage, not coincidence. Salome Dadiani — granddaughter of the Samegrelo ruling line — married Achille Murat, a descendant of Napoleon's family (the Murats, into whom Napoleon's sister Caroline had married). Through that union the Dadianis inherited a trove of Napoleonic relics by right of succession: the bronze death mask (one of only a few in the world, with others in France and England), personal effects and furniture of the Emperor, and even part of Napoleon's personal library. It's an improbable but genuine Napoleonic collection in the western Caucasus, a testament to the European connections of the 19th-century Georgian aristocracy.

faq.title: What else is in the collection besides the Napoleon mask?
faq.content: A great deal. Alongside the mask, the museum's other celebrated treasure is a robe traditionally venerated as the Virgin Mary's, said to have been brought to western Georgia after the fall of Constantinople in 1453 and, by tradition, shown to the public only once a year, in August. The wider collection of around 45,000 items includes archaeological finds from Nokalakevi (ancient Archaeopolis), medieval European armor and a weapons collection of over 200 pieces, goldwork, manuscripts and rare books (including volumes from Napoleon's library), and a portrait and painting collection — a genuinely deep holding for a regional museum.

faq.title: Who were the Dadianis, and what's the story of the palace?
faq.content: The Dadianis ruled Samegrelo (Mingrelia) as a semi-autonomous principality for centuries, until Russian administration took control in 1857 and the principality was formally abolished in 1867. The main palace was reconstructed in the 1860s in a Neo-Gothic style by Princess Ekaterine Chavchavadze-Dadiani — daughter of the poet Alexander Chavchavadze of Tsinandali — who also founded the botanical garden; a second palace belonged to her son Niko Dadiani, the last ruler of Mingrelia. The family's ties to both Russian and European nobility give the estate its distinctive blend of Georgian and European character.

faq.title: How do you visit the Dadiani Palace Museum?
faq.content: It's in central Zugdidi, the main city of Samegrelo and the gateway to Svaneti, walkable from the main streets and reachable by train and marshrutka from Tbilisi, Kutaisi, and Batumi. The palace museum is ticketed at a modest fee, while the botanical garden is generally free to enter; it's worth confirming current hours and prices before you go. Allow about 1–1.5 hours for the palace and garden together. It makes an ideal substantive stop the evening before heading up to Svaneti, or on the way back down — the kind of cultural depth that turns a transit city into a worthwhile halt.

enguriDam.heroTitle: The Enguri Dam: Georgia's Great Arch Dam on the Road to Svaneti
enguriDam.content:
The Enguri Dam is the largest hydroelectric structure in Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — and one of the most impressive arch dams in the world: a concrete arch rising 271.5 meters above the Enguri River in the deep gorge that separates the Samegrelo lowlands from the highlands of Svaneti. It is the kind of engineering achievement that commands attention regardless of any interest in infrastructure — the scale of the structure against the landscape, the vivid turquoise of the reservoir reaching back into the mountains, and the sheer fact of a near-300-meter concrete arch spanning a mountain gorge produce an impression hard to anticipate and hard to forget. For most travelers it appears as a stop on the road between Zugdidi and Mestia, a fifteen-to-thirty-minute pause in a long mountain drive. It earns that pause fully — and its quietly remarkable backstory, as a piece of infrastructure shared across one of the region's hardest political divides, gives it more depth than its scale alone.

What is the Enguri Dam?

The Enguri Dam is a 271.5-meter concrete arch hydroelectric dam on the Enguri River in northwestern Georgia, north of the town of Jvari, on the road from Zugdidi to Svaneti. Currently the second-highest concrete arch dam in the world, it was begun in 1961, became operational in 1978, and was completed in 1987. It impounds a turquoise reservoir reaching back into the gorge, and generates a major share of Georgia's electricity. Remarkably, while the dam sits on Georgian-controlled land, its power station lies across the de facto boundary in Abkhazia, and the facility is run through rare Georgian–Abkhaz cooperation. For travelers, it's one of the most dramatic stops on the Svaneti road.

Construction and engineering

The Enguri Dam was a Soviet-era project of exceptional ambition. The idea long predated it — the Georgian public figure Niko Nikoladze (1843–1928) first identified the Enguri's hydro potential before the First World War — but construction itself began in 1961. The underground power plant became operational in 1978, while the arch was still being raised to its full height, and the whole project was completed in 1987.

The choice of an arch dam suited the site exactly: a narrow gorge with hard rock walls able to bear the lateral thrust an arch transfers to its abutments, and a height requirement that made the arch form more efficient than a gravity dam of the same capacity. Building a 271.5-meter arch dam (with a crest some 728 meters wide) in remote Caucasus terrain, without today's machinery or computation, was a formidable feat — and the dam has operated continuously since, now standing as the world's second-highest concrete arch dam and generating a major share of Georgia's electricity through a power station of around 1,300 MW. It was listed as an Immovable Cultural Monument of National Significance of Georgia in 2015.

The reservoir behind the dam — the Enguri (Jvari) Reservoir — reaches several kilometers back into the valley, its level rising and falling with the seasonal cycle of snowmelt inflow and generation demand. (The reservoir is named for the nearby town of Jvari, which sits just south of the dam and remains a working town today.)

A dam shared across a divide

What makes the Enguri Dam genuinely unusual is not only its scale but its politics. The arch dam itself stands on Georgian-controlled territory, but the power station and most of its generators lie across the de facto administrative boundary, in the Gali district of Abkhazia — the Russian-occupied breakaway region. For the facility to function at all, the two sides have to cooperate, and despite the unresolved conflict they have: the dam and power station have been kept running for decades through an informal arrangement, often by workers drawn from the same regional community on both sides of the line, with the electricity it generates shared between Abkhazia and the rest of Georgia (commonly cited at roughly 40% and 60%). It is one of the few pieces of infrastructure that continues to bind the two sides together — a fact that gives the dam a strategic and human significance well beyond its visual impact, and that has also made the security of so critical a structure a subject of periodic concern.

The visual experience

The approach from Zugdidi toward Svaneti reveals the site in stages — the gorge narrowing, the reservoir appearing above the road, and then, suddenly, the full height of the dam across the valley — one of the more dramatic moments of the Zugdidi–Mestia drive. The scale of the arch becomes fully comprehensible only when something gives it human reference: a vehicle on the road, a figure at the viewpoint. That first moment of comprehension tends to produce a genuine physical response.

The color of the reservoir is one of the most striking elements. The turquoise-green — characteristic of glacier-fed mountain reservoirs, where fine suspended mineral particles scatter light in a particular way — can be intense enough on a clear day to look almost artificial, and the contrast between the vivid water, the grey concrete of the dam face, and the green forested slopes makes a composition of real impact. The color shifts with season, weather, and light, ranging from turquoise through jade to a deeper blue-green; the most photogenic conditions are usually clear mornings, when the sun lights the water directly.

Roadside viewpoints give clear sightlines over the dam and upper reservoir with no special access required — pulling off at the designated areas for ten or fifteen minutes is the standard experience and is fully satisfying. The dam face and base are not open to general visitors, for obvious security and safety reasons, but the elevated roadside perspective arguably reveals the structure's full height better than a ground-level view would.

The gorge and the surrounding landscape

The Enguri gorge here is quite different in character from both the Samegrelo lowlands to the southwest and the wide Enguri valley of Upper Svaneti to the northeast. The river has cut deeply through the mountains, producing steep valley sides that compress the landscape and give the gorge a sense of enclosure and drama that contrasts with the openness above and below. The forested slopes dropping to the water, the rock faces where forest gives way to cliff, and the visible waterline marking the boundary between drowned and surviving land all add to the visual complexity.

The road continuing northeast into Svaneti runs alongside the reservoir for a good distance, giving travelers an extended engagement with the flooded valley — the turquoise water below and beside the road, the forested slopes rising steeply opposite, the mountains of Svaneti growing ahead. It makes one of the more scenically rewarding stretches of the Zugdidi–Mestia journey: the dam is not a single viewpoint but the start of a sustained visual engagement with the impounded valley that continues some way into the mountain approach.

Context within the journey

For most travelers the Enguri Dam is one element of the journey to or from Svaneti rather than a destination in itself — and that's the right way to take it: a significant stop worth fifteen to thirty minutes that adds a dimension of engineering, landscape, and history to one of Georgia's more scenic road journeys. The combined experience of the gorge, the dam, the reservoir, and the continued approach up the narrowing valley toward Upper Svaneti gives the roughly three-hour Zugdidi–Mestia drive a variety and interest that makes it genuinely engaging rather than mere transit.

Travelers drawn to engineering, hydroelectric systems, or Soviet-era construction can look into a guided tour of the dam, which gives access to parts of the structure not visible from the public viewpoints; a tourist route across the dam has been developed in recent years. Tours require advance arrangement rather than casual drop-in, so check current availability ahead of time. For most visitors, the public viewpoints are entirely sufficient to appreciate what the Enguri Dam represents.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: On the Enguri River, north of the town of Jvari, Tsalenjikha district, Samegrelo–Zemo Svaneti, northwestern Georgia, on the main Zugdidi–Mestia road.
  • Getting there: On the road from Zugdidi (the Svaneti gateway) toward Mestia; the dam and the reservoir-side road are passed by all traffic heading up to Svaneti. No special access needed for the roadside viewpoints.
  • Time needed: 15–30 minutes at the viewpoints (longer if you take a guided tour).
  • Best time: Clear mornings for the most vivid reservoir color.
  • Combine with: The Zugdidi–Mestia journey and Upper Svaneti (Mestia, Ushguli); the Dadiani Palace in Zugdidi at the start of the drive.

faq.title: What is the Enguri Dam?
faq.content: The Enguri Dam is a 271.5-meter concrete arch hydroelectric dam on the Enguri River in northwestern Georgia, north of the town of Jvari, on the road from Zugdidi to Svaneti. It's currently the second-highest concrete arch dam in the world. Begun in 1961, operational from 1978, and completed in 1987, it impounds a vivid turquoise reservoir reaching back into the mountain gorge and generates a major share of Georgia's electricity. For travelers, it's one of the most dramatic stops on the drive up to Svaneti — and it has a genuinely remarkable backstory as infrastructure shared across the Georgian–Abkhaz divide.

faq.title: How tall is the Enguri Dam, and where does it rank?
faq.content: It stands 271.5 meters (891 feet) high, with a crest about 728 meters wide, making it currently the second-highest concrete arch dam in the world (and among the highest dams of any type). When it was completed it was, for a time, the tallest arch dam on Earth. Building a structure of this height in a remote Caucasus gorge during the Soviet era — without modern machinery or computing — was a formidable engineering achievement, and it has operated continuously ever since.

faq.title: Why is the Enguri Dam connected to Abkhazia?
faq.content: This is the dam's most unusual feature. The arch dam itself sits on Georgian-controlled territory, but the power station and most of its generators lie across the de facto administrative boundary, in the Gali district of Abkhazia, the Russian-occupied breakaway region. Because neither side can run the facility alone, the two have cooperated to keep it operating for decades despite the unresolved conflict — often with workers from the same regional community on both sides — and the electricity is shared between Abkhazia and the rest of Georgia (commonly cited as roughly 40% and 60%). It's one of the few pieces of infrastructure that still binds the two sides together.

faq.title: Was a village flooded to build the Enguri Dam?
faq.content: The reservoir is sometimes called the Jvari Reservoir after the nearby town of Jvari, but the town itself was not submerged — the dam is located just north of Jvari, which remains a working town today. The reservoir reaches several kilometers back into the Enguri valley above the dam, and its level rises and falls with the seasonal cycle of snowmelt and electricity demand. (Large Soviet hydro projects did often flood communities, but at Enguri the town of Jvari that gives the reservoir its name still stands.)

faq.title: Can you visit the Enguri Dam, and how?
faq.content: Yes — and for most people it's an easy roadside stop on the Zugdidi–Mestia drive, since the road to Svaneti passes the dam and runs alongside the reservoir. Designated roadside viewpoints give clear views of the full height of the dam and the turquoise water, needing no special access; 15–30 minutes is enough. The dam face and base aren't open to general visitors for security reasons, but for those especially interested in the engineering, guided tours of the structure have been developed in recent years (a tourist route across the dam now exists) — these need advance arrangement, so check current availability before relying on one.

intsraWaterfall.heroTitle: Intsra Waterfall: A Quiet Forest Walk in Samegrelo
intsraWaterfall.content:
The Intsra Waterfall is a roughly 26-meter waterfall on the Intsra River, hidden in the forested mountains of the Samegrelo region of western Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — near the village of Chkvaleri in the Tsalenjikha municipality. It is not one of Georgia's headline sights, and it doesn't pretend to be: it's a pretty, little-known waterfall at the end of a short, signposted forest walk, reached by those looking to get away from the crowds rather than to tick off a famous landmark. The water drops between boxwood groves and moss-covered rock into a pool below, in a setting of dense Colchic forest that draws the attention inward and downward. For travelers with a free half-day around Zugdidi who'd rather walk quietly through the woods than queue at a developed attraction, Intsra is a rewarding, low-key escape.

What is the Intsra Waterfall?

The Intsra Waterfall is a roughly 26-meter forest waterfall on the Intsra River near the village of Chkvaleri, in the Tsalenjikha municipality of Samegrelo, western Georgia. It cascades between boxwood groves and moss-covered vertical rock, with a small cave nearby from which a spring emerges. Reached by a short, well-signed walk through the Intsra valley (the falls lie just a couple of kilometers from the village), it's a little-known, uncrowded spot rather than a major sight — best visited as an easy half-day from Zugdidi, or as the first stop on the longer two-day trek up Mount Kvira.

The waterfall and its setting

Intsra is a modest but genuinely pretty waterfall — the water falling some 26 meters down a vertical, moss-darkened rock face, breaking into spray as it goes and gathering in a pool at the base before continuing downstream. What gives it its particular character is the setting: it drops between boxwood (box tree) groves and moss-covered rock, deep in the Colchic forest that covers these slopes, so the fall is the focal point of an enclosed green world rather than a feature in a wide panorama. The forest here is layer upon layer of vegetation, lush and dense in the way of a landscape that sees rain through much of the year, and the sound of the water grows steadily as you approach through the trees.

Near the waterfall is the Intsra cave, from which a spring emerges; its water is channelled through pipes laid along the path, which double as a useful guide leading you toward the falls. The flow is strongest in spring and early summer, when snowmelt supplements the rainfall and the fall is at its fullest and loudest; later in summer it slims down, though the setting keeps its appeal. Spring, summer, and autumn are all good times to visit — spring for the strongest flow and vivid green, autumn for the color of the deciduous trees among the Colchic evergreens.

The walk

Getting to Intsra is a short and mostly straightforward hike, not a wilderness expedition. The trail begins at the village of Chkvaleri, following a dirt road at first and then a forested valley, and it's well signposted — there's plenty of signage to keep you on track. After about 3 km you reach a junction: the right fork leads a few hundred meters through the woods to the waterfall (the left continues up toward Mount Kvira). Done as a loop, the whole walk is about 7.5 km and takes roughly 2–3 hours in total; the waterfall itself is only around 1.6 km from the village, so it's a genuinely accessible outing that most reasonably able adults can manage.

There is one real caution: toward the end, the path has some narrow, exposed sections above the river, and the final approach involves a steep stretch with some scrambling over large boulders. It's not technical, but it calls for good shoes with grip and a bit of care — more so after rain, when the forest soil turns slippery. Unlike the genuinely remote treks of the region, this one is short, signed, and commonly done independently, though local guides in Zugdidi can be arranged if you'd prefer company or current trail information.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: Near Chkvaleri village, Tsalenjikha municipality, Samegrelo (Samegrelo–Zemo Svaneti region), western Georgia, in the valley of the Intsra River.
  • Getting there: Zugdidi is the practical base. There's no direct bus to Chkvaleri, so the simplest option is a taxi from Zugdidi (around 40–50 GEL); alternatively, take a marshrutka toward Jvari/Mestia and a taxi onward from Jvari (around 20 GEL). A vehicle with reasonable ground clearance helps on the smaller roads near the village.
  • The walk: From Chkvaleri, a signed trail of roughly 1.6 km to the falls (or a ~7.5 km loop); 2–3 hours total. Good footwear recommended for the exposed, scrambly final section.
  • Best time: Spring through autumn — spring/early summer for the fullest flow, autumn for forest color. Avoid the path right after heavy rain, when it gets slippery.
  • Combine with: The longer two-day Chkvaleri–Kvira trek (Intsra Waterfall, the Kuakantsalia "swinging rock," and the summit of Mount Kvira, 2,038 m); and the wider Samegrelo sights — the Martvili Canyon and the taller Martvili-area waterfalls (Toba, Oniore, Ochkhomuri), Zugdidi and the Dadiani Palace, and the road to Svaneti.

faq.title: What is the Intsra Waterfall?
faq.content: The Intsra Waterfall is a roughly 26-meter forest waterfall on the Intsra River near the village of Chkvaleri, in the Tsalenjikha municipality of Samegrelo, western Georgia. It falls between boxwood groves and moss-covered rock, with a small cave nearby from which a spring emerges. It's a little-known, uncrowded spot rather than a major sight — reached by a short, well-signed walk through the Intsra valley — best enjoyed as an easy half-day from Zugdidi or as the first stop on the longer two-day trek up Mount Kvira.

faq.title: How tall is the Intsra Waterfall?
faq.content: About 26 meters. It's a pretty, modest waterfall rather than one of Georgia's giants — worth being clear about, since it's sometimes confused with the much taller waterfalls elsewhere in Samegrelo. (The region's genuinely tall falls are in Martvili municipality: Toba, at 234 m, is the highest cascading waterfall in Georgia, with Ochkhomuri at around 100–120 m and Oniore at 67 m.) Intsra's appeal isn't its height but its quiet, green forest setting and the pleasant short walk to reach it.

faq.title: How hard is the hike to Intsra Waterfall?
faq.content: It's short and mostly straightforward — a signposted trail from Chkvaleri village, following a dirt road and then a forested valley, with the waterfall only about 1.6 km from the village (or a roughly 7.5 km loop, 2–3 hours total). Most reasonably able adults can do it. The one caveat is the final stretch: there are some narrow, exposed sections above the river and a steep bit with scrambling over boulders, so good shoes with grip are genuinely useful, especially after rain when the path gets slippery. It's commonly done without a guide, though one can be arranged from Zugdidi.

faq.title: How do you get to the Intsra Waterfall?
faq.content: The base is Zugdidi. There's no direct bus to Chkvaleri village, so the easiest way is a taxi from Zugdidi (around 40–50 GEL); alternatively, take a marshrutka toward Jvari (on the Mestia road) and a taxi onward from there (around 20 GEL). From Chkvaleri, follow the signed trail through the Intsra valley to the falls. A vehicle with reasonable ground clearance helps on the smaller roads near the village.

faq.title: What else can you combine with the Intsra Waterfall?
faq.content: Intsra is the first highlight of a popular two-day trek from Chkvaleri that continues to the Kuakantsalia "swinging rock" — a 10–12 tonne boulder that rocks when pushed — and on to the summit of Mount Kvira (2,038 m), with sweeping views over mountainous Samegrelo. For a shorter trip, pair the waterfall walk with Samegrelo's other sights: the Martvili Canyon and the taller Martvili-area waterfalls (Toba, Oniore, Ochkhomuri), the Dadiani Palace in Zugdidi, and the Enguri Dam on the road up to Svaneti.

kolkhetiNationalPark.heroTitle: Kolkheti National Park: Georgia's Black Sea Wetlands and the Land of the Golden Fleece
kolkhetiNationalPark.content:
Kolkheti National Park protects one of the most ecologically significant and visually distinctive landscapes in Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus: the ancient wetland system of the eastern Black Sea coast, centered on Lake Paliastomi and a network of channels, marshes, reed beds, and Colchic forest spread across a flat, water-dominated terrain quite unlike anywhere else in the country. Established in 1998–1999 and covering roughly 289 km² (extending to about 337 km² with its protected wetlands) across the coastal lowland of Samegrelo and Guria near the port city of Poti, it preserves the surviving remnant of the Colchic wetlands that once covered far more of the Black Sea plain. What remains is internationally recognized — a Ramsar Wetland of International Importance, and since 2021 a UNESCO World Heritage Site (as part of the Colchic Rainforests and Wetlands) — supporting an extraordinary concentration of birdlife and ancient plant communities that have survived here since before the last ice age.

What is Kolkheti National Park?

Kolkheti National Park is a coastal wetland and forest national park on Georgia's Black Sea coast near Poti, spanning the Samegrelo and Guria regions. Established in 1998–1999 and covering about 289 km² (337 km² with protected wetlands), it centers on Lake Paliastomi and a maze of reed-lined channels, with relict Colchic rainforest along the rivers. It's a Ramsar wetland and, since 2021, a UNESCO World Heritage Site (within the Colchic Rainforests and Wetlands), internationally important for migratory birds. Explored mainly by boat — along Paliastomi and the Pichori River, the "Georgian Amazon" — it offers a flat, water-level landscape and birdwatching found nowhere else in the country.

A landscape steeped in the Golden Fleece

The park's name reaches into one of the oldest layers of Georgian identity. Kolkheti — Colchis in Greek — was the ancient kingdom of the eastern Black Sea coast, the land that appears in Greek myth as the destination of Jason and the Argonauts in their quest for the Golden Fleece, and these wetlands and forests are the very landscape those ancient accounts described. The connection even survives in a familiar bird's name: the common pheasant, Phasianus colchicus, takes its name from Colchis and the river Phasis (the Rioni) — by tradition, the Argonauts carried the pheasant from here to Europe. The reed beds, river channels, and dense Colchic forest are thus not only an ecological resource but a geography with one of the longest documented histories of any landscape in the Caucasus, and a visit carries that resonance alongside its natural interest.

The landscape

The terrain is defined by water in all its forms — lake, river, channel, marsh, and the brackish transition where fresh water meets the Black Sea. Lake Paliastomi, the largest water body in the park (around 18 km²), connects to the sea through a channel, forming a semi-enclosed coastal lagoon whose chemistry and ecology reflect that dual influence; its surface lies calm and reflective in settled weather, the surrounding reed beds rising from the shallows as nesting habitat for an exceptional range of waterbirds. Beyond the lake, the channels threading the wetland are narrower and enclosed, reeds forming walls on both sides and the sky reduced to a strip above — an intimacy that open water cannot give.

The Colchic forest edging and interpenetrating the wetland is among the more botanically significant plant communities in Georgia. Characterized by species such as Colchic ivy, cherry laurel, Caucasian wing-nut, Pontic rhododendron, alder, and Colchic boxwood — plants with distinctly subtropical affinities — it has survived in the western Caucasus lowlands since before the last glaciation, when the mild coastal climate allowed species to persist here that ice-age conditions eliminated from most of Europe. These are genuinely ancient (relict) plant communities, and a well-preserved Colchic woodland — dense, layered, perpetually green, humid in the way of a high-rainfall coastal forest — has a primordial richness unlike the younger forests of most of the continent.

The flatness of the park, so unusual in mostly-mountainous Georgia, creates a different relationship between observer and environment from the country's usual vertical, elevated landscapes. The scale here is horizontal — distances measured across open water and reed beds rather than up mountain faces — and moving through it by boat, at water level, surrounded by vegetation with a large sky above, engages the senses in a fundamentally different way from a mountain trail or a canyon viewpoint.

The wildlife

Kolkheti's position on the eastern Black Sea coast places it at a major node on the migratory flyways linking the breeding grounds of northern and eastern Europe and western Siberia with the wintering areas of Africa and the Middle East — the park ranks among the most important bird-migration sites in the region. Hundreds of thousands of birds pass through or pause in the wetlands during spring and autumn migration, and the diversity and volume at peak times make it one of the most rewarding birdwatching destinations in Georgia and one of the more significant in the wider Caucasus.

The wetlands support breeding herons, egrets, cormorants, and various ducks and waders through the warmer months. Dalmatian pelicans — among the more charismatic and globally threatened large waterbirds — use the lake and its surroundings, giving the park particular ornithological significance, and other notable species include black stork, crane, and great white egret. Raptors, including eagles and harriers, hunt over the open water and reed beds, while the transition zones between water, reed, and forest support a different suite of species, and the Colchic woodland adds woodland and passerine diversity — a breadth across habitats that single-habitat wetland reserves cannot match. The adjacent Black Sea is also home to dolphins, sometimes seen offshore.

Spring migration (March–May) brings the largest volume and diversity of passage birds and coincides with resident breeding, making April and May the most active and rewarding months. Autumn migration (August–October) brings a second peak with a different species mix. Summer is quieter for migration but the breeding birds are active and the Colchic vegetation at its lushest. Winter brings wintering waterfowl that favor the mild coastal climate.

Boat tours

The primary — and most distinctive — way to experience Kolkheti is by boat. Small craft (pontoon, motorboat, or kayak) navigate the channels threading the reed beds and connect Lake Paliastomi to the wider wetland, reaching habitat inaccessible on foot. The classic route runs along Paliastomi and up the Pichori River, the forest-lined waterway often called the "Georgian Amazon." Moving at water level, reeds rising on both sides, the sounds of the wetland all around — wind in the reeds, bird calls, water against the hull — is an immersive, sensory experience quite unlike the panoramic mountain and canyon sights elsewhere in Georgia, and the slow pace lets you notice the detail: birds moving through the reed stems, the plants at the water's edge, the water itself.

A boat is also the most productive way to birdwatch here, since a small craft moving quietly disturbs waterbirds far less than a walker would. Herons and egrets at the water's edge, ducks between reed-bed openings, the occasional rarer species in the vegetation — these are the rewards of a patient channel journey, and the park's guides know the seasonal patterns and the reliable spots far better than independent navigation would allow. (Sport fishing is permitted on the Pichori, and there are also eco-trails on foot, such as the Churia River trail from the visitor center, for those who want to walk the Colchic forest.)

Poti and access

The park is most easily reached from Poti, the main port city of western Georgia at the mouth of the Rioni River, on the park's doorstep. Poti connects by road to Kutaisi, Zugdidi, and Batumi, and the visitor center and main boat-tour departure points are a short drive from the city. Poti itself is Georgia's principal commercial port — an industrial and logistical center rather than a tourist town — but it provides the accommodation and services needed to organize a visit, even if its character is firmly practical.

The park's visitor infrastructure, while modest by the standards of well-funded European parks, covers the essentials: a visitor center with information on the ecology and tour options, ticketing for entry and boat excursions, and the guides needed to navigate the channels. Booking boat tours ahead is advisable in the peak spring and autumn migration seasons, and it's worth confirming current tour availability with the park administration before planning a visit, since the boat trip is central to the experience.

When to visit

  • Spring (especially April–May): the best overall — peak migration, breeding activity, and the Colchic vegetation at its freshest.
  • Autumn (September–October): the second migration peak, a different species mix, and fine light over the water.
  • Summer: the lushest vegetation and most reliably warm boating weather, but the lowest migration activity, and July–August heat and humidity on the coastal lowland can be considerable.
  • Winter: wintering waterfowl and off-season quiet, with a more austere character — rewarding for those after wintering species.

faq.title: What is Kolkheti National Park?
faq.content: Kolkheti National Park is a coastal wetland and forest national park on Georgia's Black Sea coast near Poti, spanning the Samegrelo and Guria regions. Established in 1998–1999 and covering about 289 km² (around 337 km² including its protected wetlands), it centers on Lake Paliastomi and a maze of reed-lined channels, with relict Colchic rainforest along the rivers. It's a Ramsar Wetland of International Importance and, since 2021, a UNESCO World Heritage Site as part of the Colchic Rainforests and Wetlands. Explored mainly by boat, it's internationally important for migratory birds and offers a flat, water-level landscape found nowhere else in Georgia.

faq.title: Is Kolkheti National Park a UNESCO World Heritage Site?
faq.content: Yes. In July 2021, Kolkheti was inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List as part of the "Colchic Rainforests and Wetlands," together with the Kintrishi, Kobuleti, and Mtirala protected areas — Georgia's first natural World Heritage site. The wetlands are also a Ramsar Wetland of International Importance (designated in the 1990s). These recognitions reflect the park's global significance: a rare surviving Colchic wetland-and-rainforest ecosystem, with relict plant communities that have persisted since before the last ice age and an extraordinary richness of birdlife.

faq.title: What is there to do in Kolkheti National Park?
faq.content: The signature experience is a boat tour — typically across Lake Paliastomi and up the Pichori River, a forest-lined waterway often called the "Georgian Amazon" — gliding at water level through reed channels and Colchic forest, with excellent birdwatching along the way. Birdwatching is the park's main draw, especially during the spring and autumn migrations. There's also sport fishing on the Pichori (by permit) and walking eco-trails through the Colchic forest, such as the Churia River trail from the visitor center. Boats range from pontoons to motorboats and kayaks; booking ahead is wise in peak season.

faq.title: When is the best time to visit Kolkheti for birdwatching?
faq.content: April and May are the most rewarding, combining the peak spring migration with the breeding activity of resident birds and the freshest Colchic greenery. Autumn (September–October) brings a second migration peak with a different mix of species and lovely light over the water. Summer is lush and warm but quieter for migration (and hot and humid in July–August), while winter brings wintering waterfowl and off-season calm. Hundreds of thousands of birds pass through on the African–Eurasian flyway, and species to look for include the globally threatened Dalmatian pelican, herons, egrets, black stork, and cranes.

faq.title: How do you get to Kolkheti National Park?
faq.content: The easiest base is Poti, the Black Sea port city right beside the park, connected by road to Kutaisi, Zugdidi, and Batumi; the visitor center and boat-tour departure points are a short drive away. Poti is a working port rather than a tourist town, but it has the accommodation and services to organize a visit. Because the boat tour is the heart of the experience, it's worth booking ahead in the busy spring and autumn migration seasons and confirming current tour availability with the park administration before you go.

martviliCanyon.heroTitle: Martvili Canyon: Georgia's Turquoise Limestone Gorge
martviliCanyon.content:
Martvili Canyon is one of the most visited natural sites in Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — and, by wide agreement among travelers who have covered the country, one of the most immediately impressive. A few kilometers outside the town of Martvili in the Samegrelo region, about an hour's drive northwest of Kutaisi, it is where the Abasha River has cut through a plateau of Jurassic limestone over millions of years to produce a deep, striking gorge. The combination of turquoise-green water, sheer walls draped in dense subtropical vegetation, and waterfalls dropping into the gorge along the route creates a scene that consistently exceeds expectations — one of those rare sites where the photographs, however good, genuinely undersell the experience. Best of all, it's easy: a short walkway and a gentle boat ride deliver the drama with almost no effort.

What is Martvili Canyon?

Martvili Canyon is a deep limestone gorge cut by the Abasha River in the Samegrelo region of western Georgia, about an hour from Kutaisi, near the town of Martvili. Famous for its vivid turquoise water, vegetation-draped walls, and small waterfalls, it's explored in two parts: a short rim walkway with viewpoints over the gorge, and a roughly 20–30 minute boat ride along the canyon floor through its narrowest, most enclosed stretch. Easy for all fitness levels and one of western Georgia's most popular natural attractions, it pairs naturally with the nearby Okatse Canyon and the 7th-century Martvili Monastery in town.

The geology and the water

The turquoise color of the Abasha River is the first thing most visitors notice and the detail in nearly every photograph — ranging from clear aquamarine in the shallows to deeper jade where the water is calm and deep. The color comes from the limestone geology: limestone terrain filters groundwater through porous rock over long periods, producing water of exceptional clarity with a light-scattering quality that gives such rivers their characteristic hue in the right conditions. The karst landscapes of Imereti and Samegrelo produce several rivers with similar properties — the nearby Okatse Canyon runs with comparable water — but the depth of the Martvili gorge, the density of its vegetation, and its specific proportions make the effect here particularly intense.

The canyon walls are overhung with vegetation rooted in every crack and ledge — ferns, moss, flowering plants, and the trailing roots of trees on the rim above. That lushness reflects the high rainfall of Samegrelo, one of the wettest parts of Georgia, and gives the walls a living-green quality impossible to sustain in a drier climate. In the right light — morning sun angling into the gorge, or the flat diffuse light of an overcast day that erases harsh shadows — the contrast between the green walls and the turquoise water below is extraordinary.

The walking route

The visit's upper portion follows a path along the canyon rim and a series of wooden walkways cantilevered over the gorge edge, giving views down into the canyon and access to viewpoints over the main waterfall sections. The path is well maintained, the walkways sturdy and railed, and the full route short enough to complete comfortably in thirty to forty minutes. Several viewpoints overlook the most dramatic stretches — where the walls narrow and the waterfalls drop most visibly — and these are where the canyon's scale and the intensity of the water color read most clearly.

The waterfalls vary with season and recent rainfall. In spring and early summer, when snowmelt and the heavy western-Georgian wet season drive the river at full flow, they are at their most powerful — white water dropping through green vegetation into the turquoise pool below, the sound carrying up the walls. In late summer and autumn the flow eases, the falls grow more delicate, and the water often turns even more crystalline as the suspended sediment settles. Both versions reward a visit, but spring and autumn offer genuinely distinct experiences worth understanding before you plan.

The boat ride

The boat ride is what most people come for and remember longest. Small flat-bottomed wooden boats, each carrying a few passengers and steered by a local oarsman, navigate a section of the canyon floor through the narrowest, most enclosed part of the gorge, where the walls rise steeply on both sides and the sky narrows to a strip of light overhead. The ride covers roughly a kilometer at water level, taking about twenty to thirty minutes at a pace that lets the enclosed gorge fully register.

At water level the canyon reveals dimensions the rim walkway can't show. The height of the walls becomes apparent only when you're sitting at the bottom looking up — rock rising from the waterline, vegetation overhanging from ledges, the narrow aperture of sky at the top. Waterfalls that looked like white threads from above become substantial cascades from below, some dropping straight into the water beside the boat and sending spray across the passengers. And the water color, seen over the side rather than from above, has a depth and clarity that photographs consistently struggle to capture.

The boat ride is ticketed separately from the walkway and operates subject to river conditions — water too high after heavy rain, or too low in dry periods, can suspend it. It's worth checking availability on arrival, and buying the combined ticket (walkway + boat) for the complete experience. The ride is the element that most clearly earns the canyon its reputation; visiting without it — while still worthwhile for the walkway alone — misses what's genuinely unique here.

Combining with other sites

Martvili's position makes it a natural part of several itineraries. The most common pairing is with Okatse Canyon, roughly 40 km southeast toward Kutaisi — the two complement each other well, different in character despite sharing a karst origin, and a full day covering both (with a car) gives a comprehensive picture of western Georgia's canyon landscape. Martvili town itself adds a cultural dimension: the Martvili Monastery (Chkondidi) sits on a hill above the town — its main cathedral built in the late 7th century on the site of a sacred oak (the Mingrelian name Chkondidi means "big oak"), rebuilt in the 10th century, and long a major medieval cultural and educational center, with 14th–17th-century frescoes inside. The broader Samegrelo region — quieter and less tourism-developed than Imereti — rewards the extra exploration that using Martvili as a base, rather than a day trip, allows.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: A few km outside Martvili town, Martvili municipality, Samegrelo (Samegrelo–Zemo Svaneti), western Georgia; about 1 hour (northwest) from Kutaisi.
  • Getting there: The drive from Kutaisi takes about an hour on a paved road in reasonable condition, through the mixed farmland and forest of the Rioni lowlands into the hillier Samegrelo terrain. Without a car, a taxi from Kutaisi is the most practical option — many travelers arrange for the driver to wait during the visit rather than seeking return transport from the site.
  • Facilities: Car park, ticket office, and basic facilities at the entrance; the infrastructure handles high-summer volumes competently.
  • Time needed: Around 1.5–2 hours for the walkway and the boat ride together.
  • Best time: Spring (April–June) for the fullest flow and most powerful falls; autumn (September–October) for the clearest water and fewer crowds; summer is busiest — arrive early to beat the midday boat queues. Year-round access, though the boat ride may pause in winter depending on water conditions.

faq.title: What is Martvili Canyon?
faq.content: Martvili Canyon is a deep limestone gorge cut by the Abasha River in the Samegrelo region of western Georgia, about an hour northwest of Kutaisi near the town of Martvili. It's famous for its vivid turquoise-green water, sheer walls draped in dense vegetation, and small waterfalls. The visit has two parts: a short rim walkway with viewpoints over the gorge, and a boat ride along the canyon floor through its narrowest, most enclosed stretch. Easy for all fitness levels, it's one of western Georgia's most popular natural attractions.

faq.title: What is the Martvili Canyon boat ride like?
faq.content: It's the highlight for most visitors. Small flat-bottomed wooden boats, steered by a local oarsman, carry a few passengers along about a kilometer of the canyon floor through its narrowest section, where the walls rise steeply and the sky narrows to a strip overhead — about 20–30 minutes. From water level you grasp the true height of the walls, waterfalls drop into the water beside the boat (sometimes spraying passengers), and the turquoise color is at its most vivid. The boat ride is ticketed separately from the walkway and runs subject to river conditions — it can be suspended when the water is too high after heavy rain or too low in dry spells — so it's worth checking availability on arrival.

faq.title: How long do you need at Martvili Canyon, and is it difficult?
faq.content: Allow about 1.5–2 hours for both the walkway and the boat ride. It's easy: the rim walkway and its cantilevered viewing platforms take only 30–40 minutes and are railed and well maintained, and the boat ride needs no effort beyond stepping into the boat. That combination of high visual impact and low physical demand is a big part of why it's so popular and suitable for all fitness levels and ages.

faq.title: When is the best time to visit Martvili Canyon?
faq.content: Spring (April–June) brings the fullest river flow and most powerful waterfalls, with the vegetation at its greenest. Autumn (September–October) is quieter, with the water at its clearest as the sediment settles and a warmer light on the walls. Summer is the busiest and can mean queues for the boat at midday, so arrive early in July and August. The canyon is open year-round, though the boat ride may be suspended in winter depending on water levels.

faq.title: What can you combine with Martvili Canyon?
faq.content: The classic pairing is the Okatse Canyon, about 40 km southeast toward Kutaisi — different in character but a natural companion, and the two make a full canyon day with a car. In Martvili town itself, the Martvili Monastery (Chkondidi), with its late-7th-century cathedral on a hill above town, adds a cultural stop. More widely, Martvili is a good base for the quieter Samegrelo region — the Toba, Oniore, and Ochkhomuri waterfalls, the Balda area, and beyond — and fits into a western-Georgia itinerary alongside Kutaisi and the road toward Svaneti.

martviliMonastery.heroTitle: Martvili Monastery (Chkondidi): The Great-Oak Cathedral of Samegrelo
martviliMonastery.content:
Martvili Monastery — the Martvili-Chkondidi Cathedral — stands on the highest hill at the edge of the town of Martvili, in the Samegrelo region of western Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus), its walls and tower visible across the surrounding countryside. The commanding hilltop is exactly the kind of site Georgian church builders sought, and here it carried an older significance too: the hill was a pagan sacred place before it was a Christian one. A working monastery of genuine depth, founded in the 7th century, Chkondidi was once among the most important religious and intellectual centers in all of western Georgia — its bishop a figure of national power, its scriptorium a center of medieval Georgian learning. For most travelers it appears as the companion stop to the nearby Martvili Canyon, and the two pair beautifully; but approached on its own terms, the monastery rewards far more than the usual twenty minutes.

What is Martvili Monastery?

Martvili Monastery (the Martvili-Chkondidi Cathedral) is an active Georgian Orthodox monastery on a hill above the town of Martvili, in the Samegrelo-Zemo Svaneti region of western Georgia. Its main cross-domed cathedral was built in the late 7th century on the roots of a giant oak that had been a pagan cult site — the name "Chkondidi" means "great oak" in Mingrelian, and "Martvili" derives from the word for martyr. Rebuilt in the 10th century, it became one of medieval Georgia's foremost church and educational centers; its bishop, the "Chkondideli," often served as the kingdom's chief minister, and the most famous, Giorgi Chkondideli, was mentor to King David the Builder. It preserves 14th–17th-century frescoes, offers wide views over the Samegrelo countryside, and pairs naturally with the Martvili Canyon a short distance away.

History: the great oak, St. Andrew, and the Chkondideli

The hill's story begins before Christianity. By tradition, the summit held an enormous ancient oak — chkoni in Mingrelian — worshipped as an idol of fertility and prosperity, with a grim legend of human sacrifice attached to it. When Christianity reached western Georgia, the tradition holds, St. Andrew the First-Called (credited with bringing the faith to Samegrelo) had the sacred oak cut down, and a church was raised on its roots. From that giant oak comes the cathedral's enduring name: Chkondidi, from didi chkoni — "great oak." The place-name Martvili, meanwhile, derives from the word for martyr (a Church of the Martyrs once stood here). This pattern — planting the most important early churches on the most powerful pre-Christian sites — was common across the early Christian world, and it gave Chkondidi a depth of sacred association from the start.

The first church was built in the late 7th century. After it fell into disrepair through successive invasions, it was rebuilt in the 10th century by King Giorgi II, who made Martvili-Chkondidi an episcopal center — and from there its importance grew far beyond the local. The bishopric of Chkondidi became one of the highest offices in the medieval Georgian kingdom: the title "Chkondideli" was fused with that of mtsignobartukhutsesi — the king's chief minister and chancellor — so that the bishop of this Samegrelo cathedral was, for long periods, effectively the kingdom's first minister. The most celebrated of them, Giorgi Chkondideli, was the mentor, chancellor, and trusted right hand of King David IV the Builder — remembered as the king's "companion on all roads." The cathedral was also a major center of learning: a scriptorium where monks translated and copied books and produced original works, and the home or chair of a long line of distinguished scholars (the various Chkondideli) across the centuries. King Bagrat IV is, by the Georgian Chronicles, buried here.

The difficult history of western Georgia — Arab and Ottoman incursions, Persian campaigns, the disruptions of the post-medieval centuries — left their mark, and the complex was damaged and restored repeatedly; what stands today is a composite of periods rather than a single statement. The monastery fell silent in the Soviet era and was reopened in 1998 under Patriarch Ilia II; a monastic community maintains it now.

The complex

The monastery occupies the hilltop within a wall that defines the precinct and gives the approach a real sense of arrival. Inside are the main cross-domed cathedral, a smaller two-story 10th-century church (sometimes called the Chikvani or Holy Nativity church) to the north, a bell tower to the west, ancillary buildings, and an open courtyard. The scale is modest — this is not one of Georgia's vast medieval foundations — but the quality of the setting and the weight of accumulated history give it a character that size alone doesn't explain.

The main cathedral is a central cross-domed church in the early Georgian tradition, its stone weathered to warm Samegrelo tones. The interior keeps fragments of fresco painting from the 14th–17th centuries — the full original scheme hasn't survived, but the surviving passages (the technique of the figure painting, the color where it remains, scenes including Old Testament subjects) indicate decoration worthy of so important a see, and reward close attention from anyone interested in medieval Georgian religious art.

The courtyard has the peaceful, unhurried quality of an active Georgian monastery that sees relatively few visitors. The tended grounds (the site is kept almost like a park, with lawns and flowerbeds), the functioning buildings, and the small signs of daily religious life give it a lived character that preserved-but-empty sites can't match. The sense of continuity across some fourteen centuries — from the 7th-century foundation to the community here today — is one of the most tangible impressions a careful visit leaves.

The views

The hilltop that made the site valuable to its founders is one of its best qualities for visitors. From the grounds, the views reach across the Samegrelo countryside — the Abasha and Tskhenistskali river valleys below, forested hills rolling away, the green agricultural land and woodland that give western Georgia its particular character, with Imereti beyond. It's the same Abasha River system that, a few kilometers downstream, carves the Martvili Canyon — so the monastery hill gives a geographical context to the canyon visit. In clear weather, with the Samegrelo green at its most saturated, the view is beautiful in an understated, pastoral way quite unlike the dramatic mountain panoramas of the highlands.

The late afternoon is especially good — the low sun lighting the valley below while the monastery walls catch the warm tones of the declining day, the midday visitors gone, the complex quiet. It suits both the views and the unhurried attention the place rewards, and it's the most photogenic time for the exterior.

Combining with the Martvili Canyon

The monastery and the Martvili Canyon sit a short distance apart, connected by the local roads, which makes them natural companions in a half- or full-day. The contrast is the point: the canyon is physically active, visually intense, focused on natural spectacle; the monastery is quiet, historical, architectural and artistic — each gives something the other doesn't, and the pairing makes both more vivid. A good sequence is to start at the monastery in the morning, when the light is good and the canyon crowds haven't yet peaked, then move to the canyon for the boat ride and walkway later in the morning — which also gives the monastery the unhurried time its character deserves.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: On the hill above the town of Martvili, Martvili municipality, Samegrelo-Zemo Svaneti, western Georgia.
  • Getting there: Reached by the same roads that serve the Martvili Canyon, with no special transport needed beyond whatever brings you to the Martvili area (commonly from Kutaisi, about an hour away, or another western-Georgia base). You can reach the monastery by car, on foot, or — pleasantly — by the funicular that runs up from the town.
  • Hours / admission: Open daily during daylight hours; free. As an active monastery, access may be limited during services.
  • Dress: Modest clothing required — shoulders and knees covered; head coverings for women expected (wraps are often available).
  • Time needed: 20–40 minutes, more if you engage with the interior and the views; late afternoon is the most rewarding light.
  • Combine with: The Martvili Canyon (the classic pairing), and the wider area — Okatse Canyon and Kinchkha Waterfall, Prometheus Cave, and Kutaisi as a base.

faq.title: What is Martvili Monastery?
faq.content: Martvili Monastery (the Martvili-Chkondidi Cathedral) is an active Georgian Orthodox monastery on a hill above the town of Martvili, in the Samegrelo-Zemo Svaneti region of western Georgia. Its main cross-domed cathedral was built in the late 7th century on the roots of a giant oak that had been a pagan cult site — "Chkondidi" means "great oak" in Mingrelian, and "Martvili" derives from the word for martyr. Rebuilt in the 10th century, it became one of medieval Georgia's foremost religious and educational centers, and it preserves frescoes from the 14th–17th centuries. It sits a short distance from the Martvili Canyon and pairs naturally with it.

faq.title: Why is it called Chkondidi, and what's the story of the oak?
faq.content: By tradition, the hill was a pagan sacred site crowned by an enormous ancient oak — chkoni in Mingrelian — worshipped as an idol of fertility and prosperity. When Christianity reached western Georgia, St. Andrew the First-Called is said to have had the sacred oak cut down, and a church was built on its roots. The cathedral took its name from that tree: "Chkondidi," from didi chkoni, "great oak." The town's name, Martvili, comes from the word for martyr. It's a classic example of an early church planted directly on a powerful pre-Christian sacred site.

faq.title: Why was Chkondidi so important in medieval Georgia?
faq.content: Far more than its quiet profile today suggests. After King Giorgi II rebuilt it in the 10th century and made it an episcopal center, the bishopric of Chkondidi became one of the highest offices in the kingdom: the title "Chkondideli" was fused with that of mtsignobartukhutsesi — the king's chief minister and chancellor — so the bishop of this Samegrelo cathedral was effectively the realm's first minister. The most famous, Giorgi Chkondideli, was the mentor and right hand of King David IV the Builder. The monastery was also a major center of learning, with a scriptorium where monks translated and wrote, and King Bagrat IV is said to be buried here.

faq.title: What is there to see at the monastery?
faq.content: The walled hilltop complex contains the main cross-domed cathedral, a smaller two-story 10th-century church, a bell tower, and a courtyard, all kept almost like a park. Inside the cathedral are fragments of 14th–17th-century frescoes, including Old Testament scenes — well worth a close look. The other reward is the setting: wide views over the Abasha and Tskhenistskali valleys and the green Samegrelo countryside, best in the late afternoon. It's an active monastery with the peaceful, lived-in feel of a place that sees relatively few visitors.

faq.title: How do you get there, and can you combine it with the canyon?
faq.content: The monastery is on the hill above the town of Martvili, reached by the same roads that serve the Martvili Canyon — by car, on foot, or via the funicular up from the town. Most visitors come from Kutaisi (about an hour away) or another western-Georgia base. Entry is free, it's open during daylight hours, and you should dress modestly (shoulders and knees covered; head coverings for women). It's the natural companion to the Martvili Canyon a short distance away: start at the monastery in the morning for the good light and quiet, then head to the canyon for the boat ride and walkway. Allow 20–40 minutes at the monastery, more if you linger over the frescoes and views.

nokalakevi.heroTitle: Nokalakevi (Archaeopolis): The Lost Capital of Ancient Lazica
nokalakevi.content:
Nokalakevi — known to the Byzantines as Archaeopolis and to the Georgian chronicles as Tsikhegoji — is one of the most historically important and least visited major archaeological sites in Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus). It occupies a naturally fortified promontory above the Tekhuri River in the Samegrelo region, about 70 kilometers northwest of Kutaisi. This was the capital of the ancient Kingdom of Egrisi (Lazika) and one of the decisive fortresses of late antiquity — the stronghold whose walls the Persians could not break in the great 6th-century war between Byzantium and Sasanian Persia. It's not a reconstructed or over-interpreted site: the ruins are substantial but atmospheric, the landscape around them much as it has been for centuries. Between the archaeology, the dramatic river-cliff setting, the rock-cut siege tunnel, and the deep associations with the world of Colchis and the Golden Fleece, Nokalakevi has a depth that its modest tourist profile completely fails to convey.

What is Nokalakevi?
