Nokalakevi is a major multi-period archaeological site on a promontory above the Tekhuri River in the Samegrelo-Zemo Svaneti region of western Georgia, about 70 km from Kutaisi. It carries three names from three traditions: Archaeopolis ("old city" in Byzantine Greek), Tsikhegoji ("fortress of Kuji," after a semi-legendary Colchian ruler, in the Georgian chronicles), and Nokalakevi ("the place where a town was" in Georgian). With traces of occupation going back to the Bronze Age, it became the capital of the Kingdom of Egrisi (Lazika) around the 4th century and a pivotal fortress in the 6th-century Byzantine–Sasanian Lazic War, its triple walls strengthened under Emperor Justinian. Its remains include those great fortification walls, towers and gates, a church, a small museum, and a remarkable rock-cut tunnel to the river. Quiet, atmospheric, and tied to the legend of the Golden Fleece, it's a rewarding stop for anyone drawn to ancient history.

Three names, three peoples

Few sites wear their history as plainly as Nokalakevi does in its names. To the Byzantine historians Procopius and Agathias, who wrote of it in the 6th century, it was Archaeopolis — Greek for "old city," a name that itself implies the place was already ancient by late antiquity. To the Georgian chronicles it was Tsikhegoji, "the fortress of Kuji," after Kuji, a semi-legendary Colchian eristavi (regional ruler) of the Hellenistic age. And in Georgian today it is Nokalakevi — roughly "the place where a town once stood." Three names from three traditions, all pointing to the same long-inhabited promontory.

Historical layers

There are traces of small-scale activity here as far back as the Bronze Age, and finds dating to the 8th–7th centuries BCE, so the promontory — defensible, beside a river, surrounded by fertile land — drew people early, in the landscape of ancient Colchis. By the Hellenistic period (roughly 4th–1st centuries BCE) it is described as one of the centers of Colchis, and may be the fortress of the ruler Kuji whose name the Georgian tradition preserves; most of those early defenses, however, were later overbuilt.

The site's great age came in late antiquity. Around the turn of the 3rd–4th centuries CE, Nokalakevi became the capital of the Kingdom of Egrisi — the realm the Romans and Byzantines knew as Lazika (Lazica). Its position, commanding a crossing of the Tekhuri and a strategic route into the interior, made it one of the key fortresses of the region, and it became the centerpiece of a famous struggle: during the Byzantine–Sasanian Lazic War (540–562), Archaeopolis was a principal stronghold guarding Lazika, and the Persians' repeated failure to take it eventually cost them control of the kingdom. The great fortifications — the most visible remains today — were built in three phases from the 4th to the 6th century, culminating in a major strengthening under the Byzantine emperor Justinian, whose own decrees counted Archaeopolis among the greatest and most ancient fortresses of the region.

The city continued through the early medieval Georgian period until it was destroyed in the 8th century during the campaign of the Arab commander Marwan ibn Muhammad (remembered in Georgia as "Murvan the Deaf"). After that its urban importance faded as the political map of western Georgia shifted, and the site passed into the slow deterioration of a place neither inhabited nor demolished — the massive walls surviving where their bulk resisted collapse, lesser structures vanishing into the vegetation, the whole promontory settling into the silence of a city given back to nature.

The Colchis and Golden Fleece connection

Nokalakevi sits in the heartland of ancient Colchis — the kingdom of Greek myth ruled by King Aeetes and his daughter Medea, the land the Argonauts sailed to in quest of the Golden Fleece. The myth places the Colchian royal center somewhere on the eastern Black Sea, and scholars have proposed various sites in the western Georgian lowlands; Nokalakevi, as a major fortified Colchian center thought to have been a regional capital, is among those most seriously considered. Equating myth with a specific spot is always uncertain, and this should be taken as association rather than established fact — but it isn't arbitrary: the site's antiquity, scale, and position in the Colchian heartland fit the ancient descriptions, and the modern explorer Tim Severin even sailed a reconstructed Argo to Georgia to retrace the Argonauts' route.

Whether or not the identification holds, the resonance is real. To stand within the walls of a city that was old when the Greek world was first telling the stories that became the Argonautica, looking out over the Tekhuri toward the distant coast the Argonauts sailed, is to feel the mythological and the archaeological converge. Georgia's link to the Golden Fleece runs through its ancient identity — Medea stands in Batumi's central square, among other places — and Nokalakevi is where that thread feels most physically present.

The site

The remains spread across some 20 hectares of the promontory and its surroundings, so the site takes time to walk properly. The most dramatic survivals are the fortification walls, running along the promontory edges and incorporating the natural cliffs where the terrain does the defensive work itself; the citadel was ringed by three parallel walls (hence the Georgian description of "the triple-walled fortress"). They stand in places to considerable height — weathered but structurally coherent — and walking them gives both the scale of the defensive system and the best views over the river valley.

Within the circuit, the foundations of towers, gates, a church, and internal structures emerge from the vegetation that has colonized the site over centuries. Archaeology here has been serious and sustained: excavations since 1973, and since 2001 the Anglo-Georgian Expedition (a long-running collaboration involving the University of Winchester), so the excavated zones read far more clearly than the unexcavated ones. A small museum at the entrance presents finds spanning the site's long life — ceramics, metalwork, architectural fragments — and gives the ruins their context.

The natural setting is central to the site's character. The Tekhuri River runs below the promontory, audible from the upper levels and visible from the walls above the valley; the river-side cliffs are dramatic in themselves, and the forested slopes give the archaeological zone an enclosed, slightly secretive quality quite unlike open, flat sites visible from afar. The approach — the walls emerging gradually above the trees — has the quality of discovery that the best archaeological sites in wild settings provide.

The tunnel

One of Nokalakevi's most unusual features is an ancient tunnel cut through the rock of the promontory, connecting the fortified upper area with the Tekhuri River below. It was part of the defenses and the water-supply system — letting defenders reach the river during a siege without exposing themselves — and it's one of the more impressive pieces of ancient military engineering at the site. Walking through it, the ancient stone-cutting close at hand and the light of the river entrance ahead, is one of the most memorable specific experiences Nokalakevi offers.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: Nokalakevi, Senaki municipality, Samegrelo-Zemo Svaneti, western Georgia, on the Tekhuri River — about 70 km northwest of Kutaisi (and ~15 km from Senaki, on the Martvili road).
  • Getting there: About an hour by car from Kutaisi through the forested-hill-and-lowland landscape of western Georgia. Public transport is limited, so private transport or a taxi from Kutaisi or another western-Georgia base is the practical option.
  • Hours / admission: Open regular daytime hours; a modest entrance fee covers the archaeological site and the small museum.
  • Time needed: About 1–1.5 hours for the main remains, the tunnel, and the museum; more to walk the full wall circuit.
  • Best time: Spring through autumn — the shoulder seasons (roughly April–June and September–October) are ideal, with the setting at its most photogenic and the lower remains less obscured by summer growth; winter is muddier in the forested terrain.
  • Combine with: Martvili Monastery and the Martvili Canyon (on/near the same road), and the wider Samegrelo–Imereti circuit from a Kutaisi base.

faq.content: Nokalakevi lies in the heartland of ancient Colchis — the kingdom of King Aeetes and his daughter Medea, the destination of the Argonauts in their quest for the Golden Fleece. faq.title: How do you visit Nokalakevi, and what's there to see?

Allow 1–1.5 hours for the main remains, the museum, and the rock-cut tunnel down to the river — longer to walk the full circuit of the great walls. A modest entrance fee covers the site and the small museum. The big draws are the fortification walls (with the best valley views), the atmospheric river-cliff setting, and the ancient siege tunnel. It's one of Georgia's quietest major historical sites, which is part of its appeal, and pairs well with Martvili Monastery and the Martvili Canyon nearby. The shoulder seasons (April–June, September–October) are the most rewarding.

tobavarchkhili.heroTitle: Tobavarchkhili: The Silver Lakes of the Samegrelo Highlands
tobavarchkhili.content:
Tobavarchkhili — "Silver Lake" in Megrelian — is a group of remote, high-altitude glacial lakes in the Egrisi Range of Samegrelo, in northwestern Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus), set between roughly 2,500 and 2,900 meters in a landscape of real wildness and intensity. The name is barely known outside Georgia's hiking community, and even within the country it sits in its own category: not somewhere you fit into a regional itinerary, but a destination that takes several days of committed effort to reach and amounts to an expedition in itself. For those who make that effort, Tobavarchkhili offers what almost nowhere else in Georgia can — a genuinely wild, genuinely isolated alpine environment where the absence of infrastructure, crowds, and any of the usual markers of developed tourism is total rather than partial. The silver-blue lakes, set in rocky terrain above the treeline, are the destination — but the journey through the forests and passes of the Samegrelo highlands is just as much the point.

What is Tobavarchkhili?

Tobavarchkhili (the Silver Lakes) is a system of remote glacial lakes high in the Egrisi Range of the Samegrelo region in western Georgia, the largest sitting at about 2,650 meters. The name means "Silver Lake" in Megrelian — toba (lake) and varchkhili (silver) — for the silver-blue shimmer of the water. Reaching the lakes is a serious 4–6 day, roughly 50 km wilderness trek with no roads, no accommodation, no mobile coverage, and no shortcuts: a strenuous, camping-only expedition through Colchic forest, alpine meadow, and bare high terrain, usually starting from Mukhuri village. It's among the wildest and least-visited major destinations in Georgia, for experienced, well-prepared trekkers only — and one of the most rewarding.

The lakes and the landscape

The Egrisi Range that holds Tobavarchkhili is one of the least-traveled highland areas in Georgia, divided from the more visited mountains of Svaneti to the northeast by the high Egrisi ridge — terrain that historically made the area hard to reach and has kept it largely undeveloped even as other Georgian mountain regions built up visitor infrastructure over the past two decades. The approach crosses the full altitudinal range of the western Caucasus: dense subtropical forest in the lower valleys, opening through mixed deciduous and conifer woodland, then alpine meadow and dwarf shrub above the treeline, and finally the bare rock of the highest ground, where the lakes sit in basins scoured by glaciers during the last ice age.

There are several lakes — commonly counted as around five to seven, depending on the route taken — distributed across the high terrain at slightly different elevations and linked by the mountain catchment. The largest, Tobavarchkhili Lake itself, lies at about 2,650 meters, with smaller named lakes (Didgalish, Kalalish, Tsakatskarish, Okhoje and others) scattered around it; one is said to be heart-shaped, another frozen for much of the year. Each has its own character — some deeper and more intensely colored, others shallower and more reflective — and the whole group changes appearance dramatically with the weather.

The western Caucasus pattern produces frequent cloud and mist at these elevations, and the play of cloud and light over the water is one of Tobavarchkhili's defining qualities, distinct from the clearer, drier alpine country of eastern Georgia. On a clear day the sky and surrounding peaks reflect in the still water with startling clarity (and on the clearest days you may even glimpse distant Mt. Elbrus); on overcast days the mist settles and the rocky terrain disappears and returns through the cloud, in a way visitors consistently call otherworldly. Both are part of what Tobavarchkhili is — expecting only one is a misreading of the mountain.

The surrounding peaks rise high, and the mix of glacially carved rock, permanent snowfields, and raw mineral terrain above the vegetation line gives the lake basins a character closer to the high Alps or the central Caucasus than to the lush, subtropical-influenced green of lower Samegrelo just a few hours below. That transition — from the dense, dripping forest of the lower approach, through the open alpine zone, to the stark ground around the lakes — is one of the most rewarding parts of the trek, giving it a narrative arc no single-day hike can match.

A lake wreathed in legend

The Silver Lakes carry their share of local legend. By tradition the main lake is a sacred, jealously guarded place, and the warning passed down through generations is blunt: whoever disturbs its tranquility — and especially anyone who swims in it — will bring down the wrath of the black clouds, with sudden thunder and torrential rain. Whether or not the weather obliges, the story suits the place: at this altitude, in this fast-changing mountain climate, storms genuinely do roll in with little warning, and the legend gives form to the respect the mountain commands.

The trek

Reaching Tobavarchkhili is a multi-day undertaking with no shortcuts. The usual approach combines four-wheel-drive (or Soviet-truck) access to the trailhead — most often from Mukhuri village in the Samegrelo foothills, with a rough transfer up toward about 2,000 m that saves a long, unscenic first day on foot — followed by roughly four to six days of trekking depending on the route, the group's fitness, and the weather. There is no permanent accommodation anywhere on the route: camping is the only option, and carrying all your equipment, food, and shelter is a requirement, not a style choice.

The routes cross river crossings, forest paths that can be poorly marked or overgrown, steep climbs over passes (the highest around 2,900 m), and exposed high terrain where navigating in cloud demands experience or GPS. The physical demands are significant by Georgian standards — this is not a route for occasional hikers or anyone without multi-day mountain-camping experience — and the combination of altitude, remoteness, and changeable weather means underestimating it carries real consequences. There is no mobile coverage, so you cannot rely on phoning for help. An experienced local guide is strongly recommended, both for navigation and for the on-the-ground knowledge of terrain, weather, and emergency options that only direct experience of the route provides.

The way to the lakes passes through some of the most botanically rich forest in Georgia. The Colchic forest of western Georgia — the subtropical type with species such as Caucasian wingnut, Colchic ivy, and cherry laurel, plus relict and endemic plants that have survived here since before the last glaciation — is at its most intact in these little-disturbed valleys. Walking through old-growth Colchic forest, with its dense canopy, layered understory, and filtered light, is a significant experience in its own right, independent of the destination.

River crossings are a feature of most routes and demand attention. The mountain rivers here are fed by snowmelt and rain and can rise fast after a shower higher in the catchment, turning a crossing that was easy in the morning difficult or impossible by afternoon. A guide who knows current conditions and alternative crossing points is especially valuable, and decision-making around crossings in uncertain conditions is one area where local experience directly affects safety.

Camping at the lakes

Camping at or near the Silver Lakes is a different proposition from the forest camping on the approach. The exposure of the high terrain means weather systems arrive with a speed and directness the valley nights don't prepare you for, and the temperature at 2,500–2,900 m drops sharply after sunset no matter how warm the day. The kit needed for safe, comfortable camping at this elevation — a four-season or strong three-season tent able to handle wind and rain, a sleeping bag rated well below zero, fully waterproof clothing — is not negotiable, and the distance from any help if equipment fails makes quality and redundancy in the essentials genuinely important.

What the lakeside nights give back is something no comfortable lodging can. Dawn at Tobavarchkhili — the first light reaching the water before the peaks are lit, the surface dead still before any wind — is among the more extraordinary natural experiences in Georgia. The silence at this altitude, true high-mountain silence broken only by wind, water, and the occasional sound of wildlife, has a completeness that lower, more developed places never reach. And the simple fact of having walked several days through hard terrain to stand here gives the experience a weight that easily-reached destinations can't carry in the same way.

Wildlife and nature

The Egrisi highlands around Tobavarchkhili support brown bear, wolf, lynx, chamois, and roe deer, and the remote, undisturbed character of the area makes encounters more likely here than in Georgia's busier mountains. Bear presence in particular should be taken seriously — proper food storage in camp, awareness of fresh signs along the trail, and knowing how to behave in an encounter are practical matters, not theoretical ones. A guide with local knowledge will have current information on wildlife along the route and should be consulted on precautions.

The alpine zone around the lakes has its own distinct flora — plants adapted to the short growing season, high UV, and temperature extremes of the high mountains, many endemic to the Caucasus and found nowhere else. The wildflower meadows below the rocky lake basins, in full bloom in July and August, add color and biological interest the stark high ground alone does not.

Practical preparation

A Tobavarchkhili trek needs more advance planning than almost any other destination in Georgia. The nearest real services — accommodation, food resupply, reliable vehicle access — are in the lowland towns of Samegrelo (Zugdidi, Martvili) and at the trailhead village of Mukhuri, and everything for the multi-day trek must be organized and carried from there. Guiding operators based in Zugdidi and Tbilisi run Tobavarchkhili expeditions and can handle trailhead transport, guiding, and emergency communication — using an established operator rather than attempting the approach independently is strongly advisable for anyone without prior experience of this specific route.

The visiting window runs roughly mid-July to September, with late July and August offering the best combination of snow-free passes and reasonable (never guaranteed) weather. Even then, multi-day spells of rain and cloud should be expected rather than treated as bad luck, and building a buffer day or two into the schedule — for weather delays at the high passes and the lake basin, and for the landslides that sometimes block the access road — gives a much better chance at the full experience than a rigid plan.

Physical preparation genuinely matters. Sustained daily ascent over several days, a full camping pack at altitude, and uneven terrain place demands that occasional urban exercise doesn't meet. Several weeks of progressive aerobic training, including loaded hiking on uneven ground if you can, will make the trek far more enjoyable and reduce the fitness deficit that turns mountain trips miserable.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: The Egrisi Range, Samegrelo-Zemo Svaneti, northwestern Georgia; the trailhead is usually Mukhuri village (Chkhorotsku/Martvili area), with the main lake at ~2,650 m.
  • Trek: Strenuous, roughly 4–6 days and ~50 km, camping only; highest passes around 2,900 m. Not for inexperienced hikers; an experienced guide is strongly recommended.
  • Season: Mid-July to September (late July–August most reliable); the lakes are inaccessible outside the short summer window.
  • Essential: Full multi-day camping and cold/wet-weather kit; there is no accommodation, no resupply, and no mobile coverage on the route.
  • Base towns: Zugdidi or Martvili for services; many expeditions are organized from Zugdidi or Tbilisi.

faq.title: What is Tobavarchkhili?
faq.content: Tobavarchkhili — the "Silver Lakes" — is a system of remote glacial lakes high in the Egrisi Range of the Samegrelo region in western Georgia, the largest at about 2,650 meters. The name means "Silver Lake" in Megrelian (toba, lake; varchkhili, silver), for the silver-blue shimmer of the water. The lakes sit above the treeline in stark, glacially carved terrain, and reaching them is a serious multi-day wilderness trek. It's one of the wildest and least-visited major destinations in Georgia — a true expedition rather than a day trip.

faq.title: What does the name Tobavarchkhili mean?
faq.content: It means "Silver Lake" in Megrelian (Mingrelian), the language of the Samegrelo region — from toba, "lake," and varchkhili, "silver." The name comes from the way the water shimmers in shades of silver and blue, especially when the sun catches it. The lakes are commonly referred to collectively as "the Silver Lakes."

faq.title: How hard is the Tobavarchkhili trek, and how long does it take?
faq.content: It's strenuous and serious — roughly a 4 to 6 day, about 50 km expedition with no roads, no accommodation, no resupply, and no mobile coverage along the route. You camp the whole way and carry all your own food, shelter, and equipment, crossing rivers, climbing over high passes (the highest around 2,900 m), and navigating exposed terrain that can be lost in cloud. It's not suitable for occasional hikers or anyone without multi-day mountain-camping experience, and an experienced local guide is strongly recommended for navigation, river-crossing decisions, and safety. It usually starts from Mukhuri village, often with a rough four-wheel-drive transfer to save the first day's walk.

faq.title: When is the best time to trek to the Silver Lakes?
faq.content: The window is short — roughly mid-July to September, with late July and August the most reliable for snow-free passes and reasonable weather. Even then, expect multi-day spells of cloud and rain rather than treating them as unusual, and build a buffer day or two into your plan for weather delays at the high passes and for landslides that sometimes block the access road. Outside this summer window the lakes are effectively inaccessible.

faq.title: Is there a legend about Tobavarchkhili Lake?
faq.content: Yes. Local tradition holds the main lake as a sacred, jealously guarded place, with a warning passed down through generations: anyone who disturbs its tranquility — especially by swimming in it — will bring down the wrath of the black clouds, with sudden thunder and torrential rain. It's a fitting story for the setting, since storms really do build quickly in this high, fast-changing mountain climate, and it reflects the respect the place has long commanded.

musiciansPark.heroTitle: Musicians Park, Shekvetili: Sculptures That Play Their Own Music
musiciansPark.content:
The Musicians Park in Shekvetili occupies a forested coastal strip along the Black Sea in the Guria region of Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — between Batumi to the south and Ureki to the north, beside the Black Sea Arena concert venue. It's one of the more genuinely original public attractions on Georgia's western coast: an open-air sculpture park dedicated to musicians, where each bronze or metal figure plays that artist's music as you approach. You walk between the trees of a eucalyptus grove, encounter likenesses ranging from Georgian folk and classical figures to internationally recognized icons of 20th-century popular music, and hear each one's signature song through a speaker as you draw near. The combination is more successful than it might sound in description, and it's entirely unlike anything else in the area.

What is the Musicians Park?

The Musicians Park is an open-air park in Shekvetili, Guria, on Georgia's Black Sea coast, opened in 2016 beside the Black Sea Arena. It features around 35–40 sculptures of famous Georgian and international musicians set in a eucalyptus grove, and its signature feature is interactive: each statue has a speaker that plays the musician's best-known song as you walk up to it. It's free, family-friendly, shaded, and takes about 30–60 minutes to walk. It's one of several attractions clustered in the Shekvetili area, about 45–70 km north of Batumi.

The sculptures that sing

The park's defining feature is that the sculptures play music. Each figure stands beside a speaker (with an information panel and a bench) that switches on as visitors approach, playing a signature piece from that musician's repertoire — so the walk becomes a sequence of short musical encounters rather than a silent sculpture stroll. Opened in 2016 alongside the neighboring Black Sea Arena, the park began with around two dozen metal sculptures and has grown to roughly 35–40.

The range of subjects is deliberately eclectic, mixing Georgian musicians of national importance with international figures whose music crosses cultural and linguistic boundaries. The Georgian figures span the classical and folk traditions — names associated with Georgian music such as Vano Sarajishvili, Revaz Lagidze, and the composer Gia Kancheli — and Georgia's distinctive polyphonic singing tradition (recognized by UNESCO as Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity) sits in the background of that national selection. The international figures span the major popular and classical traditions: the Beatles, Elvis Presley, Michael Jackson, Frank Sinatra, Louis Armstrong, Whitney Houston, and Jimi Hendrix among the popular icons, and Beethoven, Bach, and Mozart among the classical composers. Encountering these likenesses — and hearing "My Way" or "What a Wonderful World" drift through a Georgian coastal forest — produces the slightly surreal, genuinely charming quality that is the park's particular atmosphere.

The setting and the experience

The forest setting is one of the park's strongest practical assets. The coastal woodland here — a eucalyptus grove in the humid, mild climate of this Black Sea coastline — provides dense shade through the warmer months, which makes the walking paths comfortable to explore in the heat of July and August, when the exposed beach can be hard going. The park doesn't have direct sea views from most sections, but the sea air is present throughout, giving the sculpture-discovery walk the feel of a seaside woodland stroll. The paths are well maintained and relatively flat, so the park is accessible to visitors of all ages and mobility levels, and the sculptures are illuminated at night, with toilets at both entrances.

The interactive, musical character makes the park especially effective with children and with visitors who would not ordinarily seek out a sculpture park — hearing the statues "perform" turns a passive walk into active discovery. Walking time is flexible, from a roughly thirty-minute circuit of the main route to a more leisurely hour spent lingering at the figures whose music you want to hear in full. There are no food or drink outlets inside, so it's worth bringing water, especially in summer.

The park works particularly well as a stop during coastal travel — a break on a journey between Batumi and Kutaisi or Tbilisi that offers something other than a beach, a café, or a fuel station, and a reason to pause in a part of Guria most travelers would otherwise drive straight through.

The Shekvetili cluster

The Musicians Park is one of several attractions concentrated in the developing coastal resort area of Shekvetili, which has seen significant tourism investment in recent years. Close by are the large Shekvetili Dendrological Park (a roughly 60-hectare arboretum of giant transplanted trees, exotic plants, and bird aviaries), the Georgia in Miniatures Park (scale models of the country's monuments), the Tsitsinatela amusement park, and the Black Sea Arena concert venue right next door. (Note that the Musicians Park, the Miniatures Park, and the Dendrological Park are separate attractions, even though tour operators sometimes bundle them together — they're clustered in the same area rather than being one site.) Together they make Shekvetili a varied half-day or more of family-friendly leisure, quite different in character from the culturally intense monuments of inland Georgia.

The coastal location puts Shekvetili within easy reach of Ureki — the magnetic black-sand beach resort a short way north — and Kobuleti and Batumi to the south, making the park a natural inclusion in any western-Georgia coastal itinerary combining beach time with the cultural and natural sites of Guria and Adjara.

Practical information

  • Location: Shekvetili, Ozurgeti Municipality, Guria, on the Black Sea coast, beside the Black Sea Arena (between Ureki and Kobuleti).
  • Getting there: By car on the coastal route between Batumi and Kutaisi, roughly 45–70 km north of Batumi (about an hour); coastal marshrutkas pass the Shekvetili area, though some take the bypass highway, so confirm the route.
  • Hours: Open daily; sculptures are lit at night.
  • Admission: Commonly reported as free; confirm locally, as policies can change.
  • Time needed: About 30–60 minutes; no food/drink outlets inside, so bring water. Easily combined with the neighboring Shekvetili attractions.

faq.title: What is the Musicians Park in Shekvetili?
faq.content: It's an open-air park on Georgia's Black Sea coast at Shekvetili, in the Guria region, opened in 2016 beside the Black Sea Arena. It features around 35–40 sculptures of famous Georgian and international musicians set in a eucalyptus grove — and its signature feature is that each statue has a speaker that plays the musician's best-known song as you approach, turning a walk through the park into a musical journey. It's free, shaded, family-friendly, and takes about 30–60 minutes.

faq.title: Which musicians are in the park?
faq.content: A deliberately eclectic mix of Georgian and international figures. The international side includes popular icons like the Beatles, Elvis Presley, Michael Jackson, Frank Sinatra, Louis Armstrong, Whitney Houston, and Jimi Hendrix, plus classical composers such as Beethoven, Bach, and Mozart. The Georgian figures include nationally important musicians like Vano Sarajishvili, Revaz Lagidze, and the composer Gia Kancheli. Each sculpture plays a signature piece from that artist as you walk up to it.

faq.title: Where is the Musicians Park and how do you get there?
faq.content: It's in Shekvetili, Ozurgeti Municipality, in the Guria region, on the Black Sea coast next to the Black Sea Arena, roughly 45–70 km (about an hour) north of Batumi on the coastal route toward Kutaisi. You can drive, take a taxi, or use a coastal marshrutka — though confirm the route, as some minibuses take a bypass highway away from the parks. It's close to the Dendrological Park, the Miniatures Park, and the Tsitsinatela amusement park.

faq.title: Is the Musicians Park worth visiting?
faq.content: For families and music lovers especially, yes — the interactive "singing statues" are genuinely charming and unlike anything else on the coast, and it's a pleasant, shaded, easy walk. It's a light, fun attraction rather than a major sight, so it's best as a stop on a coastal journey or bundled with the other Shekvetili parks rather than a destination in itself. The mix of recognizable world icons and Georgian musicians, each playing their music, is what makes it memorable.

faq.title: Is it the same as the Shekvetili Dendrological Park?
faq.content: No — they're separate attractions in the same area, which sometimes causes confusion because tour operators bundle them. The Musicians Park is the sculpture park (by the Black Sea Arena) where statues play music; the Shekvetili Dendrological Park is a large, separate arboretum of giant trees, exotic plants, and bird aviaries. The Georgia in Miniatures Park and the Tsitsinatela amusement park are also nearby. They're easy to visit together, but each is its own site.

gomismta.heroTitle: Gomismta (Gomi Mountain): Guria's "Resort in the Clouds"
gomismta.content:
Gomismta is a high mountain destination in the Guria region of western Georgia, in the country in the South Caucasus, rising above the forested hills of the Ozurgeti district on the northern slope of the Meskheti Range to an elevation — roughly 2,100 to 2,755 meters — where alpine meadows and open ridgelines emerge above the cloud layer that settles over the lower terrain on many days through the warmer months. It's best known for the sea of clouds phenomenon that has made it one of the most photographed natural landscapes in Georgia: the combination of altitude, the humid Black Sea climate of western Georgia, and the topography of the Gurian hills produces conditions where clouds gather in the valleys while the summit and its settlement stay clear above, the wooden cottages and meadows appearing to float on an unbroken white surface, the peaks of the Caucasus visible beyond on clear days. The effect, most reliable in the early morning and around sunset and after unsettled weather, has made Gomismta a destination specifically sought out by travelers drawn to Georgia's atmospheric landscapes.

What is Gomismta?

Gomismta (Gomi Mountain) is a high-altitude summer resort in the Guria region of western Georgia, on the Meskheti Range at roughly 2,100–2,755 meters, about 33 km from Ozurgeti. Like the nearby Bakhmaro, it's a historic climatic (health) resort famous for its "sea of clouds" — the cloud layer that fills the valleys below the settlement while the meadows and wooden cottages stay clear above. It's an off-grid, summer-only destination (roughly June–September) with basic infrastructure, reached by a rough mountain road, and best experienced with an overnight stay for the dawn cloud display.

The setting

Gomismta sits in the landscape of Guria — the western Georgian region between Adjara to the south, Imereti to the east, and Samegrelo to the north, with a distinct cultural identity, its own culinary traditions, and a landscape of forested hills, tea plantations on the lower slopes, and the humid subtropical climate of the Black Sea zone that keeps the vegetation intensely green most of the year. Guria is one of the less-visited regions in Georgia for international travelers, and Gomismta has been its most effective calling card in recent years — a specific, reproducible natural phenomenon that gives travelers a clear reason to engage with a region they might otherwise pass through.

The terrain transitions from the forested lower slopes — the dense spruce-and-fir forest of the western Caucasus highland zone — through the upper forest margin to the open alpine meadows of the higher elevations. In summer the meadows are covered in wildflowers, the high-altitude light, green grass, and flowering plants producing a visual richness that the cloud sea, when it forms below, frames rather than replaces. The rolling ridgelines from the summit area give the panorama real depth — there's always another ridge beyond the foreground, the layers fading toward the horizon giving clear-day views a scale that's hard to anticipate from descriptions, with the Black Sea visible to the west on the clearest days.

A historic health resort

Gomismta is not a recent discovery: it's a historic climatic (health) resort with a long pedigree. Shepherds from Guria and Adjara used the mountain as summer pasture, and its air — the characteristic western-Georgian mixture of sea and mountain — was recognized for its health benefits by the early 20th century, valued for respiratory conditions like chronic bronchitis and mild asthma. The resort grew through the Soviet period (the Ozurgeti–Gomismta road was built in 1955, and by the 1980s there were well over a thousand summer cottages and thousands of annual visitors), fell into disrepair after the collapse of the Soviet Union, and has been reviving since around 2010 as Georgian and international interest in the mountain's ecotourism and photographic appeal has grown. That history gives the contemporary "sea of clouds" destination a deeper backstory than its recent fame suggests.

The sea of clouds

The sea of clouds isn't guaranteed — it needs the specific conditions of moisture, temperature differential, and light wind that let cloud form and stabilize in the valleys while the higher terrain stays clear. These arise most often in the early morning, when the difference between the cold overnight air and the warming lower terrain creates low cloud, and in the late afternoon and evening as the air cools after a warm day. After rain, when the air is saturated, the clouds often settle quickly and dramatically into the valleys, producing some of the most striking conditions at the site.

The uncertainty is part of Gomismta's character. A traveler who comes for a single afternoon may or may not catch the cloud sea, while one who stays overnight greatly improves their odds of the early-morning condition that most photographs represent. This isn't a frustration but a feature of a genuinely natural landscape that runs on atmospheric conditions rather than visitor schedules — and the overnight stay that maximizes the visual opportunity is also the fullest way to experience the mountain, the evening, night, and early morning offering a sequence of moods that a half-day can only sample.

The settlement and accommodation

The settlement consists of traditional wooden cottages and mountain guesthouses that have developed as the site has drawn increasing attention. The architecture — wooden construction, simple forms, the aesthetic of a mountain agricultural settlement adapted for hospitality without being fundamentally transformed — gives the place a visual coherence and authenticity that purpose-built resorts usually lack. Staying in one of these cottages, the meadows and ridgelines immediately outside and the cloud sea forming and dissolving below through the night and morning, gives the visit a genuine immersion that a day trip can't approximate.

A practical caveat worth knowing in advance: Gomismta is essentially off-grid. There's generally no mains electricity or piped water (water comes from mountain springs), and few or no shops, cafés, or other facilities — so come prepared, and don't expect resort comforts. The food and hospitality at the guesthouses reflect the Gurian culinary tradition — part of the broader Georgian food world but with its own character, including Gurian khachapuri (a folded, often crescent-shaped filled bread distinct from the Imeretian round and Adjarian boat-shaped versions, traditionally eaten at New Year), the spicier preparations Guria is known for, and the abundance and warmth of the highland table. Dinner at a Gomismta guesthouse while the clouds fill the valleys below is one of the more specifically atmospheric meals available in western Georgia, and dairy from the summer-pasturing herds (cheeses like chechili) is a local specialty.

Access

Gomismta is reached from the Ozurgeti area — the main town of Guria, about 50 km from Batumi — by a mountain road that climbs from the lower terrain into the highland zone where the summit meadows lie, about 33 km from Ozurgeti. The road is largely unpaved and its condition varies with recent weather: in dry conditions an ordinary car can generally manage it, but a vehicle with good ground clearance is the practical minimum and a four-wheel-drive is advisable after rain, when the unpaved sections can become genuinely difficult. Allow roughly an hour or more from Ozurgeti depending on conditions; the drive itself, climbing through the forested Gurian hills with views opening progressively, is a worthwhile introduction to the landscape. There's little or no public transport up the mountain, so a private vehicle, taxi, or organized transfer is needed for the final leg.

Ozurgeti connects to Batumi by road in about an hour and to Tbilisi in roughly four to six hours, so Gomismta fits into a western-Georgia itinerary combining Adjara, Guria, and potentially Imereti or Samegrelo. Several worthwhile spots lie nearby: the volcanic Lake Chinchkha (around 2,500 m), the Kintrishi Protected Area, and Bakhmaro — the other, better-known Gurian sea-of-clouds resort — is roughly 25 km away, though the two aren't directly connected by an easy road.

Gomismta or Bakhmaro?

The obvious question for travelers is how Gomismta compares with Bakhmaro, the region's other sea-of-clouds resort. They share the same phenomenon, the same wooden-cottage character, the same off-grid simplicity, and similar elevations. Bakhmaro is the more established and famous of the two, with a slightly larger summer community and the celebrated August horse race; Gomismta is generally quieter and less developed. For most travelers either delivers the experience — the choice often comes down to logistics and which fits a given route. Doing both, if time allows, shows two variations on the same remarkable highland world.

When to visit

June through September is the main season, the summer months offering the most reliable access and the fullest flowering of the meadows. The sea of clouds occurs across this whole window, with particular frequency in July and August when the western-Georgian summer humidity is highest and the temperature differentials most reliably present. Autumn (September–October) brings a different character — more persistent morning mists, and autumn color in the forest below set against the clear alpine terrain above, conditions many photographers prefer to full summer green. The road can be affected by early snowfall from late October, and the season effectively closes when the first significant snow arrives, typically in November; the mountain is largely empty and access difficult through winter.

Practical information

  • Location: Gomismta (Gomi Mountain), Ozurgeti Municipality, Guria, western Georgia; Meskheti Range, Bzhuzhi valley, roughly 2,100–2,755 m.
  • Getting there: ~33 km from Ozurgeti by mountain road (about an hour-plus); Ozurgeti is ~1 hour from Batumi. High-clearance/4x4 advisable; no regular public transport up the mountain. Summer access only.
  • When: June–September (July–August most reliable for clouds; September–October for autumn conditions).
  • Stay overnight: Strongly recommended to catch the dawn sea of clouds; book a cottage/guesthouse ahead.
  • Know before you go: Off-grid — generally no mains electricity or piped water, and minimal facilities; bring warm layers (cool even in summer) and supplies, and confirm road conditions.

faq.title: What is Gomismta?
faq.content: Gomismta (Gomi Mountain) is a high-altitude summer resort in the Guria region of western Georgia, on the Meskheti Range at roughly 2,100–2,755 meters, about 33 km from Ozurgeti. Like the nearby Bakhmaro, it's a historic climatic (health) resort, famous today for its "sea of clouds" — the cloud layer that fills the valleys below while the meadows and wooden cottages stay clear above. It's an off-grid, summer-only destination best experienced overnight, when the dawn cloud display is most likely.

faq.title: What is the "sea of clouds" and when can you see it?
faq.content: It's a low cloud layer that settles in the valleys below Gomismta while the summit stays clear, so you look out over a white "sea." It forms when humid Black Sea air, the elevation difference, and temperature inversions let cloud stabilize at valley level — most often in the early morning and around sunset, and especially in the days after rain. It's not guaranteed, which is why staying overnight greatly improves your chances of the classic dawn view.

faq.title: How do you get to Gomismta, and do you need a 4x4?
faq.content: It's reached by a mostly unpaved mountain road about 33 km up from Ozurgeti (roughly an hour-plus), and Ozurgeti is about an hour from Batumi. In dry conditions an ordinary car can usually manage, but a high-clearance or 4x4 vehicle is advisable, particularly after rain when the road gets difficult. There's essentially no public transport up the mountain, so you'll need a car, taxi, or organized transfer for the final stretch. Access is summer-only — snow closes the road from around November.

faq.title: Gomismta or Bakhmaro — which should I choose?
faq.content: Both are Gurian sea-of-clouds resorts with the same phenomenon, wooden cottages, off-grid simplicity, and similar elevations. Bakhmaro is the more established and famous, with a larger summer community and the August horse race; Gomismta is generally quieter and less developed. Either delivers the experience, so the choice often comes down to your route and logistics — and if you have time, doing both shows two variations on the same remarkable highland world.

faq.title: Where do you stay at Gomismta and what should you know?
faq.content: Accommodation is in traditional wooden cottages and simple guesthouses — staying overnight is the way to catch the dawn clouds, so book ahead in summer. Be prepared for off-grid conditions: generally no mains electricity or piped water (springs supply water), and few or no shops or cafés, so bring supplies and warm layers, as it's cool even in summer. The food is hearty Gurian cooking, with local cheeses from the summer pastures. The simplicity is part of the appeal, but plan for it.

georgiaInMiniatures.heroTitle: Georgia in Miniatures Park, Shekvetili: The Country's Landmarks in Miniature
georgiaInMiniatures.content:
The Georgia in Miniatures Park in Shekvetili, on the Guria coast of Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — presents scaled-down replicas of the country's most significant architectural and historical monuments in an open-air coastal setting, letting visitors walk through a compressed version of Georgia's cultural geography in about half an hour. Opened in 2016, it's one of several leisure attractions clustered in the Shekvetili area — alongside the large Dendrological Park and the Tsitsinatela amusement park — that together make this stretch of the Black Sea shore one of the more family-friendly leisure destinations on Georgia's western coast.

What is the Georgia in Miniatures Park?

The Georgia in Miniatures Park is an open-air park in Shekvetili, Guria, displaying around 50 scale models (roughly 1:25) of Georgia's most famous monuments — cathedrals, fortresses, cave cities, and Svan towers — in a single walkable space. Opened in 2016, it's a compact, inexpensive attraction (entry a few GEL; about a 30-minute visit) that works well as an orientation to Georgian architecture at the start of a trip, or a fun stop for families. It sits beside the Shekvetili Dendrological Park and the Tsitsinatela amusement park, about 45–70 km north of Batumi on the coastal route.

The models

The park presents around fifty miniature replicas at about 1:25 scale — large enough to show meaningful architectural detail and to photograph well, while keeping the whole park at a comfortable walking size. The selection covers the country's most recognizable monuments: the cave city of Vardzia, the medieval defensive towers of Svaneti, the cathedrals and churches of Tbilisi and Mtskheta, fortress complexes, and examples drawn from the regional diversity of Georgian architecture. The better models convey the essential character of the originals — the vertical drama of the Svan towers, the honeycomb of cave openings at Vardzia, the proportions and dome forms of the major cathedrals — giving visitors who haven't yet seen the real thing a genuine visual impression rather than a generic model.

For travelers who've already visited many of the sites, the models offer an interesting scale-recognition experience — the mind holding the miniature in front of it against the full-scale original in memory, a comparison that can illuminate aspects of proportion and composition that the real building doesn't always make obvious. For families with children, the human scale is the appeal: close enough to examine the detail, small enough to take in whole, the models make Georgian architecture far more legible than the monuments themselves, where scale and complexity can overwhelm younger visitors.

The experience and how to use it

The walking routes move visitors through the collection in a sequence that loosely mirrors Georgia's own geography — eastern and western regions, highland and lowland, religious and secular architecture each represented. The landscaped paths and the open-air coastal setting make the walk pleasant regardless of the specific models, and the combination of movement, visual discovery, and photography at each stop gives the relaxed, exploratory quality the miniature-park format does well. It's a compact park — most visitors find half an hour enough — so it suits a short stop rather than a half-day in itself.

The park works particularly well as a first encounter with Georgian architecture for travelers at the start of a trip who want a sense of the range and character of what's ahead. Used this way — as an orientation tool that builds visual familiarity with the names and forms of the major monuments before meeting the real thing — it offers a genuinely useful function beyond simple entertainment. It's easier to feel oriented in front of Gelati Monastery or the Svan towers of Ushguli having already seen miniature versions that established the basic visual vocabulary. (Visitors should set expectations appropriately: it's a modest, fun attraction rather than a major sight, best enjoyed for what it is.)

The Shekvetili leisure cluster

The park sits in the same stretch of the Guria coast as several other attractions, so it's rarely visited alone. The most natural pairing is the Shekvetili Dendrological Park — a large (around 60-hectare) arboretum of giant transplanted trees, exotic plants, and aviaries of birds, which also contains the Musicians Park, a walk of sculptures of Georgian and international musicians and composers that play their melodies as you approach. Nearby too is the Tsitsinatela amusement park (rides and family entertainment) and the large Black Sea Arena concert venue. Together these cover a range of themes — architectural heritage, botanical and wildlife interest, music, and amusement-park fun — within a short distance, making the Shekvetili area a practical half-day or more of varied, family-oriented leisure quite different in character from the culturally intense monuments of inland Georgia. The pine-forest-and-beach setting gives the whole visit a relaxed holiday feel.

Practical information

  • Location: Shekvetili, Ozurgeti Municipality, Guria, on the Black Sea coast (near the Dendrological Park and the Tsitsinatela amusement park).
  • Getting there: By car on the coastal route between Batumi and Kutaisi, roughly 45–70 km north of Batumi (about an hour); coastal marshrutkas stop in the Shekvetili area, though confirm the route, as some take the bypass highway away from the parks.
  • Hours: Daily, roughly from 10:00; closing time varies seasonally (commonly cited around 17:30).
  • Admission: Paid, but inexpensive (a few GEL; young children often free).
  • Time needed: About 30 minutes; combine with the neighboring parks for a fuller visit.

faq.title: What is the Georgia in Miniatures Park?
faq.content: It's an open-air park in Shekvetili, on the Guria coast of Georgia, displaying around fifty scale models (about 1:25) of the country's most famous monuments — cathedrals, fortresses, the cave city of Vardzia, the Svan towers, and more — in one walkable space. Opened in 2016, it's a compact, inexpensive attraction that works as both a quick orientation to Georgian architecture and a fun family stop, set beside the larger Shekvetili Dendrological Park.

faq.title: Where is it and how do you get there?
faq.content: It's in Shekvetili, Ozurgeti Municipality, in the Guria region, on the Black Sea coast roughly 45–70 km (about an hour) north of Batumi on the coastal route toward Kutaisi. You can drive, take a taxi, or use a coastal marshrutka — though confirm the route with the driver, since some minibuses now take a bypass highway that runs away from the parks. It's right by the Dendrological Park and the Tsitsinatela amusement park.

faq.title: How much does it cost and how long do you need?
faq.content: Admission is inexpensive — a few GEL, with young children often free — and the park is small, so about half an hour is usually enough to see it. That makes it an easy add-on rather than a destination in itself, best combined with the neighboring Shekvetili parks for a fuller visit. (Prices and hours change, so it's worth checking current details before you go.)

faq.title: Is it worth visiting?
faq.content: For families, or as a quick orientation to Georgian architecture at the start of a trip, yes — seeing miniature versions of Vardzia, the Svan towers, or the major cathedrals first makes the real monuments easier to appreciate later. It's a modest, fun attraction rather than a major sight, so it's best enjoyed for what it is: a relaxed half-hour stop, ideally bundled with the Dendrological Park and the other Shekvetili attractions on a coastal day out.

faq.title: What else is there to do in Shekvetili?
faq.content: Quite a lot for a small coastal village. The standout is the Shekvetili Dendrological Park, a large arboretum of giant transplanted trees, exotic plants, and bird aviaries, which also contains the Musicians Park (sculptures that play music as you approach). There's also the Tsitsinatela amusement park, the Black Sea Arena concert venue, and the pine-backed beach itself. Together they make Shekvetili a practical half-day or more of varied, family-friendly leisure on the Guria coast.

bakhmaro.heroTitle: Bakhmaro: Georgia's "Sea of Clouds" Mountain Resort in Guria
bakhmaro.content:
Bakhmaro is a high-altitude mountain settlement in the Guria region of western Georgia, in the country in the South Caucasus, sitting at around 2,000 meters above sea level on the Meskheti range above the Chokhatauri district — and widely regarded as one of the most atmospheric and genuinely beautiful highland destinations in the western half of the country. It shares with the nearby Gomismta mountain the defining visual phenomenon of the western Georgian highlands — the sea of clouds that forms when moisture-laden air from the Black Sea settles in the valleys while the summit terrain stays clear above — but Bakhmaro is the more established and celebrated of the two, its reputation built over generations among Georgian families who summer here to escape the lowland heat, and more recently among international travelers who've found it one of the most photogenic mountain destinations in Georgia.

What is Bakhmaro?

Bakhmaro is a high-altitude summer resort in the Guria region of western Georgia, at roughly 2,000–2,050 meters in the Meskheti mountains above Chokhatauri. It's the country's highest alpine climatic (health) resort, famous since the late 19th century for air that mixes Black Sea and mountain qualities — and for its "sea of clouds," the cloud layer that fills the valleys below the settlement at dawn and dusk. Its iconic wooden cottages stand on stilts on the meadows. It's a summer-only destination (roughly June–September), reached by a winding mountain road, best experienced with an overnight stay for the morning cloud display.

The sea of clouds

The sea of clouds at Bakhmaro is produced by the same atmospheric mechanism as at Gomismta — the high humidity of the western Georgian Black Sea climate (the coast is only about 50 km away), the elevation difference between the settlement and the surrounding valleys, and the temperature inversions that let cloud form and stabilize at valley level while the higher terrain stays above it. At Bakhmaro's elevation the chance of being above a cloud layer on a summer morning is high enough that most visitors who stay overnight will see it at least partially, and those who time their stay for the days just after rainfall — when the air is fully saturated and the clouds settle most completely into the valleys — are most likely to catch the phenomenon at its most dramatic. The effect has earned Bakhmaro the nickname the "Kingdom of Clouds."

The light is what makes the site extraordinary for photography. In the early morning, when the cloud layer is most stable and the sun low, the light rakes across the wooden cottages and open meadows above the cloud surface — long shadows, golden tones on weathered timber, the white cloud sea stretching to the horizon below — in a way that midday's overhead light eliminates. Sunset gives a different but equally striking set of conditions, the cloud surface catching the warm declining light while the sky shifts from blue through orange and red. These are the moments most photographs of Bakhmaro capture, so planning a stay around the morning and evening hours, rather than arriving midday, gives a visit its greatest visual potential.

A historic climatic resort

Bakhmaro is more than a viewpoint: it's the highest alpine climatic resort in Georgia, and has been a recognized health resort since around 1890, when roads and water pipes were first built and the summer cottages followed (the first systematic study of its climate was made in 1892, and by 1923 it was being described as one of the highest settlements in Europe). The draw was medical as much as scenic — the unusual mixture of sea and mountain air, with high humidity and clean alpine atmosphere, was, and still is, considered beneficial for respiratory conditions; local lore holds that a three-week stay sets up the immune system for the year. The surrounding mountains are also the source of the bottled Bakhmaro spring water and the nearby Nabeglavi mineral water, both widely sold across Georgia.

The settlement and its cottages

The village character of Bakhmaro — the traditional wooden cottages, the open meadows, the absence of large hotel blocks — is as much a part of what makes the place special as the landscape. The cottages have an unusual and distinctive form: built of wood and raised on tall stilts or columns, both to maximize airflow in the warm, damp summer climate and to stand above the deep winter snow, which can reach several meters. They're predominantly seasonal, occupied in the summer months by Gurian families whose lowland homes are too hot in July and August, and empty through the winter when snow covers the mountain and access becomes difficult. The summer community that forms during the warmest months gives Bakhmaro a particular social character — a temporary but recurring annual gathering of families keeping up a tradition of highland summer living that has been part of Gurian culture for generations.

Accommodation ranges from simple rooms in family guesthouses to whole cottages rented by groups who want the full experience of living in the settlement rather than just visiting. The food reflects the Gurian culinary tradition — the spicier, more intensely flavored cooking that sets Guria apart within Georgian food, with local versions of khachapuri (including the New Year dish Gurian khachapuri), walnut-based preparations, and fresh dairy from the mountain pastures. A practical note: infrastructure here remains basic — water supply is limited (some of it carried from springs) and electricity can be intermittent — which is part of the settlement's character but worth knowing before you go.

Things to do

Bakhmaro's appeal is primarily atmospheric and contemplative rather than activity-driven, and it rewards slow, attentive presence more than a structured program. Hiking in the surrounding alpine terrain — the open meadows and ridgelines above the settlement opening to ever-wider views over Guria and, on clear days, the distant Black Sea and the Greater Caucasus — is the most natural activity, with routes from short walks around the settlement to full-day ridge traverses that need proper mountain preparation.

Horseback riding is available through local providers and gives a different pace and perspective than walking; the traditional role of horses in the highland pastoral economy here gives it an authenticity that organized riding elsewhere can lack — and Gurian horsemanship is genuinely renowned (Gurian riders were even recruited into Buffalo Bill's Wild West Show in the 1880s). That heritage peaks each year on August 19, when Bakhmaro hosts a famous horse race tied to the Orthodox feast of the Transfiguration — the settlement's biggest event, drawing riders and crowds. For photographers, the changing light, the variable cloud sea, and the cottages-against-landscape compositions give material that rewards sustained engagement rather than a single quick visit.

Access

The approach from the Chokhatauri area climbs a mountain road from the lower Gurian terrain through forested slopes to the settlement. The road has been progressively improved and much of it is now paved, though it remains long and winding, and the condition of any remaining rough sections varies with recent weather; in dry summer conditions an ordinary car generally manages the drive, but after heavy rain a higher-clearance or four-wheel-drive vehicle is the safer choice. Allow roughly one and a half to two hours from Chokhatauri depending on conditions, and around two hours from Ozurgeti, the regional center of Guria. The road is generally open only in the summer season; snow makes it impassable from roughly November onward.

Chokhatauri is reachable from Batumi in around an hour and a half, and from Tbilisi in about four to four and a half hours via the main east–west highway (note that some private-transfer services quote three to four hours total from Batumi door-to-door, given the mountain section). Shared minibuses (marshrutkas) run up to Bakhmaro in the summer high season, roughly mid-June to the end of September. Bakhmaro is most naturally approached as part of a Guria itinerary — taking in Ozurgeti, the Shemokmedi Monastery, and the Gomismta mountain — or as an overnight mountain destination for travelers based in Batumi who want a highland experience within a manageable drive of the coast.

When to visit

June through September is the season, with July and August the most active months, when the seasonal community is in residence and the guesthouses are fully operating. Late August and September are especially rewarding — the late-summer and early-autumn conditions produce some of the most dramatic cloud formations of the year, with fewer visitors than the peak July weeks (and the August 19 horse race falls in this window). The road typically becomes impassable with snow from November onward, and the settlement is largely empty through winter. Early June can still see the road affected by the last of the snowmelt depending on the year, so confirming road conditions before an early-season visit is wise.

Practical information

  • Location: Bakhmaro, Chokhatauri Municipality, Guria, western Georgia; on the Meskheti range at roughly 2,000–2,050 m, on the Bakhvistskali River.
  • Getting there: By car, taxi/private transfer, or summer marshrutka, up the mountain road from Chokhatauri (about 1.5–2 hours); Chokhatauri is ~1.5 hours from Batumi, ~4–4.5 hours from Tbilisi. Summer access only.
  • When: June–September (July–August busiest; late August–September best for clouds and the horse race).
  • Stay overnight: Essential to catch the dawn sea of clouds; book guesthouses or a cottage ahead in peak season.
  • Know before you go: Basic infrastructure (limited water, sometimes intermittent electricity); bring layers (cool even in summer) and confirm road conditions, especially early/late season.

faq.title: What is Bakhmaro?
faq.content: Bakhmaro is a high-altitude summer resort in the Guria region of western Georgia, at roughly 2,000–2,050 meters in the Meskheti mountains above Chokhatauri. It's the country's highest alpine climatic (health) resort, known since the late 19th century for clean air that mixes Black Sea and mountain qualities, and famous today for its "sea of clouds" — a cloud layer that fills the valleys below the settlement at dawn and dusk. Its iconic wooden cottages stand on stilts across the alpine meadows.

faq.title: What is the "sea of clouds" at Bakhmaro?
faq.content: It's a cloud layer that settles into the valleys below Bakhmaro while the settlement stays clear above it, so you look out over a white "sea" of cloud. It forms because humid air from the nearby Black Sea, the elevation difference, and temperature inversions let cloud stabilize at valley level. It's most likely and most dramatic in the early morning and around sunset, especially in the days after rain, which is why staying overnight is the way to experience it — Bakhmaro is even nicknamed the "Kingdom of Clouds."

faq.title: How do you get to Bakhmaro, and do you need a 4x4?
faq.content: Bakhmaro is reached by a long, winding mountain road up from Chokhatauri (about 1.5–2 hours), which is itself about 1.5 hours from Batumi and 4–4.5 hours from Tbilisi. The road has been much improved and is largely paved, so in dry summer conditions an ordinary car can usually make it; after heavy rain a higher-clearance or 4x4 vehicle is safer. Summer marshrutkas also run. Access is essentially summer-only — snow closes the road from around November.

faq.title: When is the best time to visit Bakhmaro?
faq.content: The season is June through September. July and August are busiest, with the seasonal community in residence and all guesthouses open. Late August and September are arguably best — the cloud formations are at their most dramatic and there are fewer visitors, and the famous Bakhmaro horse race takes place on August 19 (tied to the Orthodox Transfiguration feast). Outside summer the road is generally snowbound and the settlement largely empty.

faq.title: Where do you stay in Bakhmaro and what should you know?
faq.content: Accommodation is in family guesthouses or rented wooden cottages — staying overnight is essential to catch the dawn sea of clouds, and you should book ahead in peak season. The food is hearty Gurian cooking (spicy by Georgian standards, with local khachapuri and mountain dairy). Be aware the infrastructure is basic: water supply is limited and electricity can be intermittent, and even in summer it's cool at altitude, so bring warm layers. It's part of the rustic charm, but worth planning for.

petraFortress.heroTitle: Petra Fortress: Justinian's Byzantine Stronghold on the Adjara Coast
petraFortress.content:
Petra Fortress occupies a rocky promontory above the Black Sea coast about 25 kilometers north of Batumi, near the village of Tsikhisdziri, in Adjara in the country of Georgia in the South Caucasus. Its ruined walls are visible from the coastal road below, and its elevated position commands views over the sea and the coastline in both directions. It's a less-visited and considerably less-developed site than Gonio to the south, and the combination of the dramatic natural setting, the genuine antiquity of the ruins, and the relative absence of organized tourism gives it a character that appeals to travelers who like their historical sites with some roughness.

What is Petra Fortress?

Petra Fortress is a 6th-century Byzantine fortress-city at Tsikhisdziri, on the Black Sea coast about 25 km north of Batumi in Adjara, Georgia. Built under Emperor Justinian I (and named "Petra," Greek for "rock"), it guarded the southern approach to the kingdom of Lazica and became a fiercely contested prize in the Lazic War between Byzantium and Sassanid Persia — the historian Procopius describes its sieges in detail. Today it's a ruined hilltop archaeological site with standing Byzantine walls, reached by a short steep walk, with sweeping sea views. It's a quieter, wilder counterpart to the Roman fortress at Gonio.

A fortress built by Justinian

The fortress was built in the 6th century during the reign of the Byzantine Emperor Justinian I, as part of the empire's effort to consolidate control over the eastern Black Sea coast and its trade routes during a period of intense competition with the Sassanid Persian Empire. It's first referred to in 535, in Justinian's own legal decrees, and was developed from an earlier small Roman fort into a fortified city named, in the emperor's honor, Petra Pia Justiniana. The site — a rocky headland rising steeply from the sea, with natural cliffs defending most sides and only a narrow neck of land connecting it to the mainland — was chosen with the eye for defensible topography that marks the best Byzantine military architecture of the period. The name Petra, meaning "rock" in Greek, describes the site's essential character directly and has been in use since antiquity.

According to the Byzantine historian Procopius, who documented Justinian's wars in detail, the fortress was founded through the efforts of the Roman official John Tzibus, who then used it to control imports into Lazica — monopolizing access to luxury goods and to the much-needed salt trade. That commercial stranglehold caused such resentment among the local Laz that it helped trigger the conflict to come.

The Lazic War

Petra's strategic position made it one of the most contested sites on the eastern Black Sea coast in the 6th century. It became a central prize in the prolonged conflict known as the Lazic War (roughly 541–562) — the struggle between Byzantium and Sassanid Persia for control of the Kingdom of Lazica, the political entity that occupied much of what is now western Georgia. The fortress changed hands more than once: the Persians took it in 541, the Byzantines besieged it unsuccessfully in 549, and after a famous and brutal siege in 550–551 — which Procopius described in vivid detail — the Byzantines retook it and then deliberately demolished it themselves, to prevent the Persians from ever using it again. The detailed presence of Petra in the contemporary historical record, through Procopius and Justinian's own decrees, gives it a documented importance that most sites of comparable size in the Caucasus lack.

The site was held, damaged, and partly rebuilt by various powers through the medieval period (it served for a time as a regional church center under Constantinople), and Ottoman forces controlled it during the centuries of Ottoman presence in Adjara before Russian annexation in the 19th century. The ruins visible today represent the accumulated construction of these successive occupations rather than a single campaign. Excavations around the site have uncovered far more than the citadel walls — 4th-century baths, an early Byzantine villa, an early church, and an extensive cemetery with gold, silver, and bronze grave goods — and Petra is now protected as an archaeological museum-reserve.

The site and the walk

Reaching the fortress requires a short but fairly steep uphill walk from the road below, gaining height quickly through the subtropical scrub and forest of the Adjarian coastal zone. The effort is modest — the ascent takes fifteen to twenty minutes for most visitors — but enough that arriving at the walls feels like having earned the views that open up at the top.

The ruins are substantial in places and fragmentary in others, the uneven preservation reflecting both the varying quality of construction across building periods and the centuries of weathering and vegetation since the site's decline. Sections of the original Byzantine curtain wall stand to considerable height, and the corner towers that defined the perimeter are identifiable even where the masonry has partly collapsed. The interior is an open space of terraced ground, grass and wildflowers, with scattered stone blocks and foundation remains throughout.

The views are the most immediately rewarding feature. The headland gives an unobstructed panorama over the Black Sea to the west, with the coastline visible both north and south from the elevated walls. The Batumi skyline, with its cluster of modern towers, is visible to the south on clear days. The forested Adjarian highlands rise immediately inland, and the contrast between the dark green of the slopes and the blue of the sea gives the view a richness that flat coastal sites can't match.

Getting there

The fortress is near the village of Tsikhisdziri, about 25 kilometers north of Batumi along the coastal road; the drive takes around 25 to 30 minutes. It's visible from the road, and the path to the ruins begins near a small parking area at the base of the promontory. Marshrutkas running the coastal road between Batumi and Kobuleti pass Tsikhisdziri, so the site is reachable without a car, though the stop leaves a short walk to the fortress base. (Tsikhisdziri also has well-regarded beaches below the headland, so the fortress combines easily with time by the sea.)

Practical information

  • Location: Tsikhisdziri village, Adjara, about 25 km (25–30 minutes) north of Batumi on the coastal road, between Batumi and Kobuleti.
  • Getting there: By car, taxi, or coastal-road marshrutka (Batumi–Kobuleti); a short steep walk (about 15–20 minutes) up to the ruins from the parking area at the base.
  • Hours/Admission: Open daily in daylight hours; an entrance fee is payable at the site (it's a managed archaeological museum-reserve).
  • Best to know: Wear proper footwear for the uphill path; the site is exposed, so bring sun protection. Pairs well with the Tsikhisdziri beaches or with Gonio Fortress (south of Batumi) for a Byzantine-and-Roman fortress pairing.

faq.title: What is Petra Fortress?
faq.content: Petra Fortress is a 6th-century Byzantine fortress-city at Tsikhisdziri, on the Black Sea coast about 25 km north of Batumi in Adjara, Georgia. Built under Emperor Justinian I and named "Petra" (Greek for "rock"), it guarded the southern approach to the Kingdom of Lazica and became a fiercely contested site in the Lazic War between Byzantium and Sassanid Persia. Today it's a ruined hilltop archaeological site with standing Byzantine walls and sweeping sea views, reached by a short steep walk.

faq.title: Who built Petra Fortress and when?
faq.content: It was built in the 6th century under the Byzantine Emperor Justinian I — first mentioned in 535 — developed from an earlier small Roman fort into a fortified city named Petra Pia Justiniana in his honor. The contemporary historian Procopius records that it was founded through the imperial official John Tzibus, who used it to control trade into Lazica. Its purpose was to anchor Byzantine power on the eastern Black Sea coast against Sassanid Persia.

faq.title: Why was Petra important in the Lazic War?
faq.content: Petra guarded the coastal southern gateway into Lazica (western Georgia), so whoever held it controlled access to the kingdom. During the Lazic War (about 541–562) it changed hands repeatedly: the Persians seized it in 541, the Byzantines failed to retake it in 549, and after a brutal siege in 550–551 — described in detail by Procopius — the Byzantines recaptured it and then demolished it themselves so the Persians couldn't use it again. That documented role makes it one of the best-recorded ancient sites in the region.

faq.title: How do you get to Petra Fortress from Batumi?
faq.content: It's about 25–30 minutes by car or taxi north along the coastal road, near Tsikhisdziri between Batumi and Kobuleti. Marshrutkas on the Batumi–Kobuleti coastal route pass Tsikhisdziri, so you can reach it without a car, with a short walk to the base. From there it's a fairly steep 15–20 minute climb up to the ruins. Tsikhisdziri also has good beaches, so it's easy to combine the fortress with time by the sea.

faq.title: Is Petra Fortress worth visiting?
faq.content: For travelers interested in Byzantine and ancient history, yes — it has genuine standing 6th-century walls, a remarkable documented history from the Lazic War, and one of the best coastal panoramas in the area. It's wilder and less developed than Gonio, so expect ruins and a short climb rather than a polished attraction. If you want a quiet, atmospheric historical site with a view and a real story behind it, it rewards the visit; the two fortresses (Petra north, Gonio south) make a natural Byzantine-and-Roman pairing.

mtiralaNationalPark.heroTitle: Mtirala National Park: Georgia's Colchic Rainforest near Batumi
mtiralaNationalPark.content:
Mtirala National Park occupies a section of the Adjarian highlands about 25 kilometers northeast of Batumi, in the country of Georgia in the South Caucasus, in the valley system of the Chakvistskali River and its tributaries. The park's name means "crying" in Georgian — a reference to the rainfall that defines this corner of the Caucasus more than any other feature. Mtirala is one of the wettest places in Georgia, receiving somewhere between roughly 2,500 and 4,500 millimeters of precipitation a year depending on elevation, and the result of that rainfall is a forest of extraordinary density and botanical richness — one of the finest surviving examples of Colchic rainforest in the entire Caucasus.

What is Mtirala National Park?

Mtirala National Park is a Colchic rainforest park in Adjara, Georgia, about 25 km northeast of Batumi, established in 2006 and covering roughly 15,700 hectares. Its name means "crying," after the very high rainfall (up to ~4,500 mm a year) that makes it one of the wettest places in the country and sustains a lush, moss-draped forest. Since 2021 it has been part of the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Colchic Rainforests and Wetlands." From the visitor center at Chakvistavi, marked trails lead to waterfalls, a turquoise river lake, rope crossings, and a zipline. It's a popular half-day or day trip from Batumi.

The Colchic rainforest

The Colchic forest — the ancient subtropical forest type that once covered much of the western Caucasus lowlands and foothills — has been reduced to scattered remnants by agriculture and development across most of its former range, and the examples preserved within Mtirala are among the most intact and ecologically significant survivors.
